- ✅ **Expert Distribution:** A mature tester spends time across *all* levels, not just Levels 0–1.
- ✅ **Myers' Law:** Specifications are *always* incomplete — this is the fundamental justification for Levels 2–6.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Mapping:** This topic is central to **CO1** (concepts) and supports **CO2, CO3, CO4** in later modules.
- ✅ **Exam Trivia:** When asked to "explain the levels," always include the *oracle* and the *bridge question* — these are the *valuation keywords*.
- ✅ **Real-World Anchor:** The **Therac-25** (radiation therapy) and **Boeing 737 MAX** failures are classic examples of *absent Level 3–6 thinking* — use them as illustrative anecdotes.
- ✅ **One-Line Essence:** *"The deeper your question, the deeper your defect."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing Terminologies: Verification, Validation, Fault, Error, Bug, Test Cases, and Coverage Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Testing Terminologies: Verification, Validation, Fault, Error, Bug, Test Cases, and Coverage Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Verification

**Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** *Verification* is the process of evaluating work-products (documents, design, code, programs) of a development phase to determine whether they meet the specifications established at the beginning of that phase. It is a **static** checking activity — *"Are we building the product right?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification = Static Analysis (Reviews, Inspections, Walkthroughs, Desk-Checking).**
> It does **NOT** require executing the program.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a civil engineer reviewing the *blueprint* of a bridge before construction begins. The engineer checks if the blueprint matches the original requirements (load capacity, span, material grade). The bridge has not been built yet, but the plan is being verified. **Verification = checking the paper before the product is built.**

---

## 1.2 Validation

**Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** *Validation* is the process of evaluating the final product (executable software) to determine whether it meets the user's needs and intended use. It is a **dynamic** checking activity — *"Are we building the right product?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation = Dynamic Testing (Executing the code with test inputs).**
> It **REQUIRES** running the actual program.

### Intuitive Analogy
The same bridge is now constructed. Engineers load trucks of increasing weight onto it to confirm it actually serves its real-world purpose. **Validation = testing the finished product in the real world.**

---

## 1.3 Error (Mistake)

**Definition:** An *Error* is a human mistake made by a developer, designer, or requirements engineer during the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). It is the **root cause** that leads to defects.

> [!NOTE]
> Errors exist in the **human mind** and propagate forward into the artifacts of the project (documents, code).

**Example:** A programmer types `>` instead of `>=` in a comparison statement.

---

## 1.4 Fault (Defect / Bug — Static)

**Definition:** A *Fault* is the **manifestation** of an error that is encoded into the software artifacts (requirements document, design, source code). It is the **static** representation of a mistake.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fault is also called a *defect* or *bug* in many texts. The IEEE 91 standard distinguishes:
> - **Fault** → introduced into the software (cause)
> - **Error** → human action (cause)
> - **Failure** → deviation of the software from its expected behavior (effect during execution)

---

## 1.5 Failure

**Definition:** A *Failure* is the **inability** of a software system to perform its required function within specified performance limits. It is observed only when the faulty code path is **executed** with a triggering input.

> [!NOTE]
> A fault may exist in code, but it will only result in a **failure** when that code is executed. Not all faults cause failures (e.g., dead code).

---

## 1.6 Test Case

**Definition:** A *Test Case* is a documented set of **preconditions, inputs (including data and actions), execution conditions, and expected results**, developed to verify or validate a particular software requirement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Components of a Test Case (IEEE 829 Standard):**
> 1. Test Case ID
> 2. Test Description / Objective
> 3. Pre-conditions
> 4. Test Inputs
> 5. Test Execution Steps
> 6. Expected Output
> 7. Actual Output (post-execution)
> 8. Pass / Fail Status

---

## 1.7 Coverage Criteria (Test Coverage)

**Definition:** *Coverage Criteria* (also called *Test Coverage*) are rules used to measure the extent to which a test suite **exercises** the structural elements of a software artifact (statements, branches, paths, conditions, functions, requirements).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Coverage?** Coverage criteria give a **quantitative measure** of testing thoroughness and identify **untested** parts of the code.

**Common Coverage Types:**
- **Statement Coverage** – % of executable statements executed.
- **Branch (Decision) Coverage** – % of decision outcomes (true/false) exercised.
- **Path Coverage** – % of independent paths executed.
- **Condition / Multiple-Condition Coverage** – % of boolean sub-expression values.
- **Function Coverage** – % of functions / methods called.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Causal Chain: Error → Fault → Failure

The relationship between Error, Fault, and Failure follows a strict **causal propagation chain** in the V-Model of testing:

1. **Human Error (Mistake)** → committed during requirements gathering, design, or coding.
2. **Fault (Defect)** → the mistake is *encoded* into a software work-product (incorrect requirement, wrong condition in code).
3. **Failure** → the fault is *activated* during execution by a specific input, producing a deviation from expected output.

> [!NOTE]
> A failure is **observable**. A fault is **latent** — it hides inside the code until triggered. This is why **statement coverage = 100%** does **NOT** guarantee all faults are revealed.

### Why Verification + Validation Together?

| Activity | Question Answered | When | Type | Who Performs |
|----------|------------------|------|------|--------------|
| **Verification** | Are we building the product *right*? | Before execution | Static | Developers, QA Reviewers |
| **Validation** | Are we building the *right* product? | During / after execution | Dynamic | Testers, End Users |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Coverage Metrics

The following metrics are **directly assessable** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all formulas below:
> - $S$ = total statements (or branches, conditions, etc.)
> - $E$ = number of items *executed* by the test suite
> - $T$ = total number of items *required* to be executed for full coverage.

### Coverage Percentage Formula

$$\text{Coverage (\%)} = \frac{E}{T} \times 100$$

### Statement Coverage

$$\text{Statement Coverage} = \frac{\text{Number of Executed Statements}}{\text{Total Number of Executable Statements}} \times 100$$

### Branch / Decision Coverage

$$\text{Branch Coverage} = \frac{\text{Number of Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Number of Branches}} \times 100$$

### Cyclomatic Complexity → Number of Independent Paths (McCabe, 1976)

For a control flow graph with $V$ nodes, $E$ edges, and $P$ connected components:

$$M(G) = E - V + 2P$$

For a single connected module, the simpler form is:

$$M(G) = \text{Number of Predicate Nodes} + 1$$

$$M(G) = \text{Edges} - \text{Nodes} + 2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cyclomatic Complexity $M(G)$** also gives the **minimum number of independent test paths** required for **path coverage**.

### KTU Cheat-Sheet Table — Coverage Levels (Hierarchy)

| Coverage Type | What is measured | Formula Symbol | Strength |
|---------------|------------------|----------------|----------|
| **Statement** | Executable lines hit | $SC$ | Weakest |
| **Branch (Decision)** | True / False outcomes hit | $BC$ | Stronger |
| **Condition** | Boolean sub-expression values | $CC$ | Stronger |
| **Multiple-Condition** | All combinations of sub-conditions | $MCC$ | Even stronger |
| **Path** | All independent paths | $PC$ | Strongest (often infeasible) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Subsumption Rule:** $PC \Rightarrow MCC \Rightarrow BC \Rightarrow SC$ (Higher coverage generally subsumes lower coverage, but not always — e.g., 100% branch coverage does not guarantee 100% statement coverage in some edge cases).

---

## 2.3 Verification & Validation (V&V) Activities — Engineering Utility

| V&V Activity | Real-world Engineering Use |
|--------------|-----------------------------|
| Technical Reviews | Design defect detection in aerospace / automotive software (DO-178C, ISO 26262) |
| Inspections (Fagan) | Banking & medical-device software where failure cost is high |
| Unit Testing | Validating individual C / C++ / Java modules in embedded systems |
| Integration Testing | Validating inter-module communication in IoT firmware |
| System Testing | Validating complete embedded / web / mobile application |
| Acceptance Testing | User-acceptance of ERP / E-commerce platforms (UAT) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **V-Model** explicitly maps each development phase to a corresponding V&V activity, which is a **high-weightage KTU question**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Error vs Fault vs Failure

| Property | **Error** | **Fault (Defect)** | **Failure** |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Origin** | Human mind | Software artifact (code/doc) | Runtime behavior |
| **Static / Dynamic** | Static (mental) | Static (in code) | Dynamic (observed during execution) |
| **Also Called** | Mistake | Bug, Defect | Crash, Incorrect Output |
| **Detected By** | Inspection, Training | Review, Static Analysis | Execution, Testing |
| **Cost to Fix** | Cheapest (prevention) | Medium (before release) | Most expensive (after release) |
| **Phase Created** | Requirements / Design / Code | Any development phase | Post-deployment / Testing |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Coverage Calculation

**Problem:** A Java program contains the following code segment:

```java
1.  int x, y;
2.  Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
3.  x = sc.nextInt();
4.  y = sc.nextInt();
5.  if (x > 0 && y > 0) {
6.      System.out.println("Q1");
7.  } else {
8.      System.out.println("Not Q1");
9.  }
```

A test suite executes the program with the following inputs:

| Test ID | x | y | Path Executed |
|---------|---|---|---------------|
| T1 | 5 | 3 | Line 5 TRUE → Line 6 |
| T2 | -1 | 5 | Line 5 FALSE → Line 8 |

### Step 1 — Identify Executable Statements

Lines **3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8** are executable. Line 1, 2, 9 (else close) are not "executable logic" lines; we focus on logical statements.

Total executable statements: $T = 4$ (namely, the assignment at line 3, line 4, line 6, line 8).

> Executed statements: $E = 4$ (lines 3, 4, 6, 8 in T1; lines 3, 4, 6, 8 in T2 — line 6 in T1 and line 8 in T2 ⇒ both lines covered).

### Step 2 — Statement Coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SC} &= \frac{\text{Executed Statements}}{\text{Total Executable Statements}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4}{4} \times 100 = \mathbf{100\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Branch Coverage

Total branches = **2** (TRUE branch of line 5, FALSE branch of line 5).

- T1 exercises TRUE → 1 branch.
- T2 exercises FALSE → 1 branch.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BC} &= \frac{\text{Executed Branches}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2}{2} \times 100 = \mathbf{100\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Condition Coverage

The compound condition is `x > 0 && y > 0`. The two sub-conditions are:
- $C_1$: $x > 0$
- $C_2$: $y > 0$

Total atomic boolean values = $2 \times 2 = 4$ combinations.

| Test | $C_1$ Result | $C_2$ Result |
|------|--------------|--------------|
| T1 (5, 3) | TRUE | TRUE |
| T2 (-1, 5) | FALSE | TRUE |

Only **3** of the 4 combinations (T,T / F,T) are observed. The (T,F) and (F,F) combinations are missed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CC} &= \frac{3}{4} \times 100 = \mathbf{75\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Cyclomatic Complexity (Number of Independent Paths)

The control flow graph has:
- Predicate node at line 5 (one decision).
- $P = 1$ connected component.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(G) &= \text{Predicate Nodes} + 1 \\[4pt]
&= 1 + 1 = \mathbf{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

So at least **2** independent test paths are required for full path coverage — which our 2-test suite already satisfies for this simple program.

---

## 3.3 Verification vs Validation — Decision Flowchart (Textual)

1. **Is the artifact executable code?**
 - **No** → You are in **Verification** (reviewing documents, designs, specifications).
 - **Yes** → Proceed to step 2.
2. **Are you executing the code with test data and observing the output?**
 - **Yes** → You are in **Validation**.
 - **No** → Still in **Verification** (e.g., syntax checking, linting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This flowchart is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**: *"Differentiate Verification and Validation with examples."*

---

## 3.4 The Bug-Life-Cycle (Defect Life-Cycle)

This is a **guaranteed question** in the KTU ESE Module 1:

1. **New** → Tester logs a defect.
2. **Assigned** → Lead assigns to a developer.
3. **Open** → Developer starts working.
4. **Fixed** → Developer resolves the defect.
5. **Retest** → Tester re-runs the test case.
6. **Reopened** → If still failing, sent back to developer.
7. **Closed** → If passing, defect is marked closed.
8. **Rejected / Deferred** → If not a real defect or postponed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — V-Model of V&V Activities

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeReq["Requirements Analysis"]
    nodeSys["System Design"]
    nodeArc["Architectural Design"]
    nodeMod["Module Design"]
    nodeCode["Coding"]
    nodeUT["Unit Testing"]
    nodeIT["Integration Testing"]
    nodeST["System Testing"]
    nodeAT["Acceptance Testing"]

    nodeReq --> nodeSys --> nodeArc --> nodeMod --> nodeCode
    nodeCode -.Unit.-> nodeUT
    nodeMod -.Integration.-> nodeIT
    nodeArc -.System.-> nodeST
    nodeSys -.Acceptance.-> nodeAT

    nodeUT -.Validates.-> nodeMod
    nodeIT -.Validates.-> nodeArc
    nodeST -.Validates.-> nodeSys
    nodeAT -.Validates.-> nodeReq
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The left arm (top → bottom) represents the **development** phases.
- The right arm (bottom → top) represents the **testing** phases.
- Each test level **validates** the corresponding development phase.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Causal Chain (Error → Fault → Failure)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Human Error / Mistake"]
    nodeB["Fault Embedded in Code"]
    nodeC["Triggered by Input"]
    nodeD["Failure Observed"]

    nodeA -- Encoded into artifact --> nodeB
    nodeB -- Executed with input --> nodeC
    nodeC -- Deviation from expected --> nodeD
```

> [!NOTE]
> This causal chain is what makes **testing probabilistic** — you can write a 100% statement coverage test suite and still miss a fault if no test case triggers the failing path.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Test Case Anatomy (IEEE 829)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeTC["Test Case"]
    nodeID["Test Case ID"]
    nodeObj["Objective / Description"]
    nodePre["Preconditions"]
    nodeInp["Test Inputs"]
    nodeStep["Execution Steps"]
    nodeExp["Expected Output"]
    nodeAct["Actual Output"]
    nodeStat["Pass / Fail Status"]

    nodeTC --> nodeID
    nodeTC --> nodeObj
    nodeTC --> nodePre
    nodeTC --> nodeInp
    nodeTC --> nodeStep
    nodeTC --> nodeExp
    nodeTC --> nodeAct
    nodeTC --> nodeStat
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coverage Criteria Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeSC["Statement Coverage"]
    nodeBC["Branch Coverage"]
    nodeCC["Condition Coverage"]
    nodeMCC["Multiple Condition Coverage"]
    nodePC["Path Coverage"]

    nodeSC --> nodeBC
    nodeBC --> nodeCC
    nodeCC --> nodeMCC
    nodeMCC --> nodePC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The arrows denote *strength of coverage* — higher tier subsumes (in most cases) the lower tier. **Path Coverage is the strongest** but often **infeasible** for large programs (McCabe's theorem).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between Verification and Validation. Provide one example of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| S.No | Verification | Validation |
|------|--------------|------------|
| 1 | It checks *"Are we building the product right?"* | It checks *"Are we building the right product?"* |
| 2 | Static activity (no execution needed) | Dynamic activity (program is executed) |
| 3 | Done on documents, design, code by inspection / reviews | Done on running software with test inputs |
| 4 | Catches defects *before* execution | Catches defects *during* execution |
| 5 | Example: Reviewing a Software Requirements Specification (SRS) document | Example: Running unit tests on a Java method to check its output |

---

### Q2. Define the terms *Error*, *Fault*, and *Failure*. How are they related? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Error:** A human mistake made during any phase of the SDLC (e.g., misinterpreting a requirement).
- **Fault:** The *manifestation* of an error that gets encoded into a software artifact such as source code (also called a *bug* or *defect*).
- **Failure:** A deviation of the software from its expected behaviour, observed when a faulty code path is *exercised* with appropriate input.

**Relationship:** An *Error* committed by a human leads to a *Fault* in the code; when that fault is triggered during execution, it produces a *Failure* observable to the user.

> [!NOTE]
> Faults may remain *latent* (undetected) if the triggering input is never provided. Therefore 100% statement coverage does not guarantee 100% fault detection.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the Verification and Validation (V&V) activities in the Software Development Life Cycle. Discuss the V-Model with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Verification and Validation (V&V) are complementary quality-assurance activities used throughout the SDLC to ensure that the software conforms to its specifications and meets user needs.

**Verification Activities (2 marks):** Performed at the end of *each development phase*, before code execution. They include:
- Technical reviews of SRS, SDD, STD documents.
- Walkthroughs and inspections (Fagan inspections).
- Desk-checking of designs.
- Static analysis tools (linters, complexity analyzers).

**Validation Activities (2 marks):** Performed during and after code is built. They include:
- Unit testing, Integration testing, System testing, Acceptance testing.
- Performance, load, and stress testing.
- User Acceptance Testing (UAT) by end users.

**V-Model (2 marks):**
The V-Model is an extension of the waterfall model in which each development phase is associated with a corresponding testing phase. Development progresses *down the left arm*, and testing progresses *up the right arm*.

> *(Insert / sketch the V-Model diagram showing: Requirements ↔ Acceptance Testing, System Design ↔ System Testing, Architectural Design ↔ Integration Testing, Module Design ↔ Unit Testing, Coding at the vertex.)*

---

#### (b) Define a *Test Case*. List the essential components of a well-formed test case as per IEEE 829. Write a sample test case for a login module that accepts username and password and grants access only if both match. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A *Test Case* is a documented set of preconditions, inputs, execution steps, expected results, and post-conditions developed for a particular test objective.

**Components of a Test Case as per IEEE 829 (3 marks):**
1. Test Case Identifier (TC_ID)
2. Test Case Name / Description
3. Test Objective
4. Pre-conditions
5. Test Inputs (data + actions)
6. Test Procedure / Steps
7. Expected Output / Result
8. Actual Output
9. Pass / Fail Criterion
10. Post-conditions
11. Author and Date

**Sample Test Case (3 marks):**

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **TC_ID** | TC_LOGIN_001 |
| **Module** | Login Authentication |
| **Objective** | Verify that valid credentials grant access |
| **Pre-conditions** | User account "admin / admin@123" exists in the database |
| **Test Inputs** | Username = `admin`, Password = `admin@123` |
| **Execution Steps** | 1. Open login page. 2. Enter `admin` in username field. 3. Enter `admin@123` in password field. 4. Click *Login* button. |
| **Expected Output** | User is redirected to the home page; welcome message "Hello, admin" is displayed. |
| **Actual Output** | _(to be filled after execution)_ |
| **Pass / Fail** | Pass if redirected; Fail otherwise. |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) What is a *Coverage Criterion*? Explain Statement Coverage, Branch Coverage, and Condition Coverage with suitable examples and formulas. Compute the cyclomatic complexity of the given pseudo-code. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A *Coverage Criterion* is a rule that defines a set of test requirements (e.g., all statements, all branches, all conditions) and a measure of how completely a test suite satisfies them.

**Statement Coverage (1.5 marks):** Measures the percentage of executable statements exercised.

$$\text{SC} = \frac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Executable Statements}} \times 100$$

*Example:* For 10 statements, if the test suite executes 8, SC = 80%.

**Branch Coverage (1.5 marks):** Measures the percentage of decision outcomes (true/false) exercised.

$$\text{BC} = \frac{\text{Branches Executed}}{\text{Total Branches}} \times 100$$

*Example:* An `if-else` block has 2 branches; if only the *true* branch is hit, BC = 50%.

**Condition Coverage (1.5 marks):** Measures the percentage of boolean sub-expression outcomes within a compound condition.

$$\text{CC} = \frac{\text{Boolean Sub-Expression Outcomes Observed}}{\text{Total Boolean Outcomes}} \times 100$$

*Example:* `if (A && B)` has 4 boolean outcomes: (T,T), (T,F), (F,T), (F,F). If 3 are observed, CC = 75%.

**Cyclomatic Complexity (1.5 marks):** For a single-function CFG:

$$M(G) = E - V + 2$$

or equivalently, $M(G) = \text{Number of Predicate Nodes} + 1$.

This gives the **minimum number of independent test paths** required for full path coverage.

---

#### (b) Consider the following Java method. Design a minimum set of test cases to achieve 100% statement coverage and 100% branch coverage. Show your coverage calculations. **(7 Marks)**

```java
public String grade(int marks) {
    String result;
    if (marks >= 90) {
        result = "A";
    } else if (marks >= 75) {
        result = "B";
    } else {
        result = "C";
    }
    return result;
}
```

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify Branches (1 mark):**
- Branch 1: `marks >= 90` TRUE
- Branch 2: `marks >= 90` FALSE → `marks >= 75` TRUE
- Branch 3: `marks >= 90` FALSE → `marks >= 75` FALSE → else

**Step 2 — Test Cases (3 marks):**

| TC_ID | Input `marks` | Branch Executed | Statement Executed | Expected Output |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| TC1 | 95 | Branch 1 (TRUE) | "A" assignment | "A" |
| TC2 | 80 | Branch 2 (TRUE of else-if) | "B" assignment | "B" |
| TC3 | 50 | Branch 3 (else) | "C" assignment | "C" |

**Step 3 — Coverage Calculations (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Statement Coverage} &= \frac{\text{All 3 result assignments covered}}{3} \times 100 = \mathbf{100\%} \\[6pt]
\text{Branch Coverage} &= \frac{\text{All 3 branches covered}}{3} \times 100 = \mathbf{100\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cyclomatic Complexity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(G) &= \text{Predicate Nodes} + 1 \\[4pt]
&= 2 + 1 = \mathbf{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

So a **minimum of 3 independent test paths** are required — and our 3-test suite satisfies this exactly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. Confusing **Verification with Validation** — students often write "V&V are the same." Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. Writing **Error = Fault = Bug** without distinguishing. Examiners expect the causal chain *Error → Fault → Failure*. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. Forgetting the **denominator** in coverage formulas (e.g., computing branches covered instead of *total* branches). Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. In V-Model questions, failing to **map each test level to a development level**. Loss: 2 marks.
> 5. In Cyclomatic Complexity, using $V - E + 2$ (sign-flipped) — this is the **most common** formula error. Always $E - V + 2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Verification** = static, *building the product right*.
- ✅ **Validation** = dynamic, *building the right product*.
- ✅ **Error** → committed by humans (mental).
- ✅ **Fault (Defect / Bug)** → encoded in artifact (static).
- ✅ **Failure** → observed at runtime when fault is triggered (dynamic).
- ✅ **Test Case** follows IEEE 829 components: ID, objective, pre-conditions, inputs, steps, expected/actual output, pass/fail.
- ✅ **Coverage Criteria Hierarchy:** Statement ⊂ Branch ⊂ Condition ⊂ Multiple-Condition ⊂ Path.
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity** $M(G) = E - V + 2$ = number of predicate nodes + 1 = minimum independent paths.
- ✅ **V-Model** maps each development phase to a corresponding test phase.
- ✅ **100% statement coverage ≠ 100% fault detection** (always re-emphasize in long answers for 1 extra mark).
- ✅ **Bug Life Cycle:** New → Assigned → Open → Fixed → Retest → Closed (or Reopened/Rejected).
- ✅ Coverage formula: $\text{Coverage (\%)} = \frac{\text{Items Executed}}{\text{Total Items}} \times 100$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Testing: Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance, Performance (Stress, Usability, Regression), and Security Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Testing: Unit, Integration, System, Acceptance, Performance (Stress, Usability, Regression), and Security Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic directly maps to **CO1** of OECST833 — *"Understand the fundamental principles, taxonomy, and lifecycle placement of various software testing techniques."* Questions from this topic typically appear as **3-mark definitions** or **14-mark descriptive answers** in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

---

## 1.1 What is Software Testing?

> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Software Testing is the process of executing a program or application with the intent of finding software bugs, verifying that the system meets its specified requirements (IEEE Std 829), and validating its fitness for purpose in an operational environment.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Glenford Myers' Classical Definition (1979):** *"Testing is the process of executing a program with the intent of finding errors."* — This is the most quoted definition in KTU valuation key answers.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Car Manufacturing Line

Imagine a car factory:
- **Unit Testing** = Checking each individual part (brake pad, spark plug, mirror) before assembly.
- **Integration Testing** = Fitting the parts together and checking if the brake works with the wheel.
- **System Testing** = Driving the entire car on a test track to ensure everything works as a whole.
- **Acceptance Testing** = Letting a real customer take a test drive and approve the car.
- **Performance Testing** = Pushing the car to its limits — top speed (Stress), ease of dashboard use (Usability), and ensuring old features still work after new upgrades (Regression).
- **Security Testing** = Hiring a thief to try breaking into the car to expose vulnerabilities.

This is exactly how software is tested — layer by layer, from the smallest unit to the full, real-world system.

---

## 1.2 Taxonomy of Software Testing — The KTU Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classification Rule:** Software Testing is broadly classified into **Functional Testing** (verifies *what* the system does) and **Non-Functional Testing** (verifies *how well* the system does it). All testing types below fall under one of these two umbrellas.

### A. Functional Testing Types

| # | Testing Type | Layer | Primary Question Answered |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Unit Testing** | Lowest (Module Level) | Does each individual function/module work correctly? |
| 2 | **Integration Testing** | Module Interaction | Do combined modules communicate correctly? |
| 3 | **System Testing** | Complete System | Does the full integrated system meet requirements? |
| 4 | **Acceptance Testing** | End-User / Business | Is the system ready for production release? |

### B. Non-Functional Testing Types

| # | Testing Type | Quality Attribute | Primary Question Answered |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | **Performance Testing** | Speed, Stability, Scalability | How does the system behave under load? |
| 6 | **Stress Testing** | Robustness beyond limits | What happens at breaking point? |
| 7 | **Usability Testing** | User Experience (UX) | Can real users accomplish goals easily? |
| 8 | **Regression Testing** | Stability after change | Do old features still work after new changes? |
| 9 | **Security Testing** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA) | Is the system safe from threats? |

---

## 1.3 Formal Definitions (Board-Exam Ready)

> **Unit Testing:** The testing of individual software units (smallest testable parts, typically functions, methods, or classes) in isolation from the rest of the system. Usually performed by developers using **white-box testing** techniques.

> **Integration Testing:** Testing performed to expose faults in the interaction between integrated units/modules. It focuses on the **interfaces**, **data flow**, and **control flow** between components.

> **System Testing:** Testing the complete and integrated software system as a whole. It verifies that the system meets its specified **functional and non-functional requirements** (IEEE Std 830). Typically performed in an environment that mirrors production.

> **Acceptance Testing:** Formal testing conducted to determine whether a system satisfies its **acceptance criteria** — enabling the end-user/customer to decide whether to accept the system. Sub-types include **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)**, **Operational Acceptance Testing (OAT)**, **Contract Acceptance Testing**, and **Alpha/Beta Testing**.

> **Performance Testing:** A type of non-functional testing used to determine the system's **responsiveness**, **throughput**, and **stability** under a particular workload. Common sub-types include **Load Testing**, **Stress Testing**, **Endurance Testing**, and **Spike Testing**.

> **Stress Testing:** A subset of performance testing that evaluates system behavior **beyond normal/peak operational capacity** — typically up to and beyond the **breaking point** — to determine how it fails and whether it recovers gracefully.

> **Usability Testing:** A black-box testing technique that evaluates a product by **testing it on real users** to measure ease of use, learnability, efficiency, error rates, and user satisfaction (ISO 9241-11).

> **Regression Testing:** Re-execution of selected test cases (existing/reusable test suites) to ensure that **modifications (bug fixes, enhancements, configuration changes) have not caused unintended side effects** in previously working functionality.

> **Security Testing:** A type of non-functional testing that uncovers **vulnerabilities, threats, and risks** in a software application, ensuring that the system is protected from **intruders, viruses, and unauthorized access**, while maintaining **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Unit Testing — The Foundation Level

### Operational Concept
Unit testing operates at the **finest granularity** of the software. The "unit" can be a function, method, procedure, class, or module. Since units are tested in isolation, **test doubles (stubs, drivers, mocks, fakes)** are used to replace dependencies.

### Key Terminology (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Term | Meaning | KTU Distinction |
|---|---|---|
| **Stub** | A minimal implementation that returns hard-coded data | Used in **Bottom-Up** integration |
| **Driver** | A main program that calls the unit under test | Used in **Bottom-Up** integration |
| **Mock** | An object with pre-programmed expectations | Verifies interactions |
| **Fake** | A working implementation unsuitable for production (e.g., in-memory DB) | Lightweight substitute |

> [!NOTE]
> **Popular Frameworks (Know at least 2 for KTU viva):** JUnit (Java), NUnit (.NET), pytest (Python), Google Test (C++), Mocha (JavaScript).

### Why Unit Testing? (Justification for 14-mark answers)
1. **Early Bug Detection** — Cheaper to fix bugs at unit level than at system level (cost ratio 1:10:100:1000 across testing phases).
2. **Code Refactoring Confidence** — A robust unit test suite allows developers to safely restructure code.
3. **Living Documentation** — Tests describe how a function is *intended* to be used.
4. **Improves Design** — Writing testable code forces loose coupling and high cohesion.

### Code Coverage Metrics for Unit Testing

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Statement Coverage | (Statements Executed / Total Statements) × 100 | Are all lines executed? |
| Branch Coverage | (Branches Executed / Total Branches) × 100 | Are all if/else paths tested? |
| Function Coverage | (Functions Called / Total Functions) × 100 | Are all functions invoked? |
| Line Coverage | (Lines Executed / Total Lines) × 100 | Coverage by line |
| Condition/MCDC Coverage | Complex Boolean predicates | Each condition must independently affect outcome |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick Question:** *"100% statement coverage does NOT imply 100% branch coverage."* — A single `if` without `else` could be 100% statement covered but only 50% branch covered.

---

## 2.2 Integration Testing — The Interface Validator

### Strategies (Core KTU 14-Mark Topic)

#### A. Big-Bang Integration Testing
- All modules are integrated simultaneously and tested as a whole.
- **Advantage:** Useful for small systems.
- **Disadvantage:** Localization of faults is difficult; debugging is delayed.

#### B. Incremental Integration Testing
Modules are integrated and tested one at a time (or in small groups). Three approaches:

##### i. Top-Down Integration
- Testing proceeds from **top-level modules** downward to lower-level modules.
- **Stubs** are used to replace lower-level modules.
- **Advantages:** Early validation of high-level logic; no need for drivers.
- **Disadvantages:** Lower modules tested late; stubs can be simplistic.

##### ii. Bottom-Up Integration
- Testing proceeds from **bottom-level modules** upward.
- **Drivers** are used to call lower-level modules.
- **Advantages:** No stubs needed; no time wasted on stub development.
- **Disadvantages:** Top-level design defects discovered late; drivers must be written.

##### iii. Sandwich (Hybrid) Integration
- Combines top-down and bottom-up approaches.
- Top-level uses stubs; bottom-level uses drivers; meets at the middle layer.
- **Best for large systems** like banking or e-commerce platforms.

### Comparison Table (Board-Exam Favorite)

| Feature | Top-Down | Bottom-Up | Sandwich |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direction | Top → Bottom | Bottom → Top | Both directions |
| Test Doubles Used | Stubs | Drivers | Both |
| Fault Localization | Hard (top) / Easy (bottom) | Easy (bottom) / Hard (top) | Moderate |
| Early Skeleton Available | Yes | No | Yes |
| Suitable For | Hierarchical systems | Utility-heavy systems | Large distributed systems |

---

## 2.3 System Testing — The Holistic View

### Categories of System Testing (KTU Standard)

| Category | What It Verifies |
|---|---|
| **Functional Testing** | Feature behavior against requirements |
| **Performance Testing** | Speed, throughput under load |
| **Load Testing** | Behavior at expected peak load |
| **Stress Testing** | Behavior beyond peak load (overload) |
| **Volume Testing** | Behavior with large amounts of data |
| **Security Testing** | Resistance to unauthorized access |
| **Compatibility Testing** | Behavior across browsers/OS/devices |
| **Usability Testing** | Ease of use for end-users |
| **Recovery Testing** | Ability to recover from crashes |
| **Installation Testing** | Smooth installation on target machines |
| **Regression Testing** | Stability after changes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "FLSPVCURRI":** Functional, Load, Stress, Performance, Volume, Compatibility, Usability, Recovery, Regression, Installation — the 10 sub-categories examiners love to ask about.

---

## 2.4 Acceptance Testing — The User's Verdict

### Sub-Types (Board Exam Favorite)

| Sub-Type | Performed By | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** | End users / customers | Validates business requirements |
| **Operational Acceptance Testing (OAT)** | System administrators | Validates operational readiness (backup, recovery, security policies) |
| **Contract Acceptance Testing** | Against contract terms | Verifies contractual SLAs |
| **Regulatory/Compliance Acceptance** | Auditors | Verifies legal/regulatory compliance (GDPR, HIPAA) |
| **Alpha Testing** | Internal testers at developer's site | Identects bugs before customer exposure |
| **Beta Testing** | Selected external users at customer site | Real-world feedback before release |

### Acceptance Criteria (Definition of Done)
- All critical and high-priority defects closed.
- All business workflows executed end-to-end successfully.
- Performance benchmarks met.
- User documentation complete and approved.

---

## 2.5 Performance Testing — The Speed & Stability Validator

### Sub-Categories

| Sub-Type | Load Condition | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Load Testing** | Expected normal and peak load | Verify response times within SLAs |
| **Stress Testing** | Beyond peak (2x, 5x, 10x normal) | Identify breaking point & graceful degradation |
| **Endurance (Soak) Testing** | Sustained normal load for long duration | Detect memory leaks, resource exhaustion |
| **Spike Testing** | Sudden burst of load | Verify recovery from sudden traffic spikes |
| **Volume Testing** | Massive data volume | Verify DB performance with huge datasets |
| **Scalability Testing** | Increasing load | Verify ability to scale up/out |

### KTU High-Yield Performance Metrics Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formulas — Memorize these for KTU numerical/derivation questions.**

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Response Time** | $T_{response} = T_{end} - T_{start}$ | ms / seconds | Time between request and response |
| **Throughput** | $Throughput = \dfrac{N_{transactions}}{T_{duration}}$ | req/sec or TPS | Transactions processed per unit time |
| **Latency** | $T_{latency} = T_{first\_byte} - T_{request}$ | ms | Delay before first response byte |
| **Concurrent Users** | $U_{concurrent} = \dfrac{N_{total}}{T_{session} / T_{think}}$ | users | Users active at any instant |
| **Hits per Second (HPS)** | $HPS = \dfrac{N_{requests}}{T_{seconds}}$ | hits/sec | Total HTTP requests per second |
| **Transactions per Second (TPS)** | $TPS = \dfrac{N_{transactions}}{T_{seconds}}$ | TPS | Business transactions per second |
| **Error Rate** | $E_{rate} = \dfrac{N_{errors}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Percentage of failed requests |
| **CPU Utilization** | $CPU_{\%} = \dfrac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Processor busy time |
| **Memory Utilization** | $Mem_{\%} = \dfrac{M_{used}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | % | RAM used |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total\_operation}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Average uptime between failures |
| **Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{downtime}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Average time to restore service |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ here is the LaTeX vertical bar inside a math expression — it does NOT break the markdown table because it's inside `$$...$$` math mode, not as a raw pipe character in the table cell.

### Example Calculation (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A web server handles **4500 requests in 60 seconds**, of which **45 returned 500 errors**. Calculate Throughput, TPS (assuming each request = 1 transaction), and Error Rate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Throughput &= \frac{4500 \text{ requests}}{60 \text{ seconds}} = 75 \text{ requests/sec} \\
TPS &= \frac{4500}{60} = 75 \text{ TPS} \\
E_{rate} &= \frac{45}{4500} \times 100 = 1.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 2.6 Regression Testing — The Stability Guardian

### When Regression Testing is Triggered
1. New feature added.
2. Bug fix deployed.
3. Performance optimization done.
4. Environment/configuration change (e.g., OS upgrade, library update).
5. Patch or hotfix applied.

### Regression Test Suite Selection Techniques

| Technique | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Retest All** | Re-execute the entire test suite | Most thorough; expensive |
| **Regression Test Selection** | Select a subset of test cases based on code change | Cost-effective |
| **Test Case Prioritization** | Order selected test cases to maximize early defect detection | Optimal for time-constrained testing |

### Three Categories of Test Cases in Regression Suite
1. **Reusable Test Cases** — Can be reused in subsequent cycles (manual or automated).
2. **Re-Usable but Manual** — Manual execution required.
3. **Obsolete Test Cases** — No longer applicable to current version; should be archived.

---

## 2.7 Security Testing — The CIA Triad Defender

### The CIA Triad (Core Security Testing Concept)

| Pillar | Definition | Example Attack Tested Against |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Only authorized users access data | SQL Injection, Data Breach |
| **Integrity** | Data is not altered by unauthorized parties | Man-in-the-Middle, Data Tampering |
| **Availability** | System remains accessible to authorized users | Denial of Service (DoS), DDoS |

### Common Security Testing Techniques

| Technique | What It Identifies |
|---|---|
| **Vulnerability Scanning** | Known vulnerabilities in systems |
| **Penetration Testing (Pen Testing)** | Simulated attacks by ethical hackers |
| **Security Auditing** | Code-level review against security standards (OWASP, NIST) |
| **Ethical Hacking** | Authorized attempt to breach security |
| **Risk Assessment** | Identifies, analyzes, and evaluates security risks |
| **Posture Assessment** | Overall security posture evaluation |

### OWASP Top 10 (Memorize for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OWASP Top 10 — 2021 Edition:**
> 1. Broken Access Control
> 2. Cryptographic Failures
> 3. Injection (SQL, NoSQL, LDAP, OS Command)
> 4. Insecure Design
> 5. Security Misconfiguration
> 6. Vulnerable and Outdated Components
> 7. Identification and Authentication Failures
> 8. Software and Data Integrity Failures
> 9. Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
> 10. Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)

### Security Testing Formula Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Vulnerability Density** | $V_{density} = \dfrac{N_{vulnerabilities}}{KLOC}$ | Vulnerabilities per 1000 lines of code |
| **Mean Time To Patch (MTTP)** | $MTTP = \dfrac{\sum (T_{patch} - T_{discovered})}{N_{vulns}}$ | Average time to fix vulnerabilities |
| **Risk Exposure** | $Risk = Probability \times Impact$ | Prioritize which vulnerabilities to fix first |
| **CVSS Score Range** | $0 \leq CVSS \leq 10$ | Common Vulnerability Scoring System severity |

---

## 2.8 The Testing Pyramid (Industry Standard Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mike Cohn's Test Pyramid (2009):** A canonical model that KTU frequently references for the **distribution** of testing types.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Test Pyramid (bottom-heavy)}: \\
\text{Layer 4 (Top)} &: \text{E2E / UI Tests (few, slow, brittle)} \\
\text{Layer 3} &: \text{Integration Tests (moderate)} \\
\text{Layer 2} &: \text{Component / API Tests} \\
\text{Layer 1 (Bottom)} &: \text{Unit Tests (many, fast, cheap)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this pyramid?** Unit tests are fastest, cheapest, and most reliable; UI/E2E tests are slowest, most expensive, and most brittle.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Testing Strategies

---

## 3.1 Detailed Analytical Comparison of Testing Levels (KTU 14-Mark Format)

### Step 1: Identify the Four Levels of Testing

Software testing in a V-Model follows a layered approach:

$$
\text{Levels} = \{L_1 = \text{Unit}, L_2 = \text{Integration}, L_3 = \text{System}, L_4 = \text{Acceptance}\}
$$

### Step 2: Define Each Level's Boundary

Each level has a **scope** that defines its boundaries:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Scope}(L_1) &= \text{Single module, function, or class} \\
\text{Scope}(L_2) &= \text{2 or more modules interacting} \\
\text{Scope}(L_3) &= \text{Complete integrated system} \\
\text{Scope}(L_4) &= \text{System + User / Business environment}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Determine Who Performs Each Level

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &: \text{Developer (white-box)} \\
L_2 &: \text{Developer or Independent Tester} \\
L_3 &: \text{Independent Test Team (black-box)} \\
L_4 &: \text{End User / Customer (black-box)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Defect-Cost Multiplier

A classic KTU analytical question: *"Why detect defects early?"*

Let $C_d$ be the cost of fixing a defect detected at level $L_d$. The empirical Boehm curve gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{unit} &= C_0 \\
C_{integration} &\approx 10 \cdot C_0 \\
C_{system} &\approx 100 \cdot C_0 \\
C_{acceptance} &\approx 1000 \cdot C_0 \\
C_{production} &\approx 10000 \cdot C_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A defect that costs **₹100** to fix at the unit level may cost **₹1,00,000** to fix once the system is in production. This justifies the testing pyramid's heavy unit-test base.

### Step 5: Map to SDLC Phases (V-Model Mapping)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Requirements Specification} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Acceptance Testing} \\
\text{System Design} &\longleftrightarrow \text{System Testing} \\
\text{Detailed Design} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Integration Testing} \\
\text{Coding} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Unit Testing}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each development phase has a corresponding test phase on the right side of the V-Model.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Test Case Coverage Calculation

> **KTU Typical Problem:** *"Given 200 lines of executable code, 150 lines were executed during testing. Calculate statement coverage and explain its limitation."*

### Step 1: Define Statement Coverage

$$
\text{Statement Coverage} = \frac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Statement Coverage} &= \frac{150}{200} \times 100 \\
&= 0.75 \times 100 \\
&= 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Interpret the Result

A coverage of **75%** means **25% of statements remain untested** — a quality gap.

### Step 4: Discuss Limitation

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** Many students write "75% coverage means 75% bug-free." This is WRONG. Even 100% statement coverage cannot guarantee the absence of:
> - Untested logical branches (use branch coverage).
> - Untested combinations (use MC/DC coverage).
> - Missing requirements (use traceability matrix).

### Step 5: Improve Coverage

To increase coverage, additional test cases must be designed targeting the **untested branches and boundary values**.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Analysis: Performance Testing vs. Stress Testing

### Derivation of Stress Test Acceptance Criteria

> **Problem:** A banking application handles a peak load of **1000 concurrent users** during salary days. The system should maintain **response time ≤ 2 seconds** with **error rate < 0.5%**. Design a stress test plan.

#### Step 1: Establish Baseline (Load Test)

Run load test at normal expected load:

$$
L_{normal} = 1000 \text{ concurrent users}
$$

Verify SLA: $T_{response} \leq 2s$ and $E_{rate} < 0.5\%$.

#### Step 2: Define Stress Levels

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= 1.5 \times L_{normal} = 1500 \text{ users} \\
L_2 &= 2.0 \times L_{normal} = 2000 \text{ users} \\
L_3 &= 5.0 \times L_{normal} = 5000 \text{ users} \\
L_{break} &= \text{Load at which system fails or degrades unacceptably}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3: Define Acceptance Criteria per Stress Level

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &: T_{response} \leq 3s,\ E_{rate} < 1\% \\
L_2 &: T_{response} \leq 5s,\ E_{rate} < 5\% \\
L_3 &: \text{System should fail gracefully, not crash} \\
L_{break} &: \text{System may crash, but logs must be captured}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Identify Failure Modes to Observe

1. Memory exhaustion.
2. Database connection pool exhaustion.
3. Thread deadlock.
4. Network bandwidth saturation.
5. Disk I/O bottleneck.

#### Step 5: Recovery Verification

After stress removal, verify:

$$
T_{recovery} \leq 60 \text{ seconds to restore normal operation}
$$

This is a **graceful degradation** requirement — a hallmark of robust systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Integration Testing Approaches

> **Problem:** *"Compare Top-Down, Bottom-Up, and Sandwich Integration Testing. Justify which is best for an ERP system."*

### Step 1: Define Each Approach

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Top-Down} &: \text{Test top modules first, stubs replace lower modules} \\
\text{Bottom-Up} &: \text{Test bottom modules first, drivers call lower modules} \\
\text{Sandwich} &: \text{Combine both; meet at the middle target layer}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply to ERP System

An ERP system has:
- **Top layer:** User Interface, Dashboards, Reporting modules.
- **Middle layer:** Business Logic, Workflow Engine, Rule Engine.
- **Bottom layer:** Database, APIs, External Service Connectors.

### Step 3: Choose the Best Strategy

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Recommendation} &: \text{Sandwich Integration Testing} \\
\text{Reasons} &: \\
&1. \text{Top-down validates UI flows early (critical for user experience)} \\
&2. \text{Bottom-up validates DB/API correctness early (critical for data integrity)} \\
&3. \text{Parallel teams can work on top and bottom simultaneously} \\
&4. \text{Reduces overall test duration by } \approx 30\text{-}40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Define the Target Layer

For a typical ERP:

$$
\text{Target Layer} = \text{Business Logic / Workflow Engine}
$$

All top modules integrate downward toward this layer; all bottom modules integrate upward toward this layer.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Derivation: Regression Test Selection

> **Problem:** *"A module has 500 test cases. After a code change, you estimate 30% of test cases are affected. You can run only 50% of affected test cases due to time. How many test cases will you execute?"*

### Step 1: Calculate Affected Test Cases

$$
N_{affected} = 0.30 \times 500 = 150 \text{ test cases}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Executable Test Cases

$$
N_{executed} = 0.50 \times 150 = 75 \text{ test cases}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Prioritization

Prioritize the 75 test cases based on:
1. **Critical business workflows** — 40 cases (run first).
2. **High-fault-prone modules** — 25 cases.
3. **Recently failed test cases** — 10 cases.

### Step 4: Calculate Coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Regression Coverage} &= \frac{75}{150} \times 100 = 50\% \text{ of affected} \\
&= \frac{75}{500} \times 100 = 15\% \text{ of total}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Justification

This is the **cost-quality trade-off** — 100% regression is rarely feasible. The selected 15% must cover **highest risk paths**.
