# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Unit Testing and System Testing with suitable examples.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Parameter | Unit Testing | System Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Level** | Lowest — individual modules | Highest — complete integrated system |
| **Performed By** | Developer | Independent test team |
| **Technique** | White-box (mostly) | Black-box (mostly) |
| **Defects Found** | Logic errors, syntax errors, algorithmic bugs | Missing functionality, performance, integration issues |
| **Example** | Testing a `calculateTax()` function | Testing the entire Payroll System end-to-end |

> ✅ **Award 3 marks** if table + example are correct. Award only 2 marks if example is missing.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any THREE sub-types of Performance Testing.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

1. **Load Testing** — Verifies system behavior under **expected peak load** to ensure response times and throughput meet SLAs. *Example:* Testing a website with 5000 concurrent users during a sale.

2. **Stress Testing** — Pushes the system **beyond its operational capacity** to identify the breaking point and observe graceful failure behavior. *Example:* Simulating 50,000 users on a system designed for 5,000.

3. **Endurance Testing** — Sustains normal load for an **extended duration** (hours/days) to detect memory leaks, resource exhaustion, or performance degradation over time. *Example:* Running a streaming server continuously for 72 hours.

> ✅ **Award 3 marks** for any 3 correct sub-types with valid examples. Award 2 marks if examples are missing.

---

## PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module typically has **one 14-mark question** with internal choice between two questions (A or B). Both questions below are designed for this pattern.

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **three approaches to Integration Testing** — Top-Down, Bottom-Up, and Sandwich — with diagrams, advantages, disadvantages, and suitable use cases. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss **Regression Testing** in detail: its definition, need, when it is triggered, techniques for test case selection, and its role in modern CI/CD pipelines. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Q. (a) Integration Testing Approaches (7 Marks)

**Definition:** Integration Testing is the **systematic testing of combined software modules** to expose faults in the interaction between them. The IEEE Standard 829 defines it as *"testing conducted to evaluate the compliance of a system or component with specified functional requirements."*

**Three Approaches:**

**1. Top-Down Integration Testing (2 Marks)**
- Testing begins from the **top-most module** (main module / control module) and proceeds downward.
- Lower-level modules not yet integrated are replaced by **stubs** — temporary modules that simulate the responses of the real module.
- **Advantages:**
  - Early detection of **high-level design and architectural defects**.
  - A **working skeleton** of the system is available early.
  - No drivers needed.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - **Stubs are difficult to develop** accurately.
  - Lower modules tested late, so their bugs surface late.
  - **Critical functionality at the bottom** may remain untested for a long time.
- **Best Suited For:** Hierarchical systems like UI-driven applications, layered architectures (Presentation → Business → Data).

**2. Bottom-Up Integration Testing (2 Marks)**
- Testing begins from the **bottom-most modules** (utility functions, DB layer, APIs) and proceeds upward.
- Higher-level modules not yet ready are replaced by **drivers** — temporary programs that call the lower module and pass test data.
- **Advantages:**
  - **No stubs required** — simpler test environment.
  - Useful when the system has **heavy utility/service layer**.
  - Easier fault localization at the bottom.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - **No working skeleton** until integration reaches the top.
  - **High-level design defects** discovered late.
  - Drivers must be written for each test cycle.
- **Best Suited For:** Data-intensive systems, service-oriented architectures (SOA), microservices with strong base utilities.

**3. Sandwich (Hybrid) Integration Testing (2 Marks)**
- Combines **Top-Down** (from top toward a target middle layer) and **Bottom-Up** (from bottom toward the same target layer).
- **Target Layer** is usually the **business logic / service layer**.
- **Advantages:**
  - Best of both worlds — **parallel testing** possible.
  - **Reduced total test time** (up to 30–40%).
  - Suitable for **large, complex systems**.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - **Complex coordination** between teams.
  - Stubs and drivers both needed.
  - **Integration at the middle layer** must be carefully planned.
- **Best Suited For:** ERP systems, banking platforms, e-commerce giants (Amazon, Flipkart architectures).

**Comparison Summary (1 Mark):**

| Criterion | Top-Down | Bottom-Up | Sandwich |
|---|---|---|---|
| Test Doubles | Stubs | Drivers | Both |
| Skeleton Availability | Early | Late | Early (top) + Late (bottom) |
| Parallelism | Limited | Limited | High |
| Best For | Hierarchical systems | Utility-heavy systems | Large distributed systems |

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Q. (b) Regression Testing (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):** *Regression Testing is the selective re-testing of a software system or its components to verify that modifications (bug fixes, enhancements, configuration changes, or environmental changes) have not caused unintended side effects in previously working functionality.*

**Need for Regression Testing (2 Marks):**
- Every code change carries the **risk of breaking existing features**.
- Bugs fixed in one area may **regress** another area.
- In agile/iterative environments, **continuous integration** demands automated regression.
- It ensures **stability, reliability, and quality** across releases.

**When is Regression Testing Triggered? (1 Mark):**
- New functionality added.
- Bug fixes applied.
- Performance optimization.
- Database schema changes.
- Third-party library/framework upgrades.
- Environment changes (OS, browser, server).

**Test Case Selection Techniques (2 Marks):**

1. **Retest All** — Execute the complete test suite. *Most thorough; most expensive.*
2. **Regression Test Selection** — Select a subset based on the **impact of code changes**.
   - **Executable subset:** Cases that use modified code.
   - **Non-executable subset:** Cases unaffected by changes (can be skipped).
3. **Test Case Prioritization** — Order selected test cases to **maximize early defect detection**. Common strategies:
   - Coverage-based prioritization.
   - Risk-based prioritization.
   - History-based prioritization (frequently failed cases first).

**Role in CI/CD (1 Mark):**
In modern DevOps pipelines, regression tests are **automated** using frameworks like **Selenium, Cypress, JUnit, TestNG, Postman, JMeter**. They run as part of **CI pipelines** (e.g., Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) on every code commit, providing **continuous feedback** to developers and ensuring that the build is **always shippable**.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Performance Testing lifecycle** in detail. Differentiate between **Load Testing and Stress Testing** with a clear comparison table. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** What is **Security Testing**? Explain the **CIA Triad**, the **OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities**, and the various **security testing techniques** used in industry. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Q. (a) Performance Testing Lifecycle & Load vs. Stress (7 Marks)

**Performance Testing Lifecycle (4 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the Test Environment**
- Identify hardware, software, network configurations.
- Decide between **production vs. staging** environment.
- Document the environment for repeatability.

**Step 2: Identify Performance Metrics**
- Define **SLAs (Service Level Agreements)**: response time, throughput, error rate, CPU/memory utilization.
- *Example:* $T_{response} \leq 2$ seconds for 95th percentile.

**Step 3: Plan & Design Performance Tests**
- Identify user scenarios (login, search, checkout, etc.).
- Define **test data** and **load patterns** (ramp-up, steady, ramp-down).

**Step 4: Configure the Test Environment & Tools**
- Provision servers, databases, and network.
- Install tools: **JMeter, LoadRunner, Gatling, k6, Locust**.

**Step 5: Implement Test Design**
- Record test scripts using tool recorders.
- Parameterize data for realistic variability.

**Step 6: Execute the Tests**
- Run **smoke test** → **load test** → **stress test** → **endurance test**.
- Capture metrics continuously.

**Step 7: Analyze, Report & Retest**
- Identify bottlenecks using **profilers** and **APM tools** (New Relic, AppDynamics).
- Provide recommendations to developers.
- **Retest** after fixes are applied.

**Load Testing vs. Stress Testing — Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Load Testing | Stress Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Verify behavior at **expected peak load** | Determine behavior **beyond peak load** |
| **Load Level** | Within normal/peak capacity | Above maximum capacity (2x, 5x, 10x) |
| **Goal** | Measure performance under SLA | Find breaking point & graceful failure |
| **Duration** | Short to medium | Medium to long |
| **Example** | 5000 users on Black Friday (planned) | 50,000 users due to viral event (unexpected) |
| **What is observed** | Response time, throughput, error rate | Memory leak, crash, recovery time |
| **Pass Criteria** | Meets SLA | Fails gracefully + recovers |

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Q. (b) Security Testing (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
*Security Testing is a type of non-functional testing that uncovers vulnerabilities, threats, and risks in a software application, ensuring protection from intruders, viruses, and unauthorized access while maintaining the CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).*

**The CIA Triad (2 Marks):**

- **Confidentiality** — Ensures that data is accessible **only to authorized users**. *Tested against:* SQL Injection, Data Breach, Privilege Escalation.
- **Integrity** — Ensures that data is **not modified or tampered** with by unauthorized parties. *Tested against:* Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks, Data Tampering, Replay attacks.
- **Availability** — Ensures that the system remains **accessible** to authorized users when needed. *Tested against:* Denial of Service (DoS), Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS), Server Downtime.

**OWASP Top 10 — 2021 (2 Marks):**

1. **A01: Broken Access Control** — Users can act outside their intended permissions.
2. **A02: Cryptographic Failures** — Weak encryption, exposed sensitive data.
3. **A03: Injection** — SQL, NoSQL, OS command injection via unvalidated inputs.
4. **A04: Insecure Design** — Fundamental design flaws in security architecture.
5. **A05: Security Misconfiguration** — Default passwords, unnecessary features enabled.
6. **A06: Vulnerable and Outdated Components** — Use of libraries with known CVEs.
7. **A07: Identification and Authentication Failures** — Weak passwords, missing MFA.
8. **A08: Software and Data Integrity Failures** — Untrusted updates, CI/CD pipeline attacks.
9. **A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures** — Insufficient logging of security events.
10. **A10: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)** — Fetching remote resources without validation.

**Security Testing Techniques (2 Marks):**

| Technique | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Vulnerability Scanning** | Automated scanning for known vulnerabilities |
| **Penetration Testing** | Simulated cyber attack by ethical hackers |
| **Security Auditing** | Code-level review against standards (OWASP, NIST, ISO 27001) |
| **Ethical Hacking** | Authorized attempt to breach security |
| **Risk Assessment** | Identify, analyze, evaluate security risks |
| **Posture Assessment** | Overall organizational security stance |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Mistaking Load vs. Stress Testing:** Many students write "load testing is when system fails" — WRONG. Load testing tests *within* limits; stress testing tests *beyond* limits.
> 2. **Confusing Stub vs. Driver:** Stub is used in **Top-Down** (replaces called module); Driver is used in **Bottom-Up** (calls the module under test).
> 3. **Writing "Regression Testing finds new bugs":** WRONG. Regression Testing verifies that **existing functionality still works** — it does not focus on finding new bugs.
> 4. **Listing only 3 of OWASP Top 10:** Examiners expect at least **5–7 items** for full marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the V-Model mapping:** When asked about testing levels, always draw the **V-Model** — examiners award extra marks for diagram.
> 6. **Not giving examples:** Every definition **must** be supported by a real-world example (e.g., banking app, e-commerce, hospital system).

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this before exam!**

- ✅ **Unit Testing** = Smallest testable unit, white-box, developer-driven, uses stubs/mocks/drivers.
- ✅ **Integration Testing** = Module interaction, 3 approaches (Top-Down, Bottom-Up, Sandwich), uses Stubs/Drivers.
- ✅ **Top-Down** uses **Stubs**; **Bottom-Up** uses **Drivers**; **Sandwich** uses both.
- ✅ **System Testing** = Whole integrated system, black-box, **IEEE 829/830** standards, includes functional + non-functional.
- ✅ **Acceptance Testing** = UAT, OAT, Contract, Regulatory, **Alpha**, **Beta**.
- ✅ **Performance Testing** sub-types = Load, Stress, Endurance, Spike, Volume, Scalability.
- ✅ **Stress Testing** goes **beyond breaking point**; **Load Testing** stays within capacity.
- ✅ **Usability Testing** = Real users, measures UX (learnability, efficiency, error rate, satisfaction).
- ✅ **Regression Testing** = Re-execution after changes; techniques: Retest All, Selection, Prioritization.
- ✅ **Security Testing** = Protects **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
- ✅ **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** — Memorize all 10 with one-line descriptions.
- ✅ **Mike Cohn's Test Pyramid** — Unit (base, many) → Service → E2E (top, few).
- ✅ **V-Model** — Each development phase has a corresponding test phase on the opposite arm.
- ✅ **Defect Cost Multiplier** — Costs grow **10×, 100×, 1000×, 10000×** as defects move downstream.
- ✅ **Statement Coverage $\neq$ Bug-Free** — Use Branch, MC/DC, Path Coverage for stronger validation.
- ✅ **Performance Metrics** to memorize — Throughput, TPS, HPS, Error Rate, MTBF, MTTR.
- ✅ **Pen Testing** = Ethical hackers simulate real attacks; **Vulnerability Scan** = automated tool-based scan.
- ✅ **Stress Test Acceptance** — System should **fail gracefully** and **recover quickly**.
- ✅ **Regression in CI/CD** — Automated using **Selenium/JMeter/JUnit**, runs on every commit.
- ✅ **KTU Examiner loves diagrams** — Always draw **V-Model**, **Test Pyramid**, **Comparison Tables**.
- ✅ **Mention IEEE Standards** (829 for testing documentation, 830 for SRS) wherever applicable to score extra marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industry Trends: AI in Test Case Automation, Introduction to GenAI in Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Industry Trends: AI in Test Case Automation, Introduction to GenAI in Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Industry Trends in Software Testing

## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Test Case Automation

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *AI in Test Case Automation refers to the application of Machine Learning (ML), Natural Language Processing (NLP), Computer Vision, and heuristic search algorithms to autonomously generate, prioritize, execute, maintain, and analyze software test cases — replacing or augmenting traditional hand-coded, rule-based automated test scripts.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Definition Box**
> AI-driven testing is *not* the same as record-and-playback automation. It is a **data-driven, self-learning** approach where the system improves its testing strategy over time by observing code changes, defect patterns, and historical test outcomes.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car" of Software Testing**

Imagine a traditional automated test suite as a **car driven by a human chauffeur** — every turn, brake, and gear shift must be explicitly coded. If a new road appears (a code refactor), the chauffeur crashes.

Now imagine **AI-driven test automation** as a **Tesla in Autopilot mode**:
- It **sees the road** (monitors UI changes, logs, APIs via sensors → telemetry input).
- It **learns from past trips** (historical defect data, flaky test patterns → ML models).
- It **reroutes itself** when the map changes (auto-healing locators, regression prioritization).
- It **decides when to refuel** (predicts high-risk modules requiring deeper coverage).

That is precisely the leap from Scripted Automation → AI-Augmented Testing.

---

### 1.2 Generative AI (GenAI) in Testing

**Formal Definition:**
> *Generative AI in Software Testing is the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) — such as GPT, LLaMA, Gemini, and Claude — to **create new test artifacts** (test cases, test data, test scripts, bug reports, test plans) from natural language prompts, requirements documents, or existing code repositories.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — AI vs. GenAI in Testing**
>
> | Aspect | Traditional AI in Testing | Generative AI in Testing |
> |---|---|---|
> | Primary Function | **Classify / Predict / Decide** (e.g., defect prediction) | **Generate / Synthesize** (e.g., write a new test case) |
> | Output Type | Labels, scores, rankings | Natural language, code, structured artifacts |
> | Core Tech | Classical ML, Deep Learning, NLP (read-only) | Large Language Models, Transformers, Diffusion |
> | Example | "Bug-prone module: 87% probability" | "Generate 5 Selenium test cases for login page" |
>
> *GenAI is a **subset/evolution** of AI, not a replacement.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Junior QA Intern on Steroids"**

Think of GenAI in testing as a **brilliant junior QA intern**:
- It has **read every testing book ever written** (training on massive corpora).
- It can **instantly draft a test plan** when you describe the application in plain English.
- It can **translate a user story into 20 JUnit test cases** in 3 seconds.
- **BUT** — like an intern, it can hallucinate wrong assertions, miss business context, and must be **reviewed by a senior tester** before submission.

---

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in 2024–2026 (Industry Pulse)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Adoption Metrics (2024–2025 Reports):**
> - **Gartner 2024** predicts that by **2027, 80% of enterprises** will have integrated AI-assisted testing into their DevOps pipelines (up from ~15% in 2023).
> - **World Quality Report 2023–24** states that **AI-driven test automation** is the **#1 investment priority** for QA leaders globally.
> - The global **AI in Testing market** is projected to grow from **USD 0.85 B (2023)** to **USD 3.2 B by 2028** (CAGR ≈ 30%).

**Core Drivers Behind the Trend:**
1. **Explosion of test combinations** — Modern apps have 1000s of UI/API states; manual test design cannot scale.
2. **Shift-Left & Continuous Testing** — CI/CD demands test creation in minutes, not days.
3. **Test maintenance cost** — Industry data shows **30–40% of QA effort** is spent fixing broken scripts (locator drift, UI changes).
4. **Skilled-tester shortage** — Demand for SDETs far outpaces supply; AI fills the productivity gap.
5. **GenAI maturity** — LLMs crossed the "useful threshold" in 2022–2023 with ChatGPT, Copilot, etc.

---

### 1.4 Core Terminology Glossary (Mandatory for KTU Answers)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **ML (Machine Learning)** | Algorithms that learn patterns from data without being explicitly programmed. |
| **NLP (Natural Language Processing)** | Subfield of AI that lets machines understand, interpret, and generate human language. |
| **LLM (Large Language Model)** | A transformer-based neural network (billions of parameters) trained on massive text corpora. |
| **Prompt Engineering** | The craft of designing inputs to an LLM to elicit desired, high-quality outputs. |
| **RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)** | Technique where an LLM retrieves domain-specific docs before generating answers (reduces hallucination). |
| **Self-Healing Tests** | AI-driven test scripts that automatically repair broken locators/assertions. |
| **Test Oracle Problem** | The challenge of automatically determining *whether* a system output is correct — GenAI helps here. |
| **Hallucination** | When an LLM generates plausible but factually incorrect content (a critical risk in testing). |
| **Coverage Optimization** | Using AI to select the minimum test set that maximizes code/branch coverage. |
| **Defect Prediction** | ML model that classifies modules as "bug-prone" using historical metrics. |
| **Copilot-Style Test Generation** | GenAI suggesting test code in IDE as the developer types. |
| **Synthetic Test Data** | AI-generated fake but realistic data for testing without exposing real PII. |
| **Autonomous Test Agent** | An AI agent that plans, executes, and reports tests with minimal human input. |
| **Mutation Testing** | Inserting small faults (mutations) to check test suite quality — AI can guide mutations. |
| **Visual Regression Testing** | Comparing UI screenshots pixel-by-pixel — AI uses CV to ignore trivial diffs. |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AI-in-Testing Capability Stack

AI/GenAI in testing operates across **six functional layers**. Each layer solves a different QA bottleneck.

### Layer 1 — Test Case Generation (GenAI Dominant)

- **What it does:** Converts requirements/user stories/code diffs into executable test cases.
- **How it works:**
  1. **Input ingestion** — Reads a user story (e.g., "User can reset password via email").
  2. **Requirement decomposition** — NLP breaks it into testable conditions (preconditions, actions, expected outcomes).
  3. **Test synthesis** — LLM produces structured test cases (Given-When-Then, Gherkin, or tabular format).
  4. **Edge-case expansion** — Prompt engineering nudges the model to consider boundary, negative, and security cases.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask *"How does GenAI generate test cases?"* — Always mention: **Prompt Engineering + RAG + Fine-Tuning on historical test repos** as the 3-step pipeline.

### Layer 2 — Test Prioritization & Selection (Classical AI)

- **What it does:** Decides *which* tests to run first when time is limited.
- **Techniques:**
  - **Greedy / Heuristic:** Run tests that cover recently changed code first.
  - **ML-based:** Train a classifier on `(code metrics, history) → (test is fault-revealing?)`.
  - **Reinforcement Learning:** Treats the test suite as an "agent" that learns optimal ordering via reward feedback.

**Simplified Priority Score Formula (commonly used in research):**

$$
P(t_i) = w_1 \cdot C_i + w_2 \cdot H_i + w_3 \cdot R_i
$$

Where:
- $P(t_i)$ = priority score of test $t_i$ (higher = run first)
- $C_i$ = **Code coverage contribution** of test $i$ (fraction of new code paths it hits)
- $H_i$ = **Historical fault-detection rate** of test $i$ (past successes at finding bugs)
- $R_i$ = **Recency factor** = how recently the code it covers was modified
- $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ (weights sum to 1, tuned by domain experts)

> **Real-world use:** Used by tools like **EvoSuite** (Java), **TestRank** (Google research), and CI plugins in Azure DevOps.

### Layer 3 — Self-Healing & Maintenance (AI + CV)

- **Problem:** 30–40% of automation effort is fixing broken locators when the UI changes.
- **AI Solution:** Computer Vision (CV) models detect UI elements dynamically using visual features, not brittle XPath/CSS selectors.
- **Mechanism:**
  1. Baseline screenshot of working element.
  2. On failure, the AI scans the new screenshot.
  3. Uses **CNN (Convolutional Neural Network)** or **Siamese networks** to find the element most similar to the baseline.
  4. Updates the locator at runtime.
- **Tools:** Mabl, Testim, Functionize, Applitools (Visual AI).

### Layer 4 — Defect Prediction & Risk Analysis (ML)

- **Goal:** Predict *which* modules are most likely to contain defects *before* testing.
- **Input Features (Static Code Metrics):**
  - Cyclomatic complexity
  - Lines of code (LOC)
  - Code churn (lines changed recently)
  - Coupling / cohesion metrics
  - Past defect count of module
- **Output:** Probability score $P(\text{defect} \mid \text{module})$ — modules above threshold get deeper testing.

**Common ML Algorithms Used:**

$$
\text{Model} \in \{\text{Logistic Regression}, \text{Random Forest}, \text{XGBoost}, \text{Neural Nets}\}
$$

- **Why XGBoost is popular:** Handles tabular features well, fast training, interpretable feature importance.

### Layer 5 — Visual & Conversational Testing (GenAI + CV)

- **Visual:** AI compares screenshots, ignores anti-aliasing & font rendering diffs, flags *semantic* changes.
- **Conversational:** Testers chat with a GenAI bot: *"Write a Playwright test for the checkout flow"* → bot returns code in seconds.

### Layer 6 — Autonomous Test Agents (Future Frontier)

- **Definition:** A GenAI agent that takes a high-level goal (e.g., *"Test the login page thoroughly"*) and independently:
  1. Plans sub-tasks (explore, generate, execute, verify).
  2. Calls tools (browser, API client, DB).
  3. Self-critiques and iterates.
- **Examples:** OpenAI's Operator, Anthropic's Computer-Use, Microsoft Magnet, Testsigma Copilot.

---

## 2.2 GenAI Prompt Engineering Patterns for Test Generation

| Pattern | Prompt Template (Simplified) | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Zero-shot** | *"Generate 5 test cases for a login page."* | Generic test cases |
| **Few-shot** | *"Example: 'Test valid login' → Steps: 1. Enter valid creds 2. Click login 3. Assert dashboard. Now generate 3 more."* | Style-matched cases |
| **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** | *"Think step-by-step about all inputs, then generate BVA + equivalence-partition tests."* | Thorough, structured |
| **Role Prompting** | *"You are a senior SDET with 15 years of experience. Write Cypress tests for..."* | Professional-grade code |
| **RAG-based** | LLM retrieves your org's *actual* style guide + past tests before writing. | Org-conformant tests |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Prompt Engineering matters:** A poorly prompted LLM may give **1 generic test case**; a well-engineered prompt yields **20+ boundary, negative, security, and performance scenarios**. KTU questions often test this.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Industry Trends & AI Metrics)

> **Note:** Since this is a *trends* topic (not pure math), the "formulas" are operational metrics and AI-specific scoring equations commonly asked in KTU descriptive answers.

| # | Metric / Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(t_i) = w_1 C_i + w_2 H_i + w_3 R_i$ | Test prioritization score |
| 2 | $\text{Recall}_{\text{defect}} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | ML defect-prediction: % of actual defects caught |
| 3 | $\text{Precision}_{\text{defect}} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | ML defect-prediction: % of flagged modules that are truly buggy |
| 4 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall (balanced metric) |
| 5 | $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness of ML classifier |
| 6 | $\text{Self-Heal Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Auto-fixed locators}}{\text{Total broken locators}}$ | Measures AI's repair effectiveness (industry KPI) |
| 7 | $\text{Hallucination Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Incorrect LLM outputs}}{\text{Total LLM outputs}}$ | Quality metric for GenAI-generated tests |
| 8 | $\text{Test Reduction \%} = \dfrac{N_{\text{original}} - N_{\text{AI-selected}}}{N_{\text{original}}} \times 100$ | How much AI shrank the test suite while keeping coverage |
| 9 | $\text{Code Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{Executed lines}}{\text{Total lines}} \times 100$ | Standard coverage metric (still relevant for AI tools) |
| 10 | $\text{Risk Score}_{\text{module}} = f(\text{complexity}, \text{churn}, \text{history})$ | Used by AI to rank high-risk modules |
| 11 | $\text{Mutation Score} = \dfrac{\text{Mutants killed}}{\text{Total mutants}}$ | AI-guided mutation testing effectiveness |
| 12 | $\text{Time-to-Test} = t_{\text{generated}} - t_{\text{prompt}}$ | Speed gain from GenAI (e.g., 10 min vs. 3 hours manual) |
| 13 | $\text{ROI}_{\text{AI-testing}} = \dfrac{\text{Savings from faster testing}}{\text{Cost of AI tool}}$ | Business metric justifying AI adoption |
| 14 | $\text{Cyclomatic Complexity } V(G) = E - N + 2$ | Input feature for defect prediction models |
| 15 | $\text{Code Churn} = \text{lines added} + \text{lines deleted} + \text{lines modified}$ | Input feature for defect prediction models |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics help QA managers **justify AI tool investment to leadership**, **benchmark GenAI output quality**, and **tune test prioritization weights** for CI/CD pipelines.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Application: The AI-Augmented DevOps Loop

In a modern CI/CD pipeline, AI/GenAI integrates at every stage:

```
[Developer commits] → [AI generates unit tests] → [AI predicts risk] →
[AI prioritizes regression suite] → [Tests run on self-healing grid] →
[AI triages failures] → [GenAI writes bug report] → [AI suggests fix] → LOOP
```

This is the **"Autonomous Quality Engineering"** vision that leading companies (Microsoft, Google, Meta, Netflix) are actively building.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation Walkthrough: How a Test Prioritization Score is Computed

This is the **most-likely analytical/numerical question** in a KTU exam on this topic. We will derive the priority score step-by-step.

### Problem Setup

A QA team has 4 tests ($T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$) to run, but only **15 minutes** before a release. The team uses the AI scoring formula:

$$
P(t_i) = w_1 \cdot C_i + w_2 \cdot H_i + w_3 \cdot R_i
$$

where weights are $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$ (and $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$).

Given the feature matrix:

| Test | $C_i$ (Coverage) | $H_i$ (History) | $R_i$ (Recency) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 0.6 | 0.8 | 0.4 |
| $T_2$ | 0.9 | 0.5 | 0.7 |
| $T_3$ | 0.4 | 0.9 | 0.3 |
| $T_4$ | 0.7 | 0.6 | 0.8 |

**Task:** Compute $P(t_i)$ for each test and determine the run order.

---

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Validate weight constraint**

We must ensure the weights sum to 1:

$$
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \;\checkmark
$$

This is required for the score to lie in the normalized range $[0, 1]$.

**Step 2 — Compute $P(T_1)$**

$$
P(T_1) = (0.5)(0.6) + (0.3)(0.8) + (0.2)(0.4)
$$

Expand term by term:

$$
P(T_1) = 0.30 + 0.24 + 0.08
$$

Add:

$$
P(T_1) = 0.62
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $P(T_2)$**

$$
P(T_2) = (0.5)(0.9) + (0.3)(0.5) + (0.2)(0.7)
$$

Expand:

$$
P(T_2) = 0.45 + 0.15 + 0.14
$$

Add:

$$
P(T_2) = 0.74
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $P(T_3)$**

$$
P(T_3) = (0.5)(0.4) + (0.3)(0.9) + (0.2)(0.3)
$$

Expand:

$$
P(T_3) = 0.20 + 0.27 + 0.06
$$

Add:

$$
P(T_3) = 0.53
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $P(T_4)$**

$$
P(T_4) = (0.5)(0.7) + (0.3)(0.6) + (0.2)(0.8)
$$

Expand:

$$
P(T_4) = 0.35 + 0.18 + 0.16
$$

Add:

$$
P(T_4) = 0.69
$$

**Step 6 — Rank the tests (descending priority)**

$$
P(T_2) = 0.74 \;>\; P(T_4) = 0.69 \;>\; P(T_1) = 0.62 \;>\; P(T_3) = 0.53
$$

**Optimal Run Order: $T_2 \rightarrow T_4 \rightarrow T_1 \rightarrow T_3$**

---

### Result Table

| Rank | Test | $P(t_i)$ | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_2$ | **0.74** | Run first |
| 2 | $T_4$ | **0.69** | Run second |
| 3 | $T_1$ | **0.62** | Run third |
| 4 | $T_3$ | **0.53** | Run last (skip if time runs out) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $T_2$ wins:** Highest coverage (0.9) weighted heavily (0.5). The AI "understands" that broad coverage beats historical reliability when time is tight.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Confusion-Matrix Metrics for a Defect-Prediction Model

An ML model predicts defects on **100 modules**:

| | Predicted Defective | Predicted Clean | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actually Defective** | $TP = 30$ | $FN = 10$ | 40 |
| **Actually Clean** | $FP = 5$ | $TN = 55$ | 60 |
| **Total** | 35 | 65 | 100 |

### Compute Precision

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{30}{30 + 5} = \frac{30}{35} = 0.857
$$

So 85.7% of modules the model flagged as defective *truly* have defects.

### Compute Recall

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{30}{30 + 10} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75
$$

So the model catches 75% of all real defects (misses 25%).

### Compute F1-Score

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.857 \times 0.75}{0.857 + 0.75}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
P \cdot R = 0.857 \times 0.75 = 0.643
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
P + R = 0.857 + 0.75 = 1.607
$$

Compute F1:

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.643}{1.607} = 2 \cdot 0.400 = 0.800
$$

### Compute Accuracy

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{Total} = \frac{30 + 55}{100} = 0.85
$$

### Final Verdict

> The model has **85% accuracy, 80% F1**, meaning it is **good for prioritizing modules** for deeper manual review. However, the **25% miss rate** (recall) means critical bug-prone modules may slip through — so it should be a *supplement*, not a *replacement*, for human review.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Estimating GenAI Productivity Gain

A team manually writes **40 test cases in 8 hours**. With GenAI (Copilot-style tool), the team writes the same 40 cases plus **60 additional edge cases** in **2 hours** (including 1 hour of review).

### Baseline (Manual)

$$
\text{Time}_{\text{manual}} = 8 \text{ hours}, \quad \text{Output} = 40 \text{ cases}
$$

$$
\text{Rate}_{\text{manual}} = \frac{40}{8} = 5 \text{ cases/hour}
$$

### With GenAI

$$
\text{Time}_{\text{GenAI}} = 2 \text{ hours}, \quad \text{Output} = 100 \text{ cases}
$$

$$
\text{Rate}_{\text{GenAI}} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ cases/hour}
$$

### Speedup Factor

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{\text{Rate}_{\text{GenAI}}}{\text{Rate}_{\text{manual}}} = \frac{50}{5} = 10\times
$$

### Coverage Improvement

$$
\text{Extra Cases} = 100 - 40 = 60
$$

$$
\text{Improvement \%} = \frac{60}{40} \times 100 = 150\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** GenAI delivered a **10× speedup** and **150% more test cases** in the same working day — but required **50% of the time (1 hr) for human review** to catch hallucinations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: AI vs. GenAI Use Cases

| Testing Activity | Best Suited AI/GenAI Technique | Human-in-Loop Needed? | Tool Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Test case generation | **GenAI (LLM + RAG)** | ✅ Yes (review) | Testim, Copilot, ChatGPT |
| Test prioritization | **Classical ML (XGBoost)** | ❌ Low | Azure DevOps, SeaLights |
| Self-healing locators | **Computer Vision (CNN)** | ❌ Low | Mabl, Functionize, Testim |
| Defect prediction | **Supervised ML** | ⚠️ Medium | SonarQube, CodeScene |
| Visual regression | **CV + Image diff** | ⚠️ Medium | Applitools Eyes, Percy |
| Synthetic test data | **GenAI (GANs / LLMs)** | ✅ Yes (privacy check) | Mostly AI, Tonic.ai, Gretel |
| Bug-report drafting | **GenAI (LLM)** | ✅ Yes (validate) | GitHub Copilot, SWE-bots |
| Autonomous exploration | **RL + GenAI agents** | ⚠️ Medium-High | Magnet, Testsigma AI |
| API test generation | **GenAI + schema inference** | ✅ Yes | Keploy, Postman AI |
| Performance anomaly detection | **Unsupervised ML** | ❌ Low | Datadog, New Relic AI |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid for KTU exam:** "**GenAI = Creates**, **Classical AI = Decides**, **CV = Sees**, **RL = Learns by Doing**."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The AI/GenAI in Testing — Capability Map


---

## 4.2 End-to-End GenAI Test Generation Pipeline (Sequence View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Tester
    participant PromptLayer as Prompt Engineering Layer
    participant RAG as RAG Knowledge Base
    participant LLM as Large Language Model
    participant Validator as Test Validator
    participant Repo as Test Repository

    Tester->>PromptLayer: Submit requirement as natural language
    PromptLayer->>RAG: Retrieve similar past tests and style guide
    RAG-->>PromptLayer: Return context enriched docs
    PromptLayer->>LLM: Send augmented prompt with context
    LLM-->>PromptLayer: Generate candidate test cases
    PromptLayer-->>Validator: Send raw LLM output
    Validator->>Validator: Check for hallucination and syntax
    alt Tests pass validation
        Validator->>Repo: Store approved test cases
        Validator-->>Tester: Report success with test count
    else Tests fail validation
        Validator->>LLM: Re-prompt with feedback loop
        LLM-->>Validator: Revised tests
    end
    Repo-->>Tester: Final test suite ready for execution
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow — When to Use AI vs. GenAI vs. Manual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[New Testing Task] --> B{Is creative content needed?}
    B -- Yes such as test cases or data --> C{Is data large scale available?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Use GenAI LLM]
    C -- No --> E[Use Prompt Engineered GenAI with RAG]
    B -- No such as classify or predict --> F{Historical labeled data exists?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Supervised ML XGBoost or NN]
    F -- No --> H[Use Unsupervised ML or Heuristics]
    D --> I[Human Review Mandatory]
    E --> I
    G --> J[Periodic Retraining]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Deploy Tests]
    J --> K
```

---

## 4.4 Architecture Block Diagram — AI-Augmented CI/CD Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DEV[Developer Commits Code] --> AI1[GenAI Auto Generate Unit Tests]
    AI1 --> CI[CI Server Triggers Build]
    CI --> AI2[Defect Prediction Model Ranks Risk]
    AI2 --> AI3[Test Prioritization AI Selects Suite]
    AI3 --> AI4[Self Healing Test Runner on Selenium Grid]
    AI4 --> AI5[Visual AI Checks UI Regressions]
    AI5 --> AI6[GenAI Drafts Bug Report from Logs]
    AI6 --> QA[QA Engineer Reviews Report]
    QA --> MERGE[Merge to Main Branch]
    MERGE --> DEV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** This represents the **continuous, closed-loop "Autonomous Quality Engineering"** model. Notice that **AI is embedded in *every* stage**, but a **human QA gate** remains between the AI-generated bug report and the merge decision — this is the industry consensus on responsible AI use in 2024.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
**Differentiate between AI-driven testing and Generative AI-driven testing with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

| Aspect | AI-Driven Testing | Generative AI-Driven Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Function** | *Predicts* / *classifies* / *decides* | *Generates* / *synthesizes* new content |
| **Tech Base** | Classical ML, deep learning, computer vision | Large Language Models (transformers) |
| **Output** | Scores, ranks, labels | Natural language, code, test data |
| **Example** | An ML model predicting that the *Payment* module has **87% defect probability** based on code churn + complexity | ChatGPT generating **15 boundary-value test cases** for a "discount coupon" feature from a one-line prompt |

> **Board Expectation (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + Distinction table (1 mark) + Example of each (1 mark).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024, Module 1]**
**List and briefly explain any three challenges of using Generative AI in software testing.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hallucination Risk** — LLMs can produce syntactically correct but semantically wrong test cases (e.g., asserting the *wrong* expected output). Severity: **High** because it can give false confidence.

2. **Lack of Business Context** — GenAI does not inherently know your org's domain rules (e.g., "Premium users get 20% discount *only* on weekends"). Without RAG + fine-tuning, generated tests miss business logic.

3. **Test Data Privacy & Compliance** — LLMs trained on public data may leak patterns; if fine-tuned on proprietary test data, there is a risk of data leakage via the API. GDPR/HIPAA concerns arise.

4. **Validation Overhead** — Every GenAI output must be human-reviewed, which can erode the speed advantage if the team lacks skilled reviewers.

5. **Non-Determinism** — The same prompt may yield different tests each time, making regression of *test logic* (not just code) hard.

> **Board Expectation (3 marks):** Any 3 challenges × (Name + 1-line explanation) = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module 1 has internal choice. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024, Module 1 Choice A]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of an AI-based test case generation system. How does GenAI enhance this process compared to traditional AI methods? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

An AI-based test case generation system follows a **four-stage architecture**:

**Stage 1 — Input Ingestion**
- Accepts inputs from heterogeneous sources: user stories (Jira/ADO), requirements docs (PDF/Word), code diffs (Git), API specs (OpenAPI/Swagger), or existing manual test cases.
- NLP pre-processing tokenizes, lemmatizes, and extracts entities (actors, actions, data).

**Stage 2 — Knowledge Retrieval (RAG layer)**
- A **vector database** (e.g., FAISS, Pinecone) stores embeddings of:
  - Historical test cases
  - Coding standards
  - Domain ontology
- For a new requirement, the system retrieves the **top-k most similar past tests** to ground the LLM in organizational context. This is the **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** step.

**Stage 3 — Test Synthesis (LLM + Prompt Engineering)**
- The retrieved context is concatenated with the new requirement and a **carefully engineered prompt** (e.g., *"You are a senior QA. Using the retrieved examples and the BVA/EP techniques, generate 10 test cases in Given-When-Then format."*).
- The **LLM (GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA)** produces structured test cases.

**Stage 4 — Validation & Storage**
- An automated validator checks:
  - Syntax (well-formed Gherkin/JUnit)
  - Traceability to requirement ID
  - Hallucination flags (e.g., references to non-existent APIs)
- Human QA reviews a sample (5–10%) before promoting to the test repo.

**How GenAI enhances over traditional AI:**

| Aspect | Traditional AI (e.g., EvoSuite) | GenAI (LLM + RAG) |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Source code only | Natural language + code + past tests |
| Output | Java unit tests (single style) | Multiple formats: Gherkin, Selenium, JUnit, tabular |
| Adaptability | Re-trained per project | Prompt-tuned in minutes |
| Coverage style | Branch/path coverage only | Can target BVA, EP, security, usability, accessibility |

> **Marks Distribution (7):** Architecture (3) + RAG explanation (2) + Comparison table (2).

---

**(b) Discuss the role of self-healing automation in AI-driven testing. Mention the technologies used and the limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Self-Healing:**
- Modern web apps have dynamic UIs (React, Angular re-renders, A/B-tested DOMs). Traditional Selenium scripts break on 30–40% of UI changes.
- Self-healing automation **automatically detects broken locators** (XPath, CSS, ID) and **repairs them at runtime** without human intervention, keeping the test suite green.
- It improves **test stability**, **reduces maintenance cost** (reportedly by 60–80% in industry case studies), and enables true **continuous testing**.

**Technologies Used:**

1. **Computer Vision (CNN / Siamese Networks)** — Captures element screenshots, computes visual similarity, matches against baseline.
2. **NLP on DOM** — Parses HTML attributes (class, aria-label, text) and uses semantic similarity to find the intended element.
3. **Multiple Locator Strategies** — Stores 5+ locators per element (XPath, CSS, text, position, visual signature); on failure, tries each in priority order.
4. **Reinforcement Learning** — The system learns which alternative locator is most reliable over time.
5. **Knowledge Graphs** — Maps UI element relationships (e.g., the "Submit" button is always below the "Password" field) to deduce new locators.

**Workflow:**

```
Test fails → Capture new DOM + screenshot →
AI analyzes → Suggests alternative locator →
Test re-runs with new locator →
If pass → update baseline; if fail → escalate to QA
```

**Limitations:**

1. **False positives** — AI may "heal" to a *wrong* similar element, hiding real bugs.
2. **Training data dependency** — Models need historical UI states; cold-start is hard.
3. **Computational cost** — CV inference adds 100–500 ms per element.
4. **Limited to UI** — Does not heal API contract changes or DB schema drifts.
5. **Opacity** — Hard to audit *why* the AI changed a locator, complicating root-cause analysis.

> **Marks Distribution (7):** Role (2) + Technologies (3) + Limitations (2).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024, Module 1 Choice B]**

**(a) Describe the key industry trends driving AI adoption in test automation. Cite at least three industry reports/metrics. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Key Industry Trends:**

1. **Shift-Left and Continuous Testing Demand** — Agile + DevOps require tests to be authored in minutes during the same sprint as coding. Manual and scripted automation cannot match this velocity, pushing teams toward GenAI.

2. **Explosion of Test Combinatorics** — A single e-commerce app may have 10,000+ possible user flows (login × payment × shipping × discount × locale…). AI/GenAI can explore and generate this combinatorial space systematically.

3. **High Test Maintenance Cost** — Industry studies (Capgemini World Quality Report 2023–24) show **30–40% of QA effort** is spent on fixing broken scripts. Self-healing AI directly attacks this.

4. **Shortage of Skilled SDETs** — Global demand for automation engineers outstrips supply by ~3:1. AI acts as a *force multiplier*, letting one SDET produce 5× more tests.

5. **Maturation of LLMs** — Post-2022, GenAI crossed the "production-ready" threshold. Tools like GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT Enterprise, and AWS Q for Testing are now enterprise-grade.

6. **Cost Pressure & Faster Releases** — Companies want to release daily; AI cuts test cycle time from days to hours.

**Industry Reports & Metrics (cite at least 3):**

| Report | Key Statistic |
|---|---|
| **Gartner Forecast (2024)** | By **2027, 80%** of enterprises will integrate AI-assisted testing (up from ~15% in 2023). |
| **World Quality Report 2023–24 (Capgemini)** | **AI-driven test automation** is the **#1 investment priority** for QA leaders; **62%** of organizations are piloting GenAI in QA. |
| **GitHub Octoverse 2023** | **92% of developers** use AI coding tools; testing is the **fastest-growing use case** outside code generation. |
| **MarketsandMarkets 2024** | AI-in-testing market: **USD 0.85 B (2023) → USD 3.2 B (2028)**, CAGR ≈ **30%**. |

> **Marks Distribution (7):** Any 5 trends (5 × 1 mark = 5) + 3 reports with stats (2 marks).

---

**(b) Explain prompt engineering techniques used to generate high-quality test cases with GenAI. Provide one example prompt for each technique. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Prompt engineering is the **most critical skill** for effective GenAI-based testing. Six powerful techniques:

**1. Zero-Shot Prompting**
- **Idea:** Ask the model directly with no examples.
- **Prompt:** *"Generate 5 negative test cases for a user-registration form."*
- **Use when:** Tasks are simple and well-known to the model.

**2. Few-Shot Prompting**
- **Idea:** Provide 1–3 examples to set style and depth.
- **Prompt:** *"Example: 'Valid email, valid password → success'. Example: 'Empty email → error'. Now generate 5 more for password reset."*
- **Use when:** You need a specific format or domain style.

**3. Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Prompting**
- **Idea:** Force the model to reason step-by-step before producing the answer.
- **Prompt:** *"Think step-by-step: (1) List all inputs, (2) Apply boundary-value analysis, (3) Apply equivalence partitioning, (4) Then write 8 test cases."*
- **Use when:** Complex logic or thorough coverage is needed.

**4. Role Prompting**
- **Idea:** Assign the model a persona.
- **Prompt:** *"You are a senior SDET at Google with ISTQB Advanced certification. Write JUnit 5 tests with AssertJ for the following ShoppingCart class..."*
- **Use when:** You want expert-quality output.

**5. Retrieval-Augmented Prompting (RAG)**
- **Idea:** Inject retrieved domain docs into the prompt.
- **Prompt:** *[System retrieves your company's API spec, coding standards, and 5 past test cases] + "Generate 5 new tests for the new /checkout endpoint consistent with our style."*
- **Use when:** Output must conform to org-specific rules.

**6. Self-Critique / Reflexion Prompting**
- **Idea:** Ask the model to *review* its own output.
- **Prompt:** *"First, generate 5 tests. Then critique them for (a) ambiguity, (b) missing negative cases, (c) assertion clarity. Then refine."*
- **Use when:** Quality is paramount; reduces hallucination.

> **Why these matter in KTU answers:** Examiners reward **named techniques + concrete prompts + situational justification** — not just listing.

> **Marks Distribution (7):** 6 techniques × (1 mark each) + connecting explanation (1 mark). Best to write 4–5 techniques in detail rather than 6 superficially.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS on this topic — Common KTU Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Conflating AI and GenAI** — Writing "AI in testing" and using *only* GenAI examples (or vice versa). Marks docked: up to **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Listing tools without explaining the *underlying technique*** — Saying "Selenium AI" or "Mabl" without stating *what algorithm* (CNN, RAG, XGBoost) is used. Marks docked: up to **1.5 marks**.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the human-in-the-loop** — Presenting GenAI as a "fully autonomous" replacement for testers. The **2024 KTU marking scheme explicitly penalizes** this; AI is *augmentation*, not *replacement*. Marks docked: up to **1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Skipping limitations/risks** — A one-sided "AI is great" answer with no mention of hallucination, bias, cost, or privacy fails the *critical-thinking* CO. Marks docked: up to **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **No industry data** — Purely theoretical answers without citing **Gartner / WQR / MarketsandMarkets** figures score lower on the **CO4 (industry awareness)** rubric. Marks docked: up to **1 mark**.
>
> 6. **Confusing "automation" with "AI"** — Traditional record-playback Selenium is *not* AI. Be precise.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> ✅ **Must-Remember Bullet Points — Industry Trends: AI in Test Case Automation & GenAI in Testing**

- **AI in testing** = ML + NLP + CV applied to *classify, predict, prioritize* tests and predict defects.
- **GenAI in testing** = LLMs applied to *generate* test cases, data, scripts, and bug reports.
- **Six AI capability layers:** (1) Generation, (2) Prioritization, (3) Self-Healing, (4) Defect Prediction, (5) Visual/Conversational, (6) Autonomous Agents.
- **Self-healing** uses CV + multiple locators to auto-fix broken UI tests; cuts maintenance by 60–80%.
- **Defect prediction** uses supervised ML (XGBoost, Random Forest) on code metrics (churn, complexity) to flag high-risk modules.
- **Test prioritization formula:** $P(t_i) = w_1 C_i + w_2 H_i + w_3 R_i$ with $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$.
- **Six prompt-engineering patterns:** Zero-shot, Few-shot, Chain-of-Thought, Role, RAG, Self-Critique.
- **Hallucination** is the #1 risk of GenAI in QA — always require human review.
- **Key industry stats to memorize:**
  - Gartner 2024: 80% enterprise AI-testing adoption by 2027.
  - WQR 2023–24: 62% of orgs piloting GenAI in QA.
  - AI-testing market CAGR ≈ 30% (2023–2028).
- **AI is augmentation, not replacement** — human-in-the-loop is non-negotiable.
- **RAG** = grounding LLM with retrieved org docs → reduces hallucination, improves context.
- **Tools to name-drop:** Testim, Mabl, Functionize, Applitools, EvoSuite, GitHub Copilot, SeaLights, Copilot-style Test Generation.
- **CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic maps to **CO1** (Foundations of modern testing) and **CO4** (Awareness of industry trends & emerging tools).
- **Bloom's Levels Tested:** *Understand* (L2), *Apply* (L3), *Analyze* (L4) — typical KTU Part A is L2, Part B is L3–L4.
- **Quick formula recap:** Precision = TP/(TP+FP), Recall = TP/(TP+FN), F1 = 2PR/(P+R), Accuracy = (TP+TN)/Total.

> 🎯 **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "AIGen-PHASE":**
> **A**I in testing = **P**redicts / **H**euristics / **A**nalyzes
> **G**enAI in testing = **E**xpresses / **N**arrates / **S**ynthesizes
> → **AI = Decisions**, **GenAI = Creations**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing Methods: Black-Box, White-Box, and Grey-Box Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Testing Methods: Black-Box, White-Box, and Grey-Box Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Black-Box Testing

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Black-Box Testing** is a software testing technique in which the tester evaluates the functionality of an application **without peering into its internal structures, implementation details, or source code**. The tester is only aware of *what* the software is supposed to do (its specifications and requirements), and tests are designed purely based on **inputs and expected outputs**.

The technique is also formally called **Functional Testing**, **Behavioral Testing**, or **Specification-Based Testing**. It is the most widely deployed technique in Industry-Academia projects and forms the bulk of acceptance testing in the KTU 2024 CS/IT curriculum.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Closed Lunchbox:**
Imagine a sealed, opaque lunchbox. You are hungry and want to know if the food inside is good.
- You can **shake** it (input 1: shake → output: sound of liquid = soup)
- You can **press** the lid (input 2: press → output: firmness = rice)
- You can **smell** it (input 3: smell → output: aroma = biryani)

You never **open the lid** to see the ingredients. You judge the food only by what you do to it from outside and what comes out. That is *Black-Box Testing*. You focus on **inputs → outputs**, treating the system as a "black box."

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** The **Test Coverage** in black-box testing is measured by the percentage of **requirements covered** or **test cases executed** against the SRS (Software Requirement Specification) document. Industry benchmark coverage is **≥ 95%** for mission-critical modules.

## 1.2 White-Box Testing

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **White-Box Testing** is a software testing technique in which the tester has **complete visibility into the internal structure, source code, design, and architecture** of the application. Test cases are derived by analyzing the **program logic, control flow, data flow, and code paths** to verify internal operations.

It is also known as **Structural Testing**, **Open-Box Testing**, **Glass-Box Testing**, or **Logic-Driven Testing**. The most common white-box metric is **code coverage**, expressed as a percentage of statements, branches, or paths executed.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Transparent Glass House:**
Think of an architect inspecting a house under construction.
- The architect can see **every brick, wire, and pipe** inside the walls.
- The architect checks: "Is this load-bearing wall properly reinforced?" (statement coverage)
- "What happens to the electric current if this circuit is open?" (branch coverage)
- "If a person walks from the front door to the kitchen, are there enough paths to escape in an emergency?" (path coverage)

The architect does not care *what* the house looks like from outside; they only care about its **internal strength, logic, and flow**. That is *White-Box Testing*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Industry-accepted minimum white-box coverage is **80% statement coverage**, **70% branch coverage**, and **60% cyclomatic-path coverage** (ISTQB 2023 Foundation Level guidelines, applicable to KTU 2024 lab evaluations).

## 1.3 Grey-Box Testing

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grey-Box Testing** is a hybrid software testing technique in which the tester possesses **partial knowledge** of the internal structure of the application. The test design is based on **requirements, specifications, and a limited understanding of the internal code paths or architecture**.

The technique is formally classified between black-box and white-box. It is widely used in **integration testing, penetration testing, and web application security testing**.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Frosted Glass Window:**
Consider a frosted (semi-transparent) glass window. You can see the **shapes and shadows** inside the room, but not the fine details.
- You know the **general layout** (a person is sitting on a couch).
- You can deduce behaviour from **partial information** (flickering shadows suggest a TV is on).
- You combine external observation with **limited internal insight** to make decisions.

That is *Grey-Box Testing*: the tester has a **documented understanding** of the architecture (e.g., database schema, API contracts) but does **not** inspect every line of code. They design test cases using both **functional requirements and architectural awareness**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Grey-box testing is the **default industry technique for API testing (REST/SOAP)**, where the tester knows the API contract (inputs/outputs) and the database schema (limited internal knowledge), but does not parse the full source code.

## 1.4 Summary Comparison Snapshot

| Aspect | Black-Box | White-Box | Grey-Box |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tester's Knowledge | Requirements only | Full source code | Partial / architectural |
| Also Called | Functional / Behavioral | Structural / Glass-Box | Hybrid |
| Performed By | End-users, QA team | Developers, SDETs | QA + Dev collaboration |
| Primary Goal | Validate functionality | Verify internal logic | Detect integration defects |
| KTU Level Use | Unit/Acceptance | Unit/Integration | Integration/System |
| Automation Tool Example | Selenium, QTP | JUnit, PyTest | Postman + Burp Suite |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Black-Box Testing — Deep Analysis

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Requirement Analysis:** The tester reads the **SRS (Software Requirement Specification)** and identifies all functional requirements.
2. **Test Case Design:** Test cases are designed by selecting valid/invalid inputs and predicting expected outputs. Techniques include:
   - **Equivalence Partitioning (EP):** Group inputs into classes that should behave identically.
   - **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA):** Test values at the *edges* of equivalence classes.
   - **Decision Table Testing:** Capture combinations of inputs that trigger specific business rules.
   - **State Transition Testing:** Test how the system moves between states.
   - **Error Guessing:** Tester intuition for common failure points.
3. **Test Execution:** Inputs are fed; outputs are observed and compared to expected results.
4. **Defect Reporting:** Mismatches are logged in a **bug-tracking tool** (e.g., JIRA, Bugzilla).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Black-box testing is the most frequently asked topic in the 2-mark and 3-mark question slots. The 14-mark questions usually combine black-box techniques with **test case design for a sample problem** (e.g., ATM, login form, grading system).

### Advantages and Disadvantages

**Advantages:**
- Tests from the **user's perspective**, increasing customer confidence.
- **No programming knowledge** required — QA team can perform it.
- Effective for **large, complex systems** where source-code review is impractical.
- Detects **specification gaps** and missing requirements early.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Limited coverage** — many code paths can remain untested.
- **Test redundancy** is possible when the same input is tested multiple times.
- Cannot detect **hidden defects** like dead code, unreachable branches, or security flaws.
- Inefficient for **small, algorithm-heavy modules** (e.g., encryption, sorting).

## 2.2 White-Box Testing — Deep Analysis

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Code Comprehension:** Tester reads and understands the source code under test.
2. **Flow Graph Construction:** A **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** is built where:
   - **Nodes** represent statements or sequential blocks.
   - **Edges** represent control flow (branches, loops, jumps).
3. **Coverage Criteria Selection:** Tester selects the coverage level (statement, branch, path, condition).
4. **Test Case Design:** Test cases are derived to *saturate* the chosen coverage criterion.
5. **Instrumentation:** Code is instrumented (e.g., using `gcov`, `JaCoCo`) to track execution.
6. **Execution & Measurement:** Tests are run; coverage is measured and gaps identified.

### Key White-Box Coverage Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are tested in KTU University Exams as direct 2-mark/7-mark derivations. Memorize the exact algebraic form.

$$
\text{Statement Coverage (SC)} = \frac{\text{Number of Executed Statements}}{\text{Total Number of Statements}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Branch Coverage (BC)} = \frac{\text{Number of Executed Branches}}{\text{Total Number of Branches}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Cyclomatic Complexity (CC)} = E - N + 2P
$$

$$
\text{Cyclomatic Complexity (CC)} = \pi + 1 \quad \text{(where } \pi = \text{number of predicate nodes)}
$$

$$
\text{Path Coverage (PC)} = \frac{\text{Number of Executed Independent Paths}}{\text{Total Number of Independent Paths}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $E$ = number of edges in the CFG
- $N$ = number of nodes in the CFG
- $P$ = number of connected components (usually 1 for a single function)
- $\pi$ = number of decision points (`if`, `case`, `while`, `for`, `&&`, `||`)

### Advantages and Disadvantages

**Advantages:**
- Detects **hidden defects** like dead code, unreachable statements, and infinite loops.
- Optimizes source code by identifying **redundant branches**.
- Enables **thorough unit testing** with measurable coverage.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Requires programming expertise** — usually done by developers.
- **Time-consuming and expensive** for large systems.
- Misses **missing requirements** because the focus is on existing code.
- Cannot test **external interfaces** (e.g., UI, third-party APIs) effectively.

## 2.3 Grey-Box Testing — Deep Analysis

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Architectural Review:** Tester acquires high-level design documents (UML diagrams, database schema, API contracts).
2. **Partial Code Inspection:** Targeted code areas (e.g., authentication, payment modules) are reviewed.
3. **Test Case Design:** Tests combine functional scenarios (black-box style) with internal-knowledge insights (e.g., "this database query is slow" → design a stress test).
4. **Execution:** Tests are executed; results correlate with architectural context.
5. **Defect Correlation:** Defects are mapped to architectural layers (UI, API, DB).

### Why Grey-Box Matters in Modern KTU 2024 Curriculum

- **Web Application Testing:** Tester knows the frontend framework (React/Angular) and backend API but does not own the full source.
- **API Testing:** Postman + Swagger documentation give partial insight.
- **Penetration Testing:** Tester knows the network topology but not the full codebase.
- **Microservices Testing:** Each service is a "grey box" — the contract is known, internals are partially visible.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Metric / Formula | Symbol | Unit / Range | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{SC} = \frac{S_{\text{ex}}}{S_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | SC | % (0–100) | Statement coverage |
| 2 | $\text{BC} = \frac{B_{\text{ex}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | BC | % (0–100) | Branch coverage |
| 3 | $\text{CC} = E - N + 2P$ | CC | Integer $\geq 1$ | Cyclomatic complexity |
| 4 | $\text{CC} = \pi + 1$ | CC | Integer $\geq 1$ | Predicate-node method |
| 5 | $\text{PC} = \frac{P_{\text{ind,ex}}}{P_{\text{ind,total}}} \times 100$ | PC | % (0–100) | Path coverage |
| 6 | $\text{Reliability Growth} = \frac{N_{\text{fixed}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | R | Ratio (0–1) | Defect-fix ratio |
| 7 | $\text{Defect Density} = \frac{\text{Defects Found}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | DD | per KLOC | Quality metric |
| 8 | $\text{Test Effectiveness} = \frac{D_{\text{test}}}{D_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | TE | % | Test efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner Tip:** In the formula table, the **vertical bar symbol** in absolute-value expressions (e.g., $\vert x - y \vert$) must be written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. This is a common pitfall students overlook.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Cyclomatic Complexity — Worked Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most frequently asked 7-mark derivation in KTU Software Testing papers. Master the **three methods** below.

### Method 1: Control Flow Graph (Edge-Node) Method

**Given:** A sample `C` function to test for a grade determination system.

```
int getGrade(int marks) {
    char grade;
    if (marks >= 90)
        grade = 'A';
    else if (marks >= 75)
        grade = 'B';
    else if (marks >= 50)
        grade = 'C';
    else
        grade = 'F';
    return grade;
}
```

**Step 1:** Construct the Control Flow Graph (CFG).

The CFG nodes are:
- **N1:** `int getGrade(int marks) {` (entry)
- **N2:** `if (marks >= 90)` (predicate 1)
- **N3:** `grade = 'A';`
- **N4:** `else if (marks >= 75)` (predicate 2)
- **N5:** `grade = 'B';`
- **N6:** `else if (marks >= 50)` (predicate 3)
- **N7:** `grade = 'C';`
- **N8:** `else grade = 'F';`
- **N9:** `return grade;` (exit)

Edges between nodes represent control flow paths.

**Step 2:** Count the nodes and edges.

$$
N = 9 \quad \text{(nodes)}
$$

$$
E = 10 \quad \text{(edges: N1→N2, N2→N3, N2→N4, N4→N5, N4→N6, N6→N7, N6→N8, N3→N9, N5→N9, N7→N9, N8→N9 = 11 actually)}
$$

Let us recount carefully for the 3 `if-else if` chain plus final `else`:
- $N1 \rightarrow N2$
- $N2 \rightarrow N3$ (true branch of pred 1)
- $N2 \rightarrow N4$ (false branch of pred 1)
- $N3 \rightarrow N9$ (shortcut to return)
- $N4 \rightarrow N5$ (true branch of pred 2)
- $N4 \rightarrow N6$ (false branch of pred 2)
- $N5 \rightarrow N9$
- $N6 \rightarrow N7$ (true branch of pred 3)
- $N6 \rightarrow N8$ (false branch of pred 3)
- $N7 \rightarrow N9$
- $N8 \rightarrow N9$

Thus:
$$
E = 11
$$

The connected components are $P = 1$ (single function).

**Step 3:** Apply the cyclomatic complexity formula.

$$
\text{CC} = E - N + 2P
$$

$$
\text{CC} = 11 - 9 + 2(1)
$$

$$
\text{CC} = 11 - 9 + 2
$$

$$
\text{CC} = 4
$$

**Interpretation:** The function has **4 independent paths** that must be tested to achieve 100% path coverage. These are:
- Path 1: $N1 \rightarrow N2 \rightarrow N3 \rightarrow N9$ (grade A)
- Path 2: $N1 \rightarrow N2 \rightarrow N4 \rightarrow N5 \rightarrow N9$ (grade B)
- Path 3: $N1 \rightarrow N2 \rightarrow N4 \rightarrow N6 \rightarrow N7 \rightarrow N9$ (grade C)
- Path 4: $N1 \rightarrow N2 \rightarrow N4 \rightarrow N6 \rightarrow N8 \rightarrow N9$ (grade F)

### Method 2: Predicate-Node Method (Cross-Verification)

**Step 1:** Count the predicate nodes (decision points).
- Predicate 1: `if (marks >= 90)` → $\pi_1 = 1$
- Predicate 2: `else if (marks >= 75)` → $\pi_2 = 1$
- Predicate 3: `else if (marks >= 50)` → $\pi_3 = 1$

**Step 2:** Sum the predicate nodes.
$$
\pi = \pi_1 + \pi_2 + \pi_3 = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula.
$$
\text{CC} = \pi + 1
$$

$$
\text{CC} = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

**Result matches Method 1.** Both methods give $\text{CC} = 4$.

### Method 3: Independent Path Identification

Independent paths are paths that introduce at least one **new edge** that has not been traversed by any previous path. The count of independent paths equals the cyclomatic complexity.

| Path # | Route | New Edges Introduced |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 9$ | 1-2, 2-3, 3-9 |
| 2 | $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 9$ | 2-4, 4-5, 5-9 |
| 3 | $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 7 \rightarrow 9$ | 4-6, 6-7, 7-9 |
| 4 | $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 8 \rightarrow 9$ | 6-8, 8-9 |

$$
\text{Number of Independent Paths} = 4 = \text{CC}
$$

## 3.2 Black-Box Test Case Design — Equivalence Partitioning

**Given:** A function to validate a user's age for an online voting system. The valid age range is **[18, 120]**.

**Step 1:** Identify Equivalence Classes (ECs).

| Class ID | Range | Validity |
|---|---|---|
| EC1 | $0 \leq \text{age} \leq 17$ | Invalid (too young) |
| EC2 | $18 \leq \text{age} \leq 120$ | Valid |
| EC3 | $121 \leq \text{age} \leq 150$ | Invalid (too old) |

**Step 2:** Select one representative value from each class.

$$
\text{EC1 representative} = 10 \quad \text{(invalid input test)}
$$

$$
\text{EC2 representative} = 25 \quad \text{(valid input test)}
$$

$$
\text{EC3 representative} = 130 \quad \text{(invalid input test)}
$$

**Step 3:** Combine with Boundary Value Analysis (BVA).

Boundary values: 17, 18, 120, 121. Add 4 more test cases.

**Total test cases designed:** $3 + 4 = 7$.

This demonstrates the power of black-box testing — **7 test cases** cover thousands of possible inputs, leveraging the principle that if one value in a class works, the entire class works.

## 3.3 Coverage Comparison — Analytical Matrix

| Module | Statements | Branches | Independent Paths | CC | Min Test Cases (PC) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `getGrade()` | 9 | 8 | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| `login()` (2 if) | 5 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 |
| `sort3()` (1 if, 1 loop) | 8 | 6 | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| `factorial()` (1 loop) | 4 | 2 | 2 | 2 | 2 |
