# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Black-Box Testing and White-Box Testing with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Black-Box Testing | White-Box Testing |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Tests functionality without knowing internal code | Tests internal structure with full code access |
| Knowledge Required | SRS / Specifications | Source code, design documents |
| Also Called | Functional / Behavioral | Structural / Glass-Box |
| Tester | QA team, end users | Developers, SDETs |
| Goal | Validate *what* the system does | Verify *how* the system works |
| Example | Testing login with valid/invalid credentials | Ensuring all `if-else` branches of login are executed |
| Automation Tool | Selenium, QTP | JUnit, PyTest, JaCoCo |
| Coverage Metric | Requirement coverage % | Statement / Branch / Path coverage % |
| Defects Caught | Missing features, wrong outputs | Dead code, unreachable branches |

**Example (3 marks structure):**
- Black-box example: Tester checks if a login form accepts `admin/admin` and rejects `admin/wrongpass` without viewing the backend PHP code. **(1 mark)**
- White-box example: Developer writes unit tests in JUnit to ensure all 4 branches of the `login()` function (valid user, wrong password, locked account, SQL injection attempt) execute at least once. **(1 mark)**
- Key differentiator: knowledge and goal. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Grey-Box Testing? List any four scenarios where it is preferred.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Grey-Box Testing is a hybrid software testing technique where the tester has **partial knowledge** of the internal structure of the application. Tests are designed using both functional specifications and limited architectural insights.

**Four Scenarios (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Web Application Testing:** Tester knows the frontend framework (e.g., React) and REST API contract but not the full backend source.
2. **API Testing:** Tester uses Swagger/OpenAPI documentation (architectural insight) to design test cases.
3. **Penetration Testing:** Tester knows the network topology and database schema but exploits the system as an outsider.
4. **Integration Testing of Microservices:** Tester knows each service's contract and shared database schema.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — Choice 1

**(a) Explain the Black-Box Testing technique in detail. List and briefly explain any four black-box test case design techniques. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction to Black-Box Testing (2 marks):**
Black-Box Testing, also known as **Functional** or **Behavioral Testing**, is a software testing method that validates the functionality of an application **without examining its internal code structure, implementation details, or internal paths**. The tester is treated as an end-user who provides inputs and verifies outputs against the expected results defined in the Software Requirement Specification (SRS). It is the most widely used technique in the industry for system, acceptance, and regression testing.

**Four Black-Box Test Case Design Techniques (5 marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Equivalence Partitioning (EP):** Input data is divided into equivalence classes that should be treated equivalently. A single test value from each class is sufficient. For example, for an age validation field accepting 18–60, classes are: invalid (0–17), valid (18–60), invalid (61–150). One value from each class is tested.

2. **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA):** Tests are designed to focus on values at the **boundaries** of equivalence classes, since defects are most likely at edges. For the age field, the boundaries are 17, 18, 60, 61. Each is tested along with one just inside.

3. **Decision Table Testing:** Captures combinations of inputs that produce different outputs. Useful for **business rules** with multiple conditions. For example, a loan approval system: (Income > 50K AND Credit Score > 700) → Approved; otherwise → Rejected.

4. **State Transition Testing:** Tests the system's behaviour as it moves between **states** in response to events. Useful for workflows (e.g., ATM: Idle → Card Inserted → PIN Entered → Transaction → Dispensing → Idle). Test cases cover valid transitions and invalid ones.

**Conclusion:** Black-box testing is essential for validating user-facing functionality and is most effective when combined with white-box testing in a layered testing strategy.

---

**(b) Explain White-Box Testing. Compute the Cyclomatic Complexity of the following code segment using any two methods. (7 marks)**

**Code Segment:**
```
int calculateBonus(int years, int rating) {
    int bonus = 0;
    if (years > 5 && rating >= 4) {
        bonus = 10000;
    } else if (years > 5 && rating < 4) {
        bonus = 5000;
    } else if (years <= 5 && rating >= 4) {
        bonus = 3000;
    } else {
        bonus = 1000;
    }
    return bonus;
}
```

**Model Solution:**

**White-Box Testing Explanation (2.5 marks):**
White-Box Testing, also called **Structural** or **Glass-Box Testing**, examines the internal structure, logic, and code paths of a program. The tester has **full access to the source code** and designs test cases based on control flow, data flow, and code paths. It is typically performed by developers at the **unit and integration testing** phases. Coverage criteria include statement coverage, branch coverage, and path coverage.

**Method 1: Control Flow Graph Method (2.25 marks):**

**Step 1:** Construct the CFG.
- Nodes: $N1$ (entry), $N2$ (predicate 1: `years > 5 && rating >= 4`), $N3$ (bonus=10000), $N4$ (predicate 2: `years > 5 && rating < 4`), $N5$ (bonus=5000), $N6$ (predicate 3: `years <= 5 && rating >= 4`), $N7$ (bonus=3000), $N8$ (bonus=1000), $N9$ (return).

**Step 2:** Count nodes and edges.
$$
N = 9
$$
$$
E = 11 \quad (N1\!\to\!N2, N2\!\to\!N3, N2\!\to\!N4, N3\!\to\!N9, N4\!\to\!N5, N4\!\to\!N6, N5\!\to\!N9, N6\!\to\!N7, N6\!\to\!N8, N7\!\to\!N9, N8\!\to\!N9)
$$
$$
P = 1
$$

**Step 3:** Apply formula.
$$
\text{CC} = E - N + 2P
$$
$$
\text{CC} = 11 - 9 + 2(1) = 4
$$

**Method 2: Predicate-Node Method (2.25 marks):**

Predicate nodes are decision points.
- Predicate 1: `years > 5 && rating >= 4` → $\pi_1 = 1$ (compound predicate with `&&` counts as 1 decision)
- Predicate 2: `years > 5 && rating < 4` → $\pi_2 = 1$
- Predicate 3: `years <= 5 && rating >= 4` → $\pi_3 = 1$

$$
\pi = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3
$$

$$
\text{CC} = \pi + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4
$$

**Result:** Cyclomatic Complexity = **4**, meaning **4 independent paths** must be tested for 100% path coverage.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — Choice 2

**(a) Discuss in detail the various White-Box Testing techniques. Explain cyclomatic complexity with an example. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** White-Box Testing focuses on the internal structure of the code. It requires programming knowledge and is performed primarily by developers during unit and integration testing.

**White-Box Testing Techniques (3.5 marks — 0.875 each):**

1. **Statement Coverage:** Ensures every executable statement runs at least once. $\text{SC} = \frac{S_{\text{ex}}}{S_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$. Weakest criterion — a single test can give 100% SC.

2. **Branch Coverage (Decision Coverage):** Ensures every branch (true/false outcome of every decision) is executed. $\text{BC} = \frac{B_{\text{ex}}}{B_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$. Stronger than SC.

3. **Path Coverage:** Ensures every independent path from entry to exit is executed. $\text{PC} = \frac{P_{\text{ind,ex}}}{P_{\text{ind,total}}} \times 100\%$. Strongest but most expensive; often impractical for loops.

4. **Condition Coverage:** Each boolean sub-expression (in `&&`/`||`) must evaluate to both true and false. Example: For `A && B`, test (A=T, B=T) and (A=F, B=F).

5. **Data Flow Testing:** Tracks variable definitions and uses to detect anomalies (e.g., defined-but-never-used, used-before-defined).

**Cyclomatic Complexity Example (2.5 marks):**
For a function with 1 `if-else` and 1 `for` loop:
- Predicate nodes: 2 (one for `if`, one for `for`)
- $\text{CC} = \pi + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$
- 3 independent paths must be tested.

This helps the tester determine the **minimum number of test cases** required for complete path coverage.

---

**(b) Compare Black-Box, White-Box, and Grey-Box Testing in a tabular form. Explain any two equivalence partitioning test cases for a function that validates passwords (must be 8–16 characters, contain at least one digit and one uppercase letter). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | Black-Box | White-Box | Grey-Box |
|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Knowledge | None | Full | Partial |
| Test Basis | SRS, use cases | Source code | Both |
| Tester | QA / End-user | Developer | Mixed |
| Coverage Metric | Requirement % | SC, BC, PC | Combined |
| Tool Example | Selenium | JUnit | Postman |
| Phase of Use | System, Acceptance | Unit, Integration | Integration |
| Defects Caught | Missing/wrong functionality | Code-level issues | Integration gaps |
| Cost | Low | High | Medium |

**Equivalence Partitioning for Password Validator (3.5 marks):**

**Equivalence Classes Identified:**

| Class | Description | Range / Condition | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| EC1 | Length below 8 | 1 ≤ length ≤ 7 | Invalid |
| EC2 | Length within range but no digit | 8 ≤ length ≤ 16, no digit | Invalid |
| EC3 | Length within range but no uppercase | 8 ≤ length ≤ 16, no uppercase | Invalid |
| EC4 | Length within range, has digit and uppercase | 8 ≤ length ≤ 16, ≥1 digit, ≥1 uppercase | **Valid** |
| EC5 | Length above 16 | length ≥ 17 | Invalid |

**Sample Test Cases (any 2 elaborated — 1.75 each):**

**Test Case 1 — Invalid (EC1):**
- Input: `"Ab1"` (length 3, has digit and uppercase)
- Expected: Rejected with error `"Password too short"`
- This tests the lower boundary on length.

**Test Case 2 — Valid (EC4):**
- Input: `"Secure123Pass"` (length 14, has digits "123" and uppercase "S, P")
- Expected: Accepted
- This tests the happy path satisfying all conditions.

These two test cases alone cover the most critical partitions and validate the business rules.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE Papers:**
> 1. **Confusing Black-Box and White-Box Definitions (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students write that black-box testing "knows the internal code." This is **incorrect**. Black-box = NO internal code knowledge.
> 2. **Forgetting `+ 2P` in Cyclomatic Complexity (–1 mark):** The full formula is $\text{CC} = E - N + 2P$. Writing only $E - N$ loses a mark. Default $P = 1$ for single functions.
> 3. **Mixing up Coverage Hierarchy:** Saying "100% branch coverage implies 100% path coverage" is **FALSE**. The correct chain is: Path ⊃ Branch ⊃ Statement.
> 4. **Not Listing Examples:** Every 7-mark question in KTU 2024 expects **at least one real-world example** (ATM, login, grading system). Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Incorrect Equivalence Class Boundaries:** For the range [18, 60], the classes are [0,17], [18,60], [61,150]. Writing [0,18] or [17,60] is wrong — it must be **non-overlapping** and **collectively exhaustive**.
> 6. **Skipping the Diagram:** For 14-mark questions, a **flowchart or comparison table** is mandatory. A purely textual answer loses 2–3 marks on presentation.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Black-Box Testing** validates *what* the system does, with **no** internal code knowledge; techniques include EP, BVA, decision tables, state transitions, and error guessing.
- **White-Box Testing** verifies *how* the system works internally; coverage criteria are **Statement, Branch, and Path Coverage**; uses formulas $\text{CC} = E - N + 2P$ and $\text{CC} = \pi + 1$.
- **Grey-Box Testing** is the **hybrid** technique with **partial** internal knowledge; widely used in **API, integration, and penetration testing**.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity** equals the **minimum number of independent paths** to be tested for 100% path coverage.
- **Path Coverage ⊃ Branch Coverage ⊃ Statement Coverage** — this hierarchy is **inviolable** and frequently asked.
- Always construct the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** before computing CC; count nodes ($N$), edges ($E$), and connected components ($P$).
- For KTU 2024, expect at least **one 14-mark question** combining testing methods with cyclomatic complexity computation.
- The **vertical bar `|` in LaTeX** must be written as `\vert` or `\mid` inside markdown tables to avoid breaking the table parser.
- For black-box test design, always specify **both valid and invalid** equivalence classes, and combine with **BVA** for boundary coverage.
- **Industry-accepted minimums:** 80% SC, 70% BC, 60% PC for white-box; ≥95% requirement coverage for black-box.
- The three testing methods are **complementary**, not alternatives — modern V-Model testing uses all three at different SDLC phases (Unit: White, Integration: Grey, System/Acceptance: Black).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automation in Testing: Introduction to Automation Tools (e.g., Selenium, Cypress, JUnit)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Automation in Testing: Introduction to Automation Tools (e.g., Selenium, Cypress, JUnit)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Test Automation** is the software engineering practice of using specialized software tools, frameworks, and scripts to execute test cases automatically, compare actual outcomes against predicted outcomes, and report results—without manual human intervention. According to the KTU 2024 Software Testing syllabus, automation in testing refers to the systematic use of dedicated tools (e.g., **Selenium**, **Cypress**, **JUnit**) to control the execution of tests, manage test data, and utilize results to improve software quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight**
> Test Automation is NOT a replacement for manual testing. It is a complementary strategy used to automate **repetitive**, **regression-prone**, and **data-intensive** test cases. The IEEE Standard 829 and ISTQB foundation level both treat automation as an *enhancement layer* over the manual test process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a large bakery that produces 5,000 loaves of bread daily.

- **Manual Testing** = A baker who tastes every single batch by hand, smells the crust, checks the texture. This works when the bakery is small, but becomes impossible at scale.
- **Automated Testing** = An industrial oven fitted with sensors (temperature, humidity, weight) connected to a robotic arm. The sensors (assertion logic) continuously check the quality of every loaf without human effort, and the robotic arm (test executor) removes defective ones automatically.

The **tools** (Selenium, Cypress, JUnit) are the *sensors and robotic arms* of the software world—they drive the application, observe its behaviour, and report defects with zero fatigue, **24/7**.

### 1.3 Why Automate? The Three Pillars

| Pillar | Meaning | Engineering Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed** | Tests run faster than humans | **Execution time** in seconds |
| **Reliability** | Eliminates human error in repetitive tasks | **Pass/Fail accuracy rate** |
| **Coverage** | Enables execution of thousands of test cases per cycle | **Test coverage %** of requirements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Why automation?" in exams, never answer "to replace manual testers." The correct KTU-accepted answer is: *to increase efficiency, repeatability, and coverage of regression testing while freeing manual testers for exploratory and usability testing.*

### 1.4 The Three Tools at a Glance (KTU Mandatory Tools)

- **Selenium** – An *open-source browser automation framework* primarily used for **functional and regression testing of web applications**. Supports multiple languages (Java, Python, C#, JS).
- **Cypress** – A *modern, JavaScript-based end-to-end testing framework* built specifically for the modern web. Runs in the browser itself, providing faster feedback than Selenium.
- **JUnit** – A *unit testing framework* for the Java programming language, used by developers to write and run repeatable test cases at the **code/component level** (white-box testing).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 When to Automate – The Decision Logic

Automation is **not** a universal solution. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following decision criteria:

1. **Stable Requirements** – The AUT (Application Under Test) must have stable, well-defined functionality.
2. **Repetitive Execution** – Tests that are run frequently (regression, smoke, sanity).
3. **Data-Driven Scenarios** – Same flow, multiple inputs (e.g., login with 1000 user credentials).
4. **Time-Critical Releases** – CI/CD pipelines where manual execution is impossible within sprint time.
5. **High Business Risk Areas** – Payment gateways, authentication, data integrity modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb (Exam Favourite):**
> - **Automate** → Regression, Smoke, Load, Data-Driven tests
> - **Do NOT Automate** → One-time tests, UI-heavy exploratory tests, usability tests

### 2.2 The Test Automation Life Cycle (TAL) – Stepwise Logic

The TAL is the structured process for introducing automation into a project. Each step has a clear "Why" and "How":

1. **Decision to Automate** → *Why:* Identify if automation is cost-effective. *How:* Use ROI and feasibility analysis.
2. **Tool Selection** → *Why:* The wrong tool causes project failure. *How:* Match tool features to project stack (web/mobile/API).
3. **Scope Definition** → *Why:* Avoid over-automation. *How:* Pick a pilot module (e.g., Login page).
4. **Test Case Design & Scripting** → *Why:* Reusable scripts. *How:* Apply data-driven or keyword-driven frameworks.
5. **Test Execution** → *Why:* Validate the application. *How:* Run via CI/CD (Jenkins, GitHub Actions).
6. **Result Analysis & Reporting** → *Why:* Identify defects. *How:* HTML/JSON reports (Allure, ExtentReports).
7. **Maintenance** → *Why:* UI changes break scripts. *How:* Page Object Model (POM) design pattern.

### 2.3 Deep Dive: Selenium Architecture (KTU High-Yield)

Selenium is **not a single tool**—it is a *suite*:

- **Selenium WebDriver** → A W3C-standard API that directly communicates with the browser via its native engine.
- **Selenium IDE** → A browser plugin (record & playback) for quick prototyping.
- **Selenium Grid** → Enables **parallel** and **distributed** test execution across multiple machines and browsers.

### 2.4 Deep Dive: Cypress vs Selenium

- **Selenium** runs *outside* the browser, communicating via the WebDriver protocol (HTTP-based).
- **Cypress** runs *inside* the browser, giving it native access to the DOM, network, and JavaScript objects → faster execution, automatic waiting, and time-travel debugging.

### 2.5 Deep Dive: JUnit

JUnit is a **developer-side white-box** testing tool. It uses **annotations** (e.g., `@Test`, `@BeforeEach`, `@AfterAll`) to define the test lifecycle. It belongs to the **xUnit** family of testing frameworks (originated with SUnit for Smalltalk).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Selenium | Cypress | JUnit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Use** | Web UI automation | Web E2E automation | Unit testing (Java) |
| **Language** | Java, Python, C#, JS, Ruby | JavaScript / TypeScript | Java |
| **Architecture** | Out-of-browser (WebDriver) | In-browser (Node.js) | In-process (JVM) |
| **Test Level** | System / Acceptance | Integration / E2E | Unit / Component |
| **Speed** | Moderate | Fast (Native) | Very Fast (In-memory) |
| **Parallel Execution** | Yes (via Grid) | Yes (via CI) | Yes (via Maven/Gradle) |
| **CI/CD Support** | Excellent (Jenkins, GitLab) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **License** | Open Source (Apache 2.0) | Open Source (MIT) | Open Source (EPL 1.0) |
| **Test Report Tool** | Allure, ExtentReports | Mochawesome, Allure | Surefire, Allure |
| **Typical KTU Mark Weight** | 3–5 marks | 2–3 marks | 3–5 marks |

### 2.7 KTU Test Automation ROI Formula (High-Yield for Numerical Questions)

The **Return on Investment (ROI)** for automation is the most commonly asked numerical in KTU ESE Module 1. The KTU-accepted formula is:

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; \frac{\text{Manual Cost (Over N cycles)} - \text{Automation Cost}}{\text{Automation Cost}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- **Manual Cost** = $\text{Cycles} \times \text{Test Cases} \times \text{Avg. Manual Execution Time} \times \text{Hourly Rate}$
- **Automation Cost** = $\text{Tool License} + \text{Script Development} + \text{Maintenance Cost}$

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Banking:** Automating 10,000 transaction scenarios in seconds for **SWIFT** and **UPI** gateways.
- **E-Commerce:** Continuous regression testing of Amazon/Flipkart cart, payment, and search modules.
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA-compliant automation of patient data workflows.
- **DevOps:** Automation is the *backbone* of CI/CD pipelines—without it, continuous delivery is impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Test Automation ROI Calculation

This is a classic **14-mark numerical** in KTU ESE. We will solve it step-by-step.

#### Problem Statement

A fintech company has **500 regression test cases**. Currently, these are run manually over **4 release cycles per year**. The average manual execution time is **10 minutes per test case**, and the tester cost is ₹600/hour. The company is evaluating automation:
- Tool License = ₹1,00,000
- Script Development = ₹3,00,000
- Annual Maintenance = ₹50,000
- Automated execution time = **2 minutes per test case** (but tester still monitors at ₹600/hour for 10 hours total per cycle).

**Calculate the ROI of automation over 3 years.**

---

#### Step 1: Compute Manual Cost (Per Year)

We first calculate the time taken manually per year.

$$
T_{\text{manual/cycle}} \;=\; 500 \;\times\; 10 \;\text{min} \;=\; 5000 \;\text{min}
$$

Now convert to hours and multiply by 4 cycles:

$$
T_{\text{manual/year}} \;=\; \frac{5000}{60} \;\times\; 4 \;\approx\; 333.33 \;\text{hours}
$$

The total manual cost per year is:

$$
C_{\text{manual/year}} \;=\; 333.33 \;\times\; 600 \;=\; \text{₹}\;2{,}00{,}000
$$

> **Logic:** We divide by 60 to convert minutes to hours, then multiply by the hourly tester rate.

---

#### Step 2: Compute Manual Cost Over 3 Years

$$
C_{\text{manual, 3yr}} \;=\; 2{,}00{,}000 \;\times\; 3 \;=\; \text{₹}\;6{,}00{,}000
$$

---

#### Step 3: Compute Automation Cost Over 3 Years

We sum all three cost components and multiply the annual maintenance by 3 (Year 1 has no prior maintenance):

$$
C_{\text{auto, 3yr}} \;=\; \underbrace{1{,}00{,}000}_{\text{license}} \;+\; \underbrace{3{,}00{,}000}_{\text{dev}} \;+\; \underbrace{50{,}000 \;\times\; 3}_{\text{maint.}} \;=\; \text{₹}\;5{,}50{,}000
$$

> **Logic:** License is one-time, development is one-time, but maintenance recurs annually.

---

#### Step 4: Compute Net Savings

$$
\text{Savings} \;=\; C_{\text{manual, 3yr}} - C_{\text{auto, 3yr}} \;=\; 6{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}50{,}000 \;=\; \text{₹}\;50{,}000
$$

> **Logic:** Note the **monitoring cost** is *not subtracted* because it is already the same in both manual and automated scenarios (the engineer supervises either way). If the examiner specifies a *fully unattended* automation, then monitoring cost may be excluded from the manual baseline.

---

#### Step 5: Apply the ROI Formula

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; \frac{\text{Savings}}{\text{Automation Cost}} \times 100\%
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; \frac{50{,}000}{5{,}50{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; 0.0909 \times 100\% \;\approx\; 9.09\%
$$

> **Logic:** A **positive ROI** indicates automation is profitable, but values below 20% in 3 years are borderline—KTU examiners usually ask students to **comment** on the result. A better business case would be obtained by running for 5 years or scaling to 2000 test cases.

---

#### Step 6: Breakeven Point (Common Follow-up)

The breakeven cycle $N$ is found when total manual cost equals total automation cost.

$$
C_{\text{manual/year}} \;\times\; N \;=\; 1{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 \;\times\; N
$$

$$
2{,}00{,}000 N \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 N
$$

$$
150{,}000 N \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000 \;\Longrightarrow\; N \;=\; 2.67 \;\text{years}
$$

> **Logic:** The project pays for itself in approximately **2 years and 8 months**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Tool Selection Criteria (KTU 7-Mark Theory)

When selecting an automation tool, the KTU 2024 scheme expects the student to evaluate on the following dimensions:

| Criterion | Weight (Typical) | What to Check |
|---|---|---|
| **Technology Fit** | 25% | Does the tool support the app's tech stack (React, Angular, Java)? |
| **Ease of Scripting** | 20% | Language support, learning curve |
| **Reporting & Logs** | 15% | Screenshot on failure, HTML reports, logs |
| **CI/CD Integration** | 15% | Jenkins, GitLab, GitHub Actions compatibility |
| **Parallel Execution** | 10% | Grid support, cloud (BrowserStack, SauceLabs) |
| **Cost & Licensing** | 10% | Open-source vs commercial |
| **Community & Support** | 5% | Stack Overflow, GitHub, documentation |

> **Logic:** The weights are subjective; what matters in the exam is showing that *the choice is multi-criteria*, not arbitrary.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Setting Up a JUnit Test (Conceptual Walkthrough)

Since this is a CS subject, a structural walkthrough of the JUnit lifecycle is appropriate.

1. **Add JUnit dependency** in the project (Maven/Gradle).
2. **Create a test class** mirroring the production class.
3. **Annotate test methods** with `@Test` so JUnit recognises them.
4. **Use `@BeforeEach`** to set up preconditions (fresh database state).
5. **Use `@AfterEach`** for cleanup.
6. **Use `assertEquals(actual, expected)`** to verify behaviour.
7. **Run the suite** → JUnit reports green (pass) or red (fail).

> **Logic:** JUnit follows the **Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA)** pattern, which is also a common KTU exam short-answer topic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Selenium WebDriver Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TS["Test Scripts\nJava / Python / C#"] --> LIB["Client Library\nSelenium API"]
    LIB --> JSON["WebDriver Protocol\nJSON Wire over HTTP"]
    JSON --> DRV["Browser Driver\nchromedriver / geckodriver"]
    DRV --> BR["Browser\nChrome / Firefox / Edge"]
    BR --> RESP["HTTP Response\nStatus + DOM State"]
    RESP --> LIB
    LIB --> REPORT["Test Report\nAllure / ExtentReports"]

    style TS fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style LIB fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style DRV fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style BR fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style REPORT fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading Guide:** The script issues a command (e.g., `click()`) → the **client library** sends it as a **JSON-over-HTTP** request → the **browser driver** translates it into a **native browser action** → the browser executes and returns the state.

---

### 4.2 Test Automation Life Cycle (TAL) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Decision\nto Automate"] --> B["Step 2: Tool\nSelection"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Define\nScope and Pilot"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Test\nScripting"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Test\nExecution"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Result\nAnalysis"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Script\nMaintenance"]
    G --> E

    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#8c564b,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#e377c2,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading Guide:** The **loop back to Step 5** is critical—maintenance is *iterative*, not one-time. This is a common KTU short-answer diagram.

---

### 4.3 Tool Categorisation Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEVEL["Test Level"]
        UL["Unit"]
        IT["Integration"]
        ST["System"]
        AC["Acceptance"]
    end

    subgraph TOOLS["Automation Tool"]
        JN["JUnit"]
        TS["TestNG"]
        SL["Selenium"]
        CP["Cypress"]
        CU["Cucumber"]
    end

    UL --> JN
    UL --> TS
    IT --> TS
    IT --> SL
    ST --> SL
    ST --> CP
    AC --> CU
    AC --> CP

    style JN fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style TS fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style SL fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style CP fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style CU fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the **tool-to-test-level mapping**. JUnit is at the **unit** level; Selenium and Cypress dominate **system/integration**; Cucumber is used for **acceptance (BDD)**.

---

### 4.4 ROI Decision Tree (When to Automate)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the test\nrepetitive?"}
    Q2{"Are requirements\nstable?"}
    Q3{"Is the data\nlarge-scale?"}
    A1["AUTOMATE\nSelenium / Cypress / JUnit"]
    A2["MANUAL\nExploratory testing"]
    A3["HYBRID\nManual for design,\nautomated for execution"]

    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2
    Q1 -->|No| A2
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3
    Q2 -->|No| A2
    Q3 -->|Yes| A1
    Q3 -->|No| A3

    style A1 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style A2 fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
    style A3 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define test automation. List any four criteria for selecting a test case for automation.*

**Model Answer:**

**Test Automation** is the use of specialized software tools to control the execution of tests, compare actual outcomes with expected outcomes, and report results—without manual intervention.

**Four Selection Criteria:**
1. The test case is run **repeatedly** (regression/smoke tests).
2. The test case is **data-driven** with multiple input combinations.
3. The functionality is **stable** and not undergoing frequent UI changes.
4. The test case is **time-consuming or error-prone** when executed manually.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Compare Selenium and Cypress on any three parameters.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Selenium | Cypress |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution Location** | Runs *outside* the browser via WebDriver | Runs *inside* the browser natively |
| **Language Support** | Java, Python, C#, JS, Ruby | JavaScript / TypeScript only |
| **Speed & Debugging** | Moderate; needs external tools | Fast; built-in time-travel debugging |

> Additional valid points: parallel execution (Grid), auto-wait mechanism, network stubbing.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Test Automation Life Cycle (TAL)** in detail with a neat diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A banking application has **800 regression test cases** executed across **4 release cycles per year**. Manual execution takes **15 minutes per test case** at a tester cost of ₹500/hour. The company is automating:
- Tool cost: ₹80,000
- Script development: ₹2,50,000
- Annual maintenance: ₹40,000

After automation, execution time drops to **3 minutes per test case**, and **10% of test cases still require manual verification** at the same rate. **Calculate the ROI of automation over 4 years** and comment on whether automation is justified. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The TAL consists of **seven phases**:
1. **Decision to Automate** – Cost-benefit analysis using ROI.
2. **Tool Selection** – Evaluate based on technology fit, cost, and reporting.
3. **Define Scope** – Choose a pilot module (e.g., login) to limit risk.
4. **Test Design & Scripting** – Use frameworks: Data-Driven, Keyword-Driven, Hybrid.
5. **Test Execution** – Run via CI/CD; capture screenshots on failure.
6. **Result Analysis** – Use Allure/ExtentReports for dashboards.
7. **Maintenance** – Refactor scripts using **Page Object Model (POM)** to handle UI changes.

> **Diagram:** Draw the flowchart from **Section 4.2** of these notes for full marks.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Manual Time per Cycle**

$$
T_{\text{manual/cycle}} \;=\; 800 \;\times\; 15 \;\text{min} \;=\; 12{,}000 \;\text{min}
$$

**Step 2: Manual Time per Year (in hours)**

$$
T_{\text{manual/year}} \;=\; \frac{12{,}000}{60} \;\times\; 4 \;=\; 800 \;\text{hours}
$$

**Step 3: Manual Cost per Year**

$$
C_{\text{manual/year}} \;=\; 800 \;\times\; 500 \;=\; \text{₹}\;4{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Manual Cost for 4 Years**

$$
C_{\text{manual, 4yr}} \;=\; 4{,}00{,}000 \;\times\; 4 \;=\; \text{₹}\;16{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Automation Cost for 4 Years**

$$
C_{\text{auto, 4yr}} \;=\; 80{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 + (40{,}000 \times 4) \;=\; \text{₹}\;4{,}90{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Compute ROI**

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; \frac{16{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}90{,}000}{4{,}90{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ROI} \;=\; \frac{11{,}10{,}000}{4{,}90{,}000} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 226.53\%
$$

**Comment:** An ROI of **~227%** over 4 years is *highly justified*. The breakeven occurs in less than 1.5 years, confirming automation is a profitable strategic decision.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of **Selenium WebDriver** with a block diagram. How does it differ from **Selenium IDE** and **Selenium Grid**? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of test automation. When should a project *avoid* automation? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **Selenium WebDriver architecture** consists of four layers:
1. **Test Script Layer** – Written in Java/Python/C# using Selenium API.
2. **Client Library / Language Bindings** – Translates script commands to WebDriver protocol.
3. **Browser Driver (e.g., chromedriver)** – A native server that receives commands over HTTP (JSON Wire Protocol) and controls the browser.
4. **Browser** – Executes the actual UI actions and returns the response.

> **Diagram:** Use the **Section 4.1** Mermaid block from these notes.

**Differences:**

| Feature | Selenium IDE | Selenium WebDriver | Selenium Grid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Record & playback | Programmatic API | Parallel execution hub |
| **Coding** | Not required | Required | Required |
| **Use Case** | Quick prototypes | Production automation | Distributed testing |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Advantages:**
1. Faster execution → thousands of tests in minutes.
2. Reusability → same script for multiple builds.
3. Improved accuracy → no manual fatigue errors.
4. Enables CI/CD → critical for DevOps.
5. Better coverage → regression on every commit.

**Limitations:**
1. **High initial cost** (tool + training + development).
2. **Maintenance overhead** when UI changes.
3. Cannot automate **usability** or **exploratory** tests.
4. Requires **skilled resources** (programmers).
5. **False sense of security** — passing automated tests ≠ bug-free software.

**When to Avoid Automation:**
- Short-lived projects (< 6 months).
- Frequently changing UI / unstable requirements.
- One-time test cases.
- Tests requiring human judgment (UX, accessibility, A/B visual checks).
- Projects with no scripting expertise.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Selenium IDE with WebDriver** — IDE is record/playback, WebDriver is programmatic. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you call WebDriver a "recording tool."
> 2. **Forgetting the formula units** — ROI must be in **percentage (%)**; cost values in **currency (₹)**. Writing "ROI = 2.26" instead of "226%" loses a mark.
> 3. **Wrong TAL sequence** — Steps must be in the order: *Decision → Tool → Scope → Scripting → Execution → Analysis → Maintenance*. Reversing "Analysis" and "Maintenance" is a common error.
> 4. **Assuming automation replaces manual testing** — The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly states automation *augments* manual testing. Any answer claiming "automation removes the need for manual testers" is marked down.
> 5. **Mixing JUnit (unit) with Selenium (UI)** — JUnit is *not* a UI tool. If a question says "automate the login page," JUnit alone is incorrect—you need Selenium/Cypress for the UI.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Test Automation** = tool-driven, repeatable, no-manual-intervention test execution.
- **TAL** has **7 phases**; maintenance is a loop, not a final step.
- **Selenium** is a *suite* of three tools: **IDE**, **WebDriver**, **Grid**.
- **Selenium WebDriver** uses **JSON Wire Protocol** over **HTTP**; runs *outside* the browser.
- **Cypress** runs *inside* the browser; JS/TS only; faster debug.
- **JUnit** is a **Java unit testing framework**; uses `@Test`, `@BeforeEach`, `@AfterAll`; belongs to the **xUnit** family.
- **Automate**: regression, smoke, data-driven, performance. **Don't automate**: exploratory, usability, one-time.
- **ROI Formula**: $\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Manual Cost} - \text{Automation Cost}}{\text{Automation Cost}} \times 100\%$
- **Breakeven** is when total manual savings = total automation cost; compute by setting $C_{\text{manual}} \cdot N = C_{\text{license}} + C_{\text{dev}} + C_{\text{maint}} \cdot N$.
- **Page Object Model (POM)** is the standard design pattern for UI test maintenance.
- **CI/CD tools**: Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI — all support Selenium, Cypress, and JUnit.
- **Reporting tools**: Allure, ExtentReports (Selenium/Cypress); Surefire (JUnit/Maven).
- A **positive ROI** does not guarantee project success — also evaluate **script maintainability** and **team skill readiness**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Automation of Unit Testing and Mutation Testing using JUnit
Unit Testing, Mutation Testing & AI-Driven Automation: Static and Dynamic Unit Testing, Control Flow Testing, Data Flow Testing, Domain Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unit Testing, Mutation Testing & AI-Driven Automation: Static and Dynamic Unit Testing, Control Flow Testing, Data Flow Testing, Domain Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Unit Testing — The Formal Definition

**Unit Testing** is the lowest level of software testing performed at the **source code** level, where individual **units** (functions, methods, classes, or modules) are tested in **isolation** from the rest of the system to validate that each unit performs as designed. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based framework, unit testing is mapped to **CO2: Apply unit-level testing techniques** and aligns with the IEEE 829 and ISTQB syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *"A unit is the smallest testable piece of code; unit testing verifies the functional correctness, control logic, and data handling of that piece in isolation."*

**Two Major Branches of Unit Testing:**

| Branch | When | Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Unit Testing** | Without executing code | Walkthroughs, Inspections, Code Reviews, Linters, Static Analyzers (SonarQube, ESLint) |
| **Dynamic Unit Testing** | By executing code | JUnit, NUnit, pytest, TestNG, xUnit |

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Car Engine Workshop

Imagine you are a quality engineer at a **car manufacturing plant**:
- **Static Testing** = Examining engine parts on a workbench — visually checking bolts, reading the design blueprints, weighing pistons — *without ever starting the engine*.
- **Dynamic Testing** = Mounting the engine on a test rig, running it at different RPMs, measuring temperature, fuel efficiency, and vibration — *actually starting and exercising the engine*.

For software:
- **Static** = Reading the code, looking for syntax errors, dead code, uninitialized variables.
- **Dynamic** = Calling the function `add(2, 3)` and checking if it returns `5`.

Both are complementary; neither replaces the other.

---

### 1.3 Control Flow Testing — Definition

**Control Flow Testing (CFT)** is a white-box dynamic testing technique in which **test cases are designed to exercise specific paths** through a program's control structure, expressed as a **flow graph (CFG)**. It is grounded in McCabe's structural complexity theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Control Flow Testing is the *most frequently asked* topic in Module 2 — expected for 7–14 marks.

### 1.4 Data Flow Testing — Definition

**Data Flow Testing (DFT)** is a white-box testing technique that focuses on the **points where variables are defined, used, and where the data flow may go wrong** (anomalies). It combines control flow with variable lifetime analysis.

### 1.5 Domain Testing — Definition

**Domain Testing** is a functional testing technique that tests the **input domain** of a program by selecting **boundary points (on-points)** and **points just inside/outside the domain (off-points)**. It is most useful for programs with **inequalities**, ranges, or piecewise logic.

### 1.6 Mutation Testing — Definition

**Mutation Testing** is a fault-based testing technique in which small syntactic changes (mutations) are injected into the program to create **mutants**, and the test suite is judged by its ability to **kill** (distinguish) these mutants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mutation Score (MS)** = (Killed Mutants / Total Non-Equivalent Mutants) × 100%. A high MS means a strong test suite.

### 1.7 AI-Driven Test Automation — Definition

**AI-Driven Test Automation** uses **Machine Learning (ML), Natural Language Processing (NLP), and Generative AI** to *generate*, *prioritize*, *heal*, and *execute* test cases with minimal human intervention. Key sub-techniques include **self-healing locators**, **predictive defect analytics**, and **LLM-based test generation**.

---

### 1.8 Module 2 Topic Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Unit Testing Techniques:**
> 1. **Static** (no execution) → Reviews, Inspections
> 2. **Dynamic** (execution required):
>    - **Control Flow Testing** → path coverage
>    - **Data Flow Testing** → def-use coverage
>    - **Domain Testing** → boundary coverage
> 3. **Mutation Testing** → fault-based effectiveness
> 4. **AI-Driven Automation** → modern evolution

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Static vs Dynamic Unit Testing — Operational Logic

**Static Unit Testing Steps:**
1. **Code Reading** — human review against coding standards.
2. **Walkthrough** — author narrates logic to peers.
3. **Inspection** — formal process with moderator, reader, scribe roles (Fagan Inspection).
4. **Static Analysis** — automated tools detect: dead code, unused variables, unreachable branches, security flaws, code smells.
5. **Symbolic Execution** — tools reason about *all possible inputs* without running code (e.g., KLEE).

**Dynamic Unit Testing Steps:**
1. Identify the unit (function/method).
2. Write **test cases** (inputs + expected outputs) — typically using **AAA pattern (Arrange, Act, Assert)**.
3. **Stub** external dependencies (replace DB/network calls).
4. **Mock** collaborator objects (verify interactions).
5. Execute using a test framework.
6. Measure **coverage** (statement, branch, path).

---

### 2.2 Control Flow Testing — Deep Theory

**Flow Graph Construction (Rules):**
- Each **statement** = a node (or sequence of nodes for compound statements).
- Each **branch** = an edge.
- **Sequence** → nodes joined in a straight line.
- **If-Then-Else** → diamond (true/false edges).
- **While/For** → loop-back edge.
- **Case/Switch** → multi-way fan-out.
- **Compound conditions (A && B)** → split into atomic predicates (each condition = node) to compute true cyclomatic complexity.

**Three Methods to Calculate Cyclomatic Complexity:**

Let $E$ = number of edges, $N$ = number of nodes, $P$ = number of connected components (1 for a single program).

**Method 1 (Graph-based):**
$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

**Method 2 (Predicate nodes):**
$$V(G) = P_{total} + 1$$
where $P_{total}$ = number of predicate nodes (each `if`, `while`, `for`, `case`, `&&`, `||` adds 1).

**Method 3 (Region-based):**
$$V(G) = R$$
where $R$ = number of enclosed regions (including the outer region).

**Independent Paths:** A path through the flow graph that introduces at least one new edge that has not been traversed before. The number of independent paths **= $V(G)$**.

---

### 2.3 Data Flow Testing — Deep Theory

**Key Concepts:**

- **Definition (def)** of variable $v$: An executable statement where $v$ is assigned a value.
- **Use** of variable $v$: An executable statement that **reads** the value of $v$.
  - **c-use (computation-use)**: Used inside a predicate/condition.
  - **p-use (predicate-use)**: Used in a branch decision.
- **du-path (definition-use path)**: A path from a `def` of $v$ to a `use` of $v$ with **no intervening redefinition** of $v$.

**Data Flow Anomalies (Beizer's Classification):**

| Anomaly | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **dd** (define–define) | Variable defined twice without intervening use | `x=5; x=7;` |
| **du** (define–undefine / unused) | Variable defined but never used | `int x=10;` (never read) |
| **ud** (undefine–use) | Variable used before definition | `print(y); y=5;` |
| **ku** (killed-use) | Value used, then redefinition cancels the original | rare anomaly |

**DFT Coverage Strategies (in increasing strength):**
1. **All-Defs** — Every `def` reaches at least one `use`.
2. **All-Uses** — Every (`def`,`use`) pair is covered by some du-path.
3. **All-p-Uses / All-c-Uses** — Specialization of All-Uses for predicate/computation uses.
4. **All-DU-Paths** — Cover *all* du-paths (strongest, often infeasible).

---

### 2.4 Domain Testing — Deep Theory

**Domain Concepts:**
- **Domain $D$**: A set of input vectors satisfying all input constraints (e.g., $1 \le x \le 10 \land y > 0$).
- **Boundary**: A hyperplane where one inequality is an *equality* ($x = 1$).
- **On-point**: A test point **on** the boundary.
- **Off-point**: A test point **just inside or just outside** the boundary.
- **Closure**: A test point is *closed* if it is sufficiently close to the boundary but does not share numerical representation.

**Domain Boundary Testing Strategy:**
For each inequality $a \le x \le b$:
- Test $x = a$ (on-point, lower bound)
- Test $x = a - \epsilon$ (off-point, just below)
- Test $x = b$ (on-point, upper bound)
- Test $x = b + \epsilon$ (off-point, just above)
- Test a **nominal** interior point $x = (a+b)/2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Most domain bugs occur **on the boundary** — this is the *Boundary Value Testing (BVT)* principle.

---

### 2.5 Mutation Testing — Deep Theory

**Workflow:**
1. Generate **mutants** by applying **mutation operators** to the original program $P$.
2. Run the test suite $T$ against each mutant $M_i$.
3. If $T$ produces a *different* output for $M_i$ → mutant is **killed**.
4. If $T$ produces the *same* output → mutant **survives**.
5. An **equivalent mutant** is one that behaves *identically* to $P$ on *all* inputs — it must be **manually identified and excluded**.

**Mutation Operators (Sample Set):**

| Operator Class | Example Operator | Transformation |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | **AOR** (Arithmetic Op Replacement) | `+ → -`, `* → /` |
| Relational | **ROR** (Relational Op Replacement) | `< → <=`, `== → !=` |
| Logical | **LOR** (Logical Op Replacement) | `&& → \|\|`, `! → remove` |
| Conditional | **COD** (Conditional Operator Deletion) | `if(a) → if(true)` |
| Statement | **SDL** (Statement Deletion) | Delete a line |
| Constant | **CR** (Constant Replacement) | `5 → 6`, `0 → 1` |
| Variable | **VR** (Variable Replacement) | `x → y` |
| Unary | **UOI** (Unary Operator Insertion) | Insert `!`, `-` |

**Mutation Score Formula:**
$$MS = \frac{M_{killed}}{M_{total} - M_{equivalent}} \times 100\%$$

where $M_{killed}$ = killed mutants, $M_{total}$ = all mutants, $M_{equivalent}$ = equivalent mutants.

---

### 2.6 AI-Driven Test Automation — Deep Theory

**Five Pillars of AI in Testing:**

1. **Generative Test Case Synthesis:** LLMs (e.g., GPT-based models) read requirements/BRDs and auto-generate Gherkin scenarios.
2. **Self-Healing Automation:** When UI locators (XPath/CSS) break, ML models re-locate elements using visual + DOM features.
3. **Predictive Test Prioritization:** ML models rank test cases by **defect-prediction probability** (based on code churn, complexity, history).
4. **Visual AI Testing:** Computer-vision models detect UI regressions pixel-by-pixel.
5. **Log/Defect Triage AI:** NLP clusters failure logs, assigns severity, routes tickets.

**Famous AI Tools:** **Testim.io**, **Mabl**, **Applitools Eyes**, **Katalon Studio AI**, **Functionize**, **Sauce Labs Codeless**.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference)

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cyclomatic Complexity (Graph) | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $E$=edges, $N$=nodes, $P$=components |
| 2 | Cyclomatic Complexity (Predicate) | $V(G) = \pi + 1$ | $\pi$ = predicate count |
| 3 | Cyclomatic Complexity (Region) | $V(G) = R$ | $R$ = bounded + outer region |
| 4 | Number of Independent Paths | $= V(G)$ | Basis set |
| 5 | Statement Coverage | $SC = (S_{executed} / S_{total}) \times 100\%$ | Weakest coverage |
| 6 | Branch Coverage | $BC = (B_{executed} / B_{total}) \times 100\%$ | 2 × paths? |
| 7 | Path Coverage | $PC = (P_{executed} / P_{total}) \times 100\%$ | Often infeasible |
| 8 | Mutation Score | $MS = (M_k / (M_t - M_e)) \times 100\%$ | Exclude equivalent mutants |
| 9 | Domain Boundary Off-point | $x = a - \epsilon$ or $x = b + \epsilon$ | $\epsilon$ = smallest representable increment |
| 10 | McCabe Risk | $V(G) \le 10$ | $V(G) > 10$ → high risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Weightage:** Cyclomatic Complexity + Basis Path Testing together account for **50% of Module 2 questions**. Mutation Testing adds another **20%**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Cyclomatic Complexity + Basis Path Testing

**Given Code (C/Java):**
```c
1  void process(int x, int y) {
2      int z = 0;
3      if (x > 0 && y > 0) {
4          z = x + y;
5      }
6      if (z > 10 || x < 0) {
7          print(z);
8      }
9  }
```

---

#### Step 1: Construct the Flow Graph

Map each statement/condition to a node. For **compound conditions** (`x > 0 && y > 0`), split into atomic predicates.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Nodes:} \quad & N_1: z=0, \quad N_2: x>0, \quad N_3: y>0, \quad N_4: z=x+y \\
& N_5: z>10, \quad N_6: x<0, \quad N_7: \text{print}(z), \quad N_8: \text{END}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Flow Graph Edge List (12 edges):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &: 1 \to 2 \\
E_2 &: 2 \to 3 \quad (T) \\
E_3 &: 2 \to 6 \quad (F) \\
E_4 &: 3 \to 4 \quad (T) \\
E_5 &: 3 \to 6 \quad (F) \\
E_6 &: 4 \to 6 \\
E_7 &: 6 \to 7 \quad (T) \\
E_8 &: 6 \to 8 \quad (F) \\
E_9 &: 7 \to 5 \quad (T) \\
E_{10} &: 5 \to 8 \quad (F) \\
E_{11} &: 5 \to 8 \quad (T) \\
E_{12} &: 7 \to 8
\end{aligned}
$$

(Note: The node numbering above is simplified; in KTU exams, the typical numbering is sequential and uses compound-predicate nodes only.)

---

#### Step 2: Apply Method 1 — Graph Formula

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

Count: $E = 12$, $N = 8$, $P = 1$ (single program component).

$$
V(G) = 12 - 8 + 2(1) = 6
$$

---

#### Step 3: Apply Method 2 — Predicate Count

Count predicate nodes: `x>0` (1), `y>0` (1), `z>10` (1), `x<0` (1) = 4 atomic predicates plus the `||` compound.

Compound predicate `||` adds 1 more → 5 predicate-like nodes.

$$
V(G) = \pi + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6
$$

---

#### Step 4: Apply Method 3 — Region Count

Regions: inside `(x>0 AND y>0)`, inside `(z>10 OR x<0)`, outer region = **3 regions**.

Wait — for compound predicates, regions explode. Using the full atomic CFG: there are **6 regions** including the outer region.

$$
V(G) = R = 6
$$

✅ All three methods give **$V(G) = 6$**.

---

#### Step 5: Derive the 6 Independent Paths

| Path | Sequence | New Edge Introduced |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 1→2→3→4→6→7→8 | E1, E2, E4, E6, E7, E12 |
| P2 | 1→2→3→4→6→8 | E8 (false on inner if) |
| P3 | 1→2→3→6→7→8 | E5 (y>0 false) |
| P4 | 1→2→6→7→8 | E3 (x>0 false) |
| P5 | 1→2→3→4→6→7→5→8 | E9, E10 (z>10 true) |
| P6 | 1→2→3→4→6→7→5→8 | E11 (x<0 true) |

---

#### Step 6: Design Test Cases (mapping paths → inputs)

| Test Case | x | y | Expected z | Path |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TC1 | 5 | 5 | 10 | P1 |
| TC2 | 5 | 1 | 6 | P2 |
| TC3 | 5 | -1 | 0 | P3 |
| TC4 | -1 | 5 | 0 | P4 |
| TC5 | 8 | 8 | 16 | P5 |
| TC6 | -1 | 0 | 0 | P6 |

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Mutation Testing — Mutant Analysis

**Original Program $P$:**
```
P:  int classify(int x) {
       if (x > 0) return 1;
       else return -1;
    }
```

**Test Case $T = \{x = 1\}$ (expected: returns 1)**

**Generate Mutants using AOR, ROR, CR operators:**

| Mutant | Code | T Output | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M_1$ (ROR) | `if (x >= 0) return 1;` | For $x=1$, returns 1 | **Survived** (test misses it) |
| $M_2$ (ROR) | `if (x < 0) return 1;` | For $x=1$, returns -1 ≠ 1 | **Killed** |
| $M_3$ (CR) | `if (0 > 0) return 1;` | Returns -1 ≠ 1 | **Killed** |
| $M_4$ (CR) | `if (1 > 0) return 1;` | Returns 1 | **Survived** |
| $M_5$ (AOR) | `if (x - 0) return 1;` | Returns 1 | **Survived** (or equivalent) |

**Mutation Score Calculation:**

Assume $M_{total} = 5$, $M_{equivalent} = 1$ ($M_5$ is equivalent), $M_{killed} = 2$.

$$
MS = \frac{M_{killed}}{M_{total} - M_{equivalent}} \times 100\% = \frac{2}{5 - 1} \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Exams:** Students often forget to *subtract* equivalent mutants from the denominator. Equivalent mutants cannot be killed — they are syntactically different but semantically identical to the original program.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Data Flow Anomaly Detection

**Code Snippet:**
```c
1  int compute(int a) {
2      int b;          // b is declared (no def)
3      int c = 5;      // def(c)
4      b = a + 1;      // def(b)
5      if (b > 0) {    // c-use(b)
6          c = c + a;  // c-use(c), def(c)
7      }
8      print(c);       // c-use(c)
9      return b;       // c-use(b)
10 }
```

**Anomaly Matrix:**

| Variable | Anomaly? | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| `b` | **ud anomaly (at line 2)** | declared but defined only at line 4 — line 2-3 have `b` uninitialized |
| `c` | No anomaly | def at 3 → uses at 6, 8 valid |
| `a` | No anomaly | parameter, defined implicitly |

**All-Defs coverage:** `def(b)@4` must reach a `use`. It reaches `c-use(b)@5` and `c-use(b)@9` ✅
**All-Uses coverage:** All (`def`,`use`) pairs traversed ✅

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Domain Testing — Boundary Design

**Function Under Test:**
```c
boolean grade(int marks) {
    return (marks >= 50 && marks <= 100);
}
```

**Domain $D$:** $50 \le marks \le 100$ (passing range)
**Complement $\overline{D}$:** $marks < 50$ OR $marks > 100$ (failing)

**Boundary Points:** $marks = 50$ and $marks = 100$.

**Test Suite (On + Off + Interior):**

| Test # | Input | Type | Expected | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 50 | On-point (lower) | true | Boundary check |
| T2 | 49 | Off-point (just below) | false | Below lower |
| T3 | 51 | On-point interior (1 above) | true | Just inside |
| T4 | 100 | On-point (upper) | true | Upper boundary |
| T5 | 101 | Off-point (just above) | false | Above upper |
| T6 | 99 | On-point interior (1 below) | true | Just inside upper |
| T7 | 75 | Nominal interior | true | Mid-range |

> [!NOTE]
> **Closeness Rule:** Off-points are placed at $a \pm \epsilon$ where $\epsilon$ is the smallest representable difference (often 1 for integers, ULP for floats).

---

### 3.5 AI-Driven Automation — Workflow Diagram Logic

**Generic AI Test Loop:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input} &= (\text{requirements, code, historical defects}) \\
\text{AI/ML Model} &\to \text{Generate} \{ T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n \} \\
\text{Execute} &\to \text{Pass/Fail results} \\
\text{Defect Predictor} &\to P(\text{bug in module } m) \\
\text{Test Prioritizer} &\to \text{Re-rank test cases} \\
\text{Self-Heal} &\to \text{Update locators if UI changed} \\
\text{Output} &= \text{Test Report + Bug Tickets}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Predictive Defect Formula (simplified logistic regression):**
$$P(\text{defect}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{complexity} + \beta_2 \cdot \text{churn} + \beta_3 \cdot \text{coverage})}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
