- $T_3 = [1, 2, 3, 5, 4, 3, 6, 5, 4]$ — executes $P_9 = [5,4,3,6,5]$? Let's check: sub-path of $T_3$ is $5 \to 4 \to 3 \to 6 \to 5$ — yes, that's $P_9$ ✓. Also $P_8 = [5,4,3,5]$ ✓ (sub-path $5 \to 4 \to 3 \to 5$).

So $T_1, T_2, T_3$ together cover **all 4 round trips**.

$$
\text{RTC} = \frac{4}{4} \times 100\% = 100\% \ \checkmark
$$

(7 marks for design + verification.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing sub-path with proper sub-path:** A path is a sub-path of itself; the proper sub-path relation (`$\subsetneq$`) is required for the maximality test. Losing 1-2 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting node 1 special rule:** A simple path starting at the entry node is automatically maximal at the front. Missing this causes incomplete lists.
> 3. **Double-counting round trips:** A round trip like $[3,5,4,3]$ may appear as a sub-path of a non-round-trip prime path $[3,5,4,3,6]$. Both still count as "executed" for RTC purposes.
> 4. **Coverage of 100% PPC does not imply 100% RTC** (and vice versa). Many students incorrectly state the reverse. RTC is a *subset* condition of PPC because every round trip is a prime path but not every prime path is a round trip.
> 5. **Misapplying McCabe's $V(G)$:** $V(G)$ counts **independent** paths, not all prime paths. Do not equate them.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Prime Path & Round Trip Coverage**

- ✅ **Prime Path:** A simple path that is **not a proper sub-path** of any other simple path. **Maximal** simple path.
- ✅ **Round Trip Path:** A prime path (or simple path) where **first node = last node**. It represents loop execution.
- ✅ **Prime Path Coverage (PPC):**
  $$\text{PPC} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Executed Prime Paths} \vert}{\vert \text{Total Prime Paths} \vert} \times 100\%$$
- ✅ **Round Trip Coverage (RTC):**
  $$\text{RTC} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Executed Round Trips} \vert}{\vert \text{Total Round Trips} \vert} \times 100\%$$
- ✅ **Node 1 Special Rule:** A simple path beginning at node 1 is automatically maximal at its starting end.
- ✅ **Maximality Test:** For every simple path, try to extend it at both ends. If any extension yields another simple path, the original is **not prime**.
- ✅ **Round Trip ⊂ Prime Path:** Every round trip is a prime path, but not every prime path is a round trip.
- ✅ **Coverage Hierarchy:** Node $<$ Edge $<$ Branch $<$ Prime Path $\leq$ Round Trip.
- ✅ **McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity:** $V(G) = E - N + 2$ gives the number of **independent** paths, which is a lower bound for prime paths.
- ✅ **Algorithm to find prime paths:**
  1. Enumerate all simple paths via DFS.
  2. Apply maximality filter.
  3. Apply node 1 special rule.
  4. Classify round trips.
- ✅ **Engineering Applications:** Compiler optimization, embedded firmware validation, protocol state-machine auditing, safety-critical domains (DO-178C, ISO 26262).
- ✅ **Common Exam Trap:** Writing `[1,2,3]` as prime when it is a sub-path of `[1,2,3,4]` — ALWAYS check maximality.
- ✅ **Verification Tip:** If your final prime path count equals $V(G)$, you have likely missed longer prime paths.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Prime Path and Round Trip Coverage**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Flow Criteria: DU Paths, DU Pairs, Subsumption Relationships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Data Flow Criteria: DU Paths, DU Pairs, Subsumption Relationships # 📘 MODULE 3 — Data Flow Testing Criteria ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Data Flow Testing? **Data Flow Testing** is a structural (white-box) testing technique that selects test paths through a program based on the locations of **definitions** and **uses** of program variables. Unlike control-flow testing (which only checks branches), data flow testing verifies that **values assigned to variables are properly carried to the points where they are consumed**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** > Data flow testing is the **bridge between control-flow and value-based testing**. It requires the student to understand both the program graph (CFG) AND the variable lifecycle. ### 1.2 Formal Terminology (KTU Board Definition Format) | Symbol | Formal Term | Meaning | |---|---|---| | `d` | **Definition** | A location where a variable's value is defined (assigned) | | `u` | **Use** | A location where a variable's value is referenced | | `c-use` | **Computation Use** | Use of a variable in a computation (predicate/expression) | | `p-use` | **Predicate Use** | Use of a variable in a decision/branch predicate | | `def(s)` | **Definition Set** | The set of all definitions in statement `s` | | `use(s)` | **Use Set** | The set of all uses in statement `s` | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Pipeline Model" 🚰 Imagine a software program as a **network of water pipes**: - A **Definition (`d`)** is like a **water tap (faucet)** that fills water into a pipe (e.g., `x = 5`). - A **Use (`u`)** is like a **shower head or tap outlet** where water is consumed (e.g., `if (x > 0)` or `print(x)`). - A **DU-Path** is the **continuous pipeline from a tap to a shower**, ensuring the water reaches without leaks. - A **DU-Pair** is the **specific tap-to-shower combination** we want to verify. > [!NOTE] > **Tester's Intuition:** If we never check whether a "value" properly flows from where it was defined to where it is used, bugs like **uninitialized variables**, **stale values**, and **wrong-branch logic** can hide in the program. ### 1.4 Definitions vs. Uses — Strict Examples ```text x = 10; // d(x) — definition of x y = x + 5; // u(x) — computation use of x; d(y) — definition of y if (x > 100) // u(x) — predicate use of x z = y; // u(y), d(z) ``` ### 1.5 Why Data Flow Testing? (KTU High-Yield Justification) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Mantra:** "A path that **executes** a definition and a use is not enough — we must verify the definition **reaches** the use, and the use is **exercised** with the right value." * Catches errors missed by branch/statement coverage. * Detects uninitialized variable usage. * Validates inter-procedural value propagation. * Forms the basis of advanced criteria like Web testing and Object-Oriented testing. # 🔬 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Pillars: `def(s)` and `use(s)` For any statement `s` in a program, the data flow criteria revolve around two formally defined sets: 1. **`def(s)`** — The set of variables whose **values are defined (assigned)** in statement `s`. 2. **`use(s)`** — The set of variables whose **values are used (referenced)** in statement `s`. A statement can simultaneously belong to both sets (e.g., `y = x + 1` defines `y` and uses `x`). ## 2.2 The Data Flow Testing Criteria Hierarchy (KTU's Most Tested Diagram) Data flow criteria form a **strict subsumption hierarchy**, meaning satisfying a stronger criterion automatically satisfies weaker ones. ### Level 1: All-Defs (AD) > Every **definition** of every variable must be exercised on **at least one path** to a use (any use, not necessarily all). ### Level 2: All-Uses (AU) > For every definition `d` of variable `v`, every **reachable use** `u` of `v` must be covered by at least one DU-path from `d` to `u`. ### Level 3: All-DU-Pairs (ADUP) > For every pair `(d, u)` where `d` is a definition and `u` is a use of the same variable, there must exist a **definition-clear path** from `d` to `u`. ### Level 4: All-DU-Paths (ADUPath) > For every pair `(d, u)`, **every** definition-clear path from `d` to `u` must be exercised by some test case. > [!NOTE] > **ADUPath ⊇ ADUP ⊇ AU ⊇ AD** (Strict containment — the hierarchy taught in KTU Module 3). ## 2.3 Definition-Clear Path — The Most Critical Concept A path is **definition-clear with respect to variable `v`** (denoted `dc(v)`) if along that path, the variable `v` is **not redefined** (i.e., no other definition of `v` appears between `d` and `u`). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters:** If `v` is redefined on the way to `u`, then the value from `d` is **clobbered** (destroyed), and `u` does not actually consume the value from `d`. Testing such a path is meaningless for the `(d, u)` pair. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Criterion | Set of Test Requirements | Strength | Key Equation (KTU Notation) | |---|---|---|---| | **All-Defs** | $\{(d, u) \mid d \in \text{Defs}(v), \exists u \in \text{Uses}(v)\}$ | Weakest data flow | $\vert R(AD) \vert = \sum_v \vert defs(v) \vert$ | | **All-Uses** | $\{(d, u) \mid d \in \text{Defs}(v), u \in \text{reach\_uses}(d, v)\}$ | Stronger | $\vert R(AU) \vert \geq \vert R(AD) \vert$ | | **All-DU-Pairs** | $\{(d, u) \mid \text{reachable definition-use pair}\}$ | Strong | $R(ADUP) = \{(d, u) \mid (d,u) \text{ is a DU pair}\}$ | | **All-DU-Paths** | $\{(d, u, P) \mid P \text{ is a } dc(v) \text{ path from } d \text{ to } u\}$ | Strongest | $R(ADUPath) = \{(d, u, P) \mid P \in \mathcal{P}_{dc}(d, u)\}$ | ### Symbols Used (KTU Board Standard) | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | $def(v)$ | Set of all definitions of variable $v$ | | $use(v)$ | Set of all uses of variable $v$ | | $P_{dc}(d, u)$ | Set of all definition-clear paths from $d$ to $u$ | | $R(C)$ | The **requirement set** for criterion $C$ (KTU calls it "Test Requirements") | | $T(C)$ | The **test set** that satisfies criterion $C$ | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Compiler Optimization Verification:** Tests that variables are correctly propagated across basic blocks. * **Embedded Systems:** Verifying sensor data (definitions) reaches controllers (uses) without corruption. * **Cyber-Physical Systems:** Data flow testing in self-driving cars ensures sensor values flow correctly to actuators. * **Safety-Critical Software (DO-178C, ISO 26262):** Required by certification bodies. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Real-World Link:** Most aerospace software (e.g., Boeing 777 flight control) is verified using data flow testing equivalent to **All-DU-Paths** on critical variables. # 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Formal Construction of a DU-Path ### Step 1: Build the Control Flow Graph (CFG) For a given program, construct a **directed graph** $G = (N, E, s, e)$ where: * $N$ = set of nodes (statements/basic blocks) * $E$ = set of edges (control transfers) * $s$ = start node * $e$ = end node ### Step 2: Annotate Nodes with `def(s)` and `use(s)` Each node $n \in N$ is tagged with two sets: $$ def(n) = \{v \mid v \text{ is assigned at node } n\} $$ $$ use(n) = \{v \mid v \text{ is referenced at node } n\} $$ ### Step 3: Identify All Definition-Use Pairs (DU Pairs) For every variable $v$ and every definition $d \in def(v)$ at node $n_d$, identify every use $u \in use(v)$ at node $n_u$ such that there exists a definition-clear path from $n_d$ to $n_u$. ### Step 4: Enumerate DU-Paths For each DU pair $(d, u)$, enumerate all paths $P$ that are **definition-clear** for $v$. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Three-Variable Program Consider the following program fragment (commonly used in KTU model papers): ```c // Node 1 x = 1; // d(x) at node 1 // Node 2 if (x > 0) // u(x) — predicate use at node 2 y = x; // d(y), u(x) at node 3 else y = -x; // d(y), u(x) at node 4 // Node 5 z = y + 2; // d(z), u(y) at node 5 ``` ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: List definitions and uses per node.** | Node | def(n) | use(n) | |---|---|---| | 1 | {x} | $\emptyset$ | | 2 | $\emptyset$ | {x} | | 3 | {y} | {x} | | 4 | {y} | {x} | | 5 | {z} | {y} | **Step 2: Identify DU pairs.** For variable `x`: definitions at {1}, uses at {2, 3, 4} - DU Pairs: (1,2), (1,3), (1,4) For variable `y`: definitions at {3, 4}, uses at {5} - DU Pairs: (3,5), (4,5) For variable `z`: definitions at {5}, no uses - DU Pairs: None (terminal definition) **Total DU Pairs: 5** $\Rightarrow$ This is the requirement count for **All-DU-Pairs**. **Step 3: Enumerate DU-paths for each pair.** | DU Pair | Definition-Clear Paths | Count | |---|---|---| | (1, 2) | [1, 2] | 1 | | (1, 3) | [1, 2, 3] | 1 | | (1, 4) | [1, 2, 4] | 1 | | (3, 5) | [1, 2, 3, 5] (note: y not redefined on this path) | 1 | | (4, 5) | [1, 2, 4, 5] | 1 | **Total DU-Paths: 5** $\Rightarrow$ This is the requirement count for **All-DU-Paths**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **In this example, the counts for All-DU-Pairs and All-DU-Paths are equal because no variable has multiple definition-clear paths from a single definition to a single use.** In programs with loops, All-DU-Paths would be much larger. ## 3.3 Subsumption Relationship — Mathematical Proof Sketch ### Theorem (KTU Module 3 — Frequently Asked) > **$T(\text{All-DU-Paths}) \Rightarrow T(\text{All-DU-Pairs})$** ### Proof: Let $T$ be a test set satisfying **All-DU-Paths**. For any DU pair $(d, u)$, by definition of All-DU-Paths, there exists a definition-clear path $P$ from $d$ to $u$ that is exercised by some test case in $T$. Since every DU pair $(d, u)$ is covered by at least one definition-clear path in $T$, all DU pairs are covered. Therefore, $T$ also satisfies **All-DU-Pairs**. $$ \boxed{\text{All-DU-Paths} \sqsupseteq \text{All-DU-Pairs} \sqsupseteq \text{All-Uses} \sqsupseteq \text{All-Defs}} $$ ### Counter-Example: All-Edges ⊉ All-Defs Consider: ```c int x; if (cond) x = 5; // definition of x in true branch print(x); // use of x — UNDEFINED if cond is false ``` A test set covering **All-Edges** may not execute the true branch where `x` is defined. Hence the use `print(x)` could be reached with `x` uninitialized. **All-Defs** would require executing the definition; thus **All-Edges does NOT subsume All-Defs**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever asked "Does A subsume B?" — provide a **counterexample** if it does NOT subsume. This is worth **2 marks** in 14-mark questions. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Coverage Hierarchy | Criterion | # Test Requirements (Sample) | Infeasibility Risk | KTU Exam Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | All-Defs | 3 | Low | High | | All-Uses | 5 | Medium | Very High | | All-DU-Pairs | 5 | Medium | High | | All-DU-Paths | 5 | High | Moderate | | All-Edges | 6 | Low | High | | All-Nodes | 5 | Very Low | Moderate | # 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Data Flow Testing Criteria — Subsumption Lattice The following Mermaid block illustrates the formal subsumption relationships among the major structural testing criteria as taught in KTU Module 3. ```mermaid graph TD A[All DU Paths
Strongest Criterion] --> B[All DU Pairs] B --> C[All Uses] C --> D[All Defs] D --> E[All Edges
Branch Coverage] E --> F[All Nodes
Statement Coverage] F --> G[Trivial Test
Weakest] H[All Edges] -.->|Does NOT subsume| C D -.->|Stronger than| E style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#000 style C fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#000 style D fill:#a9e34b,stroke:#000 style E fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#000 style F fill:#b197fc,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#ced4da,stroke:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** A solid arrow `A → B` means "A subsumes B" (A is stronger). The dashed line `H -.-> C` means "All-Edges does NOT subsume All-Uses." ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Data Flow Testing ```mermaid graph LR SRC[Source Code] --> CFG[Control Flow Graph
Builder] CFG --> ANNO[def and use
Annotator] ANNO --> DUPGEN[DU Pair
Generator] DUPGEN --> DCLS[Definition-Clear
Path Analyzer] DCLS --> CRIT[Criteria Selector
AD, AU, ADUP, ADUPath] CRIT --> TC[Test Case
Synthesizer] TC --> EXEC[Test Execution
Engine] EXEC --> COV[Coverage
Report] COV --> GTR[Graphical Test
Reporter] style SRC fill:#e3fafc,stroke:#000 style CFG fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#000 style ANNO fill:#ffe066,stroke:#000 style DUPGEN fill:#fcc419,stroke:#000 style DCLS fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#000 style CRIT fill:#ff8787,stroke:#000 style TC fill:#fa5252,stroke:#000,color:#fff style EXEC fill:#5c940d,stroke:#000,color:#fff style COV fill:#2b8a3e,stroke:#000,color:#fff style GTR fill:#1971c2,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Annotated CFG for the Worked Example ```mermaid graph TD node1["Node 1
def x: x=1"] --> node2 node2{"Node 2
use x: if x greater than 0"} -->|true| node3 node2 -->|false| node4 node3["Node 3
def y: y=x
use x"] --> node5 node4["Node 4
def y: y= -x
use x"] --> node5 node5["Node 5
def z: z=y+2
use y"] --> node6[Node 6
END] style node1 fill:#ffe8cc,stroke:#d9480f style node2 fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1864ab style node3 fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#fab005 style node4 fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#fab005 style node5 fill:#b2f2bb,stroke:#2b8a3e style node6 fill:#e9ecef,stroke:#495057 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Guide (For KTU Board Exams):** > * **Orange node** = definition-only node. > * **Blue diamond** = predicate-use node. > * **Yellow nodes** = nodes with both def and use. > * **Green node** = final computation-use node. # 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define a Definition-Use (DU) Pair with a suitable example. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **Model Answer:** A **DU Pair** is an ordered pair $(d, u)$ consisting of a **definition `d`** of a variable $v$ and a **use `u`** of the same variable $v$, such that there exists a path in the program's control flow graph from the node containing `d` to the node containing `u` on which $v$ is not redefined. **Example:** ```c x = 10; // d(x) at node 1 y = x + 1; // u(x) at node 2 ``` Here, $(1, 2)$ is a DU pair for variable `x`. --- ### Q2. Differentiate between All-DU-Pairs and All-DU-Paths coverage criteria. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | All-DU-Pairs | All-DU-Paths | |---|---|---| | **Requirement** | Every reachable DU pair $(d, u)$ must be covered by at least one definition-clear path. | Every reachable DU pair $(d, u)$ must be covered by **all** of its definition-clear paths. | | **Strength** | Weaker | Strongest data flow criterion | | **Number of Test Requirements** | $\vert R(ADUP) \vert$ | $\geq \vert R(ADUP) \vert$, often much larger | | **Use Case** | Industry standard for most projects | Safety-critical, aerospace, medical software | > [!NOTE] > **Key Line for 3-Mark Answer:** "All-DU-Paths requires covering every def-clear path between each pair, while All-DU-Pairs requires only one path per pair." --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) > **[KTU University Exam — July 2024 | 14 Marks]** > > **(a)** Explain the concept of **Definition-Clear Paths** with a suitable example. Derive the formal condition for a path $P$ to be definition-clear with respect to variable $v$. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** For the following program, construct the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**, identify all **DU pairs** for variables `x`, `y`, and `z`, and determine the **test requirements** for **All-DU-Paths** coverage. **(7 Marks)** > > ```c > 1: x = 0; > 2: y = 0; > 3: if (x > 10) > 4: y = x + 1; > 5: else > 6: y = x - 1; > 7: z = y * 2; > ``` #### Model Solution to (a) — Definition-Clear Paths (7 Marks) A path $P$ from node $n_i$ to node $n_j$ is **definition-clear with respect to variable $v$** if and only if: * $P$ begins with a definition of $v$ at $n_i$ (i.e., $v \in def(n_i)$). * $P$ ends with a use of $v$ at $n_j$ (i.e., $v \in use(n_j)$). * **No other node on path $P$ redefines $v$** — i.e., $\forall n_k \in P, k \neq i, k \neq j : v \notin def(n_k)$. Formally: $$ P = \langle n_i, n_{i+1}, \ldots, n_j \rangle \text{ is } dc(v) \iff v \in def(n_i) \land v \in use(n_j) \land \big(\forall k \in [i+1, j-1] : v \notin def(n_k)\big) $$ **Why this matters:** If a path is NOT definition-clear, then the value reaching $n_j$ does NOT originate from the definition at $n_i$. Exercising such a path is **useless** for testing the $(d, u)$ pair. **Example:** ```c x = 5; // d(x) at node 1 x = 10; // d(x) at node 2 — REDEFINES x y = x; // u(x) at node 3 ``` Path [1, 2, 3] is **not** definition-clear for `x` because node 2 redefines it. Path [2, 3] is definition-clear. #### Model Solution to (b) — CFG, DU Pairs, All-DU-Paths (7 Marks) **Step 1: Build the CFG.** ```mermaid graph TD n1["1: x=0
def x"] --> n2["2: y=0
def y"] n2 --> n3{"3: if x greater than 10
use x (p-use)"} n3 -->|true| n4["4: y = x+1
def y, use x (c-use)"] n3 -->|false| n6["6: y = x-1
def y, use x (c-use)"] n4 --> n7["7: z = y*2
def z, use y"] n6 --> n7 n7 --> n8["END"] ``` **Step 2: Identify `def` and `use` per node.** | Node | def(n) | use(n) | |---|---|---| | 1 | {x} | $\emptyset$ | | 2 | {y} | $\emptyset$ | | 3 | $\emptyset$ | {x} | | 4 | {y} | {x} | | 6 | {y} | {x} | | 7 | {z} | {y} | **Step 3: List DU pairs.** For variable `x` (defined at node 1, used at 3, 4, 6): - (1, 3) — x defined at 1, used as predicate at 3. - (1, 4) — x defined at 1, used at 4 (true branch). - (1, 6) — x defined at 1, used at 6 (false branch). For variable `y` (defined at nodes 2, 4, 6; used at node 7): - (2, 7), (4, 7), (6, 7). For variable `z` (defined at 7, no use): No DU pairs. **Step 4: Enumerate definition-clear DU-paths.** | DU Pair | Definition-Clear Paths | Path Count | |---|---|---| | (1, 3) | [1, 2, 3] | 1 | | (1, 4) | [1, 2, 3, 4] | 1 | | (1, 6) | [1, 2, 3, 6] | 1 | | (2, 7) | [1, 2, 3, 4, 7] and [1, 2, 3, 6, 7] | 2 | | (4, 7) | [1, 2, 3, 4, 7] | 1 | | (6, 7) | [1, 2, 3, 6, 7] | 1 | **Total DU-Paths: 7** $\Rightarrow$ This is the test requirement count for **All-DU-Paths**. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Expected) | 14 Marks]** > > **(a)** Draw the **subsumption hierarchy** of structural testing criteria and prove that **All-DU-Paths subsumes All-DU-Pairs** with a formal argument. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Consider a program segment with a loop. Show, with a counter-example, why **All-Edges does NOT subsume All-Defs** in data flow testing. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) — Subsumption Hierarchy & Proof (7 Marks) **Subsumption Hierarchy (KTU Diagram):** ```mermaid graph TD ADUPath["All DU Paths"] --> ADUP["All DU Pairs"] ADUP --> AU["All Uses"] AU --> AD["All Defs"] AD --> AE["All Edges"] AE --> AN["All Nodes"] style ADUPath fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff style ADUP fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#000 style AU fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#000 style AD fill:#a9e34b,stroke:#000 style AE fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#000 style AN fill:#b197fc,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` **Formal Proof — All-DU-Paths ⊇ All-DU-Pairs:** *Let* $T$ be a test set satisfying All-DU-Paths. *We must show:* $T$ also satisfies All-DU-Pairs. By the definition of All-DU-Paths: For every DU pair $(d, u)$ and for **every** definition-clear path $P$ from $d$ to $u$, there exists a test case $t \in T$ that exercises $P$. Since the set of definition-clear paths from $d$ to $u$ is non-empty for any valid DU pair, there exists **at least one path** $P_0$ from $d$ to $u$ exercised by some $t_0 \in T$. Therefore, every DU pair $(d, u)$ is covered by at least one test case in $T$. Hence, $T$ satisfies All-DU-Pairs. $$ \boxed{\forall T : [T \models \text{All-DU-Paths}] \Rightarrow [T \models \text{All-DU-Pairs}]} $$ #### Model Solution to (b) — Counter-Example (7 Marks) **Claim:** All-Edges does NOT subsume All-Defs. **Counter-Example Program:** ```c int x; // x is uninitialized int y = 0; if (y == 0) // edge: false branch x = 5; // definition of x — NOT exercised if y != 0 print(x); // use of x — may use uninitialized value ``` **Test Set Covering All-Edges:** * $T = \{(y = 1)\}$ — takes the **true branch** of `if (y == 0)`, skipping the definition `x = 5`. **This test set covers all edges of the CFG:** * Edge from "y=0" to "if (y==0)". * Edge from "if (y==0)" to "print(x)" (true branch). * Edge from "if (y==0)" to "x=5" (false branch) — **Wait, this edge IS covered only when y==0**. Let me refine the example to make the counter-example sharper: ```c int x; // declaration without initialization if (1 == 1) // always-true predicate — only one branch taken x = 5; print(x); // use of x ``` The CFG has edges for the true and false branches, but only the true branch is taken. A test set $\{x = \text{anything}\}$ covers all **reachable** edges, but the **definition `x = 5` is never exercised**. Hence All-Defs is violated. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Do NOT confuse "subsumes" with "implies".** A stronger criterion having more test requirements does not mean it is always achievable in practice — many paths are infeasible. > 2. **Always state the formal direction of subsumption:** "A subsumes B" means "A is stronger than B", not the reverse. Writing "B subsumes A" will cost **at least 1 mark** in valuation. > 3. **For counter-examples, ensure your test set actually satisfies the LHS criterion.** If your example test set doesn't satisfy All-Edges, your counter-example is invalid. > 4. **Definition-Clear Path is the heart of data flow testing.** Marks are awarded specifically for correctly identifying whether a path is `dc(v)`. Do not skip the check for redefinition of `v` along the path. > 5. **Distinguish p-use from c-use.** A common student error is to treat them as identical. p-use occurs **only in predicates**; c-use occurs in **computation statements**. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember * ✅ **Data Flow Testing** is structural testing based on variable definitions and uses — not just control flow. * ✅ A **Definition (`d`)** is where a variable is **assigned**; a **Use (`u`)** is where a variable is **read**. * ✅ **p-use** = use in a predicate; **c-use** = use in a computation. * ✅ **DU Pair** = an ordered pair $(d, u)$ of a definition and a use of the same variable. * ✅ **DU Path** = a path from $d$ to $u$ that is **definition-clear** for that variable (no redefinition in between). * ✅ **All-Defs (AD)** = every definition exercised on at least one path to a use. * ✅ **All-Uses (AU)** = every reachable use covered by at least one def-clear path. * ✅ **All-DU-Pairs (ADUP)** = every DU pair covered by at least one def-clear path. * ✅ **All-DU-Paths (ADUPath)** = every def-clear path between every DU pair must be exercised. * ✅ **Subsumption Hierarchy (Strict):** $\text{ADUPath} \sqsupset \text{ADUP} \sqsupset \text{AU} \sqsupset \text{AD}$ * ✅ **Control-flow criteria** (All-Edges, All-Nodes) do **NOT** subsume data-flow criteria. * ✅ **Infeasibility** is a major practical issue in data flow testing — many DU-paths are not executable. * ✅ **KTU Exam Trick:** Whenever you see "show that X does NOT subsume Y", always provide a **concrete counter-example program + test set**. * ✅ **Definition-Clear Path** is THE most frequently tested concept in 14-mark KTU questions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Line KTU Mnemonic for the Hierarchy:** *"**P**ath supersedes **P**air, **P**air supersedes **U**se, **U**se supersedes **D**ef."* — **PPUUD**.

topics.title: Graph Coverage for Code: Control Flow Graphs (CFGs) for Complex Structures (e.g., Loops, Exceptions)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Graph Coverage for Code: Control Flow Graphs (CFGs) for Complex Structures (e.g., Loops, Exceptions)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Control Flow Graph (CFG)?

A **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** is a directed graph representation of a program in which:
- **Nodes** represent basic blocks (sequences of statements with no internal branching).
- **Edges** represent possible control flow transfers (branches, jumps, sequential execution).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for OECST833, the CFG is the foundational model upon which structural (white-box) coverage criteria are built. It abstracts away the syntax of a programming language and exposes only the execution topology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Exact):** A Control Flow Graph is a graphical model $G = (N, E, n_0, n_f)$ where $N$ is the set of nodes (basic blocks), $E \subseteq N \times N$ is the set of directed edges (control transfers), $n_0$ is the unique entry node, and $n_f$ is the unique exit node.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Metro Map" of Code

Imagine your program as a **city metro map**:
- Each **station** = a basic block (a stretch of track with no exit/entry in the middle).
- Each **track segment** = a directed edge (a one-way route).
- The **metro driver (CPU)** enters at the "Start" station, follows the tracks, and exits at "End".

A **simple if-else** is a Y-junction (two tracks diverge). A **loop** is a circular loop line (you can ride the same track multiple times). An **exception** is an **emergency tunnel** that bypasses normal stations and dumps you directly at a recovery exit. To test ALL tracks, you must design journeys (test cases) that ride every segment — this is **graph coverage**.

## 1.3 Why CFGs Matter for Complex Structures

Sequential code is trivial. The KTU Module 3 emphasis is on **complex structures**:
- **Loops** → multi-iteration paths, zero-iteration paths, single-iteration paths.
- **Exceptions** → try/catch/finally, throw, multiple catch blocks, exception propagation.
- **Nested combinations** → loops inside try blocks, exceptions thrown from inside loops.

These structures create **non-linear topologies** that demand specialized coverage criteria beyond simple node/edge coverage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly requires students to **draw CFGs for loops and exception-handling constructs**, and to **apply graph-based coverage criteria (Node, Edge, Branch, Condition, Path)** on them. This is a **frequently repeated 7-mark and 14-mark question pattern**.

## 1.4 Standard Graph Coverage Criteria (KTU Taxonomy)

| Criterion | What it Covers | KTU Cognitive Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Node Coverage (NC)** | Every basic block executed at least once | L3 (Apply) |
| **Edge Coverage (EC)** | Every control transfer (branch) traversed at least once | L3 (Apply) |
| **Branch Coverage (BC)** | Both true and false outcomes of every decision | L4 (Analyze) |
| **Condition Coverage (CC)** | Every Boolean sub-expression evaluates to true and false | L4 (Analyze) |
| **Path Coverage (PC)** | Every entry-to-exit path executed at least once | L5 (Evaluate) |
| **Cyclomatic Complexity (CC)** | Independent paths count | L4 (Analyze) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Basic Block

A **basic block** is a maximal sequence of statements where:
- Control enters only at the first statement.
- Control leaves only at the last statement.
- No internal branches or branch targets.

The CFG is built by **partitioning the program into basic blocks** and then connecting them with directed edges based on control transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A new basic block begins at the target of a branch, the fall-through after a branch, the entry of a method, or after a label. A basic block ends at any branch, return, throw, or call to a non-trivial function (in inter-procedural CFGs).

## 2.2 CFG Construction Algorithm (The 5-Step KTU Method)

For any given code snippet, follow this **board-exam approved procedure**:

1. **Identify Leaders** — statements that begin a new basic block (first statement, branch targets, statements following branches).
2. **Group** — every statement from a leader up to (but not including) the next leader forms a basic block.
3. **Create Nodes** — one node per basic block.
4. **Create Edges** — for each possible control transfer from the last statement of block $B_i$ to the first statement of block $B_j$, add a directed edge $(B_i, B_j)$.
5. **Mark Entry/Exit** — designate the block containing `main()` entry as $n_0$ and any block with `return`/`throw` reaching program exit as $n_f$.

## 2.3 CFG Topologies for Complex Structures

### A. While Loop CFG

A `while(condition)` loop has **two exit paths**: one when the condition is true (back-edge to loop body) and one when false (fall-through to post-loop code).

### B. For Loop CFG

A `for(init; cond; update)` loop is structurally identical to a `while` loop, with `init` executed once before the entry and `update` executed at the back-edge.

### C. Do-While Loop CFG

A `do-while` loop has the **condition check at the exit** of the loop body, guaranteeing at least one iteration. This produces a CFG where the loop body is executed **before** the decision.

### D. Try-Catch-Finally CFG

A `try` block has a **normal exit** and one **exception exit per catch block**. A `finally` block sits on **every exit path** (normal or exceptional) before control leaves the structure.

### E. Nested Exception in Loop

A `throw` inside a `for` loop body propagates to the **nearest enclosing catch**, and `finally` blocks execute on **every iteration** where an exception is thrown.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Metrics

| Metric / Formula | Symbol | Equation | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe) | $V(G)$ | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Number of independent paths; minimum test cases |
| Cyclomatic Complexity (Predicate) | $V(G)$ | $V(G) = P + 1$ | Where $P$ = number of predicate nodes |
| Node Coverage % | $NC\%$ | $\frac{\text{Executed Nodes}}{\text{Total Nodes}} \times 100$ | Verifying NC criterion |
| Edge Coverage % | $EC\%$ | $\frac{\text{Traversed Edges}}{\text{Total Edges}} \times 100$ | Verifying EC/BC criterion |
| Path Coverage (Independent Paths) | $\pi$ | $\pi = V(G)$ | Minimum path set |
| Regions in CFG | $R$ | $R = E - N + 2$ | Equivalent to $V(G)$ for single function |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** For **do-while** loops, $V(G) = 3$ (entry, body, exit) — the same as a while loop, but the **minimum path set** differs because the body is traversed before the predicate test.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CFG-based coverage is the **backbone of safety-critical software certification**:
- **DO-178C (Avionics)** mandates Modified Condition/Decision Coverage (MC/DC) on CFGs.
- **ISO 26262 (Automotive)** requires structural coverage at ASIL levels.
- **Medical devices (FDA)** require CFG-based path coverage for Class III software.
- **Static analyzers** (SonarQube, Coverity, LLVM) build CFGs to detect dead code, unreachable branches, and cyclomatic hotspots.

For KTU graduates entering **TCS, Infosys, Accenture testing divisions** or **embedded automotive/medical firms**, fluency in CFG construction is a **day-one interview skill**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: CFG for a While Loop with Nested If

Consider this KTU-style code snippet:

```java
int sum = 0;
int i = 1;
while (i <= 10) {
    if (i % 2 == 0) {
        sum = sum + i;
    }
    i++;
}
return sum;
```

### Step 1 — Identify Leaders

- Statement 1: `int sum = 0;` (entry) → **Leader L1**
- Statement 3: `while (i <= 10)` → **Leader L2**
- Statement 4: `if (i % 2 == 0)` → **Leader L3**
- Statement 6: `i++` → **Leader L4**
- Statement 8: `return sum` → **Leader L5**

### Step 2 — Form Basic Blocks

- **B1**: {`int sum = 0;`, `int i = 1;`}
- **B2**: {`while (i <= 10)`} — predicate node
- **B3**: {`if (i % 2 == 0)`} — predicate node
- **B4**: {`sum = sum + i;`}
- **B5**: {`i++;`}
- **B6**: {`return sum;`}

### Step 3 — Construct Edges

- $(B1 \to B2)$: sequential entry into loop
- $(B2 \to B3)$: condition true → enter body
- $(B2 \to B6)$: condition false → exit loop
- $(B3 \to B4)$: `i` is even → execute sum update
- $(B3 \to B5)$: `i` is odd → skip sum update
- $(B4 \to B5)$: after updating, proceed to increment
- $(B5 \to B2)$: back-edge → re-evaluate loop condition

### Step 4 — Apply McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity

Using the **predicate formula** $V(G) = P + 1$ where $P$ = number of predicate nodes:

There are **2 predicate nodes** ($B2$ for the while, $B3$ for the if).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= P + 1 \\
&= 2 + 1 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

This means there are **3 independent paths** (minimum test cases for path coverage of the loop/branch structure).

### Step 5 — Enumerate Independent Paths

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &: B1 \to B2 \to B6 \quad \text{(0 iterations: condition false immediately)} \\
\pi_2 &: B1 \to B2 \to B3 \to B4 \to B5 \to B2 \to B6 \quad \text{(one even iteration, then exit)} \\
\pi_3 &: B1 \to B2 \to B3 \to B5 \to B2 \to B6 \quad \text{(one odd iteration, then exit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marker Tip:** Examiners award full marks only if you **explicitly number the paths** and **state which edge/branch each one covers**. Generic "there are many paths" answers lose 2-3 marks.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: CFG for a Do-While Loop

Code:
```java
int x = 0;
do {
    x = x + 5;
} while (x < 100);
```

### Basic Blocks

- **B1**: {`int x = 0;`}
- **B2**: {`x = x + 5;`}
- **B3**: {`while (x < 100)`} — predicate

### Edges

- $(B1 \to B2)$: enter body
- $(B2 \to B3)$: evaluate condition after body
- $(B3 \to B2)$: condition true → re-execute body (back-edge)
- $(B3 \to \text{Exit})$: condition false → leave loop

### Cyclomatic Complexity

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
&= 4 - 3 + 2(1) \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Independent Paths

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &: B1 \to B2 \to B3 \to \text{Exit} \quad \text{(1 iteration: } x = 5 \text{, then } 5 < 100 \text{ false? No, still true)} \\
\pi_2 &: B1 \to B2 \to B3 \to B2 \to B3 \to \text{Exit} \quad \text{(2 iterations, then exit)} \\
\pi_3 &: \text{(symbolic: many iterations until } x = 100 \text{, then exit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Distinction from While Loop:** In a **do-while**, the body is **always** executed at least once. A test case that never enters the body is **infeasible** and should not be counted in the independent path set. KTU examiners test this distinction explicitly.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: CFG for Try-Catch-Finally

Code:
```java
try {
    int result = divide(a, b);
    System.out.println(result);
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {
    System.out.println("Error");
} finally {
    System.out.println("Cleanup");
}
```

### Basic Blocks

- **B1**: {`try { ... }` entry — `int result = divide(a, b);`}
- **B2**: {`System.out.println(result);`} — normal continuation
- **B3**: {`catch (ArithmeticException e) { ... }`}
- **B4**: {`System.out.println("Error");`}
- **B5**: {`finally { System.out.println("Cleanup"); }`}
- **B6**: {`Exit`}

### Edges

- $(B1 \to B2)$: normal flow (no exception)
- $(B1 \to B3)$: exception thrown → jump to catch
- $(B2 \to B5)$: normal flow reaches finally
- $(B3 \to B4)$: execute catch handler
- $(B4 \to B5)$: catch completes, proceed to finally
- $(B5 \to B6)$: finally completes → exit

### Cyclomatic Complexity

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= P + 1 \\
&= 2 + 1 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 predicates: implicit "is exception thrown?" at B1, plus the catch dispatcher)

### Independent Paths

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &: B1 \to B2 \to B5 \to B6 \quad \text{(no exception path)} \\
\pi_2 &: B1 \to B3 \to B4 \to B5 \to B6 \quad \text{(exception caught path)} \\
\pi_3 &: B1 \to B2 \to B5 \to B6 \quad \text{(no exception, finally executes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that **B5 (finally)** appears on **every exit path**. This makes the finally block a **structural sink** — any test case that doesn't traverse it is **incomplete**. The CFG correctly models this by placing B5 on both the normal and exceptional exits.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Nested Exception Inside a For Loop

Code:
```java
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    try {
        process(arr[i]);
    } catch (IOException e) {
        log(e);
        if (i == 0) throw new FatalException();
    }
}
```

### CFG Topology (Conceptual)

- The for loop provides a **back-edge** from update to predicate.
- Inside the loop, the try-catch creates a **local Y-fork**: normal exit → continue loop, exception → catch block.
- The catch block has a **conditional re-throw** when `i == 0`, which propagates out of the entire structure (terminating the function).

### Independent Path Count

With 3 predicate nodes (for condition, catch entry, `i == 0` re-throw), we get:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= 3 + 1 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Four independent paths covering: (1) loop zero times, (2) loop with no exception, (3) loop with caught exception, (4) loop with re-thrown fatal exception.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often draw the re-throw edge going directly to the function exit, bypassing the finally. **In Java/C#**, a `finally` block in the outer scope would still execute. Always trace the exception propagation path **carefully** through nested structures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid CFG for While Loop with Nested If

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1["B1: int sum=0; int i=1;"] --> B2["B2: while i <= 10"]
    B2 -->|true| B3["B3: if i%2 == 0"]
    B2 -->|false| B6["B6: return sum"]
    B3 -->|true| B4["B4: sum = sum + i"]
    B3 -->|false| B5["B5: i++"]
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B2
    B6 --> B7["B7: EXIT"]
    style B1 fill:#90EE90
    style B7 fill:#FFB6C1
    style B2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style B3 fill:#FFE4B5
```

## 4.2 Mermaid CFG for Do-While Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["A1: int x = 0"] --> A2["A2: x = x + 5"]
    A2 --> A3["A3: while x less than 100"]
    A3 -->|true| A2
    A3 -->|false| A4["A4: EXIT"]
    style A1 fill:#90EE90
    style A4 fill:#FFB6C1
    style A3 fill:#FFE4B5
```

## 4.3 Mermaid CFG for Try-Catch-Finally

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1["T1: try entry divide a b"] --> T2["T2: print result"]
    T1 -->|exception| T3["T3: catch ArithmeticException"]
    T2 --> T5["T5: finally print Cleanup"]
    T3 --> T4["T4: print Error"]
    T4 --> T5
    T5 --> T6["T6: EXIT"]
    style T1 fill:#E0FFFF
    style T3 fill:#E0FFFF
    style T5 fill:#FFD700
    style T6 fill:#FFB6C1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture of Coverage Analysis

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC["Source Code"] --> LEX["Lexer / Parser"]
    LEX --> BB["Basic Block Partitioner"]
    BB --> CFG["CFG Builder"]
    CFG --> METRICS["Metrics Engine V G E N R"]
    CFG --> COV["Coverage Criteria NC EC BC CC PC"]
    METRICS --> REPORT["Coverage Report"]
    COV --> REPORT
    REPORT --> DECISION["Test Adequacy Decision"]
    style SRC fill:#B0E0E6
    style CFG fill:#FFD700
    style REPORT fill:#98FB98
    style DECISION fill:#FFB6C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagram:** This is the **functional pipeline** used by industrial tools (JaCoCo, Bullseye, VectorCAST). The source code is parsed, partitioned into basic blocks, assembled into a CFG, and then both metrics ($V(G)$, edge counts) and coverage criteria are evaluated against the CFG to produce a final adequacy report.

## 4.5 Comparison Table — CFG Topologies for Each Structure

| Structure | Nodes | Edges | Predicates | $V(G)$ | Back-Edge? | Exception Edge? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple While | 3 | 3 | 1 | 2 | Yes | No |
| Simple For | 3 | 3 | 1 | 2 | Yes | No |
| Do-While | 3 | 3 | 1 | 2 | Yes | No |
| While + If | 5 | 6 | 2 | 3 | Yes | No |
| Try-Catch | 4 | 4 | 1 | 2 | No | Yes |
| Try-Catch-Finally | 5 | 5 | 1 | 2 | No | Yes |
| Nested Try in Loop | 6+ | 7+ | 3 | 4 | Yes | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define a Control Flow Graph (CFG). What are nodes and edges in a CFG?**

**Model Answer:**
A Control Flow Graph (CFG) is a directed graph $G = (N, E, n_0, n_f)$ used to represent the control flow structure of a program.
- **Nodes ($N$)**: Represent basic blocks — maximal sequences of statements with no internal branching.
- **Edges ($E$)**: Represent directed control transfers between basic blocks (sequential flow, branch outcomes, jumps, returns, throws).
- $n_0$ is the unique entry node; $n_f$ is the unique exit node.

The CFG abstracts program syntax to expose execution topology for white-box testing. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: List any THREE graph-based coverage criteria used in white-box testing.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Node Coverage**: Every basic block is executed at least once.
2. **Edge Coverage**: Every directed edge (control transfer) is traversed at least once.
3. **Branch Coverage**: Every decision evaluates to both true and false.
4. (Optional) **Path Coverage**: Every entry-to-exit path is executed at least once. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Draw the Control Flow Graph (CFG) for the following code segment. Compute the cyclomatic complexity using all three McCabe methods, and identify the independent paths.**

```java
int n = 10;
int fact = 1;
int i = 1;
while (i <= n) {
    fact = fact * i;
    if (fact > 1000) {
        break;
    }
    i++;
}
System.out.println(fact);
```

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) CFG Construction [7 marks]

**Basic Blocks Identified:**
- **B1**: {`int n = 10; int fact = 1; int i = 1;`}
- **B2**: {`while (i <= n)`} — predicate
- **B3**: {`fact = fact * i;`}
- **B4**: {`if (fact > 1000)`} — predicate
- **B5**: {`break;`}
- **B6**: {`i++;`}
- **B7**: {`System.out.println(fact);`}
- **B8**: Exit

**Edges:**
- $(B1 \to B2)$: entry into loop
- $(B2 \to B3)$: condition true → body
- $(B2 \to B7)$: condition false → exit loop
- $(B3 \to B4)$: sequential
- $(B4 \to B5)$: condition true → break
- $(B4 \to B6)$: condition false → continue
- $(B5 \to B7)$: break jumps out
- $(B6 \to B2)$: back-edge for next iteration
- $(B7 \to B8)$: exit

**Mermaid Visualization:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    BB1["B1: init n, fact, i"] --> BB2["B2: while i less than equal n"]
    BB2 -->|true| BB3["B3: fact = fact * i"]
    BB2 -->|false| BB7["B7: print fact"]
    BB3 --> BB4["B4: if fact greater than 1000"]
    BB4 -->|true| BB5["B5: break"]
    BB4 -->|false| BB6["B6: i++"]
    BB5 --> BB7
    BB6 --> BB2
    BB7 --> BB8["B8: EXIT"]
    style BB1 fill:#90EE90
    style BB8 fill:#FFB6C1
    style BB2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style BB4 fill:#FFE4B5
```

#### (b) Cyclomatic Complexity & Independent Paths [7 marks]

**Method 1 — Edge-Node Formula:**
$E = 9$, $N = 8$, $P = 1$ (single function)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
&= 9 - 8 + 2(1) \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Method 2 — Predicate Formula:**
Predicate nodes: $B2$ (while), $B4$ (if) → $P = 2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= P + 1 \\
&= 2 + 1 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Method 3 — Region Formula:**
Regions: 1 (outer) + 1 (while loop interior) + 1 (if-then interior) = **3**

**Independent Paths:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &: B1 \to B2 \to B7 \to B8 \quad \text{(zero iterations: i=1, but } 1 \le 10 \text{ true; so this is } i > n \text{ case)} \\
\pi_2 &: B1 \to B2 \to B3 \to B4 \to BB6 \to B2 \to B7 \to B8 \quad \text{(one iteration, fact not greater than 1000)} \\
\pi_3 &: B1 \to B2 \to B3 \to B4 \to B5 \to B7 \to B8 \quad \text{(break taken: fact exceeded 1000)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Construct a CFG for the following exception-handling code. Calculate cyclomatic complexity and determine the minimum number of test cases required for path coverage. List the independent test paths.**

```java
void process(int x) {
    try {
        if (x > 0) {
            System.out.println("Positive");
        } else {
            System.out.println("Non-positive");
        }
    } catch (NullPointerException e) {
        System.out.println("Null error");
    } finally {
        System.out.println("Done");
    }
}
```

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) CFG Construction [7 marks]

**Basic Blocks:**
- **B1**: {`try {` entry, predicate `x > 0`}
- **B2**: {`System.out.println("Positive");`}
- **B3**: {`System.out.println("Non-positive");`}
- **B4**: {`catch (NullPointerException e)`}
- **B5**: {`System.out.println("Null error");`}
- **B6**: {`finally { System.out.println("Done"); }`}
- **B7**: Exit

**Edges:**
- $(B1 \to B2)$: $x > 0$ true
- $(B1 \to B3)$: $x > 0$ false (or $x = 0$)
- $(B2 \to B6)$: positive branch → finally
- $(B3 \to B6)$: non-positive branch → finally
- $(B1 \to B4)$: NullPointerException thrown → catch
- $(B4 \to B5)$: enter catch body
- $(B5 \to B6)$: catch done → finally
- $(B6 \to B7)$: finally done → exit

**Mermaid Visualization:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1["C1: try entry if x greater than 0"] -->|true| C2["C2: print Positive"]
    C1 -->|false| C3["C3: print Non-positive"]
    C1 -->|exception| C4["C4: catch NullPointerException"]
    C2 --> C6["C6: finally print Done"]
    C3 --> C6
    C4 --> C5["C5: print Null error"]
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7["C7: EXIT"]
    style C1 fill:#E0FFFF
    style C4 fill:#E0FFFF
    style C6 fill:#FFD700
    style C7 fill:#FFB6C1
```

#### (b) Cyclomatic Complexity & Test Cases [7 marks]

**Counting Edges, Nodes, Predicates:**
- $N = 7$, $E = 8$, $P = 1$ (single function, single method)
- Predicate nodes: $C1$ only (the `if`); the catch dispatch is structural, not a Boolean predicate.

**Cyclomatic Complexity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
&= 8 - 7 + 2 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= P_{\text{decision}} + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \quad \text{(decision only)} \\
V(G) &= P_{\text{all}} + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \quad \text{(treating exception dispatch as predicate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum Test Cases for Path Coverage = 3.**

**Independent Test Paths:**

| Path | Sequence | Test Input | Covers |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\pi_1$ | $C1 \to C2 \to C6 \to C7$ | $x = 5$ | Positive branch + finally |
| $\pi_2$ | $C1 \to C3 \to C6 \to C7$ | $x = -3$ | Non-positive branch + finally |
| $\pi_3$ | $C1 \to C4 \to C5 \to C6 \to C7$ | $x = null$ | Exception path + finally |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always include the `finally` block on every path.** A test that does not exercise the finally traversal is **structurally incomplete**. Many students omit finally edges and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Treat exception dispatch as a separate "pseudo-predicate"** when computing $V(G)$ via the predicate method. The single `if` gives $P=1$, but the exception jump adds another decision point, so the **comprehensive $V(G) = 3$**, not 2.
> 3. **Do not confuse path coverage with branch coverage.** Path coverage requires traversing every entry-to-exit path; branch coverage only requires both outcomes of every decision. KTU questions often ask for the **minimum number of test cases** — that is $V(G)$, the number of independent paths.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: CFGs for Complex Structures**

- ✅ **CFG Definition**: $G = (N, E, n_0, n_f)$ — nodes are basic blocks, edges are control transfers.
- ✅ **Basic Block Rule**: One entry, one exit, no internal branches. Identify **leaders** to partition code.
- ✅ **While Loop**: Predicate at entry; has a back-edge from update to predicate; can execute **zero times**.
- ✅ **For Loop**: Structurally identical to while; `init` is in entry block, `update` in back-edge path.
- ✅ **Do-While Loop**: Predicate at exit; body executes **at least once**; back-edge from predicate to body entry.
- ✅ **Try-Catch**: Each catch block is reached via a **separate exception edge** from the try block.
- ✅ **Try-Catch-Finally**: Finally block sits on **every exit path**; it is a structural sink, not a branch target.
- ✅ **Nested Exception in Loop**: Exception propagates to **nearest enclosing catch**; outer finally still executes (in Java/C#).
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity**: $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ or $V(G) = P + 1$ or $V(G) = \text{Regions}$.
- ✅ **Minimum Test Cases** = $V(G)$ = number of **independent paths**.
- ✅ **Independent Path**: A path that introduces at least one new edge not on any previously enumerated path.
- ✅ **Infeasible Paths**: May exist for loops (e.g., do-while with zero iterations); document but don't count.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Pattern**: 7-mark question asks for **CFG + $V(G)$**; 14-mark question asks for **CFG + $V(G)$ + independent paths + test cases**.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Memory Aid:** *"Stations are blocks, tracks are edges, the driver is the test case, and $V(G)$ is the number of one-way journeys needed to ride every track."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graph Coverage for Design Elements: Call Graphs, Class Inheritance Testing, and Coupling Data-Flow Pairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Graph Coverage for Design Elements: Call Graphs, Class Inheritance Testing, and Coupling Data-Flow Pairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Graph Coverage for Design Elements

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Graph Coverage for Design Elements* is a **structural testing technique** where software design artifacts (calls between methods, class inheritance hierarchies, and inter-module data interactions) are modeled as **directed graphs (DGs)**, and test cases are designed to systematically traverse these graphs to satisfy specific coverage criteria such as **Node Coverage, Edge Coverage, Path Coverage, and Data-Flow Coverage**.

In simple words, instead of testing a program line-by-line (as in statement coverage), we test the **design structure** itself — the *blueprint* of the software. We treat methods, classes, and their interactions as nodes and edges of a graph and ensure our tests "walk through" the design properly.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Office Building Blueprint

Imagine you are a **fire safety inspector** of a large office building:

- **Call Graph** = The map of *who calls whom* (e.g., the Receptionist calls the Manager, the Manager calls the CEO). You must test that every person can be reached and every call path works.
- **Class Inheritance Testing** = A **family tree**. The "Child" class inherits properties from the "Parent." If the Parent's properties break, the Child breaks too. You must test both *independently* and *as a family*.
- **Coupling Data-Flow Pairs** = The **courier service** between two departments. The HR department *sends* a salary record (data) to the Accounts department, which *receives* it. The test verifies the parcel reaches the right destination with the right content.

---

## 1.2 Call Graphs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> A **Call Graph** is a **directed graph** $CG = (N, E)$ where:
> - $N$ = set of **nodes**, each node representing a *method*, *function*, or *program unit*.
> - $E$ = set of **directed edges** $(n_i, n_j)$, indicating that method $n_i$ *invokes* (calls) method $n_j$.

### Intuition

Think of a call graph as a **phone call network diagram**. Each person (method) is a dot, and each arrow (edge) means "calls." If `main()` calls `login()`, and `login()` calls `validate()`, the call graph shows the execution flow at the **design level** — not the code level.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Node** | A method / function / program unit | $n_i$ |
| **Edge** | A call from $n_i$ to $n_j$ | $(n_i, n_j)$ |
| **Caller** | The method that initiates a call | Source of edge |
| **Callee** | The method being called | Target of edge |
| **Recursive Call** | A method calling itself (directly or transitively) | Cycle in graph |

> [!NOTE]
> **Coverage Criteria Applied to Call Graphs (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Node Coverage (NC):** Every method in the design must be called by at least one test.
> 2. **Edge Coverage (EC):** Every caller–callee relationship must be exercised.
> 3. **Path Coverage (PC):** Every possible call sequence must be traversed.
> 4. **Call Pair Coverage:** Every *pair* of methods where one calls the other must be tested.

---

## 1.3 Class Inheritance Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> *Class Inheritance Testing* is the process of testing **class hierarchies** (parent–child relationships in object-oriented design) to ensure that **inherited attributes, methods, and polymorphic behaviors** function correctly across the entire hierarchy.

### Intuition: The Family Inheritance

Consider a **family inheritance**:
- `Parent` class has property `surname = "Kumar"`.
- `Child` class extends `Parent` and adds property `nickname = "Ravi"`.

When we create a `Child` object, does it properly carry the `surname`? Does overriding `getName()` in `Child` work polymorphically when called via a `Parent` reference? Class inheritance testing answers these questions.

### Two Key Sub-Problems (KTU Frequently Asked)

1. **Inheritance Coverage (IC):** Each *inherited* method in each subclass must be exercised at least once.
2. **Polymorphic Method Coverage (PMC):** Each polymorphic method (overridden) must be called with a binding to every class in the hierarchy.

---

## 1.4 Coupling Data-Flow Pairs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> *Coupling Data-Flow Pairs (CDFP)* is an **integration-level testing technique** that examines the **data flow across module boundaries**. A *coupling pair* is formed by a **definition of a variable in one module** and its **use (c-use or p-use) in another module**, joined across the module interface.

### Intuition: The Postal Service

Imagine Module A writes a letter (defines variable X) and hands it to the postal service (the module interface). Module B receives the letter (uses variable X). **Coupling Data-Flow Testing** verifies:
- The letter was actually *sent* (definition reached the interface).
- The letter was *received* (use actually consumed it).
- The content was *correct* (correct value passed).

### Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Coupling Variable** | A variable whose definition is in one module and use in another |
| **Definition (def)** | A point where a variable is assigned/written |
| **C-Use (Computation Use)** | A point where a variable is read for computation |
| **P-Use (Predicate Use)** | A point where a variable is read in a decision/branch |
| **Coupling Pair** | $(def(m_i, x), use(m_j, x))$ where $m_i \neq m_j$ |

---

## 1.5 Summary Table — The Three Design-Element Graph Techniques

| Technique | Graph Model | Tests | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Call Graph Coverage** | Method calls as edges | Calling relationships | High |
| **Class Inheritance Testing** | Class hierarchy (DAG) | Inherited & polymorphic methods | High |
| **Coupling Data-Flow Pairs** | Data flow across modules | Cross-module data interactions | High |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Call Graph Coverage — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1: Build the Call Graph

For a given design, identify every method $m$ in the system. For every method invocation `m_i` calls `m_j`, draw a directed edge from $m_i$ to $m_j$.

### Step 2: Apply Coverage Criteria

> [!NOTE]
> **Why call graphs?**
> A program may have 100% statement coverage at the code level, yet some *unreachable methods* may exist. Call graph coverage catches such design-level issues.

#### Coverage Criteria Hierarchy (from weakest to strongest)

$$\text{Node Coverage} \subset \text{Edge Coverage} \subset \text{Path Coverage}$$

The subset symbol $\subset$ means "is strictly weaker than" — i.e., satisfying a stronger criterion automatically satisfies weaker ones.

### Step 3: Recursive Calls

> [!WARNING]
> Recursive calls create **cycles** in the call graph. A cycle of length $k$ has $k$ possible traversal paths (excluding the empty path). Always count cycles carefully during path coverage.

For a cycle with $k$ edges, the number of distinct simple paths is $k$ (one per "rotation").

### Step 4: Unreachable Methods (Dead Methods)

A method is **unreachable** if no test path from `main()` (or any entry point) can reach it. Detecting unreachable methods is one of the **primary goals** of call graph coverage.

---

## 2.2 Class Inheritance Testing — Detailed Theory

### Step 1: Build the Class Hierarchy Graph

A class hierarchy is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** where:
- Nodes = classes
- Edges = "inherits from" / "extends" relationship

### Step 2: Apply the Following Coverage Criteria

| Criterion | Definition | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Inherited Method Coverage (IMC)** | Every method inherited by a subclass must be invoked via that subclass | $\text{IMC} = \frac{\text{invoked inherited methods}}{\text{total inherited methods}}$ |
| **Inherited Attribute Coverage (IAC)** | Every inherited attribute must be read/written via the subclass | $\text{IAC} = \frac{\text{exercised inherited attributes}}{\text{total inherited attributes}}$ |
| **Polymorphic Method Coverage (PMC)** | Every polymorphic binding to every class in the hierarchy must be invoked | $\text{PMC} = \frac{\text{polymorphic bindings tested}}{\text{polymorphic bindings required}}$ |
| **Super-class Reference Coverage (SRC)** | A subclass instance must be referenced through a super-class reference | Required when polymorphism is used |

### Step 3: The Three Testing Strategies (KTU Mandatory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Inheritance-Centric Testing (Bottom-Up):**
> Test leaf classes (most derived) first, then move up the hierarchy. Forces testing of inherited members naturally.

> **2. Polymorphic-Centric Testing (Top-Down):**
> Test super-class methods first, then test polymorphic overriding in subclasses. Ensures base behavior is correct before testing variations.

> **3. Hierarchy-Centric Testing (Combined):**
> Test both inherited and newly-defined methods in every class in the hierarchy. Most thorough, but most expensive.

### Step 4: Bound/Unbound Polymorphism

- **Unbound (Static) Polymorphism:** Method binding at compile time (overloading). Easier to test.
- **Bound (Dynamic) Polymorphism:** Method binding at runtime (overriding). Requires **dynamic binding testing** — every possible binding must be triggered.

---

## 2.3 Coupling Data-Flow Pairs — Detailed Theory

### Step 1: Identify Coupling Variables

A *coupling variable* is a variable $x$ such that:
- $x$ is **defined** in module $m_i$ (at statement $d$)
- $x$ is **used** in module $m_j$ (at statement $u$)
- $m_i \neq m_j$

### Step 2: Apply Coupling Coverage Criteria

| Criterion | Definition |
|---|---|
| **All-Coupling-Defs (ACD)** | Every definition of a coupling variable in one module that is used in another module must be covered. |
| **All-Coupling-Uses (ACU)** | Every use of a coupling variable in another module must be exercised. |
| **All-Coupling-Defs-Uses (ACDU)** | Every (def, use) pair for coupling variables must be covered. |
| **All-Coupling-Pairs (ACP)** | Every pair of modules $m_i, m_j$ that share a coupling variable must be exercised. |

### Step 3: Inter- vs Intra-Module Data Flow

- **Intra-Module Data Flow:** Definition and use *within the same module* → tested by traditional data-flow testing.
- **Inter-Module Data Flow (Coupling):** Definition in one module, use in another → tested by **Coupling Data-Flow Testing** (the focus of this topic).

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU pitfall: students confuse *intra-module* data flow with *inter-module coupling*. Inter-module is the KTU-relevant concept here.

### Step 4: Reasons for Inter-Module Coupling

| Coupling Type | Description | Testability |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Coupling** | Modules share data parameters only | Easiest to test |
| **Stamp Coupling** | Modules share composite data structures | Moderate |
| **Control Coupling** | One module controls another's logic via flags | Tricky |
| **External Coupling** | Modules share external data format | Hard |
| **Common Coupling** | Modules share global data | Hardest |
| **Content Coupling** | One module directly modifies another's internals | Worst — untestable |

The *looser* the coupling, the *easier* the testing. KTU loves this trade-off question.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU board exam on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Node Coverage | $\text{NC} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Nodes Visited} \vert}{\vert \text{Total Nodes} \vert} \times 100\%$ | $\vert N_{\text{vis}} \vert / \vert N \vert$ |
| 2 | Edge Coverage | $\text{EC} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Edges Traversed} \vert}{\vert \text{Total Edges} \vert} \times 100\%$ | $\vert E_{\text{tra}} \vert / \vert E \vert$ |
| 3 | Path Coverage | $\text{PC} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Paths Executed} \vert}{\vert \text{Total Paths} \vert} \times 100\%$ | $\vert P_{\text{exe}} \vert / \vert P \vert$ |
| 4 | Inherited Method Coverage | $\text{IMC} = \dfrac{M_{\text{invoked}}^{\text{inh}}}{M_{\text{total}}^{\text{inh}}} \times 100\%$ | Inherited methods only |
| 5 | Polymorphic Method Coverage | $\text{PMC} = \dfrac{B_{\text{tested}}}{B_{\text{required}}} \times 100\%$ | $B$ = polymorphic bindings |
| 6 | Coupling Coverage (ACDU) | $\text{Cov} = \dfrac{\vert \text{def-use pairs covered} \vert}{\vert \text{Total coupling def-use pairs} \vert} \times 100\%$ | Cross-module pairs |
| 7 | Cyclomatic Complexity (graph) | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | $P$ = connected components |
| 8 | Number of test paths (lower bound) | $\text{Paths} \geq E - N + P$ | Independent paths |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Technique | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| Call Graphs | Static analysis tools (e.g., *SonarQube*, *Infer*) detect unreachable code in CI/CD pipelines |
| Inheritance Testing | Used in testing **Java/.NET frameworks** where deep inheritance hierarchies are common |
| Coupling Data-Flow | Used in **microservices integration testing** where services exchange data via APIs |

---

## 2.5 Worked Mini-Example: Call Graph Construction (Conceptual)

Consider the following design:

> `main()` calls `init()` and `process()`.
> `init()` calls `loadConfig()`.
> `process()` calls `validate()` and `save()`.
> `validate()` calls `loadConfig()`.

**Resulting Call Graph (Nodes & Edges):**

$$
N = \{\texttt{main}, \texttt{init}, \texttt{process}, \texttt{loadConfig}, \texttt{validate}, \texttt{save}\}
$$

$$
E = \{(\texttt{main},\texttt{init}),(\texttt{main},\texttt{process}),(\texttt{init},\texttt{loadConfig}),(\texttt{process},\texttt{validate}),(\texttt{process},\texttt{save}),(\texttt{validate},\texttt{loadConfig})\}
$$

So $\vert N \vert = 6$, $\vert E \vert = 6$, and $P = 1$ (single connected component).

Applying cyclomatic complexity: $V(G) = E - N + 2P = 6 - 6 + 2 = 2$.

Hence the lower bound of **independent test paths = 2**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Section Goal:** Walk through full derivations for coverage calculations and graph metrics. Every step is shown — no shortcuts.

---

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Coupling Data-Flow Pair Coverage

Suppose we have two modules $M_A$ and $M_B$ as shown in the table below.

### Step 1: Identify the Source Code of Both Modules

| Module $M_A$ | Module $M_B$ |
|---|---|
| `1. read x` | `5. read y` |
| `2. x = 10` *(def of x)* | `6. y = x + 5` *(use of x — c-use)* |
| `3. call M_B` | `7. if (y > 10)` *(use of y — p-use)* |
| `4. return` | `8. print y` *(use of y — c-use)* |
| | `9. return` |

**Key Observation:** Variable `x` is **defined in $M_A$ (line 2)** and **used in $M_B$ (line 6)**. So `x` is a **coupling variable**.

### Step 2: Enumerate the Coupling Def-Use Pairs

For variable `x`:

$$
\text{Coupling Pair}_{\text{x}} = \big(\text{def}(M_A, x, \text{line }2),\; \text{c-use}(M_B, x, \text{line }6)\big)
$$

The set of all coupling pairs for `x` is:

$$
\text{CDP}_x = \{\, (2_MA, 6_MB) \,\}
$$

### Step 3: Coverage Calculation

If our test suite triggers this pair at least once, then:

$$
\text{Coupling Coverage} = \frac{\vert \text{CDP}_x \text{ covered} \vert}{\vert \text{CDP}_x \text{ total} \vert} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{1} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

### Step 4: Extended — Multiple Coupling Pairs

If the design had two coupling variables `x` and `z` with the following pairs:

$$
\text{CDP}_x = \{(2, 6)\}, \quad \text{CDP}_z = \{(4, 11), (4, 12)\}
$$

Then the total coupling pairs is $\vert \text{CDP}_{\text{total}} \vert = 1 + 2 = 3$. If a test covers all 3:

$$
\text{Coupling Coverage} = \frac{3}{3} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

If only $(2,6)$ and $(4,11)$ are covered:

$$
\text{Coupling Coverage} = \frac{2}{3} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Call Graph Coverage Percentage

### Setup

Consider a call graph with the following metrics:

- $\vert N \vert = 10$ (total methods)
- $\vert E \vert = 14$ (total call edges)
- A test path traversed: 8 nodes and 11 edges

### Step 1: Compute Node Coverage

$$
\text{NC} = \frac{\vert N_{\text{visited}} \vert}{\vert N \vert} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{10} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

### Step 2: Compute Edge Coverage

$$
\text{EC} = \frac{\vert E_{\text{traversed}} \vert}{\vert E \vert} \times 100\% = \frac{11}{14} \times 100\% = 78.57\%
$$

### Step 3: Cyclomatic Complexity (Independent Paths Lower Bound)

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P
$$

Assuming $P = 1$ (single connected component):

$$
V(G) = 14 - 10 + 2(1) = 6
$$

Therefore, the **minimum number of independent test paths** required is **6**.

### Step 4: Path Coverage Calculation

If the design has $k$ total simple paths in the call graph, and a test executes $j$ of them:

$$
\text{PC} = \frac{j}{k} \times 100\%
$$

For example, if $k = 12$ simple paths exist and $j = 6$ are executed:

$$
\text{PC} = \frac{6}{12} \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Inherited Method Coverage

### Setup

Consider class hierarchy:

- `Class Animal` → methods: `eat()`, `sleep()` (2 inherited by all)
- `Class Dog extends Animal` → adds `bark()`, overrides `sleep()`
- `Class Puppy extends Dog` → adds `weep()`, overrides `bark()`

### Step 1: Count Inherited Methods per Class

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{inh}}^{\text{Animal}} &= 0 \text{ (root class, no inheritance)} \\
M_{\text{inh}}^{\text{Dog}} &= 2 \text{ (eat, sleep from Animal)} \\
M_{\text{inh}}^{\text{Puppy}} &= 4 \text{ (eat, sleep from Animal + bark from Dog, but bark is overridden)} \\
M_{\text{inh}}^{\text{Puppy (strict)}} &= 3 \text{ (eat, sleep, bark) considering override}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Total Inherited Methods Across Hierarchy

$$
M_{\text{total}}^{\text{inh}} = M_{\text{inh}}^{\text{Dog}} + M_{\text{inh}}^{\text{Puppy}} = 2 + 3 = 5
$$

(We exclude `Animal` as it has no inheritance.)

### Step 3: Compute Coverage

If a test invokes:
- `Dog.eat()`, `Dog.sleep()` (both inherited from Animal) — 2 inherited methods
- `Puppy.eat()`, `Puppy.sleep()`, `Puppy.bark()` (all inherited) — 3 inherited methods

$$
M_{\text{invoked}}^{\text{inh}} = 5
$$

$$
\text{IMC} = \frac{5}{5} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

If the test misses `Puppy.bark()`:

$$
M_{\text{invoked}}^{\text{inh}} = 4
$$

$$
\text{IMC} = \frac{4}{5} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation — Polymorphic Method Coverage

### Setup

Consider `sleep()` is polymorphic across `Animal`, `Dog`, `Puppy` (3 different implementations via override).

### Step 1: Count Possible Polymorphic Bindings

$$
B_{\text{required}} = \vert \{\text{Animal}, \text{Dog}, \text{Puppy}\} \vert = 3
$$

### Step 2: Compute Coverage

If the test invokes:
- `animalRef.sleep()` → binds to `Animal.sleep()`
- `dogRef.sleep()` → binds to `Dog.sleep()`
- `puppyRef.sleep()` → binds to `Puppy.sleep()`

All 3 bindings are tested:

$$
\text{PMC} = \frac{3}{3} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

If only `Animal.sleep()` and `Dog.sleep()` are tested:

$$
\text{PMC} = \frac{2}{3} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Technique

| Scenario | Recommended Technique | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Unit testing a single function | CFG-based coverage (control flow) | Fine-grained testing needed |
| Testing module interactions | **Coupling Data-Flow Pairs** | Inter-module data is the focus |
| Testing OOP hierarchies | **Class Inheritance Testing** | Inheritance and polymorphism matter |
| Static analysis of call chains | **Call Graph Coverage** | Reachability of methods |
| Microservices / APIs | **Coupling Data-Flow Pairs** | Data flows over network boundaries |
| Framework with deep inheritance | **Class Inheritance Testing** | Base classes affect all subclasses |

---

## 3.6 Coverage Criterion Equivalences (Proving Subsets)

### Claim: Node Coverage ⊆ Edge Coverage (i.e., EC is stronger than NC)

**Proof (logic):**
- Let a test path $P$ start at node $n_s$ and end at node $n_e$ with intermediate nodes $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k$.
- Every internal node $n_i$ (where $1 \le i \le k$) is reached by traversing an *incoming* edge.
- If the test exercises all $N$ nodes, it must traverse the edges that connect them.
- However, an edge $(a, b)$ can exist between two *already-visited* nodes, and simply visiting the nodes may not traverse the edge.

**Conclusion:** Edge coverage is *strictly stronger* than node coverage.

$$
\text{Node Coverage} \subsetneq \text{Edge Coverage} \subsetneq \text{Path Coverage}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **All diagrams are rendered using Mermaid syntax** with alphanumeric node identifiers and clean labels (no markdown inside node labels).

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — A Complete Call Graph (Multiple Methods)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeMain["MAIN"]
    nodeInit["INIT"]
    nodeProcess["PROCESS"]
    nodeLoad["LOADCONFIG"]
    nodeValidate["VALIDATE"]
    nodeSave["SAVE"]
    nodeLog["LOGERROR"]
    nodeCleanup["CLEANUP"]

    nodeMain --> nodeInit
    nodeMain --> nodeProcess
    nodeMain --> nodeCleanup
    nodeInit --> nodeLoad
    nodeProcess --> nodeValidate
    nodeProcess --> nodeSave
    nodeProcess --> nodeLog
    nodeValidate --> nodeLoad
    nodeValidate --> nodeLog
    nodeSave --> nodeLog
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Entry point: `nodeMain` (MAIN)
- `nodeMain` has 3 outgoing edges (to INIT, PROCESS, CLEANUP)
- `nodeLoad` (LOADCONFIG) is called from 2 different callers — tests must cover **both** call sites for full edge coverage.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Recursive Call Graph (Cycle Detection)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeFact["FACTORIAL"]
    nodeBase["BASECASE"]
    nodeRecurse["RECURSIVECALL"]

    nodeFact --> nodeBase
    nodeFact --> nodeRecurse
    nodeRecurse --> nodeFact
```

**Interpretation:**
- This forms a **cycle**: `FACTORIAL → RECURSIVECALL → FACTORIAL`.
- The number of simple paths in a cycle of length $k$ is $k$. Here $k = 2$, so we have 2 simple paths.
- Cycle detection is critical for **path coverage** — KTU expects students to count cycle-based paths.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Class Inheritance Hierarchy (DAG)

```mermaid
graph TD
    clsShape["SHAPE abstract"]
    clsCircle["CIRCLE"]
    clsSquare["SQUARE"]
    clsColored["COLOREDSHAPE"]
    clsRedCircle["REDCIRCLE"]
    clsBlueSquare["BLUESQUARE"]

    clsShape --> clsCircle
    clsShape --> clsSquare
    clsShape --> clsColored
    clsColored --> clsRedCircle
    clsColored --> clsBlueSquare
    clsCircle -. extends .-> clsRedCircle
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Root class: `clsShape` (SHAPE) — abstract, cannot be instantiated
- Direct children: CIRCLE, SQUARE, COLOREDSHAPE
- Note: A class (e.g., REDCIRCLE) can extend **multiple ancestors** in the path

**Inheritance testing implication:** Every class in this DAG must be tested for:
1. Inherited methods from all ancestors
2. Methods it overrides
3. Polymorphic bindings to its references

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Coupling Data-Flow Pairs Across Two Modules

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph modA["MODULE A"]
        aDefX["def x = 10"]
        aCallM["call MODB"]
    end

    subgraph modB["MODULE B"]
        bUseX["c-use x"]
        bComp["y = x + 5"]
        bRet["return y"]
    end

    aDefX --> aCallM
    aCallM --> bUseX
    bUseX --> bComp
    bComp --> bRet

    linkA["COUPLING VARIABLE: x"] -.-> aDefX
    linkA -.-> bUseX
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **coupling variable** `x` is defined in MODULE A and used in MODULE B.
- The dashed arrow shows the cross-module def-use relationship.
- A test must trigger the path: `def x in A` → `call MODB` → `c-use x in B` → coverage achieved.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Complete Design-Element Test Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Start: Software Design"]
    buildCG["Build Call Graph from Design"]
    buildH["Build Class Hierarchy DAG"]
    buildDF["Identify Coupling Variables"]
    applyCG["Apply Call Graph Coverage"]
    applyH["Apply Inheritance Testing"]
    applyDF["Apply Coupling Data-Flow Coverage"]
    integ["Integrate Coverage Reports"]
    done["Done: Design-Level Test Suite"]

    startA --> buildCG
    startA --> buildH
    startA --> buildDF
    buildCG --> applyCG
    buildH --> applyH
    buildDF --> applyDF
    applyCG --> integ
    applyH --> integ
    applyDF --> integ
    integ --> done
```

**Interpretation:** All three techniques (Call Graph, Inheritance, Coupling Data-Flow) operate in parallel on the design and merge into a unified test report at the end.

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Unreachable Method Detection

```mermaid
graph TD
    mainNode["MAIN"]
    alphaNode["ALPHA"]
    betaNode["BETA"]
    ghostNode["GHOST METHOD"]

    mainNode --> alphaNode
    alphaNode --> betaNode
    ghostNode:::ghost
    classDef ghost fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

**Reading:**
- `ghostNode` (GHOST METHOD) has no incoming edges — it is **unreachable** from MAIN.
- Call graph coverage flags it as dead code.
- KTU often asks: *"Identify the unreachable method in the call graph."*
