# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (As per KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks
> - Part B: 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice)
> - Module 3 carries ~20–25% weightage in the End Semester Exam.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Define a Call Graph. How is it used in design-level testing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Call Graph** is a directed graph $CG = (N, E)$ where each node $n_i \in N$ represents a method or function in the software design, and each directed edge $(n_i, n_j) \in E$ represents a *call* from method $n_i$ to method $n_j$.

In design-level testing, the call graph is used to:
1. **Identify unreachable methods** (nodes with no incoming reachable path from entry points).
2. **Apply coverage criteria** like node coverage, edge coverage, and call-pair coverage to ensure every caller-callee relationship is exercised.
3. **Detect recursive cycles** and ensure proper path coverage across them.

(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: uses, 1 mark: any coverage criterion mentioned)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **What is a Coupling Data-Flow Pair? Give an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Coupling Data-Flow Pair** is a pair of program points $(d, u)$ where $d$ is a *definition* of a variable $x$ in module $m_i$ and $u$ is a *use* of the same variable $x$ in a *different* module $m_j$ (i.e., $m_i \neq m_j$). Such a variable $x$ is called a **coupling variable**.

**Example:** If Module A defines `x = 10` and passes it to Module B, which then computes `y = x + 5`, then the pair $(def_A(x), use_B(x))$ is a coupling data-flow pair. Testing requires at least one test path that exercises this cross-module def-use relationship.

(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: terminology, 1 mark: example)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **skip showing graph construction steps** — full marks require explicitly drawing the graph and labeling nodes/edges.
> - For inheritance questions, students forget to **distinguish inherited vs. newly-defined methods**. Always clarify.
> - For coupling data-flow, students confuse **intra-module** with **inter-module** data flow. The question explicitly says *coupling* — ensure the def and use are in **different modules**.
> - Calculations must include the **formula first, then substitution, then result**. Marks are awarded per step.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

> Consider the following software design:
>
> **(a)** Construct the **call graph** for the design described below. Apply **node and edge coverage** criteria and determine the minimum number of test cases required. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Identify the **inherited methods** for each non-root class in the hierarchy below and compute the **Inherited Method Coverage (IMC)** if a test suite invokes 6 out of 8 inherited methods. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Design for (a):

| Caller | Calls |
|---|---|
| `main()` | `start()` |
| `main()` | `end()` |
| `start()` | `read()` |
| `start()` | `parse()` |
| `parse()` | `validate()` |
| `validate()` | `read()` |
| `end()` | `write()` |

#### Hierarchy for (b):

- `Class A` — methods: `m1()`, `m2()`
- `Class B extends A` — adds `m3()`, overrides `m2()`
- `Class C extends B` — adds `m4()`, overrides `m1()`

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify nodes and edges (2 marks)**

Nodes:
$$
N = \{\texttt{main}, \texttt{start}, \texttt{end}, \texttt{read}, \texttt{parse}, \texttt{validate}, \texttt{write}\}
$$

So $\vert N \vert = 7$.

Edges:
$$
E = \{(\texttt{main},\texttt{start}),(\texttt{main},\texttt{end}),(\texttt{start},\texttt{read}),(\texttt{start},\texttt{parse}),(\texttt{parse},\texttt{validate}),(\texttt{validate},\texttt{read}),(\texttt{end},\texttt{write})\}
$$

So $\vert E \vert = 7$.

**Step 2: Call graph diagram (1 mark)**

```mermaid
graph TD
    nMain["MAIN"]
    nStart["START"]
    nEnd["END"]
    nRead["READ"]
    nParse["PARSE"]
    nValid["VALIDATE"]
    nWrite["WRITE"]

    nMain --> nStart
    nMain --> nEnd
    nStart --> nRead
    nStart --> nParse
    nParse --> nValid
    nValid --> nRead
    nEnd --> nWrite
```

**Step 3: Node coverage (1 mark)**

For 100% node coverage, every node must be visited. So we need test cases that visit all 7 nodes.

**Step 4: Edge coverage (1 mark)**

For 100% edge coverage, all 7 edges must be traversed. We need at least one test that visits all 7 edges.

**Step 5: Compute cyclomatic complexity (1 mark)**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P = 7 - 7 + 2(1) = 2
$$

So the **minimum number of independent test paths = 2**.

**Step 6: Verify (1 mark)**

Two independent test paths could be:
- Path 1: `main → start → read` (covers 3 nodes, 2 edges)
- Path 2: `main → start → parse → validate → read` (covers 5 nodes, 4 edges) + `main → end → write` (covers 3 nodes, 2 edges)

Combined, all 7 nodes and 7 edges are covered.

**Final Answer for (a):** Minimum 2 test cases are required for full edge coverage. With 1 test case, you cannot cover all edges (since `end → write` is on a disjoint branch from `start → read`).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Enumerate methods of each class (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Class A: } & \{m_1, m_2\} \\
\text{Class B: } & \{m_1^{\text{(inherited)}}, m_2^{\text{(overridden)}}, m_3^{\text{(new)}}\} \\
\text{Class C: } & \{m_1^{\text{(overridden)}}, m_2^{\text{(inherited)}}, m_3^{\text{(inherited)}}, m_4^{\text{(new)}}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Identify inherited methods per class (1 mark)**

- Class B inherits from A: $\{m_1\}$ (1 method — $m_2$ is overridden, not "inherited" in the strict sense; we count $m_1$ as inherited and treat $m_2$ as overridden).
- Class C inherits from B: $\{m_2, m_3\}$ (2 methods — $m_1$ is overridden in C).

**Step 3: Total inherited methods (1 mark)**

$$
M_{\text{total}}^{\text{inh}} = M_{\text{inh}}^B + M_{\text{inh}}^C = 1 + 2 = 3
$$

Wait — let me re-examine. The question states "8 inherited methods" in the problem setup.

> **Correction (re-interpreting with all inherited members counted):**
> - Class B inherits $m_1$ from A and provides its own $m_2$ override, plus $m_3$. Strict inherited = 1.
> - Class C inherits $m_2$ (from B's override, which itself overrides A's $m_2$), $m_3$ from B. Plus C's own $m_1$ override, plus $m_4$. Strict inherited = 2.

For the problem to make sense with 8 inherited methods, we count *all* methods that are accessible to a subclass instance (including overridden ones from ancestors).

- Class B: $m_1$ (inherited), $m_2$ (inherited, then overridden in B), $m_3$ (new) → 2 inherited, 1 new.
- Class C: $m_1$ (inherited from A, overridden in C), $m_2$ (inherited from A, overridden in B), $m_3$ (inherited from B), $m_4$ (new) → 3 inherited, 1 new.

Total inherited across non-root classes: $2 + 3 = 5$ (not 8 as in the question, suggesting the question intends a larger hierarchy). For the sake of the calculation:

Given the problem states total inherited = 8, and 6 are invoked:

**Step 4: Compute IMC (1 mark)**

$$
\text{IMC} = \frac{M_{\text{invoked}}^{\text{inh}}}{M_{\text{total}}^{\text{inh}}} \times 100\% = \frac{6}{8} \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

**Step 5: Comment (2 marks)**

The IMC is **75%**, which is **inadequate** for full quality assurance. The 2 missed inherited methods may contain latent bugs that surface only when those methods are called. To improve coverage:
1. Add test cases that explicitly invoke the missed inherited methods via subclass instances.
2. Use **polymorphic reference testing** — bind a super-class reference to the subclass instance and call the missed method.
3. Use **hierarchy-centric testing** strategy, which exercises both inherited and newly-defined members in every class.

> **Final IMC = 75%** — Improvement recommended by adding 2 more test cases.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

> **(a)** Consider two modules $M_1$ and $M_2$. $M_1$ defines variables $x$ and $y$. $M_2$ uses $x$ in a computation and uses $y$ in a predicate. List all **coupling data-flow pairs**. Design a minimum set of test cases to achieve **100% coupling coverage (ACDU)**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the three main strategies for **class inheritance testing** with a suitable example. Compare their relative strengths. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Identify definitions and uses (2 marks)**

In $M_1$:
- $\text{def}(x)$ — at some statement $d_1$
- $\text{def}(y)$ — at some statement $d_2$

In $M_2$:
- $\text{c-use}(x)$ — at statement $u_1$ (computation)
- $\text{p-use}(y)$ — at statement $u_2$ (predicate/decision)

**Step 2: Enumerate coupling pairs (2 marks)**

Coupling pairs (def in $M_1$, use in $M_2$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CDP}_1 &= \big(\text{def}_{M_1}(x),\, \text{c-use}_{M_2}(x)\big) \\
\text{CDP}_2 &= \big(\text{def}_{M_1}(y),\, \text{p-use}_{M_2}(y)\big) \\
\text{CDP}_3 &= \big(\text{def}_{M_1}(y),\, \text{c-use}_{M_2}(y)\big) \quad \text{(if a c-use of y also exists in M_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Design test cases (2 marks)**

| Test Case | $x$ value | $y$ value | Covers Pair(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| TC1 | 5 | 10 (so $y > 5$ is true) | $\text{CDP}_1, \text{CDP}_2$ |
| TC2 | 7 | 3 (so $y > 5$ is false) | $\text{CDP}_1, \text{CDP}_2$ |

After TC1 and TC2: $\text{CDP}_1$ and $\text{CDP}_2$ are both covered twice (once for true branch, once for false branch). The p-use of $y$ is fully covered.

**Step 4: Verify 100% ACDU (1 mark)**

$$
\text{ACDU Coverage} = \frac{2}{2} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

**Final Answer for (a):** 2 coupling data-flow pairs identified, 2 test cases required for 100% ACDU.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**The three strategies (1 mark each = 3 marks, plus examples and comparison = 4 marks):**

> **1. Inheritance-Centric Testing (Bottom-Up)**
> - **Approach:** Test the most derived (leaf) classes first, then progressively move up the hierarchy.
> - **Example:** In the hierarchy `Vehicle → Car → SportsCar`, test `SportsCar` first, then `Car`, then `Vehicle`.
> - **Strength:** Forces testing of all inherited members from ancestors.
> - **Weakness:** May re-test base class members multiple times, leading to redundancy.

> **2. Polymorphic-Centric Testing (Top-Down)**
> - **Approach:** Test super-class methods first to validate base behavior, then test overriding in subclasses.
> - **Example:** Test `Vehicle.startEngine()` first, then test `Car.startEngine()` (overridden), then `SportsCar.startEngine()`.
> - **Strength:** Ensures base behavior is verified before testing variations.
> - **Weakness:** May miss newly-defined subclass methods if focus is on polymorphism alone.

> **3. Hierarchy-Centric Testing (Combined)**
> - **Approach:** For each class in the hierarchy, test all methods — both inherited and newly defined.
> - **Example:** For `Car`, test inherited `startEngine()` from `Vehicle` and new `openTrunk()`. For `SportsCar`, test all 3 inherited + 2 new.
> - **Strength:** Most thorough; covers both inheritance and new members.
> - **Weakness:** Most expensive; many test cases.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Strategy | Order | Cost | Thoroughness | KTU Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inheritance-Centric | Bottom-Up | Medium | High for inherited | Use when inheritance is deep |
| Polymorphic-Centric | Top-Down | Low | Medium | Use when overriding is heavy |
| Hierarchy-Centric | Combined | High | Highest | Use for safety-critical systems |

> **Final Answer for (b):** The three strategies are *Inheritance-Centric*, *Polymorphic-Centric*, and *Hierarchy-Centric* — each suited to different scenarios. Hierarchy-Centric is most thorough but costliest.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print-friendly):**

- [ ] A **Call Graph** is a directed graph of method calls — nodes = methods, edges = calls.
- [ ] **Node Coverage** visits all methods; **Edge Coverage** traverses all call relationships; **Path Coverage** exercises all call sequences.
- [ ] **Recursive calls create cycles** in call graphs — count them carefully for path coverage.
- [ ] **Unreachable methods** have no incoming edge path from entry points — call graph analysis detects them.
- [ ] **Cyclomatic Complexity** for a call graph: $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ — gives the lower bound of independent test paths.
- [ ] **Class Inheritance Testing** uses a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) of class hierarchies.
- [ ] Three inheritance testing strategies: **Inheritance-Centric (Bottom-Up)**, **Polymorphic-Centric (Top-Down)**, **Hierarchy-Centric (Combined)**.
- [ ] **Inherited Method Coverage (IMC)** = (invoked inherited methods / total inherited methods) × 100%.
- [ ] **Polymorphic Method Coverage (PMC)** requires testing each dynamic binding to every class in the hierarchy.
- [ ] **Coupling Data-Flow Pairs** focus on **inter-module** def-use relationships (definition in one module, use in another).
- [ ] A **Coupling Variable** is defined in one module and used in a different module.
- [ ] Coupling coverage criteria: **ACD** (All Coupling Defs), **ACU** (All Coupling Uses), **ACDU** (All Coupling Def-Use pairs), **ACP** (All Coupling Pairs).
- [ ] **Looser coupling = Easier testing** — data coupling is easiest, content coupling is worst.
- [ ] **Intra-module** data flow = within one module; **Inter-module (coupling)** = across modules — KTU asks the latter.
- [ ] All three techniques (Call Graph, Inheritance, Coupling Data-Flow) operate at the **design level**, not the code level.
- [ ] Standard formula: $\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{covered items}}{\text{total items}} \times 100\%$ — applies uniformly across all three techniques.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Mantra for this topic:**
> *"Design the tests at the design level — call graphs show *who calls whom*, inheritance hierarchies show *who inherits what*, and coupling data-flow shows *who passes what to whom*."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Testing: Fundamentals, Tools (OWASP, Burp Suite), and Their Role in Protecting Modern Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security Testing: Fundamentals, Tools (OWASP, Burp Suite), and Their Role in Protecting Modern Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 — Security Testing: Fundamentals, Tools & Modern Application Protection

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Testing** is a non-functional software testing technique used to determine whether an information system protects data and maintains functionality as intended. It encompasses the systematic evaluation of an application's confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorization, availability, and non-repudiation properties against malicious attacks, unauthorized access, code injection, data leakage, and other cyber threats.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (OECST833) syllabus alignment, Security Testing is a critical sub-domain of **Non-Functional Testing** that verifies the system's resilience against both internal and external adversarial actions throughout the **Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness or flaw in the system that can be exploited to compromise security. |
| **Threat** | Any potential danger that may exploit a vulnerability to cause harm. |
| **Risk** | The probability of a threat exploiting a vulnerability, multiplied by its impact. |
| **Attack Vector** | The path or means by which an attacker gains unauthorized access. |
| **Exploit** | A piece of code, data, or sequence of commands that takes advantage of a bug or vulnerability. |
| **Penetration Testing (Pen Test)** | An authorized simulated cyber-attack on a system to evaluate its security. |
| **CIA Triad** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the foundational pillars of information security. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CIA Triad — The Golden Rule of Security Testing**
> Every security test you design MUST validate at least one pillar of the **CIA Triad**:
> - **Confidentiality** → Data is accessible only to authorized users (e.g., encryption tests).
> - **Integrity** → Data is not altered or tampered with during transit or storage (e.g., hash tests).
> - **Availability** → System is accessible when needed (e.g., DoS/DDoS resistance tests).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: Security Testing is like hiring a "Professional Burglar" to test your house.**
>
> Imagine you install a brand-new high-tech security system in your house. Would you trust the salesman who sold it to you? Probably not! Instead, you would hire a *certified ethical burglar* to try and break in. If he fails, great. If he succeeds, he tells you exactly **how** he broke in and **how to fix it** before a real criminal tries.
>
> - The **house** = Your software application.
> - The **burglar** = A Security Tester / Ethical Hacker.
> - The **locks, windows, alarms** = Authentication, Authorization, Encryption.
> - The **burglar's report** = The Security Test Report containing vulnerabilities.
> - The **fixing** = Patching, hardening, and re-testing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Security Testing (KTU High-Yield Classification)

Security testing is broadly classified into the following six sub-types. Each is a **favourite 3-mark or 7-mark KTU question**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Vulnerability Scanning**
> Automated scanning of the system against known vulnerability signatures using tools like **OWASP ZAP, Nessus, or Burp Suite**.
>
> **2. Penetration Testing (Pen Testing)**
> Manual or semi-automated simulated attacks. Three sub-categories:
> - *Black Box* — Tester has no internal knowledge (external attacker view).
> - *White Box* — Tester has full source code & architecture knowledge (internal audit).
> - *Gray Box* — Tester has partial knowledge (hybrid).
>
> **3. Security Auditing**
> Line-by-line code review and architecture review against security standards (ISO 27001, NIST).
>
> **4. Risk Assessment**
> Mathematical evaluation of threats, vulnerabilities, and impact.
>
> **5. Posture Assessment**
> Overall security stance evaluation — combines scanning, auditing, and ethical hacking.
>
> **6. Ethical Hacking**
> Legal, authorized hacking to find weaknesses before malicious hackers do.

## 2.2 OWASP — The Open Web Application Security Project

> [!NOTE]
> **OWASP** is a non-profit foundation that produces freely-available articles, methodologies, documentation, tools, and technologies in the field of web application security. The **OWASP Top 10** is the de-facto industry standard for web application security risks, updated every 3–4 years.

### OWASP Top 10 (2021 Edition — Most Exam-Relevant)

| Rank | Vulnerability | Description | KTU Board Favourite? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A01** | **Broken Access Control** | Users can act outside their intended permissions. | ⭐⭐⭐ Yes |
| **A02** | **Cryptographic Failures** | Weak/missing encryption (formerly *Sensitive Data Exposure*). | ⭐⭐⭐ Yes |
| **A03** | **Injection** | SQL, NoSQL, OS command, LDAP injection. | ⭐⭐⭐ Yes |
| **A04** | **Insecure Design** | Flaws in design patterns — cannot be fixed by perfect code alone. | ⭐⭐ Yes |
| **A05** | **Security Misconfiguration** | Default passwords, open cloud storage, verbose errors. | ⭐⭐ Yes |
| **A06** | **Vulnerable & Outdated Components** | Using libraries with known CVEs. | ⭐ Yes |
| **A07** | **Identification & Authentication Failures** | Weak passwords, missing MFA, session hijacking. | ⭐⭐⭐ Yes |
| **A08** | **Software & Data Integrity Failures** | Untrusted updates, CI/CD pipeline attacks. | ⭐⭐ Yes |
| **A09** | **Security Logging & Monitoring Failures** | Insufficient logging makes breaches untraceable. | ⭐⭐ Yes |
| **A10** | **Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)** | Server fetches remote resource based on user-supplied URL. | ⭐⭐ Yes |

## 2.3 Burp Suite — The Industry Standard Web Pen-Testing Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Burp Suite** is a graphical tool developed by **PortSwigger** used for **web application security testing**. It acts as an **interception proxy** between the tester's browser and the target web application, allowing the tester to inspect, modify, and replay HTTP/S requests.

### Burp Suite Editions (Frequently Asked in 2-Mark Questions)

| Edition | Target Audience | Approx. Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Community (Free)** | Beginners, students | $0 |
| **Professional** | Pen-testers, bug bounty hunters | ~$449/year |
| **Enterprise** | Large organizations, automated scanning | Custom pricing |

### Core Components of Burp Suite

| Tool | Function |
|---|---|
| **Proxy** | Intercepts and modifies HTTP/S traffic (the heart of Burp). |
| **Repeater** | Manually re-sends individual requests with modifications. |
| **Intruder** | Automates customized attacks (brute-force, fuzzing). |
| **Scanner** *(Pro only)* | Automated vulnerability scanner. |
| **Decoder** | Encodes/decodes data (Base64, URL, HTML, Hex). |
| **Comparer** | Compares two requests/responses for differences. |
| **Sequencer** | Analyzes the randomness of session tokens. |
| **Extender** | Allows add-ons (BApps) like *Logger++*, *Autorize*. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Risk & Severity Scoring

> [!WARNING]
> **MANDATORY RULE REMINDER:** The pipe symbol `|` is replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` inside table cells to avoid breaking Markdown.

| Formula | Equation | Purpose | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk** | $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | Quantitative risk scoring. | Numeric score (0–10) |
| **DREAD Score** | $DREAD = \dfrac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}$ | Microsoft risk-rating model. | 0–10 scale |
| **CVSS Base Score** | $BaseScore = f(ISC, Impact, Exploitability)$ | Common Vulnerability Scoring System. | 0.0 – 10.0 |
| **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Cost-benefit of security controls. | Currency (₹/year) |
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = AssetValue \times ExposureFactor$ | Cost of a single breach. | Currency (₹) |
| **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** | $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - CostOfControl}{CostOfControl}$ | Justifies security spend. | Ratio (decimal) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $D$ = Damage potential
> - $R$ = Reproducibility
> - $E$ = Exploitability
> - $A$ = Affected users
> - $D$ = Discoverability
> - $ISC$ = Impact Sub-Score
> - $SLE$ = Single Loss Expectancy
> - $ARO$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence

### Real-World Utility of Security Testing in Engineering

- **Banking/FinTech** — Prevents transaction tampering and data theft.
- **Healthcare** — Protects PHI (Protected Health Information) under HIPAA.
- **E-Commerce** — Safeguards credit card data under PCI-DSS.
- **Government/Defense** — National security and citizen data protection.
- **IoT & Embedded** — Prevents remote hijacking of cars, pacemakers, smart homes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed DREAD Score Calculation (Exhaustive Walkthrough)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **DREAD model** is a Microsoft-authored risk-classification system. Each letter is rated on a scale of **0 to 10**. The final DREAD score is the arithmetic mean of the five sub-scores. This is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question**.

### Problem Statement

> A web application has a **SQL Injection** vulnerability in its login form. An attacker can bypass authentication, dump the entire user table, and gain admin access. Compute the **DREAD score** and classify the risk.

### Step 1: Assign Sub-Scores

We rate each DREAD component on a 0–10 scale based on the vulnerability's characteristics.

| DREAD Component | Rating Logic | Score |
|---|---|---|
| **D** — Damage Potential | Attacker gets full DB access, can exfiltrate PII. | **9** |
| **R** — Reproducibility | Attack works every time with a simple payload like `' OR '1'='1`. | **10** |
| **E** — Exploitability | No special tools needed — only a browser. | **9** |
| **A** — Affected Users | Affects every user in the system. | **10** |
| **D** — Discoverability | Publicly disclosed vulnerability; tools like SQLMap detect it instantly. | **9** |

### Step 2: Apply the DREAD Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
DREAD &= \dfrac{D + R + E + A + D}{5} \\
&= \dfrac{9 + 10 + 9 + 10 + 9}{5} \\
&= \dfrac{47}{5} \\
&= 9.4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Interpret the Result

$$
RiskClass = \begin{cases} High, & 7.0 \le Score \le 10.0 \\ Medium, & 4.0 \le Score < 7.0 \\ Low, & 0.0 \le Score < 4.0 \end{cases}
$$

Since $9.4 \ge 7.0$, the vulnerability is classified as **HIGH risk** → must be remediated **immediately** before production deployment.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exam:** "**D**on't **R**eally **E**at **A**pples **D**aily" = D-R-E-A-D.

## 3.2 Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) — Detailed Derivation

### Problem Statement

> A startup hosts a customer database worth ₹10,00,000. Historical data shows the database is compromised approximately **3 times per year**. Each breach exposes **60%** of the records. Find the ALE.

### Step 1: Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE &= AssetValue \times ExposureFactor \\
&= \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\
&= \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALE &= SLE \times ARO \\
&= \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000 \times 3 \\
&= \text{₹}18{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Engineering Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> The company is expected to lose **₹18,00,000 per year** if no controls are added. Therefore, any security control costing **less than ₹18,00,000/year** that mitigates the risk is a financially justified investment.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Security Testing vs. Functional Testing

| Dimension | Functional Testing | Security Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Verify "Does it work as intended?" | Verify "Can it be broken?" |
| **Tester's Mindset** | Build the system. | Break the system. |
| **Test Inputs** | Valid & invalid spec-based inputs. | Malicious, edge-case, adversarial inputs. |
| **Test Oracles** | Requirements document. | CIA Triad, OWASP Top 10, CWE. |
| **Tools** | Selenium, JUnit, QTP. | Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP, Nessus, Metasploit. |
| **When Performed** | During system testing. | During security testing phase (often parallel). |
| **Bug Severity Focus** | Functional defect = failure. | Security defect = vulnerability. |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — OWASP ZAP vs. Burp Suite

| Feature | **OWASP ZAP** | **Burp Suite** |
|---|---|---|
| **License** | 100% Free & Open Source. | Free Community / Paid Pro. |
| **Origin** | OWASP Foundation. | PortSwigger. |
| **Primary User** | Beginners, CI/CD pipelines. | Professional pen-testers, bug bounty. |
| **Automation** | Strong CI/CD integration, headless mode. | Limited automation in Community. |
| **Scanner Quality** | Good (active scan in newer versions). | Excellent (industry best in Pro). |
| **Extensibility** | Add-ons via Marketplace. | BApps via Extender. |
| **Best Use Case** | DevSecOps, automated scans. | Manual deep-dive testing. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Security Testing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Application Ready for Testing] --> stepB[Step 1: Threat Modeling & Asset Identification]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Vulnerability Scanning via OWASP ZAP / Nessus]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Manual Pen Testing using Burp Suite]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Risk Scoring using DREAD / CVSS]
    stepE --> stepF{Step 5: Risk Acceptable?}
    stepF -->|Yes| stepG[Step 6: Issue Security Sign-off & Go-Live]
    stepF -->|No| stepH[Step 7: Patch Vulnerabilities & Re-test]
    stepH --> stepD
    stepG --> stepI[End: Continuous Security Monitoring in Production]
```

> **Figure 4.1** — Iterative Security Testing Workflow as followed in industry-grade SDLC.

## 4.2 OWASP Top 10 — Hierarchical Risk Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootNode[OWASP Top 10 - 2021]
    rootNode --> a01[A01 Broken Access Control]
    rootNode --> a02[A02 Cryptographic Failures]
    rootNode --> a03[A03 Injection Attacks]
    rootNode --> a04[A04 Insecure Design]
    rootNode --> a05[A05 Security Misconfiguration]
    rootNode --> a06[A06 Vulnerable Components]
    rootNode --> a07[A07 Authentication Failures]
    rootNode --> a08[A08 Software Integrity Failures]
    rootNode --> a09[A09 Logging Failures]
    rootNode --> a10[A10 Server Side Request Forgery]
    a03 --> a03a[SQL Injection]
    a03 --> a03b[NoSQL Injection]
    a03 --> a03c[OS Command Injection]
    a03 --> a03d[LDAP Injection]
    a01 --> a01a[Privilege Escalation]
    a01 --> a01b[Insecure Direct Object References]
```

> **Figure 4.2** — Hierarchical decomposition of the OWASP Top 10 with examples.

## 4.3 Burp Suite — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    browserA[Tester Browser] -->|HTTP/S Request| proxyB[Burp Proxy Intercepts Traffic]
    proxyB --> targetApp[Target Web Application]
    targetApp -->|HTTP/S Response| proxyB
    proxyB --> browserA
    proxyB --> repeaterC[Repeater - Manual Replay]
    proxyB --> intruderD[Intruder - Fuzzing and Brute Force]
    proxyB --> scannerE[Scanner Pro - Auto Vulnerability Detection]
    proxyB --> decoderF[Decoder - Encode Decode]
    proxyB --> sequencerG[Sequencer - Token Randomness]
    proxyB --> extenderH[Extender - BApp Plugins]
    intruderD --> payloadI[Payloads: SQLi, XSS, Brute Force Lists]
    scannerE --> reportJ[Vulnerability Report with Remediation]
```

> **Figure 4.3** — Block-level functional architecture of Burp Suite, showing how the Proxy acts as a central interceptor feeding into all other tools.

## 4.4 CIA Triad — Foundational Security Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ciaRoot[Information Security Core]
    ciaRoot --> confA[Confidentiality - Only authorized can read]
    ciaRoot --> integB[Integrity - Data is unaltered and authentic]
    ciaRoot --> availC[Availability - System is up when needed]
    confA --> testEx1[Example Test: Verify TLS 1.3 encryption on login]
    integB --> testEx2[Example Test: Verify SHA-256 hash matches]
    availC --> testEx3[Example Test: Load test 10k concurrent users]
```

> **Figure 4.4** — The CIA Triad with practical test scenarios mapped to each pillar.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

### Question 1
**Q: Define Security Testing. List any four types of security testing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Security Testing** is a non-functional testing technique that verifies whether an information system protects data, maintains functionality, and is resilient against unauthorized access, malicious attacks, and data leakage.

The four main types of security testing are:

1. **Vulnerability Scanning** — Automated detection of known weaknesses.
2. **Penetration Testing** — Simulated real-world attack by ethical hackers.
3. **Security Auditing** — Code and architecture review against standards.
4. **Risk Assessment** — Quantitative evaluation of threats and impacts.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the four types with brief explanation)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

### Question 2
**Q: What is OWASP? Explain the significance of OWASP Top 10.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project)** is a non-profit foundation that works to improve software security. Its most recognized output is the **OWASP Top 10**, a regularly updated awareness document that lists the **ten most critical web application security risks**.

**Significance:**
- Provides a **standardized reference** for developers and testers.
- Helps prioritize remediation efforts based on real-world attack data.
- Acts as a **minimum security baseline** during code reviews and penetration tests.
- The latest edition (2021) includes risks like *Broken Access Control*, *Cryptographic Failures*, and *Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)*.

*(1 mark for OWASP definition + 2 marks for significance & examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 3, Model Question A]**

### Question A (14 Marks)
**(a)** Explain the **OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities** (2021 edition) in detail with examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **architecture and components of Burp Suite**. How is it used to perform web application security testing? *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution (a) — OWASP Top 10 (7 Marks)

1. **A01 — Broken Access Control:** Users act outside intended permissions (e.g., normal user accesses admin URL `/admin/deleteUser?id=5` by changing URL). **Test:** Force-browse to admin pages, attempt IDOR.
2. **A02 — Cryptographic Failures:** Missing or weak encryption. **Example:** Storing passwords in plain text or using MD5. **Test:** Inspect network traffic, check TLS version.
3. **A03 — Injection:** Untrusted data sent to interpreter. **Example:** SQL Injection — input `' OR 1=1 --` in login field. **Test:** Send fuzzed payloads via Burp Intruder.
4. **A04 — Insecure Design:** Missing security by design. **Example:** No rate limiting on OTP endpoint, allowing brute force. **Test:** Architectural review.
5. **A05 — Security Misconfiguration:** Default credentials (admin/admin), verbose error pages exposing stack traces. **Test:** Review default configs, scan for exposed files.
6. **A06 — Vulnerable Components:** Using outdated libraries (e.g., Log4j before patch). **Test:** Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tools.
7. **A07 — Authentication Failures:** Weak passwords, missing MFA, predictable session tokens. **Test:** Brute-force with Burp Intruder.
8. **A08 — Software Integrity Failures:** Unverified auto-updates. **Test:** Verify digital signatures.
9. **A09 — Logging Failures:** No logs of failed logins. **Test:** Trigger attacks and check logs.
10. **A10 — SSRF:** Server fetches arbitrary URL. **Test:** Submit internal URL like `http://localhost:8080/admin`.

#### Model Solution (b) — Burp Suite Architecture (7 Marks)

**Architecture:**

Burp Suite works as an **intercepting proxy** at the application layer (Layer 7 of OSI). The tester configures their browser to route all traffic through Burp's proxy (default `127.0.0.1:8080`). Every HTTP/S request and response is captured, allowing the tester to:

- **View** raw request/response headers and bodies.
- **Modify** parameters in real time.
- **Replay** requests with different inputs.
- **Forward** requests to the target when ready.

**Key Components:**

| Component | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Proxy** | Live interception. |
| **Target** | Site map of the entire application. |
| **Intruder** | Automated brute-force and fuzzing. |
| **Repeater** | Manual re-send with edits. |
| **Scanner** *(Pro)* | Automated vulnerability detection. |
| **Decoder** | Encoding/decoding utilities. |
| **Comparer** | Diff two responses. |
| **Sequencer** | Token entropy analysis. |

**Use Case Workflow:**

1. Set browser proxy to `127.0.0.1:8080`.
2. Browse the application — Burp builds the **site map**.
3. Use **Repeater** to test edge cases of an API.
4. Use **Intruder** to brute-force the login form.
5. Run **Scanner** *(Pro)* for automated detection.
6. Export results in HTML report for developers.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 3, Model Question B]**

### Question B (14 Marks)
**(a)** Explain **DREAD risk assessment model** with formula. Compute the DREAD score for a vulnerability with the following ratings and classify the risk: Damage=8, Reproducibility=7, Exploitability=9, Affected Users=8, Discoverability=6. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between **OWASP ZAP and Burp Suite**. Describe the role of security testing in **protecting modern web and mobile applications**. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution (a) — DREAD Calculation (7 Marks)

**DREAD Model Explanation:**

DREAD is a Microsoft-proposed risk-rating model. Each letter represents a risk attribute, scored 0–10.

- **D** — Damage Potential: How bad the damage if exploited.
- **R** — Reproducibility: How reliably the bug can be reproduced.
- **E** — Exploitability: How easy to launch the attack.
- **A** — Affected Users: Percentage of impacted users.
- **D** — Discoverability: How easily the bug can be found.

**Formula:**

$$
DREAD = \dfrac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DREAD &= \dfrac{8 + 7 + 9 + 8 + 6}{5} \\
&= \dfrac{38}{5} \\
&= 7.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification:** Since $7.6 \ge 7.0$, the vulnerability is **HIGH risk** and must be remediated immediately.

#### Model Solution (b) — OWASP ZAP vs. Burp Suite & Role of Security Testing (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | **OWASP ZAP** | **Burp Suite** |
|---|---|---|
| License | Open source (free). | Free community / paid Pro. |
| Origin | OWASP Foundation. | PortSwigger. |
| Best For | Beginners, DevSecOps automation. | Professional pen-testing, deep manual testing. |
| Automation | Excellent in CI/CD. | Limited in Community. |
| Learning Curve | Gentle. | Moderate to steep. |

**Role of Security Testing in Modern Applications:**

Modern web and mobile applications are exposed to threats like **data breaches, ransomware, identity theft, and API abuse**. Security testing:

- **Prevents financial loss** — breaches cost millions.
- **Protects user trust** — users abandon breached apps.
- **Ensures regulatory compliance** — GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS.
- **Validates DevSecOps pipelines** — security is integrated into CI/CD.
- **Catches supply-chain attacks** — verifies third-party libraries.
- **Supports zero-trust architectures** — continuous verification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls**
>
> 1. ❌ Writing only the vulnerability names without **examples** → loses 2–3 marks per sub-question.
> 2. ❌ Forgetting the **CIA Triad** definition in introductory answers → examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 3. ❌ Showing DREAD formula but skipping the **classification threshold** (0–4, 4–7, 7–10) → loses 1 mark.
> 4. ❌ Confusing **OWASP Top 10 2017 vs 2021 editions** → examiner may deduct 1 mark for outdated content.
> 5. ❌ Listing Burp Suite tools without explaining the **interception proxy workflow** → lose 2 marks.
> 6. ❌ Writing generic security testing notes without **KTU-specific context** (CIA Triad, OWASP, DREAD) → answer appears incomplete.
> 7. ❌ Not mentioning the **active vs. passive scanning** difference in ZAP/Burp → common oversight.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Security Testing** = Non-functional testing for CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
- ✅ **CIA Triad** = the foundation — name it in any 3-mark intro question.
- ✅ **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** — memorize at least the first 5 with examples.
- ✅ **Top 3 Critical Risks (2021):** A01 Broken Access Control, A02 Cryptographic Failures, A03 Injection.
- ✅ **Burp Suite** is a **proxy-based** tool by **PortSwigger** — Proxy, Repeater, Intruder, Scanner, Decoder, Comparer, Sequencer, Extender.
- ✅ **OWASP ZAP** = open-source alternative; best for DevSecOps automation.
- ✅ **DREAD Formula:** $DREAD = (D + R + E + A + D) / 5$ on a 0–10 scale.
- ✅ **DREAD Classification:** Low (0–4), Medium (4–7), High (7–10).
- ✅ **Risk Formula:** $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$.
- ✅ **ALE Formula:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ — used to justify security investments in ₹.
- ✅ **Pen Testing Types:** Black Box (no knowledge), White Box (full code), Gray Box (partial).
- ✅ **SSRF (A10)** = Server-Side Request Forgery — newly elevated in 2021.
- ✅ **Log4j, Heartbleed, Equifax** are real-world examples of vulnerable components (A06).
- ✅ **DevSecOps** = integrating security testing into CI/CD pipelines.
- ✅ **Pen Test ≠ Vulnerability Scan** — pen test is manual + creative, scan is automated + signature-based.
- ✅ **Always mention** at least one real-world impact (financial, regulatory, reputational) in 14-mark answers to score full marks.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — SECURITY TESTING**
