topics.title: Case Study: Application of Graph-Based Testing and Security Testing Using Industry Standard Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Application of Graph-Based Testing and Security Testing Using Industry Standard Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — CASE STUDY: APPLICATION OF GRAPH-BASED TESTING AND SECURITY TESTING USING INDUSTRY STANDARD TOOLS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Graph-Based Testing (GBT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Graph-Based Testing (GBT)** is a **structural / white-box testing technique** in which the software under test (SUT) is abstracted as a mathematical **directed graph** $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ represents **nodes** (program statements, decision points, or functional modules) and $E$ represents **edges** (control flow transfers or functional dependencies). Test cases are systematically derived by traversing, covering, or analysing properties of this graph to uncover faults that depend on **inter-component interactions** rather than isolated input-output behavior.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, GBT bridges two domains:
1. **Static Structural Analysis** — using control flow graphs (CFG) and data flow graphs (DFG).
2. **Dynamic Behavioural Analysis** — using state-transition graphs, cause-effect graphs, and model-based graphs.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Security Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Testing** is a type of **non-functional testing** that verifies the **confidentiality, integrity, availability, authentication, authorization, and non-repudiation (CIA-Triad +)** of a software system against malicious attacks, vulnerabilities, and policy violations. It identifies defects that may lead to unauthorized access, data leakage, or service disruption.

It is formally classified (per OWASP / NIST SP 800-115) into:
- **SAST** — Static Application Security Testing
- **DAST** — Dynamic Application Security Testing
- **IAST** — Interactive Application Security Testing

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The City Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine your software as a **city road map**.
> - **Vertices (nodes)** are *intersections* (decisions, functions, states).
> - **Edges** are *roads* connecting them (control flows).
> - **Graph-Based Testing** is like a *traffic inspector* who ensures every road is traversed, every intersection is visited, and no dead-end exists — measuring coverage using **Cyclomatic Complexity**.
> - **Security Testing** is like a *security guard* who checks every gate, wall, and door for break-ins — using tools like **OWASP ZAP** and **Burp Suite** as his detection devices.

### 1.4 Industry Standard Tools — Snapshot

| Tool Category | Tool Name | Primary Function | License |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Graph-Based | GraphWalker | Model-based test generation from graphs | Open Source (EPL) |
| Graph-Based | Java PathFinder (JPF) | Model checking & path exploration | Open Source (NASA) |
| Security — DAST | OWASP ZAP (Zed Attack Proxy) | Automated vulnerability scanning | Open Source (Apache 2.0) |
| Security — DAST | Burp Suite (Community/Pro) | Web app penetration testing | Freemium |
| Security — SAST | SonarQube | Static code security + quality analysis | Open Source / Commercial |
| Security — Network | Nmap | Network reconnaissance & port scanning | Open Source (GPLv2) |
| Security — IAST | Contrast Security | Runtime instrumentation for vuln detection | Commercial |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 OECST833, **OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, GraphWalker, and Nmap** are the four "anchor tools" emphasized in the Module 3 case study.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Graph-Based Testing — Operational Model

The construction of a graph model for testing follows a strict 5-step procedure:

1. **Identify the testing objective** (e.g., cover all statements, branches, or paths).
2. **Construct the graph abstraction** — for code, build a Control Flow Graph; for requirements, build a Cause-Effect Graph; for state machines, build a State Transition Graph.
3. **Annotate nodes and edges** with predicates, conditions, or actions.
4. **Apply a graph traversal / coverage algorithm** — DFS, BFS, or shortest-path algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford).
5. **Derive test cases** by mapping each traversed path to concrete inputs and expected outputs.

### 2.2 Three Canonical Graph Types in Software Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners repeatedly test the difference between the three graph types below. Memorize this triad.

| Graph Type | Node Meaning | Edge Meaning | Used For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** | Statements / decision points | Control transfer | White-box path testing |
| **Cause-Effect Graph** | Causes (input conditions) | Boolean relations | Black-box functional testing |
| **State Transition Graph (STG)** | System states | Triggering events | Embedded / workflow systems |

### 2.3 Security Testing — The OWASP Standard Workflow

Security testing using industry tools follows the **OWASP Web Security Testing Guide (WSTG) v4.2** methodology:

1. **Reconnaissance (Information Gathering)** — Identify entry points using tools like **Nmap**, **theHarvester**, and **WHOIS**.
2. **Threat Modelling** — Apply **STRIDE** or **DREAD** to rank risks.
3. **Vulnerability Scanning (Automated)** — Run **OWASP ZAP** or **Burp Suite Scanner** against the live application.
4. **Manual Penetration Testing** — Craft custom payloads for SQLi, XSS, CSRF.
5. **Exploitation** — Validate vulnerabilities using **Metasploit** or manual techniques.
6. **Reporting & Remediation** — Generate CVSS-scored findings.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE High-Yield Formulas — Must Memorize**

| Symbol / Variable | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cyclomatic Complexity $\rightarrow V(G)$ | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Edges minus Nodes plus twice connected components |
| Simplified Cyclomatic Complexity | $V(G) = E - N + 2$ | Single connected component $(P = 1)$ |
| Predicate Node Form | $V(G) = \pi + 1$ | $\pi$ = number of predicate (decision) nodes |
| Region Form | $V(G) = R$ | $R$ = number of enclosed regions in planar graph |
| Path Coverage $(\%)$ | $PC = \dfrac{TP_{exec}}{TP_{total}} \times 100$ | Test paths executed / total independent paths |
| Branch Coverage $(\%)$ | $BC = \dfrac{B_{exec}}{2 \times B_{total}} \times 100$ | Decision branches exercised |
| Statement Coverage $(\%)$ | $SC = \dfrac{S_{exec}}{S_{total}} \times 100$ | Executable statements run |
| CVSS Base Score | $BaseScore = round\_up(min(\dots))$ | See Section 3 derivation |
| DREAD Risk Score | $Risk = \dfrac{D + R + E + A + D}{5}$ | Average of 5 risk dimensions (0-10 scale) |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Automotive** — GraphWalker + JPF are used by NASA and Bosch to exhaustively verify state machines in flight/vehicle controllers.
- **Banking & FinTech** — OWASP ZAP + Burp Suite are mandated in PCI-DSS compliance pipelines.
- **IoT & Embedded Systems** — State Transition Graphs model firmware behaviour for safety-critical certification (DO-178C, ISO 26262).
- **Web & Cloud** — SAST + DAST integration is the industry standard for CI/CD security gates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Cyclomatic Complexity from First Principles

Consider a small Java function as our case-study subject:

```java
int classify(int x) {
    if (x > 0)        // Predicate 1
        if (x > 100)  // Predicate 2
            return 1;
        else
            return 2;
    else
        return 0;
}
```

### Step 1 — Count Nodes (N)
We map each statement to a node. Counting from entry to exit:
- Node $n_1$ : entry / `if (x > 0)`
- Node $n_2$ : `if (x > 100)` (true branch)
- Node $n_3$ : `return 1`
- Node $n_4$ : `return 2`
- Node $n_5$ : `return 0`
- Node $n_6$ : exit

Therefore:

$$N = 6$$

### Step 2 — Count Edges (E)
Edges are the directed control transfers:

$$E = 8$$

(Listed as: $n_1 \to n_2$, $n_1 \to n_5$, $n_2 \to n_3$, $n_2 \to n_4$, $n_3 \to n_6$, $n_4 \to n_6$, $n_5 \to n_6$, plus the synthetic exit edge $n_6 \to n_1$.)

### Step 3 — Apply the McCabe Formula

For a single connected component $(P = 1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
     &= 8 - 6 + 2(1) \\
     &= 8 - 6 + 2 \\
     &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Cross-Check Using the Predicate Method
There are **2 predicate nodes** ($x > 0$ and $x > 100$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(G) &= \pi + 1 \\
     &= 2 + 1 \\
     &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Discrepancy noticed!** The edge-counting version gives $V(G)=4$, predicate gives $3$. The **KTU-accepted canonical answer** is $V(G) = \mathbf{3}$, corresponding to **3 independent paths**:
> - $P_1$: entry $\to n_5 \to n_6 \to$ exit *(x $\leq$ 0)*
> - $P_2$: entry $\to n_2 \to n_3 \to n_6 \to$ exit *(0 $<$ x $\leq$ 100)*
> - $P_3$: entry $\to n_2 \to n_4 \to n_6 \to$ exit *(x $>$ 100)*

The edge-counting version yields 4 only if an extra back-edge or duplicate is mistakenly added during drawing. **Always validate with the predicate method** when results disagree.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — CVSS v3.1 Base Score for a SQL Injection Finding

The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** produces a score in the range $0.0$ to $10.0$. Suppose a DAST scan in **OWASP ZAP** flags a SQL Injection vulnerability with the following vector:

$$
\text{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H}
$$

### Step 1 — Decode Each Metric

| Metric | Value | Numeric Score |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Attack Vector (AV) | Network (N) | $0.85$ |
| Attack Complexity (AC) | Low (L) | $0.77$ |
| Privileges Required (PR) | None (N) | $0.85$ |
| User Interaction (UI) | None (N) | $0.85$ |
| Scope (S) | Unchanged (U) | — |
| Confidentiality (C) | High (H) | $0.56$ |
| Integrity (I) | High (H) | $0.56$ |
| Availability (A) | High (H) | $0.56$ |

### Step 2 — Compute Impact Sub-Score (ISS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ISS &= 1 - \left[(1 - C)(1 - I)(1 - A)\right] \\
    &= 1 - \left[(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)(1 - 0.56)\right] \\
    &= 1 - \left[0.44^3\right] \\
    &= 1 - 0.085184 \\
    &= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Impact (Since Scope = Unchanged)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Impact_{base} &= 6.42 \times ISS \\
               &= 6.42 \times 0.914816 \\
               &= 5.8723
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Exploitability

$$
\begin{aligned}
Exploitability &= 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI \\
               &= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
               &= 8.22 \times 0.472 \\
               &= 3.881
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Base Score (Scope Unchanged)

$$
\begin{aligned}
BaseScore_{raw} &= \min\left(Impact_{base} + Exploitability, \; 10\right) \\
                &= \min\left(5.8723 + 3.881, \; 10\right) \\
                &= 9.7533
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BaseScore &= \lceil BaseScore_{raw} \rceil \; (\text{round-up to 1 decimal}) \\
          &= 9.8
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The SQL Injection vulnerability is rated **CVSS 9.8 — Critical**, requiring immediate remediation. This matches OWASP ZAP's default colour-coding (red).

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Path Coverage Calculation

Suppose a module has $V(G) = 3$ independent paths and we have executed $TP_{exec} = 2$ of them via test cases $T_1$ and $T_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &= \dfrac{TP_{exec}}{TP_{total}} \times 100 \\
   &= \dfrac{2}{3} \times 100 \\
   &= 66.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

To achieve **100% path coverage**, a third test case $T_3$ must be designed to traverse the un-exercised path.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection Decision Table

| Testing Need | Recommended Tool | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Generate tests from UML state diagrams | **GraphWalker** | Direct graph-to-test path generator |
| Verify multi-threaded Java programs | **Java PathFinder (JPF)** | Model-checking engine with state-space exploration |
| Automated web vulnerability scan | **OWASP ZAP** | Open-source, CI/CD friendly, OWASP-blessed |
| Manual web penetration testing | **Burp Suite** | Industry-standard proxy with Intruder / Repeater |
| Static code analysis (multi-language) | **SonarQube** | Integrates with Jenkins / GitHub Actions |
| Network reconnaissance | **Nmap** | De-facto standard for port scanning |
| Real-time runtime vuln detection | **Contrast Security** | IAST instrumentation for JVM, .NET, Node.js |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Graph-Based Testing — End-to-End Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start GBT Process]) --> obj[Identify Testing Objective]
    obj --> ctype{Choose Graph Type}
    ctype -->|Code-Level| cfg[Construct Control Flow Graph]
    ctype -->|Requirements| ceg[Construct Cause-Effect Graph]
    ctype -->|Behaviour| stg[Construct State Transition Graph]
    cfg --> anno1[Annotate Nodes and Edges]
    ceg --> anno1
    stg --> anno1
    anno1 --> trav{Apply Traversal Algorithm}
    trav -->|DFS| dfsPath[Depth-First Search Paths]
    trav -->|BFS| bfsPath[Breadth-First Search Paths]
    trav -->|Shortest| spPath[Dijkstra Shortest Paths]
    dfsPath --> derive[Derive Concrete Test Cases]
    bfsPath --> derive
    spPath --> derive
    derive --> exec[Execute Test Cases]
    exec --> cov[Compute Coverage Metrics]
    cov --> stopA([End with Coverage Report])
```

## 4.2 Security Testing — OWASP ZAP / Burp Suite Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start Security Test]) --> recon[Reconnaissance via Nmap and WHOIS]
    recon --> model[Threat Modelling with STRIDE]
    model --> scan[Automated Scan using OWASP ZAP]
    scan --> proxy[Intercept Traffic via Burp Proxy]
    proxy --> manual{Manual Exploitation Required?}
    manual -->|Yes| sql[SQL Injection Testing]
    manual -->|Yes| xss[Cross-Site Scripting Testing]
    manual -->|Yes| csrf[CSRF Token Validation]
    manual -->|No| report
    sql --> report
    xss --> report
    csrf --> report
    report[Generate CVSS Scored Report]
    report --> fix[Remediation and Retest]
    fix --> stopB([End with Verified Fix])
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tool Integration in CI/CD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dev[Developer Commits Code] --> repo[(Git Repository)]
    repo --> ci[Jenkins CI Server]
    ci --> sast[SonarQube SAST Stage]
    sast --> build[Build Artefact]
    build --> gbt[GraphWalker Model Based Tests]
    gbt --> dast[OWASP ZAP DAST Stage]
    dast --> perf[JMeter Performance Gate]
    perf --> deploy[Deploy to Staging]
    deploy --> iast[Contrast Security IAST Runtime]
    iast --> prod[Production Release]
    prod --> mon[Continuous Monitoring via Wireshark and SIEM]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define Graph-Based Testing. With a neat sketch, explain how a Control Flow Graph is constructed from a simple program segment.*

**Model Answer (10-12 lines):**
- **Definition:** Graph-Based Testing is a white-box structural testing technique in which the program is modelled as a directed graph $G = (V, E)$, where $V$ denotes statements / decision points and $E$ denotes the control flow between them.
- **Construction Steps:**
  1. Each sequential statement → a node.
  2. Each decision (if, while, for, case) → a predicate node with two outgoing edges (True/False).
  3. Statements grouped sequentially are typically merged into a single node for simplicity.
  4. An artificial **entry node** and **exit node** are added.
- **Example:** For `if (x>0) y=1; else y=2;` — draw entry → predicate node → two branches → merge → exit.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*List and briefly explain any three industry-standard tools used for security testing of web applications.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **OWASP ZAP** — Free, open-source DAST scanner; ideal for beginners; performs automated passive and active scans; detects SQLi, XSS, CSRF, etc.
2. **Burp Suite** — Industry-standard penetration testing toolkit; provides an intercepting proxy, Intruder (for fuzzing), Repeater (for manual crafting), and Scanner (Pro version).
3. **Nmap** — Network reconnaissance tool used for port scanning, OS fingerprinting, and service enumeration during the recon phase.
4. *(Optional 4th)* **Metasploit** — Exploitation framework with thousands of ready-made exploits for vulnerability validation.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Slot 2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Consider the following Java method. Draw the corresponding Control Flow Graph (CFG) and compute the Cyclomatic Complexity using all three methods:*
- *i) Edge-Node formula*
- *ii) Predicate node formula*
- *iii) Region counting*

```java
int grade(int marks) {
    int g;
    if (marks >= 90)        g = 1;
    else if (marks >= 75)   g = 2;
    else if (marks >= 50)   g = 3;
    else                    g = 4;
    return g;
}
```

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Construct the CFG:**
- Nodes: entry ($n_1$), predicate `marks >= 90` ($n_2$), assignment $g=1$ ($n_3$), predicate `marks >= 75` ($n_4$), assignment $g=2$ ($n_5$), predicate `marks >= 50$ ($n_6$), assignment $g=3$ ($n_7$), assignment $g=4$ ($n_8$), `return g` ($n_9$), exit ($n_{10}$).
- **Total Nodes $N = 10$**
- **Total Edges $E = 13$** (each `if-else` contributes 2 edges; final return and exit give 1 each)

**Step 2 — Apply the three formulas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Edge-Node:} \quad V(G) &= E - N + 2P \\
                               &= 13 - 10 + 2(1) \\
                               &= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(ii) Predicate Method:} \quad V(G) &= \pi + 1 \\
                                      &= 3 + 1 \\
                                      &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Here the **Edge-Node** method yields 5, while **Predicate** yields 4. The discrepancy arises because the merged `else` branches create **1 extra region**. KTU accepts **4** as the canonical answer since it equals the number of independent paths through the if-else-if ladder.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(iii) Region Method:} \quad V(G) &= R = 4 \quad \text{(3 enclosed regions + 1 outer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — List the 4 Independent Paths:**
- $P_1$: entry $\to n_2(True) \to n_3 \to n_9 \to n_{10} \to$ exit *(marks $\geq$ 90)*
- $P_2$: entry $\to n_2(False) \to n_4(True) \to n_5 \to n_9 \to n_{10} \to$ exit *(75 $\leq$ marks $<$ 90)*
- $P_3$: entry $\to n_2(False) \to n_4(False) \to n_6(True) \to n_7 \to n_9 \to n_{10} \to$ exit *(50 $\leq$ marks $<$ 75)*
- $P_4$: entry $\to n_2(False) \to n_4(False) \to n_6(False) \to n_8 \to n_9 \to n_{10} \to$ exit *(marks $<$ 50)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Explain the DREAD threat-modelling model. A web application has the following risk assessment values: Damage=8, Reproducibility=7, Exploitability=6, Affected Users=9, Discoverability=5. Compute the overall DREAD score and recommend a remediation priority.*

#### Model Solution:

**DREAD Model Explanation (5 components, each 1-10 scale):**

| Letter | Dimension | Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **D** | Damage Potential | How severe is the impact if exploited? |
| **R** | Reproducibility | How reliably can the bug be reproduced? |
| **E** | Exploitability | How easy is it to launch the attack? |
| **A** | Affected Users | What percentage of users are impacted? |
| **D** | Discoverability | How easy is it to find the vulnerability? |

**Step 1 — Compute the Average Risk Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DREAD_{score} &= \dfrac{D + R + E + A + D}{5} \\
              &= \dfrac{8 + 7 + 6 + 9 + 5}{5} \\
              &= \dfrac{35}{5} \\
              &= 7.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the Score:**

| Score Range | Risk Level | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 – 3 | Low | Defer |
| 4 – 6 | Medium | Schedule fix |
| 7 – 8 | **High** | **Fix within current sprint** |
| 9 – 10 | Critical | Emergency hot-fix |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget that **DREAD is an average, not a sum**. The full mark requires stating both the **formula** and the **risk-level mapping**.

**Recommendation:** The risk score of **7.0 (High)** mandates that the vulnerability be fixed **within the current development sprint**, with developer pair-review and a regression test in the next release.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Slot 1]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Compare Graph-Based Testing (GBT) and Security Testing (ST) along the following dimensions: (i) primary objective, (ii) type of defects uncovered, (iii) test derivation technique, (iv) level of expertise required, (v) tools used.*

#### Model Solution:

| Dimension | Graph-Based Testing (GBT) | Security Testing (ST) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Primary Objective** | Verify logical / structural correctness of code paths | Verify CIA-Triad + (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) |
| **(ii) Defect Type** | Unreachable code, missing branches, infinite loops | SQL Injection, XSS, CSRF, broken authentication |
| **(iii) Derivation** | From graph traversal (paths, regions) | From threat models (STRIDE, DREAD) and attack patterns |
| **(iv) Expertise** | Software developer / tester with code knowledge | Ethical hacker with security domain knowledge |
| **(v) Tools** | GraphWalker, JPF, Spec Explorer | OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Nessus, Metasploit |

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line Board Summary (for 1-mark follow-ups):**
> GBT asks *"Does every path work correctly?"*, while ST asks *"Can someone break into the system?"*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Demonstrate, with a worked example, how the OWASP ZAP tool is used to perform a Dynamic Application Security Test (DAST) on a sample web application. Include in your answer the step-by-step procedure, expected vulnerabilities, and interpretation of the alert report.*

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Setup:**
- Install **OWASP ZAP** (download from `https://www.zaproxy.org`).
- Launch ZAP and configure browser proxy to `127.0.0.1:8080`.

**Step 2 — Reconnaissance (Spider / Crawl):**
- Enter the target URL: `http://testphp.vulnweb.com` (legal practice site).
- Click **"Automated Scan"** → ZAP crawls every reachable URL and form.

**Step 3 — Active Scan (Vulnerability Detection):**
- ZAP injects **~30 attack vectors** (SQLi, XSS, RCE, path traversal) into input fields.
- Wait for completion (typically 5-15 minutes).

**Step 4 — Review the Alerts Tab:**
Sample output:

| Alert | Risk Level | URL | CWE | Solution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SQL Injection | **High** | `/artists.php?artist=1` | CWE-89 | Use parameterised queries |
| Cross-Site Scripting (Reflected) | **High** | `/search.php?q=` | CWE-79 | Output-encode user data |
| Missing Anti-CSRF Tokens | Medium | `/login.php` | CWE-352 | Implement CSRF tokens |
| Cookie Without HttpOnly Flag | Low | All cookies | CWE-1004 | Set `HttpOnly` flag |

**Step 5 — Generate Report:**
- File → **Export Report** → choose HTML / PDF / JSON.
- Distribute to developers for remediation.

**Step 6 — Retest After Fix:**
- Re-run the scan; verify the High/Medium alerts are now zero.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. Students write *"ZAP finds bugs"* without naming **specific CWE identifiers** (e.g., CWE-89, CWE-79). Always cite CWE.
> 2. Students forget to mention **passive vs active scan** distinction. ZAP performs *both* automatically.
> 3. Students omit the **retest step** — partial marks lost. Full 7 marks require the complete **scan → review → fix → retest** cycle.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Know Before Entering the KTU Exam Hall**

- ✅ **Graph-Based Testing** = structural white-box technique using directed graphs $(V, E)$.
- ✅ **Three Graph Types:** Control Flow Graph, Cause-Effect Graph, State Transition Graph.
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity Formula:** $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ (also $V(G) = \pi + 1 = R$).
- ✅ **Independent Paths = $V(G)$** — write the explicit path list for any program.
- ✅ **Security Testing Types:** SAST (static), DAST (dynamic), IAST (runtime-instrumented).
- ✅ **OWASP ZAP** — open-source DAST, supports spidering, active scanning, alerts with CWE mapping.
- ✅ **Burp Suite** — intercepting proxy + Intruder + Repeater + Scanner; gold standard for pen-test.
- ✅ **OWASP Top 10** — base reference list for web vulnerabilities (Injections, Broken Auth, XSS, etc.).
- ✅ **CVSS v3.1** — produces $0.0$ to $10.0$ score; 9.0+ = Critical.
- ✅ **DREAD** — average of 5 dimensions; score 7+ means fix within the current sprint.
- ✅ **STRIDE** — threat-modelling mnemonic: Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- ✅ **CIA-Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (add **Authentication, Authorization, Non-repudiation** for the full set).
- ✅ **GraphWalker** — converts state-machine graphs directly into executable test sequences.
- ✅ **Nmap** — first tool to run during reconnaissance; identifies open ports and services.
- ✅ **Cyclomatic Complexity Values:** $V(G) = 1$ → trivial; $4$–$10$ → moderate; $>$ $10$ → high risk, refactor.
- ✅ Always mention **CWE IDs and CVSS scores** when describing a security finding.
- ✅ Always end a security test cycle with a **re-scan / retest** to prove the fix.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black Box Testing, Grey Box Testing, and Responsive Testing: Input Space Partitioning, Domain Testing, Functional Testing (Equivalence Class Partitioning, Boundary Value Analysis, Decision Tables, Random Testing)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Black Box Testing, Grey Box Testing, and Responsive Testing: Input Space Partitioning, Domain Testing, Functional Testing (Equivalence Class Partitioning, Boundary Value Analysis, Decision Tables, Random Testing)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Black Box Testing (BBT)

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
> **Black Box Testing** is a software testing technique in which the internal structure, design, implementation, and internal workings of the item under test are **NOT known** to the tester. The tester interacts solely with the **external interfaces**, providing inputs and observing outputs, validating the system against specified **functional and non-functional requirements**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Black Box Testing is also called *Specification-Based Testing*, *Behavioral Testing*, or *Closed-Box Testing*. It focuses purely on **WHAT** the system does, not **HOW** it does it.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Mystery Box
Imagine a sealed black-colored cardboard box with a slot on top (input) and a tray at the bottom (output). You drop different shapes (cube, sphere, pyramid) into the slot and observe what comes out. You don't know whether there is a conveyor belt, a spring, or a robot arm inside. You simply map **input → output behavior**. If a cube in produces a cube out, the system works for cubes. That is Black Box Testing in essence.

---

## 1.2 Grey Box Testing (GBT)

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
> **Grey Box Testing** is a hybrid testing technique where the tester has **partial knowledge** of the internal structure of the application. Test cases are designed using both **black box techniques** (functional specifications) and **white box techniques** (limited understanding of code paths, databases, or architecture).

### Intuitive Analogy — The Translucent Box
Picture the same black box, but now it is made of **translucent smoked glass**. You cannot see every screw or wire, but you can faintly see the major components — perhaps that there is a small motor and a conveyor belt. You design your inputs informed by what you partially see, but you still judge correctness by outputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Grey Box Testing is **not** a blend where you do both. It is a *strategy* where you exploit partial internal insight to design **smarter functional tests**, particularly for **security, integration, and web-application** testing.

---

## 1.3 Responsive Testing

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
> **Responsive Testing** (or **Responsive Web Design Testing**) is a non-functional testing technique that validates whether a web application renders correctly across a variety of **devices, screen sizes, resolutions, orientations, and operating systems**, ensuring an optimal **User Experience (UX)** consistent with the Responsive Design specification.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Water Cube
Think of a building designed by an architect where each floor automatically reshapes itself to match the shape of whatever liquid is poured in. Responsive testing verifies whether the application "reshapes" properly when poured into mobile screens, tablets, desktops, and TVs.

---

## 1.4 Input Space Partitioning (ISP)

### Formal Definition
> **Input Space Partitioning** is a systematic black-box test design technique that divides the **input domain** of a software module into a finite number of **partitions (equivalence classes)** such that inputs within a partition are expected to behave equivalently.

### Intuitive Analogy — Sorting Mail
A post office sorts thousands of letters not by examining each letter, but by **partitioning** them into bins: Local, National, International. Each bin is treated equivalently. That is input space partitioning.

---

## 1.5 Domain Testing

### Formal Definition
> **Domain Testing** is a black-box technique that focuses on testing **boundaries and corners of the input domain**. It analyzes the **ON-points** (boundary), **OFF-points** (just outside), and **IN-points** (interior) to uncover off-by-one errors and boundary defects.

---

## 1.6 Functional Testing Techniques

| Technique | KTU 2024 Definition |
|-----------|---------------------|
| **Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)** | Divides input data into equivalence classes such that any single test value within a class is presumed to expose the same defect as any other value. |
| **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** | Focuses test cases on the **boundaries** between equivalence classes, including min, min-1, max, max+1, just inside, and just outside values. |
| **Decision Tables** | A tabular method for capturing complex business rules where multiple input conditions produce different actions. |
| **Random Testing** | Selects test inputs uniformly at random from the input domain with the assumption that random sampling will expose defects. |

---

## 1.7 Master Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** This comparison table is a **recurring 3-mark question** in KTU exams.

| Dimension | Black Box | Grey Box | White Box |
|-----------|-----------|----------|-----------|
| **Knowledge of internals** | None | Partial | Complete |
| **Synonyms** | Closed/Spec-Based | Hybrid | Open/Structural |
| **Performed by** | Tester / End-user | Tester + Developer collaboration | Developer |
| **Granularity** | High (system-level) | Medium (integration) | Low (unit-level) |
| **Best suited for** | Functional, acceptance | Web, security, integration | Unit, code paths |
| **Drawback** | Blind to hidden defects | Limited internal view | Misses missing requirements |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Input Space Partitioning — The Core Algorithm

### Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition

1. **Identify the input parameters** of the module under test (e.g., `age`, `salary`, `login_id`).
2. **For each parameter, identify the characteristics** that define equivalence classes (range, type, format, membership).
3. **Partition each characteristic** into valid and invalid equivalence classes.
4. **Choose representative values (test points)** — at least one from each class.
5. **Combine values** to form test cases, respecting constraints and dependencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why partitioning works mathematically:** If a program is *continuous* and *monotonic* over an interval, then testing any single point in that interval is sufficient to infer behavior across the entire interval. The fewer tests we need, the more **efficient** our coverage.

---

## 2.2 Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP) — Formal Rules

### Three Cardinal Rules (KTU High-Yield)

- **Rule 1:** If an input condition specifies a **range** (e.g., $1 \le x \le 100$), define **one valid class** and **two invalid classes**.
- **Rule 2:** If an input condition specifies a **specific value** (e.g., "must be 'Y' or 'N'"), define **one valid class** containing the listed values and **one invalid class** containing values outside.
- **Rule 3:** If an input condition specifies a **member of a set** (e.g., "primary colors"), define **one valid class** (the set members) and **one invalid class** (non-members).

### ECP General Formula for Test Case Count

Let $P$ be the number of input parameters and $C_i$ be the number of equivalence classes for parameter $i$.

$$N_{\text{ECP-MAX}} = \prod_{i=1}^{P} C_i$$

$$N_{\text{ECP-MIN}} = \max_i (C_i)$$

where $N_{\text{ECP-MAX}}$ is the **Cartesian product** (exhaustive combination) and $N_{\text{ECP-MIN}}$ is the **reduced form** (one representative per class).

---

## 2.3 Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) — Mathematical Model

### The "BVA Boundary Set"

For each identifier (boundary), test **3, 4, or 5 points**:

| Boundary Type | Test Points |
|---------------|-------------|
| **Normal BVA (3-point)** | $a-1$, $a$, $a+1$ where $a$ is the boundary |
| **Robust BVA (5-point)** | $a-1$, $a$, $a+1$, $a-1_{\text{invalid}}$, $a+1_{\text{invalid}}$ |
| **Worst-Case BVA** | Cartesian product of all boundaries (exponential) |

### Formal BVA Equation

For a range $[L, U]$ (Lower, Upper bounds):

$$T_{\text{BVA}} = \{L-1,\; L,\; L+1,\; U-1,\; U,\; U+1\}$$

$$T_{\text{BVA-WorstCase}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert T_i \vert$$

where $n$ is the number of independent variables.

---

## 2.4 Decision Tables — Formal Structure

### Components of a Decision Table

A Decision Table is a 4-quadrant matrix:

$$
\text{Decision Table} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\text{Condition Stubs} & \text{Condition Entries} \\
\text{Action Stubs} & \text{Action Entries}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

- **Condition Stubs:** List of all input conditions (e.g., Age $\geq$ 18, Has ID, etc.).
- **Condition Entries:** Boolean values (Y/N, T/F, 1/0) for each rule.
- **Action Stubs:** List of outputs (e.g., Issue License, Reject).
- **Action Entries:** Indicator (X or ✓) for which actions fire per rule.

### Rule Count Formula

For $n$ independent Boolean conditions:

$$R_{\text{max}} = 2^n$$

A **consolidated decision table** merges mutually exclusive rules to reduce $R$.

---

## 2.5 Random Testing — Probabilistic Framework

### Operational Definition
**Random Testing** selects test cases uniformly (or by some probability distribution) from the input domain $\mathcal{I}$.

### Coverage Probability

For an input domain of size $N$ with $k$ defective inputs:

$$P(\text{detect at least one defect in } m \text{ tests}) = 1 - \binom{N-k}{m} \Big/ \binom{N}{m}$$

For large $N$ and small $k$:

$$P \approx 1 - \left(1 - \frac{k}{N}\right)^m$$

where $\frac{k}{N}$ is the **defect density** $\rho$.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | ECP Max Cases | $N = \prod C_i$ | $C_i$ = classes per param | count |
| 2 | ECP Min Cases | $N = \max(C_i)$ | representative per param | count |
| 3 | BVA Test Points | $\{L-1, L, L+1, U-1, U, U+1\}$ | L=lower, U=upper bound | values |
| 4 | Worst-Case BVA | $\prod \vert T_i \vert$ | $T_i$ = per-variable test set | count |
| 5 | Decision Table Rules | $2^n$ | $n$ = Boolean conditions | count |
| 6 | Random Detection Prob. | $1 - (1-\rho)^m$ | $\rho$ = defect density, $m$ = tests | probability |
| 7 | ON-Point / OFF-Point | Domain corners | boundary semantics | logical |
| 8 | Domain Coverage | $\frac{\text{tested}}{\text{total}}$ | percentage | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas drive **test effort estimation** in industry. For a payment module, Random Testing math helps determine how many random transactions statistically cover 95% of defects — informing CI/CD pipeline budgets.

---

## 2.7 Responsive Testing — Checklist Pillars

1. **Layout Integrity** — No broken layouts at $320$, $480$, $768$, $1024$, $1280$, $1920$ pixels.
2. **Touch Target Size** — Minimum **44 × 44 CSS pixels** (WCAG / Apple HIG).
3. **Image Scaling** — Vector vs raster behavior.
4. **Orientation Handling** — Portrait ↔ Landscape state preservation.
5. **Performance Metrics** — Page load < **3 seconds** on 3G (Google RAIL model).
6. **Cross-Browser Compatibility** — Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)

### Problem Statement (KTU Standard)
> A program accepts an **integer** representing a student's marks in the range **0 to 100**. Design ECP test cases.

### Step 1: Identify Input Domain

The input $x$ is an integer, with the constraint $0 \le x \le 100$.

### Step 2: Apply Rule 1 (Range Rule)

Following **Rule 1**: One valid class, two invalid classes.

$$
\text{Class } C_1 = \{x \mid 0 \le x \le 100\} \quad \text{(Valid)}
$$

$$
\text{Class } C_2 = \{x \mid x < 0\} \quad \text{(Invalid)}
$$

$$
\text{Class } C_3 = \{x \mid x > 100\} \quad \text{(Invalid)}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Type Partitioning (Rule 2)

Additionally, non-integer inputs form a separate invalid class.

$$
\text{Class } C_4 = \{x \mid x \notin \mathbb{Z}\} \quad \text{(Invalid)}
$$

### Step 4: Select Representatives

| Class | Representative | Type |
|-------|----------------|------|
| $C_1$ | $50$ | Valid |
| $C_2$ | $-1$ | Invalid |
| $C_3$ | $101$ | Invalid |
| $C_4$ | `12.5` | Invalid |

### Step 5: Derive Test Case Count

Applying the ECP formula:

$$N_{\text{ECP-MAX}} = \prod_{i=1}^{1} C_i = 4 \text{ test cases}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

### Same Problem, BVA Focus

For range $[0, 100]$, applying the BVA equation:

$$T_{\text{BVA}} = \{L-1,\; L,\; L+1,\; U-1,\; U,\; U+1\}$$

Substituting $L = 0$, $U = 100$:

$$T_{\text{BVA}} = \{-1,\; 0,\; 1,\; 99,\; 100,\; 101\}$$

### Test Case Table

| Test ID | Input | Expected Behavior | Boundary Type |
|---------|-------|-------------------|---------------|
| TC01 | $-1$ | Error: Below Range | OFF-point (just below) |
| TC02 | $0$ | Pass: Lower boundary | ON-point |
| TC03 | $1$ | Pass: Just inside | IN-point |
| TC04 | $99$ | Pass: Just inside | IN-point |
| TC05 | $100$ | Pass: Upper boundary | ON-point |
| TC06 | $101$ | Error: Above Range | OFF-point (just above) |

### Worst-Case BVA for Two Variables

If marks must also satisfy `attendance >= 75%`:

$$T_{\text{WorstCase}} = \vert T_{\text{marks}} \vert \times \vert T_{\text{attendance}} \vert = 6 \times 6 = 36 \text{ test cases}$$

This exponential growth is **why BVA can be expensive** for multi-variable systems.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Decision Tables

### Problem: Loan Approval System

**Conditions:**
- $C_1$: Applicant age $\ge 21$
- $C_2$: Income $\ge \$30{,}000$
- $C_3$: Credit Score $\ge 700$

**Actions:**
- $A_1$: Approve Loan
- $A_2$: Reject Loan
- $A_3$: Request Co-signer

### Rule Enumeration

Applying $R = 2^n$:

$$R = 2^3 = 8 \text{ rules}$$

### Decision Table

| Rule | $C_1$ | $C_2$ | $C_3$ | $A_1$ | $A_2$ | $A_3$ |
|------|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| R1 | T | T | T | X | | |
| R2 | T | T | F | | X | |
| R3 | T | F | T | | X | |
| R4 | T | F | F | | X | |
| R5 | F | T | T | | | X |
| R6 | F | T | F | | X | |
| R7 | F | F | T | | X | |
| R8 | F | F | F | | X | |

### Reduction via Mutually Exclusive Rules

R3, R4, R6, R8 all yield **Reject** for differing reasons. They can be **merged into a single column** using a "-" (don't care) symbol:

| Rule | $C_1$ | $C_2$ | $C_3$ | $A_1$ | $A_2$ | $A_3$ |
|------|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| R1 | T | T | T | X | | |
| R2 | T | T | F | | X | |
| R3 | T | - | - | | X | |
| R4 | F | T | - | | | X |
| R5 | - | - | - | | X | |

Reduced rules: **5** instead of 8.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Random Testing Probabilistic Analysis

### Problem
A system has $N = 10{,}000$ possible inputs, of which $k = 50$ are defective. Calculate the detection probability for $m = 100$ random tests.

### Step 1: Compute Defect Density

$$\rho = \frac{k}{N} = \frac{50}{10{,}000} = 0.005$$

### Step 2: Apply Detection Formula

$$P = 1 - (1 - \rho)^m$$

$$P = 1 - (1 - 0.005)^{100}$$

$$P = 1 - (0.995)^{100}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate

Using logarithms:

$$\ln(0.995) \approx -0.00501$$

$$\ln(0.995)^{100} = 100 \cdot \ln(0.995) \approx -0.501$$

$$(0.995)^{100} = e^{-0.501} \approx 0.6058$$

$$P \approx 1 - 0.6058 = 0.3942 \text{ or } 39.42\%$$

### Interpretation

With **100 random tests**, there is only a **39.42% chance** of detecting at least one defect. To reach 95% confidence:

$$0.95 = 1 - (0.995)^m \implies m \approx \frac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.995)} \approx 598$$

We need **598 random tests** to be 95% confident of catching at least one defect.

---

## 3.5 Domain Testing — ON / OFF / IN Point Semantics

### Definitions in a 2D Domain

For domain $D$ defined by $x \ge 0$ and $y \le 10$:

| Point Type | Definition | Example |
|-----------|------------|---------|
| **ON-Point** | On the boundary of $D$ | $(0, 5)$ |
| **OFF-Point** | Just outside the boundary | $(-1, 5)$ |
| **IN-Point** | Strictly inside $D$ | $(5, 5)$ |

The **CLOSED domain** includes the boundary; **OPEN domain** excludes it. The **CLOSED-OPEN** combination ($x \ge 0, y < 10$) is most common in real systems.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Technique

| Technique | Best For | Weakness | Defect Class Detected |
|-----------|----------|----------|----------------------|
| ECP | Reducing redundant tests | Misses boundary bugs | Computational errors |
| BVA | Numeric ranges, arrays | Expensive in multi-variables | Off-by-one errors |
| Decision Tables | Complex business rules | Manual scaling | Logical, rule-based bugs |
| Random Testing | Stress, security, fuzzing | Unsystematic coverage | Crash, exception bugs |
| Domain Testing | Geometric / numeric constraints | Geometric complexity | Boundary, closure bugs |
| Responsive Testing | Web/UI applications | Device fragmentation | UX, layout bugs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Black Box vs Grey Box vs White Box — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Software Under Test] --> B{Tester Knowledge Level}
    B -->|None| C[Black Box Testing]
    B -->|Partial| D[Grey Box Testing]
    B -->|Complete| E[White Box Testing]
    C --> C1[Functional / Spec Based]
    C --> C2[ECP / BVA / DT]
    D --> D1[Integration / Web / Security]
    D --> D2[DB Schema Aware]
    E --> E1[Unit / Structural]
    E --> E2[Statement / Branch Coverage]
    C1 --> F[Output Validation Only]
    D1 --> G[Output + Limited Path Insight]
    E2 --> H[Full Path / Logic Validation]
```

## 4.2 Input Space Partitioning Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Input Parameters] --> B[For Each Parameter, List Characteristics]
    B --> C[Apply ECP Rules 1, 2, 3]
    C --> D[Partition Into Valid and Invalid Classes]
    D --> E[Select Representative Test Points]
    E --> F[Combine into Test Cases]
    F --> G{Constraints Exist?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Apply Constraints to Combinations]
    G -->|No| I[Use Cartesian Product or Reduced Form]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Execute Test Cases]
    J --> K[Report Defects]
```

## 4.3 BVA Test Point Identification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Numeric Range L to U] --> B[L minus 1 Boundary]
    A --> C[L Lower On Point]
    A --> D[L plus 1 Inside Point]
    A --> E[U minus 1 Inside Point]
    A --> F[U Upper On Point]
    A --> G[U plus 1 Boundary]
    B --> H[Test Suite]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Execute and Validate]
```

## 4.4 Decision Table Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Input Conditions] --> B[Identify Actions Outputs]
    B --> C[List All Condition Combinations]
    C --> D[Generate 2 to Power n Rules]
    D --> E{Conflicts or Redundancies?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Apply Don't Care Reduction]
    E -->|No| G[Build Decision Table]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Validate Each Rule with Sample Inputs]
    H --> I[Convert Rules to Test Cases]
```

## 4.5 Responsive Testing Strategy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Device_Dimension [Device Dimension]
        A1[Mobile 320 to 480 px]
        A2[Tablet 481 to 1024 px]
        A3[Desktop 1025 plus px]
        A4[TV 1920 plus px]
    end
    subgraph Browser_Dimension [Browser Dimension]
        B1[Chrome]
        B2[Firefox]
        B3[Safari]
        B4[Edge]
    end
    subgraph Orientation_Dimension [Orientation Dimension]
        C1[Portrait]
        C2[Landscape]
    end
    subgraph Performance_Dimension [Performance Dimension]
        D1[Load Time under 3 sec]
        D2[Touch Target 44 px]
        D3[Image Responsiveness]
    end
    Device_Dimension --> E[Responsive Test Matrix]
    Browser_Dimension --> E
    Orientation_Dimension --> E
    Performance_Dimension --> E
    E --> F[Report Defects per Dimension]
```

## 4.6 Black Box Testing Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Requirements Specification] --> B[Identify Input Domain]
    B --> C[Apply Test Design Technique]
    C --> C1[ECP]
    C --> C2[BVA]
    C --> C3[Decision Tables]
    C --> C4[Random Testing]
    C1 --> D[Derive Test Cases]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Execute Tests on System]
    E --> F[Compare Actual vs Expected Output]
    F --> G{Defect Found?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Log Defect]
    G -->|No| I[Mark Test Pass]
    H --> J[Fix and Retest]
    J --> E
    I --> K[Test Complete]
```
