<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Black Box Testing. List any two advantages and two limitations.**

**Model Answer:**
> **Black Box Testing** is a software testing technique in which the internal structure, design, and implementation of the item being tested are **not known** to the tester. The tester focuses on inputs and expected outputs derived from requirements specifications.
>
> **Advantages:**
> 1. Tester need not have programming knowledge or access to source code.
> 2. Effective for verifying functional correctness from the user's perspective.
>
> **Limitations:**
> 1. Cannot detect hidden defects such as missing code paths or unreachable branches.
> 2. Test case design without internal knowledge may lead to redundancy or gaps in coverage.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Distinguish between Equivalence Class Partitioning and Boundary Value Analysis with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
> | Aspect | ECP | BVA |
> |---|---|---|
> | Focus | Reduces input domain into equivalence classes | Tests values at and around the boundary |
> | Selection | One representative per class | Multiple values at boundary |
> | Error Class | Computational errors | Off-by-one errors |
>
> **Example:** For input range $0 \le x \le 100$:
> - **ECP representatives:** $-1$, $50$, $101$
> - **BVA points:** $-1$, $0$, $1$, $99$, $100$, $101$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)** technique in detail with its rules. Derive the ECP test cases for the following problem: **[7 Marks]**

> *A program accepts a student's grade point as a floating-point number in the range 0.0 to 10.0. Values below 0.0 and above 10.0 should be rejected. Additionally, negative inputs must be flagged with "Invalid".*

**(b)** Explain **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** and design the BVA test cases for the above problem. Compare BVA with worst-case BVA. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Define Input Domain**

The input $g$ is a float with constraint $0.0 \le g \le 10.0$.

**Step 2: Apply ECP Rule 1 (Range)**

Applying the rule, we obtain three classes:

$$C_1 = \{g \mid 0.0 \le g \le 10.0\} \quad \text{(Valid)}$$

$$C_2 = \{g \mid g < 0.0\} \quad \text{(Invalid — Negative)}$$

$$C_3 = \{g \mid g > 10.0\} \quad \text{(Invalid — Excessive)}$$

**Step 3: Apply ECP Rule 2 (Type)**

$$C_4 = \{g \mid g \notin \mathbb{R}\} \quad \text{(e.g., alphanumeric)}$$

**Step 4: Select Test Points**

| Class | Test Value | Type |
|-------|------------|------|
| $C_1$ | $5.5$ | Valid |
| $C_2$ | $-1.0$ | Invalid |
| $C_3$ | $10.5$ | Invalid |
| $C_4$ | `"abc"` | Invalid |

**Step 5: Verify against "Negative flag" requirement**

Note that $C_2$ values must trigger the *"Invalid"* message per specification — this is verified through the representative $-1.0$.

$$N_{\text{ECP}} = 4 \text{ test cases}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Apply BVA Equation**

For range $[0.0, 10.0]$, $L = 0.0$, $U = 10.0$:

$$T_{\text{BVA}} = \{L-1, L, L+1, U-1, U, U+1\}$$

$$T_{\text{BVA}} = \{-1.0,\; 0.0,\; 1.0,\; 9.0,\; 10.0,\; 11.0\}$$

**Step 2: Test Case Table**

| TC | Input | Expected | Boundary |
|----|-------|----------|----------|
| T1 | $-1.0$ | Invalid (Negative) | OFF |
| T2 | $0.0$ | Valid | ON |
| T3 | $1.0$ | Valid | IN |
| T4 | $9.0$ | Valid | IN |
| T5 | $10.0$ | Valid | ON |
| T6 | $11.0$ | Invalid (Excessive) | OFF |

**Step 3: Worst-Case BVA Comparison**

Worst-case BVA takes the Cartesian product of all boundary sets across variables.

For single variable:

$$T_{\text{WorstCase}} = 6 \text{ test cases}$$

This equals normal BVA. Worst-case exceeds normal BVA only when **multiple variables** have ranges. For $n$ variables with $k$ boundaries each:

$$N_{\text{WorstCase}} = k^n$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** What are **Decision Tables**? Explain their components. Construct a complete decision table for a **library book issue system** with the following rules: **[7 Marks]**

> - Rule 1: If the user is a member AND the book is available AND no fine is pending → Issue the book.
> - Rule 2: If the user is a member AND the book is available AND a fine is pending → Reject with fine-clear message.
> - Rule 3: If the user is a member AND the book is NOT available → Add to waitlist.
> - Rule 4: If the user is NOT a member → Reject with non-member message.

**(b)** Explain **Random Testing** with its mathematical model. A system has 5,000 inputs with 25 defects. Compute the probability of detecting at least one defect with 50 random tests. How many tests are needed for 90% confidence? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Components of Decision Table**

A decision table has four quadrants:

$$
\text{Decision Table} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\text{Condition Stubs} & \text{Condition Entries (Y/N)} \\
\text{Action Stubs} & \text{Action Entries (X)}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Identify Conditions and Actions**

- Conditions: $C_1$ = Member, $C_2$ = Book Available, $C_3$ = No Fine Pending
- Actions: $A_1$ = Issue Book, $A_2$ = Reject (fine), $A_3$ = Waitlist, $A_4$ = Reject (non-member)

**Step 3: Build the Table**

| Rule | $C_1$ | $C_2$ | $C_3$ | $A_1$ | $A_2$ | $A_3$ | $A_4$ |
|------|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| R1 | Y | Y | Y | X | | | |
| R2 | Y | Y | N | | X | | |
| R3 | Y | N | — | | | X | |
| R4 | N | — | — | | | | X |

**Step 4: Validate Sample Test Cases**

- Input: (Member=Y, Avail=Y, NoFine=Y) → Triggers R1 → Issue Book ✓
- Input: (Member=N) → Triggers R4 regardless of other conditions ✓

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Define Random Testing Mathematically**

For input domain of size $N$ and $k$ defective inputs, the probability of detecting at least one defect in $m$ tests is:

$$P = 1 - \left(\frac{N-k}{N}\right)^m$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

Given $N = 5000$, $k = 25$, $m = 50$:

$$\rho = \frac{25}{5000} = 0.005$$

$$P = 1 - (1 - 0.005)^{50}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate**

$$\ln(0.995)^{50} = 50 \times \ln(0.995) \approx -0.251$$

$$(0.995)^{50} \approx e^{-0.251} \approx 0.7781$$

$$P \approx 1 - 0.7781 = 0.2219 \text{ or } 22.19\%$$

**Step 4: Compute Tests for 90% Confidence**

Set $P = 0.90$:

$$0.90 = 1 - (0.995)^m$$

$$(0.995)^m = 0.10$$

$$m = \frac{\ln(0.10)}{\ln(0.995)} = \frac{-2.3026}{-0.00501} \approx 460$$

$$m \approx 460 \text{ tests}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing ECP with BVA:** ECP picks *one* representative per class; BVA picks *multiple* points around boundaries. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for conflating the two.
> 2. **Forgetting the Invalid Class:** Always list at least one invalid class — KTU examiners specifically look for the *"two invalid classes for ranges"* rule.
> 3. **Decision Table Action Entries:** Use **X** or **✓**, never just blank; a blank indicates "do not care" only in *reduced* tables with explicit "-" markers.
> 4. **Random Testing Formula Misuse:** Do not confuse $(1-\rho)^m$ with $(1-m\rho)$. The former is correct only for sampling without replacement approximations; for small $m$ and large $N$, both are equivalent, but KTU expects the binomial form.
> 5. **Responsive Testing Oversight:** Always mention **WCAG accessibility**, not just pixel sizes. Examiners reward accessibility-aware answers.
> 6. **Grey Box vs White Box:** Grey Box is a *test strategy*, not a *test design technique*. Never claim "Grey Box uses statement coverage."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These:**

- [ ] **Black Box** = no internal knowledge; synonymous with *specification-based* testing.
- [ ] **Grey Box** = partial internal knowledge; used in *web, security, integration* testing.
- [ ] **Responsive Testing** = validates UI across *devices, browsers, orientations*.
- [ ] **ISP** = divides input domain into *equivalence partitions*.
- [ ] **ECP Rule 1** (range) → 1 valid + 2 invalid classes.
- [ ] **ECP Rule 2** (specific value) → 1 valid + 1 invalid class.
- [ ] **ECP Rule 3** (set membership) → 1 valid + 1 invalid class.
- [ ] **BVA boundary set** = $\{L-1, L, L+1, U-1, U, U+1\}$.
- [ ] **Worst-case BVA** = Cartesian product → $k^n$ tests.
- [ ] **Decision Tables** = $2^n$ rules; use *don't care* (-) for reduction.
- [ ] **Random Testing** = $P = 1 - (1-\rho)^m$; $\rho = k/N$.
- [ ] **Domain Testing** = ON-points (boundary), OFF-points (just outside), IN-points (interior).
- [ ] **Touch target** minimum in responsive UI = $44 \times 44$ CSS pixels.
- [ ] **WCAG** accessibility is a *mandatory dimension* of responsive testing.
- [ ] **Grey Box is a strategy**, not a technique.
- [ ] Always prefer **BVA after ECP** — BVA is a *refinement* of ECP at the boundaries.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grey Box Testing: Introduction, Advantages, and Methodologies (Matrix Testing, Regression Testing, Orthogonal Array Testing)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Grey Box Testing: Introduction, Advantages, and Methodologies (Matrix Testing, Regression Testing, Orthogonal Array Testing) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Grey Box Testing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Grey Box Testing** is a software testing technique that combines elements of both **Black Box Testing** and **White Box Testing**. The tester has **partial knowledge** of the internal structure, algorithms, and data structures of the application, but tests the software primarily at the **functional / input-output level**. The test cases are designed using intelligent assumptions about the program internals, derived from architectural documents, state diagrams, data flow information, or business logic rules. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, Grey Box Testing is positioned as a hybrid testing strategy, where the test engineer works with a **"grey" level of transparency** — neither fully opaque (black box) nor fully transparent (white box). ### Taxonomy of Testing Approaches | Testing Type | Internal Knowledge | Testing Focus | Tester Type | |---|---|---|---| | **Black Box** | None | Functional / I/O behavior | End user / QA | | **White Box** | Complete (source code) | Internal logic paths | Developer | | **Grey Box** | Partial (architecture, DB) | Functional + targeted internals | QA with developer insight | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Hotel Maintenance Inspector**: > Imagine a hotel maintenance inspector. A **black box** inspector would only check if lights turn on in each room (functional). A **white box** inspector would dismantle every switch and rewire the circuitry (code-level). A **grey box** inspector, however, knows the **floor plan layout**, the **electrical wiring diagram**, and the **water pipeline map** — but still tests the room by switching on the light and checking the tap. They *design* their inspection tour based on the layout, but *execute* the test from the user's perspective. That is exactly what Grey Box Testing achieves in software engineering. ## 1.3 Why Grey Box Testing? Grey Box Testing is essential when: - The tester has access to **architectural documents** but not full source code. - Testing **distributed systems**, **web services**, or **embedded systems** where internal structure is partially known. - The team needs to design **intelligent test cases** that target probable defects using limited internals. - **Integration Testing** requires knowledge of data flows between modules. > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Insight:** Grey Box Testing is the *de facto* standard for **Web Application Testing**, **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** validation, and **API testing**, since testers usually have design documents and database schemas available. ## 1.4 When is Grey Box Testing Used? - **Web Application Testing** (UI flows + DOM knowledge) - **Integration Testing** (interface contracts known) - **Database Testing** (schema known, queries tested as I/O) - **Security Testing** (knowing architecture to craft targeted attacks) - **Distributed Component Testing** (knowing message-passing flow) - **Business Intelligence / Data Warehousing Testing** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Advantages of Grey Box Testing > [!NOTE] > **Core Advantages (Board-Favorite List — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams):** 1. **Intelligent Test Authoring** — Test cases are designed using a smart combination of inputs, reducing redundant tests. 2. **Unbiased / Neutral Perspective** — Since the tester is *not* the developer, the test remains user-centric, yet informed. 3. **Non-Intrusive Testing** — No need to modify source code; only structural knowledge is used. 4. **Time and Cost Efficient** — Combines the speed of black box with the accuracy of white box. 5. **Efficient Defect Discovery** — Targets *risky* internal pathways that are statistically more defect-prone. 6. **Bridges the Communication Gap** — Encourages better collaboration between developers and testers. 7. **Improved Product Quality** — Identifies defects that pure black box or pure white box might miss. 8. **Effective for Distributed Systems** — Works well when internal component structure is partially exposed. ## 2.2 Disadvantages of Grey Box Testing - **Limited Code Access** — Some code paths may remain untested. - **Defect Association Difficulty** — Tracing defects to specific code segments is harder than in white box. - **Test Design Overhead** — Requires testers to understand the architecture. - **Incomplete Coverage** — Cannot achieve 100% path coverage like white box. ## 2.3 Methodologies of Grey Box Testing The KTU 2024 syllabus specifies **three core methodologies** under Grey Box Testing: ### (A) Matrix Testing ### (B) Regression Testing ### (C) Orthogonal Array Testing (OAT) --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the following mathematical and structural formulas — frequently asked in KTU 2024 Part A & Part B.** | Concept | Formula / Notation | Description | |---|---|---| | Total Combinations (Brute Force) | $N = L^F$ | $L$ = levels, $F$ = factors | | Orthogonal Array Strength 2 | $OA(N, k, t, 2)$ | $N$ runs, $k$ factors, $t$ levels | | Minimum Runs for Strength 2 | $N \geq t^2 + 1$ | Lower bound for pair-wise | | L4 Array | $OA(4, 3, 2, 2)$ | 4 runs, 3 factors, 2 levels | | L9 Array | $OA(9, 4, 3, 2)$ | 9 runs, 4 factors, 3 levels | | L12 Array | $OA(12, 11, 2, 2)$ | 12 runs, 11 factors, 2 levels | | L16 Array | $OA(16, 15, 2, 2)$ | 16 runs, 15 factors, 2 levels | | L27 Array | $OA(27, 13, 3, 2)$ | 27 runs, 13 factors, 3 levels | | Reduction Ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{OA}}{L^F} = \dfrac{N_{OA}}{N_{total}}$ | Efficiency of OAT | | Regression Risk Index | $R_r = \dfrac{\text{Defects in Re-test}}{\text{Total Re-tests}}$ | Risk metric | | Matrix Coverage | $C_m = \dfrac{\text{Cells Covered}}{\text{Total Cells}}$ | Matrix completeness | > [!WARNING] > When using mathematical symbols in tables above, $\vert$ and $\mid$ are used for conditions such as $N \geq t^2 + 1 \mid t \geq 2$. **Do not use `|`** (the markdown pipe) inside table rows — it breaks the table layout. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Avionics systems are tested using Orthogonal Array Testing to validate thousands of parameter combinations efficiently. - **Automotive:** ECU firmware grey box testing with partial knowledge of CAN bus protocols. - **Healthcare Medical Devices:** Grey box testing of infusion pumps, where firmware internals are known to the manufacturer but the FDA requires functional validation. - **Banking:** Integration testing of distributed transaction systems (SWIFT, UPI) where only service interfaces are exposed. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Methodology A — Matrix Testing ### 3.1.1 Definition > [!NOTE] > **Matrix Testing** is a Grey Box methodology that builds a two-dimensional matrix of **business-critical requirements** versus **test cases / program modules**, ensuring every requirement is exercised and every module is tested for each requirement. Also known as **Requirements-vs-Tests Matrix** or **Module-vs-Function Matrix**. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Procedure 1. **Identify Program Modules** (M1, M2, M3, ...) from design documents. 2. **Identify Business Requirements / Variables** (R1, R2, R3, ...) from the requirement specification. 3. **Construct the Matrix** — rows = modules, columns = requirements (or vice versa). 4. **Mark each cell** with a test identifier if a test exists that exercises that module-requirement combination. 5. **Detect Gaps** — empty cells indicate untested combinations. 6. **Add Test Cases** to fill gaps and re-evaluate the matrix. 7. **Compute Coverage Ratio** $C_m$. ### 3.1.3 Analytical Example — Matrix Testing Suppose a banking application has: - **3 Modules:** M1 (Login), M2 (Fund Transfer), M3 (Statement View) - **3 Requirements:** R1 (Authentication), R2 (Authorization), R3 (Audit Logging) **Initial Matrix:** | Module $\mid$ Req | R1 (Auth) | R2 (Author) | R3 (Audit) | |---|---|---|---| | M1 Login | T1 | T1 | — | | M2 Transfer | T2 | T2 | T3 | | M3 Statement | T4 | — | T5 | **Analysis:** - Cell (M1, R3) is empty → **Gap Detected** - Cell (M3, R2) is empty → **Gap Detected** **Coverage:** $C_m = 7/9 = 77.78\%$ **Action:** Add new tests T6 and T7 to fill gaps → $C_m = 100\%$. ### 3.1.4 Why Matrix Testing Matters - Visualizes **structural and functional coverage** simultaneously. - Helps the test manager identify **unexplored regions** of the code-requirement space. - Aligns with **Kaner, Bach, & Pettichord's** Grey Box framework — given internal knowledge, the tester maps the public variables to subsystem behaviors. --- ## 3.2 Methodology B — Regression Testing ### 3.2.1 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Regression Testing** is the selective re-testing of a software system to verify that modifications (bug fixes, enhancements, configuration changes, or new module additions) have **not adversely affected** existing, previously validated functionality. ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Procedure 1. **Identify changed components** from version control diff / change request. 2. **Identify affected components** via impact analysis. 3. **Select regression test cases** from the existing test suite (full or prioritized subset). 4. **Prioritize** using risk, frequency-of-use, or code-coverage metrics. 5. **Execute selected tests** on the updated build. 6. **Compare actual vs. expected results** for unchanged functionality. 7. **Report regression defects** and re-test after fix. ### 3.2.3 Types of Regression Testing | Type | Description | When to Use | |---|---|---| | **Unit Regression** | Re-test the modified unit only | Developer-level after fix | | **Partial Regression** | Re-test modified module + dependencies | Small to medium changes | | **Full Regression** | Re-execute the entire test suite | Major release / milestone | ### 3.2.4 Regression Test Selection (RTS) Formula A commonly used selection heuristic is: $$R_{selected} = \{ t_i \mid \text{code}(t_i) \cap \text{code}(\Delta) \neq \emptyset \}$$ Where: - $t_i$ is a test case - $\text{code}(t_i)$ is the code exercised by test $t_i$ - $\text{code}(\Delta)$ is the changed code - $\emptyset$ denotes the empty set > **Interpretation:** A test is selected if and only if it exercises at least one line of the changed code. We use $\emptyset$ to avoid breaking the markdown table — note that the symbol $\emptyset$ means "empty set" and must be written as $\emptyset$ in LaTeX. ### 3.2.5 Prioritization Formula A simple risk-weighted prioritization score is: $$P(t_i) = w_1 \cdot F(t_i) + w_2 \cdot R(t_i) + w_3 \cdot C(t_i)$$ Where: - $F(t_i)$ = failure history weight - $R(t_i)$ = requirement criticality - $C(t_i)$ = code coverage contribution - $w_1, w_2, w_3$ = tunable weights (sum to 1) --- ## 3.3 Methodology C — Orthogonal Array Testing (OAT) ### 3.3.1 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Orthogonal Array Testing (OAT)** is a systematic, statistical Grey Box testing technique that uses **orthogonal arrays** (from the Taguchi method of experimental design) to design test cases. It ensures that all pair-wise (or higher-order) combinations of input parameters are tested with **minimum number of test cases**, achieving maximum coverage with minimum redundancy. ### 3.3.2 Mathematical Foundation — Why OAT Works Consider a system with $F$ factors, each with $L$ levels. The total exhaustive combinations are: $$N_{total} = L^F$$ For $F = 4$ and $L = 3$: $$N_{total} = 3^4 = 81 \text{ test cases}$$ Using the **L9 Orthogonal Array**, only **9 test cases** are needed, yet **every pair of factors** appears with all 9 possible pair-wise level combinations. **Reduction Ratio:** $$\eta = \frac{N_{OA}}{N_{total}} = \frac{9}{81} = 0.1111 = 11.11\%$$ This means we test only **11.11%** of all combinations but still achieve **pair-wise coverage**. ### 3.3.3 Mathematical Derivation of L9 Array **Given:** - $k = 4$ factors - $t = 3$ levels per factor - Strength $\lambda = 2$ (pair-wise) **Step 1:** Compute the lower bound: $$N \geq t^\lambda + 1 = 3^2 + 1 = 10$$ **Step 2:** Use the standard L9 array, where $N = 9$ is sufficient because the Taguchi design satisfies: $$N = t^2 \text{ (for } \lambda = 2, t = 3\text{)} = 9$$ **Step 3:** The L9 array is constructed such that any two columns contain **all 9 pair-wise combinations** of levels $(0,1) \times (0,1) \times (0,1)$ exactly once. ### 3.3.4 The Standard L9 Orthogonal Array $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Col: } & \quad C_1 \quad C_2 \quad C_3 \quad C_4 \\ \hline \text{Run 1:} & \quad 0 \quad 0 \quad 0 \quad 0 \\ \text{Run 2:} & \quad 0 \quad 1 \quad 1 \quad 2 \\ \text{Run 3:} & \quad 0 \quad 2 \quad 2 \quad 1 \\ \text{Run 4:} & \quad 1 \quad 0 \quad 1 \quad 1 \\ \text{Run 5:} & \quad 1 \quad 1 \quad 2 \quad 0 \\ \text{Run 6:} & \quad 1 \quad 2 \quad 0 \quad 2 \\ \text{Run 7:} & \quad 2 \quad 0 \quad 2 \quad 2 \\ \text{Run 8:} & \quad 2 \quad 1 \quad 0 \quad 1 \\ \text{Run 9:} & \quad 2 \quad 2 \quad 1 \quad 0 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3.5 Worked Example — E-Commerce Web Application > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** Test the checkout page of an e-commerce web application with the following parameters: > - **F1 (Browser):** Chrome (0), Firefox (1), Safari (2) > - **F2 (OS):** Windows (0), Linux (1), Mac (2) > - **F3 (Connection):** DSL (0), Cable (1), Dial-up (2) > - **F4 (User Type):** Guest (0), Registered (1), Premium (2) **Step 1 — Calculate exhaustive combinations:** $$N_{total} = 3^4 = 81$$ **Step 2 — Choose L9 array:** $$N_{OA} = 9$$ **Step 3 — Map array columns to test parameters:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Test 1:} & \quad \text{Chrome} \mid \text{Windows} \mid \text{DSL} \mid \text{Guest} \\ \text{Test 2:} & \quad \text{Chrome} \mid \text{Linux} \mid \text{Cable} \mid \text{Premium} \\ \text{Test 3:} & \quad \text{Chrome} \mid \text{Mac} \mid \text{Dial-up} \mid \text{Registered} \\ \text{Test 4:} & \quad \text{Firefox} \mid \text{Windows} \mid \text{Cable} \mid \text{Registered} \\ \text{Test 5:} & \quad \text{Firefox} \mid \text{Linux} \mid \text{Dial-up} \mid \text{Guest} \\ \text{Test 6:} & \quad \text{Firefox} \mid \text{Mac} \mid \text{DSL} \mid \text{Premium} \\ \text{Test 7:} & \quad \text{Safari} \mid \text{Windows} \mid \text{Dial-up} \mid \text{Premium} \\ \text{Test 8:} & \quad \text{Safari} \mid \text{Linux} \mid \text{DSL} \mid \text{Registered} \\ \text{Test 9:} & \quad \text{Safari} \mid \text{Mac} \mid \text{Cable} \mid \text{Guest} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Verify pair-wise coverage** for any two factors (e.g., F1 × F2): all 9 pairs $(0,0), (0,1), (0,2), (1,0), (1,1), (1,2), (2,0), (2,1), (2,2)$ appear exactly **once** — confirming orthogonality. **Step 5 — Reduction achieved:** $$\eta = \frac{9}{81} \times 100\% = 11.11\%$$ > [!TIP] > **Engineering Takeaway:** OAT achieves **pair-wise interaction coverage** with only **11.11%** of the exhaustive test cases. Empirically, this catches **~90% of all defects** because most software defects surface at the level of single-variable or pair-wise interactions. ### 3.3.6 L4 Orthogonal Array (2-Level Factors) For 3 factors, each with 2 levels (Low / High), use **L4** with 4 runs: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Run:} & \quad F_1 \quad F_2 \quad F_3 \\ 1: & \quad 0 \quad 0 \quad 0 \\ 2: & \quad 0 \quad 1 \quad 1 \\ 3: & \quad 1 \quad 0 \quad 1 \\ 4: & \quad 1 \quad 1 \quad 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Total exhaustive combinations:** $$N_{total} = 2^3 = 8$$ **OAT test cases:** $$N_{OA} = 4$$ **Reduction:** $$\eta = \frac{4}{8} = 50\%$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Grey Box Testing Position in the Testing Spectrum ```mermaid flowchart LR A[BLACK BOX TESTING
No Internal Knowledge
Functional Validation] --> C{GREY BOX TESTING
Partial Internal Knowledge
Hybrid Approach} B[WHITE BOX TESTING
Complete Internal Knowledge
Structural Validation] --> C C --> D[Matrix Testing] C --> E[Regression Testing] C --> F[Orthogonal Array Testing] C --> G[Pattern Testing] C --> H[State Transition Testing] ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Matrix Testing Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Requirements Spec Available] --> B[Identify Program Modules] B --> C[Identify Business Variables / Requirements] C --> D[Construct M x N Matrix] D --> E{Each Cell Mapped to a Test?} E -->|Yes| F[Compute Coverage Ratio Cm] E -->|No| G[Identify Gap Cells] G --> H[Design Additional Test Cases] H --> D F --> I{Cm equals 100%?} I -->|No| G I -->|Yes| J[Finalize Test Suite] J --> K[End] ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Regression Testing Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Software Change Detected] --> B[Perform Impact Analysis] B --> C[Identify Affected Modules] C --> D[Select Regression Test Suite] D --> E[Prioritize Tests by Risk P t_i] E --> F[Execute Tests on New Build] F --> G{All Tests Pass?} G -->|Yes| H[Mark Build as Stable] G -->|No| I[Report Regression Defect] I --> J[Developer Fixes Defect] J --> D H --> K[End] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Orthogonal Array Testing Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify System Parameters] --> B[Determine Number of Factors k] B --> C[Determine Levels per Factor t] C --> D{Choose Suitable OA} D -->|k=3, t=2| E1[L4 Array] D -->|k=4, t=3| E2[L9 Array] D -->|k=11, t=2| E3[L12 Array] D -->|k=15, t=2| E4[L16 Array] E1 --> F[Map Factors to Columns] E2 --> F E3 --> F E4 --> F F --> G[Generate N Test Cases] G --> H[Verify Pair-wise Coverage] H --> I[Execute Test Cases] I --> J[End] ``` ## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Grey Box Testing Methodologies Comparison Block ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph GreyBox["GREY BOX TESTING METHODOLOGIES"] MT[MATRIX TESTING
Requirement-Module Matrix] RT[REGRESSION TESTING
Re-test after changes] OAT[ORTHOGONAL ARRAY TESTING
Statistical Pairwise Coverage] end MT --> P1[Output: Gap Detection] RT --> P2[Output: Stability Confirmation] OAT --> P3[Output: Optimal Test Set] P1 --> R[Reduced Defect Leakage] P2 --> R P3 --> R ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Pattern]** **Define Grey Box Testing. List any four advantages of Grey Box Testing.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Grey Box Testing is a hybrid software testing technique in which the tester has *partial knowledge* of the internal structure (architecture, database, or algorithms) but executes tests at the *functional / black box level*. Test cases are designed using intelligent assumptions derived from limited internal insight. **Advantages (4 × 0.5 = 2 Marks):** 1. **Intelligent test authoring** — fewer redundant tests. 2. **Unbiased and user-centric** — tests are functional yet informed. 3. **Non-intrusive** — no need to modify source code. 4. **Time and cost efficient** — combines speed of black box with depth of white box. --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Pattern]** **What is Orthogonal Array Testing? State the formula for the minimum number of runs required for a strength-2 orthogonal array.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (2 Marks):** Orthogonal Array Testing (OAT) is a statistical Grey Box testing technique that uses orthogonal arrays (Taguchi method) to design test cases. It ensures **pair-wise interaction coverage** of all input parameters with the *minimum number of test cases*. > **Formula (1 Mark):** The minimum number of runs for a strength-2 OA is: $$N \geq t^\lambda + 1 = t^2 + 1$$ where $t$ is the number of levels and $\lambda = 2$ (pair-wise strength). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a) Explain Grey Box Testing in detail. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **1. Definition and Concept (2 Marks):** Grey Box Testing is a hybrid testing methodology that combines features of black box and white box testing. The tester possesses limited knowledge of the internal workings — typically architectural design, database schema, or business logic — but executes the test as a black box at the user interface or API level. This approach allows designing **intelligent, targeted test cases** rather than exhaustive enumeration. **2. When to Apply (1 Mark):** - Web application testing - Integration testing - Distributed component testing - Database testing - Security / penetration testing **3. Advantages (2.5 Marks):** - Combines benefits of both black and white box testing. - Unbiased perspective; non-intrusive. - Efficient defect identification in risky paths. - Encourages developer-tester collaboration. - Time and cost effective. **4. Limitations (1.5 Marks):** - Limited code access — cannot test all paths. - Requires testers with architectural knowledge. - Difficult to associate defects with specific code segments. - Cannot achieve 100% structural coverage. --- **(b) Explain Matrix Testing and Regression Testing with suitable examples. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **Matrix Testing (3.5 Marks):** > Matrix Testing is a Grey Box methodology that creates a two-dimensional mapping between **program modules** and **business requirements** to ensure complete coverage. **Example:** Consider a login module with 3 sub-modules (M1 = UI, M2 = Validation, M3 = Session) and 3 requirements (R1 = Authentication, R2 = Authorization, R3 = Audit). | Module $\mid$ Req | R1 | R2 | R3 | |---|---|---|---| | M1 | ✓ | ✓ | — | | M2 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | M3 | — | ✓ | ✓ | Empty cells indicate untested combinations, prompting the addition of new test cases. This is one of the **favourite KTU 2024 board questions** — expect this in the exam. **Regression Testing (3.5 Marks):** > Regression Testing is the **selective re-testing** of an already-tested program after modifications to ensure that no new defects have been introduced and that previously working functionality continues to work. **Types:** 1. **Unit Regression** — re-test the modified unit only. 2. **Partial Regression** — re-test the changed module and its direct dependencies. 3. **Full Regression** — re-execute the entire test suite (used in major releases). **Example:** After fixing a bug in the payment gateway module, regression tests verify that login, cart, and order confirmation modules still function correctly. The selection formula is: $$R_{selected} = \{ t_i \mid \text{code}(t_i) \cap \text{code}(\Delta) \neq \emptyset \}$$ --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a) Explain Orthogonal Array Testing in detail. How does it differ from pair-wise testing? (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **1. Definition (2 Marks):** Orthogonal Array Testing (OAT) is a systematic, statistical method derived from **Taguchi's experimental design**. It generates a minimal set of test cases that ensures **pair-wise (or higher-order) coverage** of all input parameters. **2. Mathematical Basis (2 Marks):** - For $F$ factors and $L$ levels, exhaustive combinations = $L^F$. - OAT reduces this to $L^2$ runs for $L$-level pair-wise (strength-2) coverage. - Notation: $OA(N, k, t, \lambda)$. **3. Common Arrays (1 Mark):** | Array | Factors | Levels | Runs | |---|---|---|---| | L4 | 3 | 2 | 4 | | L9 | 4 | 3 | 9 | | L12 | 11 | 2 | 12 | | L16 | 15 | 2 | 16 | **4. Difference from Pair-wise Testing (2 Marks):** | Aspect | OAT | Pair-wise Testing | |---|---|---| | Origin | Statistical experimental design | Combinatorial testing | | Strength | Variable (2, 3, ...) | Always 2 | | Tool | Manual / All-pairs | Allpairs, ACTS, PICT | | Coverage | Pair-wise (and higher with strength) | Strictly pair-wise | | Scalability | Standardized arrays | Algorithmic generation | > **Verdict:** OAT is a *standardized implementation* of pair-wise testing using pre-defined orthogonal arrays, but can extend to **3-way** or **higher-order interactions** when required. --- **(b) A web application has 3 input parameters, each with 2 valid values (Low/High). Design the test cases using the L4 orthogonal array. Compute the reduction ratio. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Identify Parameters (1 Mark):** - **F1 = Browser:** Chrome (0), Firefox (1) - **F2 = OS:** Windows (0), Linux (1) - **F3 = Network:** Wired (0), Wireless (1) **Step 2 — Calculate Exhaustive Combinations (1.5 Marks):** $$N_{total} = L^F = 2^3 = 8 \text{ test cases}$$ **Step 3 — Use L4 Orthogonal Array (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Run:} & \quad F_1 \quad F_2 \quad F_3 \\ 1: & \quad 0 \quad 0 \quad 0 \\ 2: & \quad 0 \quad 1 \quad 1 \\ 3: & \quad 1 \quad 0 \quad 1 \\ 4: & \quad 1 \quad 1 \quad 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Map to Real Values (1 Mark):** | Run | Browser | OS | Network | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Chrome | Windows | Wired | | 2 | Chrome | Linux | Wireless | | 3 | Firefox | Windows | Wireless | | 4 | Firefox | Linux | Wired | **Step 5 — Compute Reduction Ratio (1.5 Marks):** $$\eta = \frac{N_{OA}}{N_{total}} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5 = 50\%$$ > **Conclusion:** Using L4 OA, only **4 test cases** (50% of exhaustive 8) achieve complete pair-wise coverage — saving 50% testing effort. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do not** confuse **Matrix Testing** with **Decision Table Testing** — Matrix Testing is module-vs-requirement, while Decision Tables are condition-vs-action. > - **Do not** mistake the L9 formula as $3^3$ — it is specifically $3^2$ runs (square of levels) for pair-wise strength. > - Always write the **reduction ratio** in Part B answers; omitting the numerical computation is a common reason for losing 1–2 marks. > - For **Regression Testing**, clearly distinguish between **Unit**, **Partial**, and **Full Regression** — examiners specifically test this distinction. > - In **OAT problems**, always **map the array values (0, 1, 2)** to the actual domain values of the factors — never leave them as raw numbers. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Board Exam Ready** ### 🔹 Grey Box Testing — Key Facts - Hybrid approach: partial internal knowledge + functional testing. - Used in **web apps, integration testing, database testing, security testing**. - **Advantages:** intelligent tests, unbiased, non-intrusive, time-efficient. - **Disadvantages:** limited code access, requires architectural knowledge, hard defect association. ### 🔹 Matrix Testing — Key Facts - 2D mapping of **modules × requirements** (or vice versa). - Empty cells = **uncovered combinations** = test gaps. - Coverage metric: $C_m = \dfrac{\text{Cells Covered}}{\text{Total Cells}}$. - Visual, simple, and aligned with KTU 2024 syllabus. ### 🔹 Regression Testing — Key Facts - **Selective re-testing** after changes. - 3 Types: **Unit, Partial, Full Regression**. - Trigger events: bug fixes, enhancements, configuration changes. - Selection formula: $R_{selected} = \{ t_i \mid \text{code}(t_i) \cap \text{code}(\Delta) \neq \emptyset \}$ - Prioritization: $P(t_i) = w_1 \cdot F(t_i) + w_2 \cdot R(t_i) + w_3 \cdot C(t_i)$. ### 🔹 Orthogonal Array Testing — Key Facts - Based on **Taguchi's experimental design**. - Notation: $OA(N, k, t, \lambda)$. - **L4** = 3 factors × 2 levels × 4 runs. - **L9** = 4 factors × 3 levels × 9 runs. - **L12** = 11 factors × 2 levels × 12 runs. - **L16** = 15 factors × 2 levels × 16 runs. - Minimum runs for strength-2: $N \geq t^2 + 1$. - Reduction ratio: $\eta = N_{OA} / L^F$. - Catches **~90% of defects** with only **~10–20% of test cases**. ### 🔹 Common KTU 2024 Mistakes to Avoid - ❌ Confusing OAT with Decision Tables. - ❌ Forgetting the **strength-2 minimum bound formula**. - ❌ Not mapping the array indices to **actual domain values**. - ❌ Skipping the **coverage / reduction ratio calculation** in OAT problems. - ❌ Omitting the **types of regression testing** in answers. - ❌ Writing raw `0/1/2` values without context in OAT. > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip for KTU 2024 ESE Preparation:** When asked about Grey Box Testing, always begin with the **definition + Venn diagram positioning (between black and white box)**, list **advantages as bullet points**, and conclude with the **three core methodologies: Matrix Testing, Regression Testing, and Orthogonal Array Testing**. This gives the examiner a structured, complete answer.

topics.title: Performance Testing: Network Latency Testing, Browser Compatibility, Responsive Testing Across Multiple Devices (e.g., BrowserStack, LambdaTest)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Performance Testing: Network Latency Testing, Browser Compatibility, Responsive Testing Across Multiple Devices (e.g., BrowserStack, LambdaTest)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Performance Testing — The Umbrella Concept

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (OECST833 — Software Testing), **Performance Testing** is a non-functional testing discipline that evaluates the *speed, responsiveness, stability, scalability, and reliability* of a software system under a defined workload. It is **not** concerned with finding defects in business logic (that is functional testing), but with measuring *Quality of Service (QoS)* parameters such as response time, throughput, latency, and resource utilisation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim from Module 4):**
> *"Performance Testing is a type of testing to determine the system's performance in terms of responsiveness and stability under a particular workload. It can include load, stress, endurance, spike, and volume testing."*

Within this broad umbrella, the current topic zooms into **three specialised sub-domains**:

| # | Sub-domain | Primary Question Answered |
|---|------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Network Latency Testing** | *"How fast does a single bit/ packet travel from client to server and back?"* |
| 2 | **Browser Compatibility Testing** | *"Does my web app render and behave correctly across Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge, Opera, on Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android?"* |
| 3 | **Responsive Testing** | *"Does the layout adapt gracefully across phones, tablets, laptops, desktops, 4K monitors?"* |

## 1.2 Network Latency Testing — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Tap-to-Whisper game):**
> Imagine you are in classroom A and your friend is in classroom B. You whisper a question through a pipe. The time between *you speak* and *friend replies* is the **Round-Trip Time (RTT)**. Now imagine the pipe is *clogged* (low bandwidth) or *winding* (high propagation delay). **Network Latency Testing** is the process of measuring, profiling, and validating that the "pipe" between client and server delivers data fast, predictably, and within agreed **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)**.

**Formal Definition:** Network Latency Testing is the systematic measurement of the delay experienced by a data packet as it travels from source to destination (and optionally back) over a network. It is measured in **milliseconds (ms)** and reported using metrics such as **RTT, TTFB, First Paint, and Time to Interactive (TTI)**.

## 1.3 Browser Compatibility Testing — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Multilingual Restaurant Menu):**
> A restaurant chain opens branches worldwide. The same dish (web app) must look, taste, and behave identically whether served in Tokyo, Paris, or New York — but the *cutlery, plates, and dietary laws* (browsers + OS) differ. **Browser Compatibility Testing** ensures the *dish* is consistently delivered regardless of the *cultural setting*.

**Formal Definition:** Browser Compatibility (or *Cross-Browser*) Testing is the process of validating that a web application renders, functions, and performs uniformly across a matrix of browser–operating-system–device combinations, including legacy versions that real users still employ.

## 1.4 Responsive Testing — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Liquid in Containers):**
> Pour water into a tall glass, a wide bowl, or a tiny shot-glass — the water *adapts* its shape. **Responsive Testing** verifies that a web page's UI *flows* and *reflows* to fit screens of any size, from a 320 px smartwatch to a 3840 × 2160 4K television, without horizontal scroll, content clipping, or unreadable text.

**Formal Definition:** Responsive Testing validates that a web application's layout, typography, images, and interactive elements adapt seamlessly to varying **viewports, pixel densities (DPI/PPI), and orientations (portrait/landscape)** by using techniques such as *fluid grids, flexible images, and CSS media queries*.

## 1.5 Cloud-Based Cross-Browser Platforms — BrowserStack & LambdaTest

Both **BrowserStack** and **LambdaTest** are *Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)* cloud platforms offering **real-device-cloud** testing. Instead of buying 50 physical phones and laptops, testers rent remote access to them through a WebSocket-based stream. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists these as reference platforms, so the architecture and working model are examinable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics in Bold (to be memorised for KTU exams):**
> **TTFB** (Time to First Byte), **FCP** (First Contentful Paint), **LCP** (Largest Contentful Paint), **CLS** (Cumulative Layout Shift), **TTI** (Time to Interactive), **RTT** (Round-Trip Time), **TPS** (Transactions Per Second), **P95 / P99 Latency** (95th/99th percentile).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network Latency Testing — Theoretical Model

The end-to-end network latency (often called **Total Round-Trip Time**, RTT) of a packet traversing the internet is modelled as the sum of **four canonical delay components**, defined by the *Kurose-Ross networking model* adopted in KTU's CS curriculum.

### 2.1.1 The Four Canonical Delay Components

1. **Transmission Delay (d_trans)** — Time taken to push *all* the packet's bits onto the physical link.
2. **Propagation Delay (d_prop)** — Time taken for a single bit to travel across the physical medium at the speed of light (or copper speed).
3. **Processing Delay (d_proc)** — Time taken by routers/switches to examine the packet header and decide the next hop.
4. **Queuing Delay (d_queue)** — Time the packet spends waiting in router buffers during congestion.

### 2.1.2 Why Each Component Matters

- **Why transmission?** Because a 1 MB image cannot be "teleported" — bits must be serialised onto the wire at the link's bit-rate. Larger packets → more transmission time.
- **Why propagation?** Because even at the speed of light, a signal travelling from India to the USA covers ~12,000 km and physically takes ~40 ms one-way.
- **Why processing?** Routers must inspect IP/TCP headers, run ACLs, update TTL — microseconds, but multiplied across 15+ hops.
- **Why queuing?** Unpredictable — it is the *only* component that depends on network load (M/M/1 queuing theory).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — All Key Equations (verbatim for KTU exam hall).**

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|------------------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Transmission Delay | $d_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds | L = packet length (bits), R = link rate (bits/s) |
| 2 | Propagation Delay | $d_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | seconds | d = physical distance (m), s = propagation speed (~2 × 10⁸ m/s in copper) |
| 3 | Total Nodal Delay | $d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$ | seconds | Sum at a single router |
| 4 | End-to-End Delay | $d_{end} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_{nodal,i}$ | seconds | Sum across N routers |
| 5 | Round-Trip Time (RTT) | $RTT = 2 \times d_{end,one-way}$ | seconds | Neglects processing at receiver |
| 6 | Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n)$ | bits/s | Bottleneck-link formula |
| 7 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = R \times d_{prop}$ | bits | Max un-ACKed data in flight |
| 8 | Packet Loss % | $PL\% = \dfrac{P_{lost}}{P_{sent}} \times 100$ | % | Critical metric for VoIP/video |
| 9 | Jitter | $J = \vert t_{i+1} - t_i \vert_{avg}$ | ms | Variation in inter-arrival time |
| 10 | Page Load Time (PLT) | $PLT = TTFB + DOM + Render$ | ms | TTFB + DOM parse + paint |
| 11 | P95 Latency | Sort latencies, pick 95th | ms | Better than average — captures tail |
| 12 | Apdex Score | $Apdex = \dfrac{S + \tfrac{T}{2}}{T}$ | 0–1 | S = satisfied, T = tolerating, T = total |

### 2.2.1 Explanation of Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telemedicine / HFT (High-Frequency Trading):** A 1 ms latency drop can mean ₹10 crores in arbitrage. Engineers use these formulas to *justify* moving servers to co-located exchanges.
- **CDN Selection:** Using $d_{prop} = d/s$, you compute the *geographic* latency to choose the nearest Point-of-Presence (PoP).
- **Capacity Planning:** $BDP$ tells you the optimal TCP window size — the basis for *TCP window scaling* in RFC 1323.

## 2.3 Browser Compatibility — The Testing Matrix

### 2.3.1 Logical Decomposition

A **Cross-Browser Test Matrix** is a Cartesian product:

$$
M_{compatibility} = B \times OS \times D \times R
$$

where:
- $B$ = set of Browsers (Chrome 120, Edge 119, Firefox 121, Safari 17, Opera 105, Samsung Internet 22, …)
- $OS$ = set of Operating Systems (Windows 11, macOS Sonoma, Ubuntu 22.04, iOS 17, Android 14, …)
- $D$ = set of Devices (Pixel 8, iPhone 15, Galaxy S23, …)
- $R$ = set of Resolutions (360 × 640, 768 × 1024, 1920 × 1080, …)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why cannot we test ALL combinations?**
> If $\vert B \vert = 10$, $\vert OS \vert = 5$, $\vert D \vert = 8$, $\vert R \vert = 3$, then $\vert M \vert = 10 \times 5 \times 8 \times 3 = \mathbf{1200}$ *combinations*. Cloud platforms like BrowserStack solve this by offering **Parallel Test Execution** across their device farm.

### 2.3.2 Tools & Techniques

| Layer | Tool/Technique | Purpose |
|-------|----------------|---------|
| **Manual Visual** | BrowserStack Live, LambdaTest Live | Real-time manual interaction on real devices |
| **Automated Functional** | Selenium WebDriver, Playwright, Cypress | Programmatic cross-browser assertions |
| **CSS Validation** | Can I Use, Autoprefixer, Stylelint | Vendor-prefix compatibility |
| **JS Engine Coverage** | Babel + @babel/preset-env | Transpile ES2024 → ES5 for legacy browsers |
| **Visual Regression** | Percy, Applitools, BackstopJS | Pixel-level diff between browsers |
| **Network Simulation** | Chrome DevTools — Throttling, Charles Proxy | Simulate 3G, 4G, packet loss |

## 2.4 Responsive Testing — Breakpoints & Strategies

### 2.4.1 Mobile-First vs Desktop-First

- **Mobile-First:** Write base CSS for $\leq 600$ px, then use `min-width` media queries to add complexity.
- **Desktop-First:** Write base CSS for $\geq 1280$ px, then use `max-width` media queries to strip complexity.

### 2.4.2 Standard Breakpoints (Bootstrap 5 / KTU-recommended)

| Breakpoint | Class Infix | Dimensions (px) | Target Device Class |
|------------|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| X-Small | (none) | < 576 | Portrait phones |
| Small | `sm` | ≥ 576 | Landscape phones |
| Medium | `md` | ≥ 768 | Tablets |
| Large | `lg` | ≥ 992 | Desktops |
| X-Large | `xl` | ≥ 1200 | Large desktops |
| XX-Large | `xxl` | ≥ 1400 | 4K / Ultra-wide |

## 2.5 BrowserStack vs LambdaTest — Comparative Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Direct comparison questions are common in Part A. Memorise the differentiating column.

| Feature | BrowserStack | LambdaTest |
|---------|--------------|------------|
| Founded | 2011 (Mumbai) | 2017 (San Francisco) |
| Real Device Cloud | Yes (3,000+ devices) | Yes (3,000+ devices) |
| HyperExecute (Parallel) | Yes (proprietary) | Yes (HyperExecute) |
| Pricing Model | Per-concurrent-session | Per-concurrent-session (cheaper) |
| Geo-Location Testing | 100+ countries | 60+ countries |
| Free Tier | Trial-only | Limited free plan (LT Browser) |
| Open-Source Discount | 1 year free for OSS | Limited |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is **exhaustive and derivation-heavy**, in line with the KTU Board's 14-mark valuation pattern. Every algebraic transition is shown in full.

## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End RTT across a Multi-Hop Path

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A packet is sent from a client in Trivandrum to a server in Frankfurt. The path traverses **N = 12 routers**. The link rate on every hop is $R = 1\,\text{Gbps}$. The packet length is $L = 1500\,\text{bytes}$ (standard MTU). The one-way physical distance is $d = 7500\,\text{km}$. Propagation speed $s = 2 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$. Processing delay at each router is $d_{proc} = 0.5\,\text{ms}$, and average queuing delay is $d_{queue} = 2\,\text{ms}$. Compute the **Total Round-Trip Time (RTT)**.

### Step 1 — Convert Packet Length to Bits

$$
L = 1500 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 12{,}000 \text{ bits}
$$

*(Explanation: Network links transmit in bits, not bytes. 1 byte = 8 bits, therefore 1500 bytes = 12,000 bits.)*

### Step 2 — Compute Transmission Delay per Hop

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/s}} = 12{,}000 \times 10^{-9}\text{ s} = 12\,\mu\text{s} = 0.012 \text{ ms}
$$

*(Explanation: To push 12,000 bits onto a 1 Gbps link, we need 12,000 / 10⁹ seconds = 12 microseconds.)*

### Step 3 — Compute One-Way Propagation Delay (Total Path)

$$
d_{prop,total} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{7{,}500{,}000\,\text{m}}{2 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}} = 0.0375\,\text{s} = 37.5\,\text{ms}
$$

*(Explanation: Signal travels at 2 × 10⁸ m/s in fibre. Distance is 7,500 km = 7.5 × 10⁶ m. Division yields 37.5 ms one-way.)*

### Step 4 — Compute One-Way Per-Router Processing + Queuing Delay

$$
d_{proc+queue,per-hop} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} = 0.5\,\text{ms} + 2\,\text{ms} = 2.5\,\text{ms}
$$

*(Explanation: Each router adds 0.5 ms of header inspection and 2 ms of average queue wait.)*

### Step 5 — Sum All Components for One-Way End-to-End Delay

We sum the nodal delays across all 12 routers, plus one transmission at the source, plus propagation once across the physical medium:

$$
d_{end,one-way} = (N \times d_{proc+queue}) + (N \times d_{trans}) + d_{prop,total}
$$

*(Explanation: Each of the 12 routers contributes processing + queuing + transmission, and the link adds one propagation delay equal to the full physical distance.)*

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{end,one-way} &= (12 \times 2.5\,\text{ms}) + (12 \times 0.012\,\text{ms}) + 37.5\,\text{ms} \\
&= 30\,\text{ms} + 0.144\,\text{ms} + 37.5\,\text{ms} \\
&= 67.644\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Double for Round-Trip

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTT &= 2 \times d_{end,one-way} \\
&= 2 \times 67.644\,\text{ms} \\
&= \mathbf{135.288\,\text{ms}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Explanation: Round-trip means the ACK must also travel back through the same path. With negligible ACK size, we simply double the one-way delay.)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** An RTT of ~135 ms is excellent for a transcontinental VoIP/streaming call. The **Google Web Vitals** threshold for "good" LCP is **< 2.5 s** *after* TTFB; thus this RTT is acceptable.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) and TCP Window Sizing

**Problem Statement:** Given $R = 100\,\text{Mbps}$ and one-way $d_{prop} = 60\,\text{ms}$, what is the minimum **TCP receive window** required to keep the pipe full?

### Step 1 — Convert Units

$$
R = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}, \quad d_{prop} = 60 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply BDP Formula

$$
BDP = R \times d_{prop} = (100 \times 10^6) \times (60 \times 10^{-3}) = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Bytes

$$
BDP = \frac{6 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}}{8} = 750{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 732 \text{ KB}
$$

*(Explanation: At 100 Mbps with 60 ms one-way delay, the network can hold ~750 KB "in flight" before the first ACK returns. The TCP receive window must be at least this large to saturate the link — this is why **TCP window scaling (RFC 1323)** extended the original 16-bit 64 KB limit.)*

## 3.3 Derivation: Apdex Score Calculation

**Problem Statement (Past KTU pattern):** A web app is used by **T = 1000** users in 1 hour. **S = 700** were satisfied (response < 1 s), **F = 50** were frustrated (response > 4 s), and the rest were tolerating.

### Step 1 — Compute Tolerating Count

$$
T_{tol} = T - S - F = 1000 - 700 - 50 = 250
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Apdex Formula

$$
Apdex = \frac{S + \frac{T_{tol}}{2}}{T} = \frac{700 + \frac{250}{2}}{1000} = \frac{700 + 125}{1000} = \frac{825}{1000} = \mathbf{0.825}
$$

*(Explanation: 0.825 falls in the "Good" range (0.85–0.94 is "Good", ≥ 0.95 is "Excellent"). Tolerating users are given half-credit because they *did* get a response, just slower than the satisfied threshold.)*

## 3.4 Derivation: Cross-Browser Matrix Cardinality & Sampling

**Problem Statement:** A startup targets **B = 5** browsers × **OS = 4** platforms × **D = 6** device classes × **R = 3** resolutions. Compute total combinations, then propose a **Pareto-prioritised sample** covering 80% of real-user traffic.

### Step 1 — Total Cardinality

$$
M = 5 \times 4 \times 6 \times 3 = \mathbf{360 \text{ combinations}}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the **80/20 Rule (Pareto)**

Analytics reveal 80% of users are on **Chrome + Android + Mobile** or **Safari + iOS + Mobile**. Reduced matrix:

$$
M_{reduced} = (1 \times 1 \times 1 \times 3)_{Chrome} + (1 \times 1 \times 1 \times 3)_{Safari} + (1 \times 2 \times 1 \times 2)_{others} = 11
$$

*(Explanation: We test the 80% traffic on 3 resolutions each, then sample 4 more for the long tail. Total effort drops from 360 → 11, a 96% reduction in test time.)*

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Tools to Sub-Domains

| Sub-Domain | Metric of Interest | Open-Source Tool | Commercial / Cloud Tool | KPI Threshold |
|------------|--------------------|------------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| **Network Latency** | RTT, Jitter, Loss | `ping`, `traceroute`, `mtr`, `iperf3` | Catchpoint, ThousandEyes, Pingdom | RTT < 100 ms (intra-continent) |
| **Browser Compat** | Visual/Functional parity | Selenium, Playwright, Cypress, TestCafe | BrowserStack, LambdaTest, Sauce Labs | 100% parity on top 5 browsers |
| **Responsive** | Layout reflow, No h-scroll | Chrome DevTools, Firefox Responsive Mode, Viewporter | BrowserStack Screenshots, LT Browser | No horizontal scroll on 320 px width |
| **Load/Stress** | TPS, P99 latency | JMeter, Gatling, k6, Locust | BlazeMeter, LoadRunner Cloud | P95 < 2 s at peak load |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are designed for **KTU board-exam reproduction**. Each uses only alphanumeric node IDs and raw uppercase labels, per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

## 4.1 End-to-End Network Latency Test — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientA[CLIENT BROWSER TRIVANDRUM] -->|HTTPS REQUEST| ispA[ISP EDGE ROUTER 1]
    ispA -->|HOP 1| coreA[BACKBONE ROUTER A]
    coreA -->|HOP 2| coreB[BACKBONE ROUTER B]
    coreB -->|HOP 3| coreC[TRANS-OCEANIC CABLE]
    coreC -->|HOP 4| ispB[ISP EDGE ROUTER 2]
    ispB -->|HOP 5| serverB[WEB SERVER FRANKFURT]
    serverB -->|HTTP RESPONSE| ispB
    ispB -->|REVERSE PATH| coreC
    coreC -->|REVERSE PATH| coreB
    coreB -->|REVERSE PATH| coreA
    coreA -->|REVERSE PATH| clientA
    clientA -.->|RTT MEASURED| testTool[APM TOOL NEW RELIC]
    serverB -.->|SERVER RESPONSE TIME| testTool

    coreA -.->|QUEUING DELAY| testTool
```
