# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model answers are written in KTU board-answer style: crisp, 3–4 sentences, with a key term in bold.**

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Network Latency. List any four metrics used to measure it.**

**Model Answer:**
**Network latency** is the time delay between a request being sent from a client and the corresponding response being received, measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.

Four metrics used to measure latency are:
1. **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** — time for a packet to go to server and back.
2. **Time to First Byte (TTFB)** — time until the first byte of the response arrives.
3. **Jitter** — variation in successive packet arrival times.
4. **Packet Loss Percentage** — ratio of lost to sent packets.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Browser Compatibility Testing and Responsive Testing with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Browser Compatibility Testing | Responsive Testing |
|--------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Focus** | Same functionality across different **browsers/OS** | Same layout across different **screen sizes/orientations** |
| **Example** | Testing login page in Chrome 120, Firefox 121, Safari 17 | Testing login page on 360 px (mobile), 768 px (tablet), 1920 px (desktop) |
| **Tools** | BrowserStack, Selenium Grid | Chrome DevTools, LT Browser |
| **Dimension Varied** | Software platform (engine) | Hardware viewport |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer ONE of the two choices. Each 14-mark question has two 7-mark sub-parts.**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **four canonical delay components** that contribute to end-to-end network latency. Derive the formula for **Total Nodal Delay**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A packet of size **1200 bytes** is transmitted over a link of bandwidth **2 Mbps** with one-way propagation delay of **25 ms**. Compute the **Transmission Delay**, **Propagation Delay**, and the **Bandwidth-Delay Product**. Comment on whether the **default TCP window of 64 KB** is sufficient. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The **four canonical delay components** are:

1. **Transmission Delay ($d_{trans}$):** Time to push all bits of a packet onto the link. $d_{trans} = L/R$ where $L$ is packet length in bits and $R$ is link rate in bits/s.
2. **Propagation Delay ($d_{prop}$):** Time for one bit to physically traverse the medium. $d_{prop} = d/s$ where $d$ is distance and $s$ is propagation speed.
3. **Processing Delay ($d_{proc}$):** Time a router spends inspecting the packet header, checking bit-errors, and determining the outgoing interface.
4. **Queuing Delay ($d_{queue}$):** Time the packet waits in the router's buffer before being transmitted. This is the *only* component dependent on network congestion.

**Derivation of Total Nodal Delay:**
At a single router $i$, the total time the packet spends is the sum of the four components:

$$
d_{nodal,i} = d_{proc,i} + d_{queue,i} + d_{trans,i} + d_{prop,i}
$$

For an **end-to-end** path with $N$ routers, we sum the nodal delays and add the propagation across the *full* physical distance (one accumulation, not per-hop):

$$
d_{end} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(d_{proc,i} + d_{queue,i} + d_{trans,i}\right) + d_{prop,total}
$$

The **Round-Trip Time** is twice the one-way end-to-end delay (assuming symmetric path and negligible ACK transmission time):

$$
RTT = 2 \times d_{end}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Convert packet size to bits.**

$$
L = 1200 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 9600 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Transmission Delay.**

$$
d_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{9600 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 4.8 \times 10^{-3}\text{ s} = \mathbf{4.8\,ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Propagation Delay is given.**

$$
d_{prop} = \mathbf{25\,ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Bandwidth-Delay Product.**

$$
BDP = R \times d_{prop} = (2 \times 10^6) \times (25 \times 10^{-3}) = 50{,}000 \text{ bits} \approx \mathbf{6.25\,KB}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with 64 KB default TCP window.**

The default TCP receive window is $64 \text{ KB} = 65{,}536$ bytes. Since $BDP = 6.25 \text{ KB} \ll 64 \text{ KB}$, the default window **is sufficient** to keep the pipe full. No window scaling is required.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` (Internal Choice)**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **working architecture of BrowserStack**. List any **four advantages** of cloud-based cross-browser testing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Your team is developing a responsive e-commerce site. The design must work on **360 px, 768 px, 1024 px, and 1920 px** widths. Write **CSS media queries** to handle each breakpoint and list the **3 main test categories** you would perform with one example test case per category. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Working Architecture of BrowserStack:**

1. The developer pushes code to a Git repository.
2. A **CI tool** (Jenkins, GitHub Actions) triggers a Selenium/TestNG script.
3. The script instructs the BrowserStack **Hub** to spin up a remote session on a real device (e.g., Pixel 8, Android 14, Chrome 120).
4. BrowserStack uses a **WebSocket-based remote-automation protocol** to stream the device screen back to the developer's browser and relay commands.
5. Test results, screenshots, video recordings, and console logs are uploaded to the BrowserStack dashboard.
6. Reports are integrated with **Allure / ExtentReports / Slack**.

**Four Advantages of Cloud-Based Cross-Browser Testing:**

1. **No Hardware CapEx:** Zero need to buy 50 physical phones/laptops — rent them on demand.
2. **Parallel Execution:** Run 100+ tests simultaneously, cutting wall-clock time by 90%.
3. **Real Devices, Not Emulators:** Higher fidelity than Android Studio emulators for camera/GPS/biometric tests.
4. **Geo-Location Testing:** Test from Mumbai, Tokyo, Frankfurt — verify CDN behaviour and localised content.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**CSS Media Queries (Mobile-First approach):**

```css
/* Base styles — apply to ALL widths (extra-small, phones) */
body { font-size: 14px; }

/* Small devices (landscape phones, 576px and up) */
@media (min-width: 576px) {
  body { font-size: 15px; }
}

/* Medium devices (tablets, 768px and up) */
@media (min-width: 768px) {
  body { font-size: 16px; }
  .product-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; }
}

/* Large devices (desktops, 1024px and up) */
@media (min-width: 1024px) {
  .product-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr; }
}

/* Extra-large devices (large desktops, 1920px and up) */
@media (min-width: 1920px) {
  .product-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; }
  body { font-size: 18px; }
}
```

**Three Main Test Categories for Responsive E-Commerce:**

| # | Category | Example Test Case |
|---|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Layout Reflow Test** | Verify product cards stack in 1 column on 360 px, 2 columns on 768 px, 3 columns on 1024 px. |
| 2 | **Touch vs Mouse Interaction** | Verify the "Add to Cart" button is ≥ 44 × 44 px on mobile (Apple HIG guideline) and shows hover state on desktop. |
| 3 | **Image Optimisation & Performance** | Verify the hero image is served as `image/avif` on 4G connections and falls back to `image/jpeg` on 2G with a < 200 KB payload. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing latency with bandwidth** — students frequently write *"high latency = high bandwidth"*. Wrong! Latency is *time* (ms), bandwidth is *capacity* (bits/s). Memorise the unit difference.
> 2. **Forgetting to double for RTT** — the *one-way* delay must be multiplied by 2 to get RTT. Single biggest error in derivations.
> 3. **Ignoring the "ACK transmission is negligible" assumption** — for small ACKs this is true; for large ACKs the exam may mark you down. State the assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Writing `min-width` in mobile-first vs `max-width` in desktop-first** — the direction matters and determines your *base* CSS.
> 5. **Mixing up "real device" vs "emulator"** — BrowserStack uses real hardware; Android Studio emulates. KTU expects you to know the difference.
> 6. **In Apdex, forgetting the "tolerating" half-credit** — many students write $(S+F)/T$ and lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (last 60 seconds before exam):**

- **Performance Testing ≠ Functional Testing** — measures QoS, not correctness.
- **Four Canonical Delays:** Processing, Queuing, Transmission, Propagation — remember order **P-Q-T-P** with the formula $d_{nodal} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$.
- **RTT Formula:** $RTT = 2 \times \sum d_{nodal,i}$ — always double the *one-way* delay.
- **Transmission Delay = L/R**, **Propagation Delay = d/s** — units must match (bits with bps, metres with m/s).
- **Bottleneck Throughput = min(R_i)** — never the *sum*, the *minimum* of all link rates.
- **BDP = R × d_prop** — governs TCP window sizing; default 64 KB window fails for high-bandwidth long-distance links.
- **Apdex Score = (S + T_tol/2) / T_total** — ranges 0 to 1; ≥ 0.85 is "Good".
- **Cross-Browser Matrix Cardinality = B × OS × D × R** — can exceed 1000; apply **Pareto / 80-20** to reduce.
- **BrowserStack vs LambdaTest:** Both are SaaS real-device clouds; BrowserStack has wider geo-coverage, LambdaTest is cheaper.
- **Responsive Breakpoints (Bootstrap 5):** 576, 768, 992, 1200, 1400 — know the device classes.
- **Mobile-First** uses `min-width`; **Desktop-First** uses `max-width` — exam favourite.
- **Core Web Vitals (Google 2024):** LCP < 2.5 s, CLS < 0.1, INP < 200 ms.
- **Real Device > Emulator** for camera, GPS, biometric, push-notification tests.
- **Visual Regression tools:** Percy, Applitools, BackstopJS — pixel-level diff, not just functional assertion.
- **Throttling tools:** Chrome DevTools Network tab, Charles Proxy, Lighthouse — simulate 3G/4G loss.
- **KTU Pattern Alert:** "Differentiate X and Y" → always produce a 2-column table; "Explain with diagram" → Mermaid-style block diagram earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to PEX: Symbolic Execution, Parameterized Unit Testing, Symbolic Execution Trees, and Their Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to PEX: Symbolic Execution, Parameterized Unit Testing, Symbolic Execution Trees, and Their Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is PEX?

**PEX (Program EXploration)** is an automatic white-box test generation tool developed by **Microsoft Research** (originally by Nikolai Tillmann and Jonathan de Halleux). It is integrated as an extension into **Visual Studio** and is used to systematically explore .NET code paths by generating minimal test suites with high code coverage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> PEX is essentially a practical, industrial implementation of **Dynamic Symbolic Execution (DSE)**, also known as *Concolic Testing* (Concrete + Symbolic). It combines concrete execution with symbolic reasoning.

## 1.2 Symbolic Execution — Formal Definition

**Symbolic Execution** is a formal, static program-analysis technique where program variables are treated as **symbolic input values** rather than concrete data. Instead of executing the program with specific values, the engine tracks *symbolic expressions* along all possible execution paths and builds logical formulas (called **path conditions** or **path predicates**) that describe the input constraints required to traverse each path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** A program with `n` branches can have up to **2ⁿ** distinct paths. Symbolic execution explores these paths by building a *Symbolic Execution Tree* (SET).

## 1.3 Parameterized Unit Testing (PUT) — Formal Definition

A **Parameterized Unit Test (PUT)** is an extension of the traditional unit testing paradigm in which test inputs are not hard-coded constants but are **symbolic parameters** bound to a *TestClass* by an *exploration engine* (like PEX). Each PUT contains:
- A **TestClass** — the system under test (SUT).
- A **TestMethod** — annotated with `[PexMethod]` (in PEX) containing symbolic parameters.
- **Constraints** (preconditions) and **Assumptions** (`PexAssume`).

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Robot Maze Explorer"

Imagine a robot dropped into a **maze** (your program) with infinite balls of different colors (inputs):

| Concept | Maze Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Concrete Execution** | Robot throws a single red ball — sees only one path. |
| **Symbolic Execution** | Robot has a "ghost" ball that represents *any* color — it marks every fork with a rule ("to go left, ball must be blue"). |
| **Path Condition** | A written note on the wall: *"If ball is blue AND weight < 5kg, door opens"*. |
| **Constraint Solver (Z3)** | A magic wand that, given the rule, finds the *exact* ball that satisfies it. |
| **PEX** | The full robot + wand system, automated to cover the entire maze. |
| **Parameterized Unit Test** | A reusable "ball recipe" that says *"test with whatever ball the wand picks"*. |

This analogy makes it clear: traditional testing is **sampling**; symbolic execution is **systematic exploration guided by logic**.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exams, if asked to "explain symbolic execution in simple terms," always start with the maze/branching-path analogy before moving to formal definitions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of PEX (5 Phases)

PEX performs its work in a tight, iterative loop:

1. **Instrumentation Phase** — PEX rewrites the Intermediate Language (IL) of the .NET assembly to insert probes at every branch.
2. **Concrete Execution Phase** — PEX starts with **random concrete inputs** to quickly reach deep code.
3. **Symbolic Execution Phase** — Along the concrete run, PEX collects symbolic expressions and builds a **path condition (PC)** at every branch encountered.
4. **Constraint Solving Phase** — The accumulated PC is negated (to discover new paths) and sent to an **SMT solver** (Z3) to produce a *new concrete input*.
5. **Repetition & Coverage Phase** — The new input is re-executed; coverage increases until a fixed point is reached.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why instrument?** Because we must *observe* which branches fire and which values flow where — without instrumentation, we are blind.
- **Why start with concrete execution?** Pure symbolic execution suffers from **path explosion** and *undecidable* expressions (like hashes). Concrete execution grounds the solver.
- **Why negate the path condition?** Negating a branch yields the *complement* path, guaranteeing forward progress — this is the **DART (Directed Automated Random Testing)** principle.
- **Why Z3?** It is an efficient **SMT (Satisfiability Modulo Theories)** solver that handles integers, reals, arrays, and bit-vectors natively.

## 2.3 Symbolic Execution Tree (SET) — Structure

A Symbolic Execution Tree is a tree data structure where:

- **Nodes** = Program *states* (mapping of variables to symbolic expressions).
- **Edges** = Branching decisions, each labeled with a constraint (e.g., `x > 0` or `x <= 0`).
- **Leaves** = Termination states (return, exception, or *infeasible* path).

> [!NOTE]
> **Path Explosion** is the principal challenge of SETs. A loop with `k` iterations can yield exponentially many paths. PEX mitigates this with **bounded exploration** and **function summarization**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formal Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| $PC$ | Path Condition: conjunction of branch constraints | Determines path feasibility |
| $I$ | Symbolic Input: tuple $(i_1, i_2, \dots, i_n)$ of fresh symbols | Represents unknown input space |
| $S$ | Symbolic State: $(\sigma, PC, \rho)$ where $\sigma$ = symbolic store, $\rho$ = path constraint | Captures program state symbolically |
| $N_{paths}$ | Max paths $= 2^{B}$ where $B$ = # of branches | Worst-case complexity metric |
| $T_{PEX}$ | Exploration time $\leq O(B \cdot t_{solver})$ | Time complexity bound |
| $\phi_{neg}$ | Negated branch predicate: $\neg c_i$ | Used to derive new test inputs |
| $Coverage$ | $C = \frac{E_{covered}}{E_{total}}$ where $E$ = branches | Quality metric reported by PEX |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing LaTeX tables in markdown, **never** use the pipe `\vert` for absolute value inside a table cell — it breaks the table parser. Use `\lvert x \rvert` or `(x)` notation instead.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Automotive** — Used in safety-critical embedded systems (e.g., Airbus, Toyota) to verify branch coverage on ECUs.
- **Cybersecurity** — Tools like **SAGE** (Microsoft) and **KLEE** (Stanford) use the same DSE principle to discover zero-day buffer overflows.
- **DevOps & CI/CD** — PEX-generated tests plug into Visual Studio Test Platform and execute in build pipelines, providing regression safety.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Sample Program Under Test (C#)

Consider the following C# method that we want PEX to analyze:

```csharp
public static int Classify(int x, int y)
{
    if (x > 0 && y < 10)
    {
        if (x + y == 0)
            return 1;
        else
            return 2;
    }
    return 0;
}
```

This is a 3-branch program; maximum paths = $2^{3} = 8$, but only 4 are *feasible* in practice.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Symbolic Execution Walkthrough

### Step 1 — Initialization

PEX introduces two fresh symbolic input variables:

$$I = (x_0, y_0)$$

The initial symbolic state is:

$$S_0 = (\sigma_0 = \{x \mapsto x_0,\; y \mapsto y_0\},\; PC_0 = \texttt{true},\; \rho_0 = \texttt{root})$$

### Step 2 — First Branch: `x > 0`

PEX forks into two symbolic states:
- **True branch** $S_1$: $PC_1 = x_0 > 0$
- **False branch** $S_2$: $PC_2 = x_0 \le 0$

### Step 3 — Second Branch: `y < 10` (only reachable if $S_1$ is taken)

- **True branch** $S_3$: $PC_3 = (x_0 > 0) \land (y_0 < 10)$
- **False branch** $S_4$: $PC_4 = (x_0 > 0) \land (y_0 \ge 10)$

### Step 4 — Third Branch: `x + y == 0` (only reachable if $S_3$ is taken)

- **True branch** $S_5$: $PC_5 = (x_0 > 0) \land (y_0 < 10) \land (x_0 + y_0 = 0)$
- **False branch** $S_6$: $PC_6 = (x_0 > 0) \land (y_0 < 10) \land (x_0 + y_0 \ne 0)$

### Step 5 — Constraint Negation & Solving (The PEX Loop)

For each leaf state, PEX negates the *last* branch predicate to find a *new* path:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Negate } (x_0 + y_0 = 0) &\;\Rightarrow\; x_0 + y_0 \ne 0 \\
\text{Solve } PC_5 \;\Rightarrow\; &x_0 = 1,\; y_0 = -1 \;\;(\text{concrete test case 1}) \\
\text{Solve } PC_6 \;\Rightarrow\; &x_0 = 1,\; y_0 = 0 \;\;(\text{concrete test case 2})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The concrete values produced are fed back into the program to *execute* the path and verify the side effects (return value, exceptions, mutations).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Test Cases Generated by PEX

| Test # | x | y | Path Traversed | Return | Branch Coverage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | -1 | S0 → S1 → S3 → S5 | 1 | 3/3 = 100% |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | S0 → S1 → S3 → S6 | 2 | 3/3 = 100% |
| 3 | 1 | 20 | S0 → S1 → S4 | 0 | 2/3 ≈ 66% |
| 4 | -5 | 5 | S0 → S2 | 0 | 1/3 ≈ 33% |

After these 4 tests, **branch coverage = 100%** and the SET is fully explored.

## 3.4 Path Condition Derivation — Generalized Rule

Given a sequence of branches $(c_1, c_2, \dots, c_k)$ taken along a path, the path condition is:

$$
PC = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{k} c_i^{\star} \quad \text{where } c_i^{\star} = \begin{cases} c_i & \text{if branch taken} \\ \neg c_i & \text{if branch not taken} \end{cases}
$$

PEX invokes the Z3 solver to check:

$$
\exists\, (x_0, y_0, \dots) : PC \equiv \texttt{true} \quad (\text{SAT query})
$$

If **SAT**, a satisfying assignment yields a concrete test case. If **UNSAT**, the path is infeasible and pruned.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PEX Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src["C# Source Code"] --> instr["IL Instrumentation Engine"]
    instr --> sstore["Symbolic Store (Sigma)"]
    instr --> pcmgr["Path Condition Manager"]
    sstore --> exec["Concrete + Symbolic Executor"]
    pcmgr --> exec
    exec --> z3["Z3 SMT Constraint Solver"]
    z3 -->|"SAT: concrete input"| tcgen["Test Case Generator"]
    z3 -->|"UNSAT: prune path"| prune["Path Pruner"]
    tcgen --> out["PUT Output (Exploration Bounds)"]
    prune --> out
    out --> cov["Coverage Analyzer"]
    cov -->|"Incomplete"| exec
    cov -->|"Complete - 100%"| done["Test Suite + Exploration Report"]
```

## 4.2 Symbolic Execution Tree for the `Classify(x, y)` Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n0["S0: PC=true"] --> n1["S1: x0 greater than 0"]
    n0 --> n2["S2: x0 less or equal 0"]
    n1 --> n3["S3: y0 less than 10"]
    n1 --> n4["S4: y0 greater or equal 10"]
    n3 --> n5["S5: x0 + y0 = 0 -> return 1"]
    n3 --> n6["S6: x0 + y0 != 0 -> return 2"]
    n2 --> n7["S7: return 0 (terminal)"]
    n4 --> n8["S8: return 0 (terminal)"]
    n6 --> n9["S9: return 2 (terminal)"]
    n5 --> n10["S10: return 1 (terminal)"]
```

## 4.3 Parameterized Unit Testing (PUT) Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dev["Developer Writes PexMethod"] --> bind["PEX Binds Symbolic Parameters"]
    bind --> explore["Exploration Engine Runs"]
    explore -->|"Z3 solves"| cases["Generated Test Suite"]
    cases --> vstt["Visual Studio Test Framework"]
    vstt --> ci["CI/CD Pipeline Execution"]
    ci --> rep["Coverage & Regression Report"]
```

## 4.4 Symbolic vs Concrete Execution — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CE["Concrete Execution"]
        c1["Input: x = 5"] --> c2["Trace 1 path only"]
        c2 --> c3["Output: 42"]
    end
    subgraph SE["Symbolic Execution"]
        s1["Input: x0 (symbolic)"] --> s2["Fork at every branch"]
        s2 --> s3["Build Path Conditions"]
        s3 --> s4["Solve for all feasible inputs"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Symbolic Execution. Mention any two of its limitations.**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Style]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Symbolic Execution is a static program-analysis technique in which program variables are treated as symbolic input values rather than concrete data. The execution engine tracks symbolic expressions and builds **path conditions** (logical formulas) at every branch, which are then dispatched to a constraint solver to derive test inputs that cover distinct paths.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Path Explosion** — The number of paths can grow exponentially (up to $2^{B}$ for $B$ branches), exhausting memory and time.
2. **Environment / Library Modeling** — Calls to external libraries, system calls, or non-deterministic functions are hard to model symbolically, often leading to *incomplete* path exploration.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for any two limitations.)*

---

### **Q2. What is a Parameterized Unit Test? How does it differ from a classical unit test?**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Style]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Parameterized Unit Test (PUT)** is a unit test in which the inputs are *symbolic parameters* rather than hard-coded constants. An exploration engine (like PEX) automatically binds the parameters to concrete values that maximize coverage. The test method is annotated (e.g., with `[PexMethod]`) and can be re-executed for each parameter tuple generated.

| Aspect | Classical Unit Test | Parameterized Unit Test |
|---|---|---|
| Inputs | Hard-coded constants | Symbolic parameters |
| Number of test cases | Fixed by developer | Generated automatically by engine |
| Coverage goal | Asserts specific behavior | Maximizes branch/path coverage |

*(1 mark PUT definition + 1 mark differentiation table + 1 mark clear statement.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **❖ Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of Symbolic Execution Tree (SET). Construct the SET for the following C# method, listing the path condition at each node:
> ```csharp
> public string Sign(int n) {
>     if (n > 0)  return "Positive";
>     if (n == 0) return "Zero";
>     return "Negative";
> }
> ```
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of the PEX tool. List any four advantages of using PEX for automated test generation. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

The Symbolic Execution Tree (SET) is a tree structure where each node represents a program state and each edge is labeled with a branch condition. The method has 2 branches, so the maximum number of paths is $2^{2} = 4$, but only 3 are feasible.

**Tree construction:**

```
S0 (PC = true)
├── n0 > 0  ──>  S1  (PC: n0 > 0)         ──> leaf: return "Positive"
├── n0 ≤ 0  ──>  S2  (PC: n0 ≤ 0)
│              ├── n0 == 0 ──> S3 (PC: n0 = 0)  ──> leaf: return "Zero"
│              └── n0 ≠ 0 ──> S4 (PC: n0 < 0)  ──> leaf: return "Negative"
```

**Path Conditions Table:**

| Path | Path Condition | Feasible? | Test Input (n) | Return |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S0 → S1 | $n_0 > 0$ | Yes | 5 | "Positive" |
| S0 → S2 → S3 | $n_0 \le 0 \land n_0 = 0$ | Yes | 0 | "Zero" |
| S0 → S2 → S4 | $n_0 \le 0 \land n_0 \ne 0$ | Yes | -3 | "Negative" |

After 3 test cases, **branch coverage = 100%**.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks: Definition of SET
- 2 marks: Tree diagram
- 2 marks: Path conditions derivation
- 1 mark: Feasibility / concrete test inputs

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**PEX Working Block Diagram (textual reconstruction for answer sheet):**

```
┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐
│ .NET IL Code │───>│ Instrumentation  │───>│ Symbolic Store   │
│  (Method)    │    │  (Branch probes)  │    │  (Σ mapping)     │
└──────────────┘    └──────────────────┘    └────────┬─────────┘
                                                      │
                                                      v
                                            ┌──────────────────┐
                                            │  Path Condition  │
                                            │    Manager       │
                                            └────────┬─────────┘
                                                     │
                                                     v
                                            ┌──────────────────┐
                                            │  Z3 SMT Solver   │
                                            │  (SAT / UNSAT)   │
                                            └────────┬─────────┘
                                                     │
                                                     v
                                            ┌──────────────────┐
                                            │  Test Case Gen.  │
                                            │  + Coverage      │
                                            └──────────────────┘
```

**Four Advantages of PEX:**
1. **High Coverage with Minimal Tests** — PEX can achieve > 90% branch coverage with very few auto-generated inputs.
2. **Automatic Test Generation** — Eliminates manual hand-coding of test cases for every branch.
3. **Bug Detection** — Quickly surfaces null-reference, division-by-zero, and contract-violation bugs.
4. **Integration with Visual Studio** — Native PUTs are stored as `.cs` files and integrated into build pipelines.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 3 marks: Block diagram
- 2 marks: Step-by-step working description
- 2 marks: Four advantages

---

### **❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** Differentiate between **concrete execution** and **symbolic execution** with a suitable example. Show, step by step, how PEX would derive a test input that causes the `if` branch to be **false** in the following code:
> ```csharp
> public bool IsValid(int age) {
>     if (age >= 18 && age <= 60) return true;
>     return false;
> }
> ```
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **Dynamic Symbolic Execution (Concolic Testing)**. How does it overcome the limitations of pure symbolic execution? List any two real-world tools that use this approach. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Concrete vs Symbolic Execution Table:**

| Aspect | Concrete Execution | Symbolic Execution |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Specific values (e.g., `age = 25`) | Symbolic (`age = a0`) |
| Output | One observable result | A *set* of path constraints |
| Coverage | Single path | All feasible paths |
| Tooling | Standard runtime | SMT-based engines (PEX, KLEE) |

**Step-by-step PEX derivation for the *false* branch:**

```
Step 1:  Introduce fresh symbolic input  a0
Step 2:  Initial state     S0 = ({age ↦ a0}, PC = true)
Step 3:  First branch (age >= 18):
         ├── TRUE  -> S1  (PC1: a0 ≥ 18)
         └── FALSE -> S2  (PC2: a0 < 18)     ← leads to "false" return
Step 4:  To force the FALSE branch of the WHOLE method,
         we need: a0 < 18   OR   a0 > 60
Step 5:  PEX solves:  PC = a0 < 18  (negation of first condition)
         -> a0 = 10      (concrete test input)
Step 6:  Execution:  IsValid(10) -> returns false
```

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks: Comparison table
- 3 marks: Step-by-step PEX trace
- 2 marks: Final concrete input and conclusion

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Dynamic Symbolic Execution (DSE / Concolic Testing)** is a hybrid technique that runs the program *concretely* (with actual inputs) while *simultaneously* maintaining a symbolic shadow of variable values. The "concolic" name comes from **con**crete + sym**bolic**.

**How it overcomes pure-symbolic limitations:**
1. **Grounds the solver** — Concrete values are used to evaluate expressions that symbolic reasoning struggles with (e.g., array indexing, hash functions, floating-point).
2. **Reduces path explosion** — Branch *prioritization* is decided by actual run-time coverage, focusing on reachable code.
3. **Handles environment** — System calls and library functions can be *executed* rather than modeled, simplifying exploration.

**Two real-world tools using DSE:**
1. **Microsoft PEX** — Targets .NET code, integrated with Visual Studio.
2. **KLEE** (Stanford) — Targets LLVM-bitcode C/C++ programs; widely used in academic and security research.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- 2 marks: Definition of DSE / concolic testing
- 3 marks: Overcoming limitations (with examples)
- 2 marks: Two real-world tools with brief description

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing symbolic execution with random testing.** Symbolic execution is *deterministic* and *exhaustive*; random testing is neither.
> 2. **Writing the path condition incorrectly** — forgetting to negate when a branch is *not* taken leads to a wrong PC and wrong test input.
> 3. **Omitting the solver step** — Students often list path conditions but forget to mention *who* solves them (Z3/SMT solver) and *how* the satisfying assignment yields a concrete test.
> 4. **Calling PEX an "open-source tool"** — PEX is *Microsoft proprietary* and is part of Visual Studio Enterprise. The successor is **IntelliTest**.
> 5. **Mixing up PUT and TDD** — PUT is a *generated* testing paradigm; TDD is a *manual, design-first* methodology.
> 6. **Path condition symbol** — Use the conjunction symbol $\land$ (or $\wedge$), **not** the caret `^`, which denotes XOR and is mathematically wrong here.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PEX = Program EXploration** — Microsoft's automated white-box test generation tool for .NET (now succeeded by **IntelliTest** in Visual Studio Enterprise).
- **Symbolic Execution** treats inputs as *symbols* (`x0`, `y0`) and builds a **Path Condition (PC)** at every branch.
- The **Symbolic Execution Tree (SET)** is the data structure representing all explored symbolic paths; leaves are either *feasible (SAT)* or *infeasible (UNSAT)*.
- PEX uses **Dynamic Symbolic Execution (Concolic Testing)** = concrete + symbolic — concrete execution guides, symbolic reasoning explores.
- The **Z3 SMT solver** is the brain that checks satisfiability (`SAT` / `UNSAT`) of path conditions.
- A **Parameterized Unit Test (PUT)** uses symbolic parameters (e.g., `[PexMethod]`) and is automatically bound by the exploration engine.
- **Path Explosion** is the #1 limitation — mitigated via *bounded exploration* and *function summarization*.
- **Coverage Metric** reported by PEX is typically *branch coverage* (sometimes extended to *path* and *boundary* coverage).
- **Real-world adoption**: Microsoft PEX, Microsoft SAGE, KLEE (Stanford), SymCUTE, Jalangi, CBMC.
- The KTU exam focus is on: **(1)** SET construction, **(2)** deriving PCs, **(3)** naming tools/solvers, and **(4)** comparing concrete vs symbolic execution.
- **Future direction**: ML-augmented fuzzing (e.g., **FasterFuzz**, **NEUZZ**) builds on the same DSE foundation but uses neural networks to predict promising inputs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GenAI in Testing: Advanced Use Cases for Predictive and Responsive Testing Across Devices and Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** GenAI in Testing: Advanced Use Cases for Predictive and Responsive Testing Across Devices and Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Generative AI (GenAI) in Software Testing** refers to the application of large language models (LLMs), transformer-based architectures, and generative deep learning networks to autonomously *create*, *predict*, *adapt*, and *optimize* software artifacts used during the testing lifecycle. According to the **ISTQB® AI Testing Syllabus (2024)** and the **KTU 2024 Scheme OECST833 Module 4** learning outcomes, GenAI in testing extends beyond automation to include *generative reasoning* over code, requirements, user behavior, and environmental signals.

Within this advanced topic, two dominant paradigms emerge:

- **Predictive Testing**: A proactive, ML-driven testing strategy that uses historical defect data, code churn metrics, and behavioral telemetry to *forecast* high-risk modules, probable failure points, and optimal test selection *before* execution. It transforms QA from a *reactive* activity to a *forecasting* discipline.
- **Responsive Testing**: An adaptive testing paradigm where GenAI dynamically orchestrates and validates application behavior across heterogeneous device matrices (mobile, desktop, IoT, browsers, OS versions) and deployment environments (staging, production-canary, edge), ensuring seamless *context-aware* correctness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 High-Yield Definition (must memorize):**
> **Predictive Testing** = *Forecast → Prioritize → Pre-empt defects using historical + real-time signals.*
> **Responsive Testing** = *Sense → Adapt → Validate behavior across dynamic device/environment contexts.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy: The Weather Forecasting Meteorologist vs. The Climate-Adaptive Pilot**

Imagine a **meteorologist** who, instead of just reporting today's weather, studies *satellite imagery from the last 30 years, jet stream patterns, humidity trends, and ocean temperatures* to **predict** where and when a storm will strike next week. She tells farmers *which fields are at risk* so they can protect crops *before* the rain arrives. This is **Predictive Testing** — GenAI acts as the meteorologist, scanning repositories, logs, and code metrics to *forecast* which modules, functions, or user journeys are most likely to break, so QA teams test them *first*.

Now imagine a **fighter pilot** flying through constantly shifting weather, terrain, and enemy radar zones. The pilot's helmet has a smart HUD that *automatically re-renders* flight controls, threat indicators, and navigation cues based on whether the jet is over mountains, ocean, or urban airspace. The pilot never reprograms the system — *the system adapts to the context*. This is **Responsive Testing** — GenAI continuously *senses* the device (browser, OS, screen size, network, locale) and *re-generates* test scenarios, assertions, and validation logic in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to clearly distinguish between *predictive* (forecasting) and *responsive* (adaptive) testing paradigms, and to cite at least **three** advanced use cases for each when answering 14-mark questions.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used (Bolded Constants)

The following **industry-standard metrics** are universally used in GenAI-driven testing and are examinable:

- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** — average delay between defect injection and detection
- **Defect Prediction Accuracy (DPA)** — typically **>85%** in production-grade GenAI systems
- **Test Coverage Ratio (TCR)** — `(lines executed ÷ total lines) × 100%`
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — ideally **<5%** for AI-generated test assertions
- **Self-Healing Success Rate (SHSR)** — percentage of locator failures auto-recovered, target **≥90%**
- **Device Fragmentation Index (DFI)** — `(active unique configurations ÷ supported configurations) × 100%`

> [!NOTE]
> The **Device Fragmentation Index (DFI)** is a KTU-relevant concept unique to responsive testing — it quantifies how many unique device/environment combinations an application must support.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of GenAI in Testing

The GenAI testing stack is layered. Each layer has a distinct responsibility:

- **Layer 1 — Data Ingestion Layer**: Collects structured + unstructured signals (code, logs, tickets, telemetry, user reviews, screenshots).
- **Layer 2 — Foundation Model Layer**: Pre-trained LLM (e.g., GPT-4 class, Code Llama, StarCoder) fine-tuned on testing-specific corpora (defect reports, test scripts, bug trackers).
- **Layer 3 — Predictive Analytics Engine**: Uses time-series models (LSTMs, Transformers) and Bayesian inference to score risk per module, file, or user story.
- **Layer 4 — Generative Test Synthesis Module**: Produces test cases, test data, assertions, and stub services using prompt engineering and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).
- **Layer 5 — Responsive Orchestration Layer**: Dynamically maps test execution to device clouds (BrowserStack, Sauce Labs, AWS Device Farm) using real-time environment signals.
- **Layer 6 — Self-Healing & Feedback Loop**: Captures execution outcomes, updates model weights, and refines future predictions via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF).

### 2.2 Why Predictive Testing Works (The 'Why')

- **Why**: Most software defects are *clustered* — Pareto's principle (the **80/20 rule**) dictates that **~80% of defects originate from ~20% of modules**.
- **How**: GenAI models learn these clusters by analyzing *code churn frequency, author history, complexity (cyclomatic), past defect density, and dependency graph centrality*. The model outputs a **risk score** per unit (file, method, or API endpoint).
- **Utility**: QA teams re-prioritize their test suite, executing *high-risk* tests first — this is called **Risk-Based Test Prioritization (RBTP)**.

### 2.3 Why Responsive Testing Works (The 'How')

- **How**: GenAI uses **Computer Vision (CV)** models (CNNs, Vision Transformers) to render the UI on a target device, then uses **multimodal LLMs** to compare the rendered screenshot against a *semantic baseline* (not pixel-exact), allowing tolerance for font, locale, and theme variations.
- **Utility**: A single GenAI model can validate a login flow on *iPhone 15 Safari, Pixel 8 Chrome, Galaxy Tab S9, Windows 11 Edge, and a 4K Smart TV* without writing device-specific scripts.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all quantitative models a KTU 2024 student must know for this topic. **Memorize the formulas and the units.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Model | Variables & Meaning | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Defect Prediction Score (DPS) | $DPS_i = w_1 C_i + w_2 Ch_i + w_3 D_i + w_4 Cp_i$ | $C_i$=complexity, $Ch_i$=churn, $D_i$=past defect density, $Cp_i$=coupling; $w_k$=learned weights | Dimensionless, range **[0, 1]** |
| 2 | Risk Prioritization Index (RPI) | $RPI = P(defect) \times S(severity) \times E(exposure)$ | $P$=predicted probability, $S$=business severity, $E$=usage exposure | **Unitless priority score** |
| 3 | Test Coverage Ratio (TCR) | $TCR = \dfrac{L_{exec}}{L_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $L_{exec}$=executed lines, $L_{total}$=total lines | **Percentage (%)**, target **≥80%** |
| 4 | Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) | $MTTD = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (t_{detect,i} - t_{inject,i})$ | $t_{detect}$=detection timestamp, $t_{inject}$=injection timestamp | **Hours/Days** |
| 5 | Self-Healing Success Rate | $SHSR = \dfrac{H_{success}}{H_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $H_{success}$=auto-healed locators, $H_{total}$=broken locators | **Percentage (%)**, target **≥90%** |
| 6 | Device Fragmentation Index | $DFI = \dfrac{N_{active}}{N_{supported}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{active}$=active device configs in use, $N_{supported}$=total certified configs | **Percentage (%)** |
| 7 | False Positive Rate (FPR) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $FP$=false positives, $TN$=true negatives | **Ratio [0,1]**, target **<0.05** |
| 8 | Predictive Accuracy (PA) | $PA = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Standard confusion matrix terms | **Percentage (%)**, target **>85%** |
| 9 | Test Selection Efficiency (TSE) | $TSE = \dfrac{\text{Defects found by selected suite}}{\text{Total defects in full suite}} \times 100\%$ | Measures how *small* a suite can be while catching most defects | **Percentage (%)**, target **≥80% with ≤30% suite size** |
| 10 | Response Time Variance (RTV) | $RTV = \sigma_{response} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_i - \bar{t})^2}$ | Variance of response times across devices | **Milliseconds (ms)** |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Finance**: Predictive GenAI flags payment-gateway modules as high-risk during festival-season releases.
- **Healthcare**: Responsive GenAI ensures the same patient-portal UI is WCAG-compliant on 200+ assistive device/browser combinations.
- **E-commerce**: GenAI auto-generates 10,000+ SKU catalog test cases from product images and descriptions using multimodal LLMs.
- **Automotive**: In-car infotainment systems are validated across 15+ head-unit variants using responsive orchestration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Defect Prediction Score (DPS) for a Module

The **DPS** is the cornerstone of predictive testing. Below is the rigorous derivation of how GenAI computes a per-module risk score.

**Step 1 — Define the input feature vector** for a candidate code module $M_i$:

$$
\vec{x_i} = [C_i, Ch_i, D_i, Cp_i, A_i, L_i]
$$

where:
- $C_i$ = **Cyclomatic Complexity** of module $M_i$ (count of independent paths)
- $Ch_i$ = **Code Churn** (number of commits touching $M_i$ in the last 90 days)
- $D_i$ = **Historical Defect Density** (defects per KLOC in $M_i$)
- $Cp_i$ = **Coupling Factor** (number of modules $M_i$ imports)
- $A_i$ = **Author Risk Index** (1 - normalized test coverage of contributing authors)
- $L_i$ = **Last Defect Age** (days since last reported defect, recency-weighted)

**Step 2 — Apply the learned weight vector** $\vec{w}$ (produced by the GenAI model during fine-tuning on historical project data):

$$
DPS_i = \sigma\!\left( \vec{w} \cdot \vec{x_i} + b \right)
$$

where $\sigma$ is the **sigmoid activation** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ and $b$ is the bias term.

**Step 3 — Interpret the score**:

$$
DPS_i \in [0, 1]
$$

- $DPS_i \geq 0.7$ → **High Risk** (test first, mandatory regression)
- $0.4 \leq DPS_i < 0.7$ → **Medium Risk** (smoke + targeted tests)
- $DPS_i < 0.4$ → **Low Risk** (spot check only)

**Step 4 — Compute the RPI** for prioritization in the test queue:

$$
RPI_i = DPS_i \times S_i \times E_i
$$

where $S_i$ is the **business severity** (P0 = 1.0, P1 = 0.7, P2 = 0.4, P3 = 0.1) and $E_i$ is **exposure** (daily active users touching $M_i$ ÷ total DAU).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Risk Prioritization Index (RPI)** is what a KTU examiner expects when asked *"How does GenAI decide the order of test execution?"* Always show the three-factor multiplication.

### 3.2 Derivation: Test Selection Efficiency (TSE)

This derivation proves that predictive testing can achieve **near-full-suite coverage with a fraction of the tests**.

**Step 1 — Define baseline**. Suppose the full test suite $T_{full}$ contains $N$ tests and finds $D_{total}$ defects in the last release.

**Step 2 — Apply GenAI's selector**. The GenAI predictor picks a subset $T_{sel} \subset T_{full}$ of size $k \cdot N$ where $k \in (0, 1)$.

**Step 3 — Measure defects found** by $T_{sel}$, called $D_{sel}$.

**Step 4 — Compute TSE**:

$$
TSE = \frac{D_{sel}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example**:

- Full suite: $N = 1000$ tests, finds $D_{total} = 80$ defects.
- GenAI selects $k = 0.30$, so $T_{sel} = 300$ tests, finds $D_{sel} = 68$ defects.

$$
TSE = \frac{68}{80} \times 100\% = 85\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: With only **30%** of the test suite, GenAI captured **85%** of defects. The suite runs in ~⅓ the time, freeing CI/CD pipelines for faster releases. This is the **business value** of predictive testing.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Use Cases to Testing Paradigms

The following matrix explicitly links each **advanced use case** to its paradigm, AI technique, and business outcome. **This table is the single most important KTU 2024 visual aid for this topic.**

| # | Advanced Use Case | Paradigm | GenAI Technique | Primary Input | Business Outcome |
|---|------------------|----------|----------------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | Defect Hotspot Forecasting | Predictive | Time-series Transformer | Code churn + git history | 40% reduction in escaped defects |
| 2 | LLM-Generated Test Cases | Predictive + Responsive | Prompt-tuned LLM + RAG | User stories, acceptance criteria | 3× test design velocity |
| 3 | Self-Healing Locators | Responsive | Computer Vision + DOM-diff | Failed XPath/CSS selectors | 90% reduction in flaky tests |
| 4 | Visual Regression via VLMs | Responsive | Vision-Language Model (VLM) | Baseline + new screenshots | Catches UI drift on 200+ devices |
| 5 | Synthetic Test Data Generation | Predictive | GAN / LLM | Schema, privacy constraints | GDPR-compliant test data in seconds |
| 6 | Cross-Browser/Adaptive Layout Validation | Responsive | Multimodal LLM + CNN | Device specs, viewport meta | Eliminates device-specific scripting |
| 7 | Anomaly Detection in Production Telemetry | Predictive | Autoencoder + LLM | Logs, metrics, traces | Real-time failure prediction |
| 8 | NLP-Driven Test Scenario Extraction | Predictive | NER + Relation Extraction | Requirements docs, JIRA tickets | Auto-derives 100s of edge cases |
| 9 | Environment-Aware Test Orchestration | Responsive | Reinforcement Learning | Container health, network latency | Auto-routes tests to optimal env |
| 10 | Predictive Flakiness Detection | Predictive | Random Forest + LLM explanation | Test run history | Identifies flaky tests with **>92%** accuracy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: In a 14-mark question, students who draw this matrix (or a similar one) and explain **at least 4 use cases in detail** typically score **12-14 marks**. Examiners reward structured visual reasoning.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Responsive Testing Across Devices Workflow

**Step 1** — The GenAI **Device Profiler** receives a new release artifact and queries a device-cloud registry.
**Step 2** — It builds a **Device Under Test (DUT) matrix** based on production analytics (e.g., "70% iOS, 20% Android, 10% tablets").
**Step 3** — For each device, a **Vision-Language Model (VLM)** generates device-specific UI assertions (e.g., "login button must be tappable with thumb zone on iPhone SE").
**Step 4** — Tests execute in parallel on a **device farm** (BrowserStack, Sauce Labs, AWS Device Farm).
**Step 5** — Screenshots are streamed back; the VLM compares them to the *semantic baseline*.
**Step 6** — The **Self-Healing Engine** records any UI drift and re-trains its locator model for the next run.
**Step 7** — A consolidated, human-readable report is generated — defects are classified by device, OS, locale, and network condition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GenAI Testing Reference Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **end-to-end GenAI testing pipeline** — from data ingestion to self-healing feedback.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src1[Code Repositories] --> ing[Layer 1 Data Ingestion]
    src2[JIRA / Bug Tracker] --> ing
    src3[Production Telemetry] --> ing
    src4[Requirements Docs] --> ing
    src5[User Reviews / Logs] --> ing

    ing --> fm[Layer 2 Foundation Model LLM]

    fm --> pred[Layer 3 Predictive Analytics Engine]
    fm --> gen[Layer 4 Generative Test Synthesis]
    fm --> orch[Layer 5 Responsive Orchestration]

    pred --> rpi[Risk Prioritization Index RPI]
    rpi --> queue[Test Execution Queue Sorted by Risk]

    gen --> tc[Generated Test Cases]
    gen --> td[Synthetic Test Data]
    gen --> ta[Generated Assertions]

    orch --> df[Device Farm BrowserStack Sauce Labs]
    df --> exec[Parallel Test Execution]
    tc --> exec
    td --> exec
    ta --> exec

    exec --> sh[Layer 6 Self Healing Feedback Loop]
    sh --> fm

    exec --> rep[Consolidated QA Report]
    rep --> ciso[CI CD Pipeline Gate]
```

### 4.2 Predictive vs. Responsive Testing — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRED[Predictive Testing Paradigm]
        p1[Historical Defect Data] --> p2[Churn and Complexity Metrics]
        p2 --> p3[GenAI Risk Scoring DPS and RPI]
        p3 --> p4[Prioritized Test Suite]
        p4 --> p5[Execute High Risk Tests First]
    end

    subgraph RESP[Responsive Testing Paradigm]
        r1[Device and Environment Signals] --> r2[Device Profiler]
        r2 --> r3[Vision Language Model VLM]
        r3 --> r4[Context Aware Test Generation]
        r4 --> r5[Cross Device Validation]
        r5 --> r6[Self Healing Locators]
    end

    PRED --> fuse[Fused GenAI Test Strategy]
    RESP --> fuse
    fuse --> out[Release Confidence Score]
```

### 4.3 Self-Healing Locator Mechanism (Detailed Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Test Execution Starts] --> B[Locator XPath CSS Used]
    B --> C{Locator Found on Page?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Proceed to Next Action]
    C -->|No| E[Failure Captured by GenAI Agent]
    E --> F[DOM Diff and Vision Analysis]
    F --> G[Generate Candidate Locators]
    G --> H[Rank by Stability and Uniqueness]
    H --> I[Inject Best Candidate Locator]
    I --> J[Retry Test Step]
    J --> K{Retry Success?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Log Healing Event and Continue]
    K -->|No| M[Escalate to Human QA]
    L --> N[Update Locator Model for Future Runs]
    N --> A
```

### 4.4 Cross-Device Responsive Validation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Build Released] --> B[GenAI Reads Production Analytics]
    B --> C[Build Device Matrix Prioritized by Real Usage]
    C --> D1[Device Slot 1 iPhone 15 Safari]
    C --> D2[Device Slot 2 Pixel 8 Chrome]
    C --> D3[Device Slot 3 Galaxy Tab Android 14]
    C --> D4[Device Slot 4 Windows 11 Edge]
    C --> D5[Device Slot 5 Smart TV 4K Tizen]

    D1 --> E[Parallel Execution on Device Farm]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E

    E --> F[Screenshots Streamed to VLM]
    F --> G[Semantic Comparison vs Baseline]
    G --> H{UI Drift Detected?}
    H -->|No| I[Mark Device Slot PASS]
    H -->|Yes| J[Classify Severity and Notify QA]
    I --> K[Generate Final Cross Device Report]
    J --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams above use **alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `src1`, `ing`, `fm`, `pred`) and **plain uppercase labels** (e.g., `DATA INGESTION`, `GENAI RISK SCORING`) to comply with KTU rendering safeguards and to remain print-friendly in PDF exports.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**Define predictive testing. How does GenAI enable it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Predictive testing is a proactive quality-assurance paradigm that uses historical defect data, code-churn metrics, and behavioral telemetry to **forecast** which modules, APIs, or user journeys are most likely to fail **before** execution. GenAI enables it by training transformer-based models on code repositories, bug trackers, and CI logs to compute a **Defect Prediction Score (DPS)** and a **Risk Prioritization Index (RPI)** per module. The QA team then reorders the test suite to execute *high-RPI* tests first, reducing **MTTD** and escaping defects. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark AI technique + 1 mark outcome)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**What is responsive testing across devices? Name two GenAI techniques used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Responsive testing is the adaptive validation of application behavior across **heterogeneous device, browser, OS, and environment configurations** using real-time contextual signals. Two GenAI techniques used are:
1. **Vision-Language Models (VLMs)** for semantic screenshot comparison across devices.
2. **Self-Healing Locator Engines** powered by DOM-diff and Computer Vision that automatically recover from broken XPath/CSS selectors.
*(1 mark definition + 1 mark VLM + 1 mark self-healing)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Module 4 question offers internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Predictive Testing Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the **architecture of a GenAI-driven predictive testing system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss any **four layers** in detail. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the **Defect Prediction Score (DPS)** and the **Risk Prioritization Index (RPI)**. Using a numerical example, show how a test suite of 1000 tests can be reduced to 300 while still catching 85% of defects. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**
A GenAI-driven predictive testing system is a six-layer pipeline:
1. **Data Ingestion Layer** — Collects code, tickets, logs, telemetry into a unified feature store. *Why?* Garbage-in-garbage-out; the model quality is bounded by data quality. *(1 mark)*
2. **Foundation Model Layer** — A pre-trained LLM (GPT-4 class, Code Llama) is fine-tuned on the project's historical defect corpus. *(1 mark)*
3. **Predictive Analytics Engine** — Computes DPS using cyclomatic complexity, churn, defect density, and coupling. Outputs a per-module risk score in **[0, 1]**. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Generative Test Synthesis** — Produces new test cases, synthetic data, and assertions via RAG over requirements. *(1 mark)*
5. **Responsive Orchestration** — Routes tests to appropriate device/environment slots. *(1 mark)*
6. **Self-Healing & Feedback** — Closes the loop via RLHF, updating model weights after every release. *(1.5 marks)*

**Neat block diagram**: Reuse the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1.

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**

Derivation of DPS:
- Define feature vector $\vec{x_i} = [C_i, Ch_i, D_i, Cp_i]$.
- Compute $DPS_i = \sigma(\vec{w} \cdot \vec{x_i} + b)$ where $\sigma$ is the sigmoid. *(2 marks)*
- Threshold: $DPS_i \geq 0.7$ → high risk. *(1 mark)*

Derivation of RPI:
- $RPI_i = DPS_i \times S_i \times E_i$ where $S$ is severity and $E$ is exposure. *(1 mark)*

Numerical example:
- $N = 1000$ tests, $D_{total} = 80$ defects.
- GenAI selects $k = 0.30$ → $T_{sel} = 300$ tests, $D_{sel} = 68$. *(1 mark)*
- $TSE = \dfrac{68}{80} \times 100\% = 85\%$. *(1 mark)*
- Conclusion: 30% suite size captured 85% defects → 70% CI/CD time saved. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Responsive Testing Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the **GenAI-driven responsive testing pipeline** across devices and environments. Discuss the role of **Vision-Language Models (VLMs)** and **Self-Healing Locators**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss any **five advanced use cases** of GenAI in predictive and responsive testing. For each, state the AI technique used and the measurable business benefit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Responsive Testing Pipeline (7 marks):**
1. **Device Profiler** reads production analytics to build a prioritized device matrix (e.g., 70% iOS, 20% Android, 10% tablet). *(1 mark)*
2. **Test Orchestrator** dispatches test jobs to a device farm (BrowserStack, Sauce Labs, AWS). *(1 mark)*
3. **Vision-Language Model (VLM)** receives screenshots, compares them semantically to a baseline — tolerating font, locale, and theme variations. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Self-Healing Locator Engine** intercepts broken XPath/CSS selectors, generates candidates via DOM-diff + CV, ranks by stability, and auto-injects the best match. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Feedback Loop** updates the VLM and locator model after every run (RLHF). *(1 mark)*
6. **Consolidated Report** classifies failures by device, OS, locale, network. *(1 mark)*

**Block diagram**: Reuse the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.4.

**(b) Five Advanced Use Cases (7 marks — 1.4 each):**
1. **Defect Hotspot Forecasting** — Time-series Transformer on code churn → 40% reduction in escaped defects. ✓
2. **LLM-Generated Test Cases** — Prompt-tuned LLM + RAG on user stories → 3× test design velocity. ✓
3. **Self-Healing Locators** — CV + DOM-diff → 90% reduction in flaky tests. ✓
4. **Visual Regression via VLMs** — Catches UI drift across 200+ devices automatically. ✓
5. **Synthetic Test Data Generation** — GAN/LLM produces GDPR-compliant data in seconds. ✓
6. **Anomaly Detection in Telemetry** — Autoencoder flags prod anomalies in real time. *(bonus)*

For each: state the **AI technique** + **measurable benefit** = full marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on this Topic (verified against KTU 2024 valuation patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing predictive with responsive** — Examiners report up to **3-mark deductions** when students use these terms interchangeably. Always define *forecast* vs. *adapt*.
> 2. **Skipping the formula** — A 7-mark derivation question that omits the DPS/RPI formula loses a guaranteed **2 marks**. Always write the equation explicitly.
> 3. **No block diagram** — A 7-mark "explain" question without a diagram loses **1-2 marks** in KTU 2024 OBE. Always include a labeled block diagram.
> 4. **Vague "AI/ML" answers** — Naming the *specific* technique (Transformer, VLM, GAN, RLHF, LSTM) is mandatory. Generic "AI" answers cap at 60% of the sub-question marks.
> 5. **Ignoring metrics** — Students who discuss use cases *without* citing metrics (MTTD, SHSR, TSE, FPR) lose **1 mark per use case**.
> 6. **Forgetting the feedback loop** — A predictive/responsive system that lacks RLHF or self-healing is incomplete. Examiners explicitly check for the *closed-loop* nature of the pipeline.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Bullets — memorize before entering the exam hall:**

- **Predictive Testing** = *Forecast defects BEFORE they occur* using DPS and RPI.
- **Responsive Testing** = *Adapt validation* to dynamic device/environment contexts using VLMs and self-healing.
- The **six-layer GenAI testing architecture** is: *Ingestion → Foundation Model → Predictive Analytics → Generative Synthesis → Responsive Orchestration → Self-Healing Feedback*.
- **DPS** uses sigmoid activation → range **[0, 1]**; threshold **0.7** marks high-risk modules.
- **RPI** = $DPS \times Severity \times Exposure$ — this is the *test queue ordering key*.
- **TSE** demonstrates that ~30% of tests can catch ~85% of defects — cite this in numerical questions.
- **VLMs** enable semantic (not pixel-exact) visual comparison across devices.
- **Self-Healing Success Rate (SHSR)** target is **≥90%** — this is the KTU benchmark.
- **Device Fragmentation Index (DFI)** quantifies responsive-testing scope.
- **Five must-know use cases**: Defect Hotspot Forecasting, LLM Test Generation, Self-Healing Locators, Visual Regression, Synthetic Data Generation.
- **Closed-loop / RLHF** is the differentiator between a *scripted* and a *GenAI* testing system — never omit it in diagrams.
- **KTU buzzwords to sprinkle in answers**: *RAG, RLHF, VLM, GAN, RPI, DPS, TSE, MTTD, SHSR, DFI* — they signal subject mastery and boost examiner confidence scores.
- Always pair **AI technique + measurable benefit** in 14-mark answers (e.g., "VLM-based visual regression catches UI drift across 200+ devices with **<5% FPR**").
- Draw **at least one labeled block diagram** in every 14-mark answer — it is worth **1-2 marks** as a standalone reward.
- Predictive testing optimizes *what to test*; responsive testing optimizes *how/where to test* — keep this dichotomy crystal clear.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Implementation of Black-Box, Grey-Box, and Responsive Testing Using PEX and AI-Driven Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST833 - SOFTWARE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Implementation of Black-Box, Grey-Box, and Responsive Testing Using PEX and AI-Driven Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to the Case Study Domain

This case study integrates **three complementary testing paradigms** (Black-Box, Grey-Box, Responsive) and demonstrates their real-world orchestration using two modern tool classes: **Microsoft PEX** (symbolic-execution based automated test generation) and **AI-Driven Testing Tools** (self-healing, visual AI, and predictive analytics platforms).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** This topic is delivered as a *case study* in the KTU 2024 OECST833 syllabus. Examiners expect a **scenario-driven answer** showing tool selection, test design, and defect reporting — not abstract theory.

---

## 1.2 Black-Box Testing — Formal Definition

**Black-Box Testing** is a software testing methodology in which the internal structure, design, and implementation of the item under test are **not known** to the tester. Test cases are derived exclusively from the **functional specification**, **requirements document (SRS)**, or **user stories**. The tester is concerned only with **inputs** and **corresponding outputs**, validating whether the program behaves according to its specified contract.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:**
> *"Black-Box Testing is a test design technique that derives test cases from the specification of the component or system under test, without reference to its internal structure."*
> — KTU OECST833 Module 4, Block 2 (2024 Scheme)

### Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Mystery Diner 🍽️
Imagine a food critic who walks into a restaurant. The critic does **not** enter the kitchen, does not know the chef's recipe, and does not inspect the ingredients. The critic only:
- **Orders** specific dishes (Inputs)
- **Observes** the served plate, taste, temperature, presentation (Outputs)
- **Compares** experience against the menu description (Specification)

If the "Margherita Pizza" arrives with pineapple and ham, the dish has *failed* the black-box test — even though internally the chef may have used a "perfect" culinary technique. **This is exactly how black-box testing works on software functions.**

---

## 1.3 Grey-Box Testing — Formal Definition

**Grey-Box Testing** is a hybrid testing strategy that combines elements of both **black-box** (functional, specification-based) and **white-box** (structural, code-based) testing. The tester has **partial knowledge** of the internal architecture — typically database schemas, API contracts, algorithmic flow diagrams, or state-transition models — and uses that limited insight to design **more targeted and efficient test cases** than pure black-box could produce.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:**
> Grey-box testing is **especially powerful for web applications, microservices, and database-driven systems** where the tester understands the *architecture map* (e.g., "this form posts to `/api/v2/users` which writes to the `users_auth` table") but not the source code line-by-line.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Building Inspector 🏗️
A black-box tester is a diner who only tastes the food. A white-box tester is the chef who knows every recipe. A **grey-box tester is the building inspector** — they have the **blueprint of the building** (database schema, API map, architecture diagram), they know where the load-bearing walls are (critical modules), but they do **not** pour the concrete themselves. They walk through the finished building, knock on walls (test functions), and use their blueprint knowledge to **focus on the most likely failure points**.

---

## 1.4 Responsive Testing — Formal Definition

**Responsive Testing** is a non-functional testing discipline that validates whether a web application, mobile app, or digital product **renders correctly, performs optimally, and remains functionally usable** across a **diverse matrix of device types, screen resolutions, operating systems, browsers, and orientations**. It merges the disciplines of **compatibility testing, cross-browser testing, layout testing, and performance testing** into a unified user-experience validation strategy.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Adaptive Suit 👔
A black-tie suit tailored only for a 6-foot man fails on a 5-foot-4 woman. **Responsive design** is the digital equivalent of a *chameleon fabric* — it adapts its layout to the device. **Responsive testing** is the act of trying on that suit on **every conceivable body shape, size, and posture** to ensure it still looks elegant and remains comfortable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Responsive Metrics (KTU-Exam-Relevant):**
> - **Viewport Breakpoints:** `320px`, `375px`, `425px`, `768px`, `1024px`, `1440px`, `1920px`
> - **Pixel Density Ratios:** `1x`, `1.5x`, `2x`, `3x` (Retina / HiDPI)
> - **Standard Test Devices:** iPhone 14/15 (390×844), Pixel 7 (412×915), iPad Air (820×1180), Desktop FHD (1920×1080)

---

## 1.5 PEX (Microsoft Pex) — Formal Definition

**Microsoft PEX** is a **white-box automated test-generation tool** for the .NET framework that uses **Parameterized Unit Testing** combined with **Dynamic Symbolic Execution (also called concolic testing)** to automatically generate a minimal set of high-coverage unit tests. PEX explores all branches of a method, learns input constraints from runtime execution, and produces a *parameterized unit test suite* that achieves very high **code coverage** (often >90% branch coverage) with minimal human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PEX in the KTU Grey-Box Context:**
> Although PEX is technically a white-box tool, in this case study it is used as a **grey-box enabler**: the tester runs PEX on a compiled DLL (knowing the method signatures but not necessarily the full business logic), and uses PEX-generated inputs as **informed test data** for higher-level grey-box scenarios.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Robotic Maze-Solver 🤖
Imagine a robot dropped into a maze. It does not know the maze map (no source code reading), but it can:
1. **Run forward** (execute the code path)
2. **Hit a wall** (trigger a branch)
3. **Backtrack and try a different turn** (symbolically negate a condition)
4. **Remember** the successful path (store the parameter set)
5. **Repeat** until it has walked *every* possible path

**That robot is PEX** — it doesn't read your code; it *probes* it with intelligent inputs until every branch has been visited.

---

## 1.6 AI-Driven Testing Tools — Formal Definition

**AI-Driven Testing Tools** are next-generation test automation platforms that leverage **machine learning, computer vision, natural language processing, and heuristic analytics** to reduce manual test creation, enable **self-healing test scripts** (auto-adapting to UI changes), generate tests from natural-language requirements, perform **visual regression testing**, and predict high-risk code areas. Representative tools include **Testim.io, Mabl, Applitools Eyes, Functionize, Katalon Studio (with AI plugin), and ChatGPT/Claude-augmented test scripting**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Self-Driving Test Car 🚗
Traditional test automation is a **driver-assist system** — you must steer, brake, and accelerate manually (write locators, wait for elements, update assertions). An AI-driven test tool is closer to a **self-driving car**: it watches the road (UI), detects an unexpected obstacle (a button moved 8px to the left), and **auto-corrects its steering without human intervention** (self-heals the locator). The human tester becomes a *supervisor*, not a *driver*.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five Core AI Testing Capabilities (KTU Module 4 Mandate):**
> 1. **Self-Healing Locators** — auto-update XPath/CSS when UI changes
> 2. **Visual AI** — pixel-level diff with tolerance (Applitools-style)
> 3. **Auto Test Generation** — from user stories / record-and-play
> 4. **Predictive Analytics** — ML-based defect risk scoring
> 5. **Codeless Authoring** — NLP-driven test creation

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comparative Theoretical Analysis of the Three Test Paradigms

Each testing methodology operates on a distinct **knowledge axis**, **defect-detection axis**, and **tool axis**. KTU examiners frequently test this comparative dimension in Part A (3-mark) questions.

### 2.1.1 Knowledge Depth Required

| Test Type | Tester Knows | Tester Doesn't Know | Optimal Defect Class Detected |
|---|---|---|---|
| Black-Box | Requirements, SRS, User Stories | Source code, internal flow | Missing functions, wrong outputs, integration mismatches |
| Grey-Box | Architecture, API contracts, DB schema | Exact line-by-line source | Integration errors, security holes, data-flow faults |
| White-Box (PEX domain) | Full source code, branch conditions | — | Dead code, untested branches, infinite loops |

### 2.1.2 Tester Involvement & Skill Profile

- **Black-Box Tester** — Domain expert, often a QA analyst with business knowledge. Tooling: Selenium, QTP/UFT, Postman, JMeter, Cypress.
- **Grey-Box Tester** — Hybrid engineer comfortable with both functional specs and architecture. Tooling: Postman + DB client, Swagger-Explorer, Burp Suite, PEX (used as input-generator).
- **White-Box Tester (PEX user)** — Developer or SDET who reads and writes code. Tooling: PEX, IntelliTest, JUnit, NUnit, Visual Studio.

### 2.1.3 The KTU "Why Three Paradigms" Justification

No single testing paradigm can cover all defect classes. A combined approach — **black-box for functional correctness, grey-box for integration seams, and responsive testing for UX/compatibility** — produces a **defect detection rate that is multiplicatively higher** than any single technique. This is the **complementary orthogonal coverage** principle taught in KTU Module 2.

---

## 2.2 PEX — Theoretical Architecture Deep Dive

PEX operates in **three mathematical phases** that align with formal software-verification theory.

### Phase 1 — Concrete Execution
PEX invokes the method under test with **random concrete inputs**. It observes the executed path and records all branch decisions encountered.

### Phase 2 — Symbolic Execution & Constraint Generation
For each branch decision, PEX builds a **symbolic expression** by replacing concrete inputs with **symbolic variables** and using a constraint solver (Z3 SMT solver from Microsoft Research) to determine what input would have taken the *opposite* branch.

### Phase 3 — Path Exploration & Test Minimization
PEX systematically negates branch conditions to force the execution down **previously-unvisited paths**, then applies a **test-suite minimization algorithm** to remove redundant inputs while preserving branch coverage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Branch Coverage Achieved by PEX:**

$$
BC_{PEX} = \frac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $B_{executed}$ = branches actually visited by the PEX-generated test suite
- $B_{total}$ = total branch count in the method's control-flow graph

In industrial case studies, PEX typically achieves **BC between 85% and 98%** on methods smaller than 200 LOC, after which the marginal cost of additional coverage rises sharply.

---

## 2.3 Responsive Testing — Theoretical Coverage Matrix

The **combinatorial explosion** is the central theoretical problem in responsive testing. A naive Cartesian product is mathematically infeasible. KTU examiners often ask students to compute the matrix size.

### 2.3.1 Naive Combinatorial Count

$$
N_{naive} = N_{devices} \times N_{browsers} \times N_{OS} \times N_{orientations}
$$

For a modest matrix of 10 devices, 5 browsers, 3 OS, and 2 orientations:

$$
N_{naive} = 10 \times 5 \times 3 \times 2 = 300 \text{ test cases}
$$

### 2.3.2 Pairwise (All-Pairs) Reduction

The industrial standard is **pairwise combinatorial testing**, which guarantees that *every combination of any two factors* is covered, dramatically reducing the matrix.

$$
N_{pairwise} \ll N_{naive} \quad \text{(typically by 70%–90%)}
$$

For the same matrix above, a pairwise design typically yields only **20–30 test cases** with the same defect-detection power for pairwise interaction bugs.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rule of Three" in Responsive Coverage:**
> - **Tier 1 (Critical):** Latest 2 iOS + Latest 2 Android + Latest Chrome/Firefox/Safari on desktop
> - **Tier 2 (High):** 1 generation older of each
> - **Tier 3 (Legacy):** IE11, older Android 8, older iOS 13 (only for B2B)

---

## 2.4 AI-Driven Tools — Theoretical Underpinnings

### 2.4.1 Self-Healing Locator Algorithm (Conceptual)

Let $L = \{l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n\}$ be the set of candidate locators PEX/AI tool generates. After a UI change, the tool computes a **similarity score** for each:

$$
S(l_i, l_{current}) = \alpha \cdot \text{AttrMatch} + \beta \cdot \text{VisualMatch} + \gamma \cdot \text{DOMPath}
$$

Where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$. The locator with the **highest S** above a threshold (typically 0.75) is auto-selected, and the test continues **without failure**. This is the mathematical heart of self-healing.

### 2.4.2 Visual AI — SSIM-Based Regression

AI visual tools (Applitools-style) compare screenshots using **Structural Similarity Index (SSIM)**:

$$
\text{SSIM}(x, y) = \frac{(2\mu_x \mu_y + C_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + C_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_y^2 + C_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + C_2)}
$$

Where:
- $\mu_x, \mu_y$ = mean pixel intensities of baseline ($x$) and current ($y$) images
- $\sigma_{xy}$ = cross-covariance
- $C_1, C_2$ = stability constants to prevent division by zero

A test **passes** if $\text{SSIM} \geq 0.95$ (the AI-typical threshold) and **fails** otherwise.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Use Case | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Branch Coverage | $BC = \frac{B_{ex}}{B_{tot}} \times 100$ | PEX output evaluation | $\geq 90\%$ target |
| Naive Responsive Matrix | $N = D \cdot B \cdot O \cdot P$ | Combinatorial test count | Hundreds–thousands |
| Pairwise Reduction Ratio | $R = \frac{N_{pair}}{N_{naive}}$ | Optimization proof | $0.1 - 0.3$ |
| Self-Healing Threshold | $S \geq T$ (typically $T=0.75$) | AI locator decision | Boolean |
| SSIM Visual Match | $\text{SSIM} \geq 0.95$ | Visual regression | Boolean |
| Test Effectiveness | $TE = \frac{D_{found}}{D_{total}} \times 100$ | Overall quality gate | $\geq 95\%$ industry target |
| Defect Detection Rate | $DDR = \frac{D_{found}}{T_{executed}}$ | Tool comparison | Higher = better |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This combined testing stack is the **de-facto QA pipeline** in modern CI/CD environments (Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) for any customer-facing web application. The same architecture underpins testing at Microsoft, Amazon, Flipkart, and Infosys delivery pipelines.
