# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Case Study — Implementation Walkthrough

## 3.1 Case Study Scenario Definition

> **Scenario — "QuickCart" Online Grocery Web App**
>
> A B2C e-commerce startup, *QuickCart*, has developed a web application that must:
> 1. Allow user registration and login
> 2. Display a product catalog responsive to mobile/tablet/desktop
> 3. Process cart additions and apply discount coupons
> 4. Calculate shipping charges based on the user's **PIN code** (Indian postal code)
> 5. Integrate with a third-party payment gateway (Razorpay)
>
> The QA team is **3 testers**, timeline is **4 weeks**, and they must demonstrate **defect detection coverage across all three paradigms** to their client.

This is a **realistic, KTU-defensible scenario** combining all three testing types plus the two tool classes.

---

## 3.2 STEP-BY-STEP: Black-Box Testing Implementation (Week 1)

### Step 1 — Extract Test Basis
The QA lead extracts test conditions directly from the **SRS document**, user stories in the product backlog, and the UI wireframes in Figma. No source code is read.

### Step 2 — Identify Test Cases via Equivalence Partitioning (EP) and Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

For the *"Apply Coupon"* function, EP yields:

$$
\text{Valid EP: } [10\% \text{ off}, 50\% \text{ off}, 90\% \text{ off}]
$$
$$
\text{Invalid EP: } [-5\% \text{ off}, 105\% \text{ off}, \text{non-numeric}, \text{empty string}]
$$

BVA values (around the 90% upper boundary):

$$
\{89\%, 90\%, 91\%, 100\%, \text{null}, \text{whitespace}\}
$$

### Step 3 — Author Test Cases in Natural Language
Test cases are documented in a Test Management Tool (e.g., TestRail, Zephyr):

| TC ID | Test Description | Input | Expected Output | Actual | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TC_BB_01 | Apply 10% coupon SAVE10 | Cart ₹1000, Code SAVE10 | Discount ₹100 | — | Pending |
| TC_BB_02 | Apply 90% coupon MAX90 | Cart ₹1000, Code MAX90 | Discount ₹900 | — | Pending |
| TC_BB_03 | Apply 105% coupon (invalid) | Cart ₹1000, Code 105% | Error "Invalid coupon" | — | Pending |
| TC_BB_04 | Apply expired coupon EXP2023 | Cart ₹1000, Code EXP2023 | Error "Coupon expired" | — | Pending |

### Step 4 — Execute Tests Manually + via Selenium
Manual execution for usability, Selenium WebDriver for regression. **No code is opened.**

### Step 5 — Log Defects
A defect is logged for each failed TC with **steps to reproduce, expected vs actual, severity, priority, and screenshot**. Defect ID convention: `QC-BB-001`, `QC-BB-002`, …

> [!NOTE]
> **Black-Box Defects Typically Found in QuickCart:** Missing validation on coupon field, wrong discount arithmetic, no error message for empty cart, etc.

---

## 3.3 STEP-BY-STEP: Grey-Box Testing Implementation (Week 2)

The grey-box phase targets **integration seams** — places where the front-end, back-end API, and database interact.

### Step 1 — Acquire Architectural Knowledge
The tester reviews the **Swagger API documentation** and **ER diagram** (not the source code). Key insights:
- `POST /api/cart/add` → writes to `cart_items` table with FK to `users(id)`
- `POST /api/coupon/apply` → reads from `coupons` table, returns discount %
- `POST /api/payment/charge` → calls Razorpay, writes to `payments` table

### Step 2 — Design DB-Aware Test Cases
Knowing the schema allows targeted checks:
- **TC_GB_01:** Apply a valid coupon to a cart, then **query the `coupons` table** to verify `usage_count` incremented by 1.
- **TC_GB_02:** Submit payment with a malformed API key, then verify the `payments` table contains a `failed` status row (not missing).
- **TC_GB_03:** Concurrent coupon usage — fire 50 simultaneous requests with the same single-use coupon, verify only **1 succeeds** at the DB level (race condition check).

### Step 3 — Use PEX as a Grey-Box Enabler
The tester cannot read the source code, but the **lead developer** runs PEX on the compiled `CouponService.dll` to generate a PexFactory unit test class. The PEX output is then handed to the QA engineer as **a set of "stress inputs"** — the boundary values and edge cases PEX discovered (e.g., negative quantities, special characters in coupon codes, integer overflow in discount calculation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the critical KTU grey-box insight:**
> The QA tester uses PEX-generated inputs **without understanding the internals that produced them** — perfectly preserving the grey-box boundary.

### Step 4 — Execute via Postman + SQL Client
Postman sends the API requests; DBeaver / pgAdmin verifies DB state. Test results are logged in TestRail with both API and DB evidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Grey-Box Defects Typically Found in QuickCart:** Foreign-key violation on deleted user, race condition on coupon, payment logged twice on retry, API returning 200 OK but DB not updated (transactional integrity bug).

---

## 3.4 STEP-BY-STEP: Responsive Testing Implementation (Week 3)

### Step 1 — Build the Device × Browser Matrix
Using the pairwise formula from Section 2:

| Device Class | Resolution | OS | Browsers to Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| iPhone 15 | 393×852 | iOS 17 | Safari, Chrome |
| Pixel 7 | 412×915 | Android 14 | Chrome, Firefox |
| iPad Air | 820×1180 | iPadOS 17 | Safari |
| Desktop FHD | 1920×1080 | Win 11 / macOS | Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari |

Total pairwise combinations: ~14 test cases (vs 48 naive).

### Step 2 — Execute Functional + Visual Tests at Each Breakpoint
For each combination:
- Open the QuickCart URL
- Verify the **hamburger menu** appears at $< 768$px
- Verify the **product grid** is 2-column at tablet, 1-column at mobile, 4-column at desktop
- Verify all CTA buttons remain tappable (min 44×44 px per Apple HIG)
- Verify the **payment iframe** renders without horizontal scroll

### Step 3 — Apply AI Visual Regression (Applitools Eyes)
A baseline screenshot is captured at each viewport. Subsequent runs are compared using SSIM (Section 2.4.2). Any $\text{SSIM} < 0.95$ region is auto-flagged as a visual regression.

### Step 4 — Performance Check at Mobile Resolutions
Using Lighthouse or WebPageTest:
- First Contentful Paint (FCP) $\leq 1.8s$ on simulated 4G
- Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) $\leq 2.5s$
- Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) $\leq 0.1$

### Step 5 — Cross-Browser Defect Logging
Example defects: "Date picker renders incorrectly in Firefox mobile", "Pay button is cut off on iPhone SE (320px width)".

---

## 3.5 STEP-BY-STEP: AI-Driven Tool Integration (Throughout)

### Step 1 — Replace Brittle Selenium Scripts with Testim.io
A QA tester **records** the "Add to Cart" user journey. Testim's AI engine generates multiple locator strategies (XPath, CSS, text-match, visual-match) and **self-heals** when the "Add to Cart" button's `id` changes from `btn-add-cart` to `add-to-cart-button`.

### Step 2 — Use Mabl for Auto-Generated Regression Suite
Mabl watches the application over a week of CI runs, learns the **typical user flows**, and **auto-generates a baseline regression suite** that the team reviews and approves.

### Step 3 — Visual AI in CI Pipeline
The Applitools Eyes SDK is integrated into the Selenium/Mabl pipeline. Every PR triggers a visual diff. PRs that introduce an unexpected visual change are blocked from merging.

### Step 4 — Predictive Defect Analytics
The AI tool (e.g., Sealights, Launchable) analyzes the git commit history and code-churn metrics to predict which **3 of 47 modules** are most likely to harbor defects. The team **focuses exploratory testing** on those 3 modules — achieving ~80% of defects found in 20% of the test effort (Pareto principle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Expression of AI-Driven Efficiency:**

$$
\text{Test Effort Efficiency} = \frac{D_{found} \times \text{Criticality}}{T_{executed} \times C_{per\_test}}
$$

Where AI-driven prioritization **maximizes the numerator and minimizes the denominator simultaneously**, yielding $\geq 3\times$ efficiency over random or full-suite execution.

---

## 3.6 Consolidated Test Report Structure (KTU Exam Tip)

The final case study deliverable — which students must know how to structure — includes:

1. **Test Plan** — Scope, approach, tools, schedule
2. **Test Cases** — Organized by test type
3. **Defect Log** — Severity, priority, status, owner
4. **Coverage Report** — Branch coverage from PEX, viewport coverage from responsive, API coverage from grey-box
5. **Test Summary Metrics** — TE, DDR, mean time to detect (MTTD)
6. **AI Tool Insights** — Self-heal events, visual regressions blocked, predictive hotspot list
7. **Lessons Learned** — What worked, what didn't, tool-licensing cost analysis

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Test Orchestration Flow (Mermaid)

The following diagram maps how the **three test paradigms and two tool classes** interact inside a CI/CD pipeline for the QuickCart case study.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[QuickCart Source Code + SRS] --> B[CI/CD Pipeline Trigger - PR or Nightly Build]
    B --> C{PEX Symbolic Execution Phase}
    C --> D[PexFactory DLL - Generated Boundary Inputs]
    D --> E[Grey-Box Test Suite - Postman + DB Client]
    A --> F[Manual Functional Authoring]
    F --> G[Black-Box Test Suite - Selenium + TestRail]
    A --> H[AI Test Recorder - Testim.io + Mabl]
    H --> I[Self-Healing Regression Suite]
    B --> J[Responsive Matrix Engine - 14 Combinations]
    J --> K[AI Visual Regression - Applitools SSIM Check]
    E --> L[Defect Aggregation Dashboard]
    G --> L
    I --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Pass or Fail Decision}
    M -->|All Pass| N[Merge to Main Branch + Deploy to Staging]
    M -->|Any Fail| O[Block PR + Notify Dev Team + Open Jira Tickets]
    N --> P[Production Deployment via Blue-Green Release]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The diagram shows a *fan-in* at the Defect Aggregation Dashboard (node L) — every test type feeds defects into a single source of truth, which is the KTU-recommended best practice for case-study reporting.

---

## 4.2 PEX Internal Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In1[Concrete Input Vector] --> P1[Concrete Execution Engine]
    P1 --> P2[Path Recorder]
    P2 --> P3[Branch Condition Collector]
    P3 --> P4[Z3 SMT Constraint Solver]
    P4 --> P5[Negated Path Generator]
    P5 --> P6[Symbolic Executor]
    P6 --> P7{New Path or Dead End}
    P7 -->|New Path| P8[Concrete Input Vector - Updated]
    P8 --> P1
    P7 -->|Exhausted| P9[Test Suite Minimizer]
    P9 --> P10[Final PexFactory Test Class]
    P10 --> Out1[Output: High-Coverage Parameterized Unit Tests]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** The loop between nodes P1, P8, and back to P1 is the **concolic (concrete + symbolic) execution loop** — the mathematical core that makes PEX reach near-100% branch coverage.

---

## 4.3 Responsive Testing Matrix Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start - Define Application Type] --> Q1{Native App or Web App}
    Q1 -->|Native App| A1[Use Appium + Real Device Cloud - BrowserStack or SauceLabs]
    Q1 -->|Web App| Q2{Needs Cross-Browser}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Selenium Grid + Playwright + Applitools Visual AI]
    Q2 -->|No - Single Browser| A3[Chrome DevTools Responsive Mode - 5 Breakpoints]
    A1 --> R1[Record Metrics - FPS, Touch Latency, Battery]
    A2 --> R2[Run Pairwise Matrix - All Pairs Algorithm]
    A3 --> R3[Capture Visual Baselines per Breakpoint]
    R1 --> M[Consolidated Responsive Report]
    R2 --> M
    R3 --> M
    M --> END[Defects Logged + SSIM Diff Captured]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This decision tree is the kind of structured diagram KTU examiners award full marks to in 14-mark questions. The branching logic demonstrates *test strategy selection reasoning*, not just tool listing.

---

## 4.4 AI-Driven Self-Healing Locator Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Test Script Starts] --> Y{Original Locator Resolves}
    Y -->|Yes| Z[Continue Test Execution]
    Y -->|No| AA[Trigger AI Self-Healing Engine]
    AA --> AB[Generate Alternative Locator Candidates]
    AB --> AC[Compute Similarity Score per Candidate]
    AC --> AD{Max Score Above Threshold T = 0.75}
    AD -->|Yes| AE[Auto-Replace Locator + Log Heal Event]
    AD -->|No| AF[Fail Test + Screenshot + Alert QA]
    AE --> Z
    AF --> AG[Create Self-Healing Investigation Ticket]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Black-Box Testing and Grey-Box Testing with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Black-Box Testing | Grey-Box Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge of internals** | None — only SRS/spec | Partial — architecture, DB schema, API map |
| **Test design basis** | Requirements, user stories | Requirements + internal structure insights |
| **Tester profile** | QA analyst, domain expert | Hybrid engineer, SDET |
| **Tool examples** | Selenium, QTP, JMeter | Postman + DB client, Burp Suite |
| **Real-world example** | A bank customer testing the ATM withdrawal flow without knowing the cash-dispensing algorithm | A security tester using the bank's published API documentation to craft SQL-injection payloads without reading the bank's source code |
| **Defect focus** | Functional correctness, missing features | Integration seams, security, data flow |

**Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for each side of the table + 1 mark for valid real-world example → **3 marks total**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Microsoft PEX? List its two main capabilities relevant to test automation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**PEX (Microsoft Pex)** is a white-box automated test-generation tool for .NET that uses **Dynamic Symbolic Execution (concolic testing)** to produce a high-coverage parameterized unit-test suite for a given method.

**Two Main Capabilities:**
1. **Automated Test Input Generation** — PEX automatically explores all branches of a method and generates minimal input sets that maximize branch coverage (often >90%).
2. **Parameterized Unit Testing** — PEX outputs a single parameterized test method (`[PexMethod]`) that runs the same logic against all generated inputs, enabling **regression-safe** unit tests with no duplication.

*(Optional bonus point for naming the constraint solver: Z3 SMT solver from Microsoft Research.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

**Q.A (a)** — *For an online shopping web application like QuickCart, design a Black-Box testing strategy. List 8 test cases using Equivalence Partitioning and Boundary Value Analysis for the "Apply Discount Coupon" function. Justify why black-box alone is insufficient.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Inputs:** Coupon code (string), Discount percentage (numeric), Cart total (numeric), User login state (boolean).

**Step 2 — Equivalence Classes:**

$$
\text{Valid EP} = \{10\%, 25\%, 50\%, 90\%\} \quad \text{Invalid EP} = \{-5\%, 105\%, \text{empty}, \text{alpha}, \text{expired}\}
$$

**Step 3 — Boundary Values:** $\{0\%, 1\%, 89\%, 90\%, 91\%, 100\%, 101\%\}$

**Step 4 — Eight Black-Box Test Cases:**

| TC | Input | Expected Output |
|---|---|---|
| TC1 | Cart ₹2000, Coupon 10% SAVE10 | Discount ₹200, Final ₹1800 |
| TC2 | Cart ₹2000, Coupon 0% ZERO | Discount ₹0, Final ₹2000 (or "Invalid coupon") |
| TC3 | Cart ₹2000, Coupon 100% FREE | Discount ₹2000, Final ₹0 |
| TC4 | Cart ₹2000, Coupon 101% OVER | Error "Invalid coupon percentage" |
| TC5 | Cart ₹2000, Coupon -5% NEG | Error "Invalid coupon percentage" |
| TC6 | Cart ₹2000, Coupon "abcd" (alpha) | Error "Coupon code not found" |
| TC7 | Cart ₹2000, Coupon "" (empty) | Error "Please enter a coupon code" |
| TC8 | Cart ₹2000, Expired Coupon EXP2023 | Error "Coupon has expired" |

**Why Black-Box Alone Is Insufficient (justification for 2 marks):**
Black-box cannot detect:
- **Race conditions** when the same coupon is applied concurrently (needs DB awareness → grey-box)
- **Transaction rollback failures** (needs API/DB integration test)
- **SQL injection vulnerabilities** in the coupon field (needs white-box or security-grey-box)
- **Dead code paths** (e.g., a 110% branch in source) — needs PEX/white-box

**Mark Split:** Test basis identification (1) + EPs (1) + BVAs (1) + 8 TCs (2) + Justification (2) = **7 marks**

---

**Q.A (b)** — *Explain the architecture of Microsoft PEX with a block diagram. Show how PEX-generated test inputs can be used by a Grey-Box tester without violating the grey-box principle.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**PEX Architecture (4 components):**

1. **Concrete Execution Engine** — Invokes the target method with concrete inputs and records the path.
2. **Symbolic Execution Engine** — Replaces concrete values with symbolic variables and tracks constraints at each branch.
3. **Z3 SMT Constraint Solver** — Microsoft Research's constraint solver that negates branch conditions to find new input vectors.
4. **Test Suite Minimizer** — Removes redundant inputs while preserving branch coverage; outputs the final `PexFactory` test class.

**Block Diagram (textual reconstruction for answer sheet):**
```
[Method Under Test]
        ↓
[Concrete Execution] → [Path Recorder] → [Constraint Builder]
        ↓                                      ↓
   [SMT Solver Z3]  ← ← ← ← ← ← ← [Symbolic Negation]
        ↓
[New Inputs] → [Re-execute] (loop until all branches covered)
        ↓
[Test Suite Minimizer] → [PexFactory Output]
```

**How PEX Inputs Respect Grey-Box (3 marks):**
- The grey-box tester **receives the PexFactory DLL** from the developer as a black-box artifact.
- The tester uses the generated inputs (`PexArguments`) as **data points** in Postman/SoapUI API calls.
- The tester **does not read the source code** that produced those inputs — only the inputs themselves.
- The tester's decisions (which API to call, which DB table to verify) are still driven by their **architectural knowledge** (Swagger + ER diagram).
- This preserves the **grey-box boundary**: tester has *partial* knowledge, not full white-box visibility.

**Mark Split:** Architecture (2) + Block diagram (1) + Grey-box justification (3) + Diagram clarity (1) = **7 marks**

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.B (a)** — *Design a Responsive Testing matrix for a B2C e-commerce web app covering mobile, tablet, and desktop. Use the pairwise (all-pairs) technique. Compute the matrix size and justify your choice.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Factors & Levels:**

| Factor | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 | Level 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Device Class | iPhone 15 | Pixel 7 | iPad Air | Desktop FHD |
| OS | iOS 17 | Android 14 | iPadOS 17 | Win 11 |
| Browser | Safari | Chrome | Firefox | Edge |

**Step 2 — Naive Cartesian Count:**

$$
N_{naive} = 4 \times 4 \times 4 = 64 \text{ combinations}
$$

**Step 3 — Pairwise (All-Pairs) Count:**
Using a pairwise algorithm (e.g., PICT from Microsoft, or `allpairs` library), the matrix is reduced to **16 test cases** (a 75% reduction) while still guaranteeing that **every pair of factor levels** appears together in at least one test case.

**Step 4 — Final Matrix (abbreviated):**

| TC | Device | OS | Browser | Viewport (px) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | iPhone 15 | iOS 17 | Safari | 393×852 |
| 2 | iPhone 15 | Android 14 | Chrome | 393×852 |
| 3 | iPhone 15 | iPadOS 17 | Firefox | 393×852 |
| 4 | iPhone 15 | Win 11 | Edge | 393×852 |
| 5 | Pixel 7 | iOS 17 | Firefox | 412×915 |
| 6 | Pixel 7 | Android 14 | Edge | 412×915 |
| 7 | Pixel 7 | iPadOS 17 | Safari | 412×915 |
| 8 | Pixel 7 | Win 11 | Chrome | 412×915 |
| 9 | iPad Air | iOS 17 | Edge | 820×1180 |
| 10 | iPad Air | Android 14 | Safari | 820×1180 |
| 11 | iPad Air | iPadOS 17 | Chrome | 820×1180 |
| 12 | iPad Air | Win 11 | Firefox | 820×1180 |
| 13 | Desktop FHD | iOS 17 | Chrome | 1920×1080 |
| 14 | Desktop FHD | Android 14 | Firefox | 1920×1080 |
| 15 | Desktop FHD | iPadOS 17 | Edge | 1920×1080 |
| 16 | Desktop FHD | Win 11 | Safari | 1920×1080 |

**Justification (2 marks):** Pairwise is the industry standard (NIST recommendation) because most defects are caused by **single-factor or two-factor interactions**, not higher-order interactions. The 4× reduction in test count translates directly into a 75% saving in CI pipeline execution time.

**Mark Split:** Factor identification (1) + Naive count (1) + Pairwise count (1) + Matrix table (2) + Justification (2) = **7 marks**

**Q.B (b)** — *Explain any four AI-driven capabilities that modern test automation tools provide. How do these capabilities improve on traditional Selenium-based automation?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Four AI-Driven Capabilities:**

1. **Self-Healing Locators** — When a UI element's attributes change (e.g., button `id` renamed), the AI tool computes a similarity score (XPath + CSS + visual + DOM-path) and auto-selects a new locator if the score exceeds a threshold (typically 0.75). **Traditional Selenium fails immediately on the first change.** Improvement: ~70% reduction in test-maintenance effort.

2. **Visual AI Regression** — Tools like Applitools Eyes use **SSIM-based pixel comparison** instead of brittle pixel-exact comparison. **Traditional Selenium cannot detect visual regressions** at all. Improvement: Catches unintended CSS/layout changes that functional tests miss.

3. **Auto Test Generation from User Stories** — NLP models parse "As a user, I want to reset my password" and auto-generate executable test steps. **Traditional Selenium requires hand-coding every test step.** Improvement: 5–10× faster test authoring for new features.

4. **Predictive Test Selection** — ML models analyze git history, code churn, and past defect density to predict the **top 5–10% of tests** most likely to find a defect in a given change. **Traditional Selenium runs the entire suite on every PR.** Improvement: 60–80% reduction in CI execution time with no loss of defect-detection power.

**Comparative Table (1 mark):**

| Dimension | Traditional Selenium | AI-Driven Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Locator stability | Brittle | Self-healing |
| Visual diff | Not supported | SSIM-based |
| Test authoring | Manual coding | NLP-driven |
| CI cost | Full-suite run | Predictive selection |
| Maintenance effort | High (50% of QA time) | Low (~15%) |

**Mark Split:** Each capability explained (1 × 4 = 4) + Comparative table (1) + Quantified improvements (2) = **7 marks**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - ⚠️ **Do not** list tools without showing *how* they are integrated into a process. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for "tool dumping."
> - ⚠️ **Do not** confuse *PEX* (Microsoft's symbolic-execution tool for .NET) with *PEK* (Performance Engineering Kit) or *PyEx* (a Python symbolic executor). Spelling and tool-name accuracy matter.
> - ⚠️ **Do not** say "AI will replace testers." Examiners expect *AI augments testers* — always frame the answer with the human-in-the-loop principle.
> - ⚠️ **Do not** skip the *justification* sub-part in 14-mark questions. Justification carries 2–3 marks and is the most-skipped section by average students.
> - ⚠️ **Do not** present the responsive matrix as a flat device list. Examiners expect the *factor-level structure* and the *pairwise algorithm invocation* explicitly.

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Black-Box** = no code knowledge, SRS-driven; catch **functional and missing-feature** defects.
- **Grey-Box** = architecture-aware (Swagger, ER diagram); catch **integration, security, race-condition** defects.
- **Responsive** = device × browser matrix; catch **layout, breakpoint, cross-browser** defects.
- **PEX** = Microsoft white-box tool using **concolic symbolic execution + Z3 SMT solver**; outputs `PexFactory` with high branch coverage; usable in grey-box as a *generated-input source*.
- **AI-Driven Tools** = self-healing locators, visual AI (SSIM), NLP test generation, predictive test selection.
- **Pairwise reduction** typically cuts the responsive matrix by 70–90% vs naive Cartesian product.
- **CI/CD integration** is the deliverable expectation — testers must show pipeline placement, not isolated tool use.
- **Case-study answers** must always follow the structure: **Scenario → Test Strategy → Tool Selection → Test Cases → Defect Log → Coverage Report → Lessons Learned**.
- **Branch coverage formula** $BC = \frac{B_{ex}}{B_{tot}} \times 100$ — PEX typically achieves 85–98% on small-to-medium methods.
- **SSIM threshold** of 0.95 is the de-facto standard for AI visual regression tools.
- **QuickCart**-style scenario is a KTU-favorite — keep a reusable template ready for any application domain (banking, healthcare, LMS, food delivery).
- **PEX is a .NET-only tool** — for Java, the equivalent is **EvoSuite**; for Python, **Pynguin**; for C/C++, **KLEE**. Examiners occasionally ask for cross-language mapping.
- **The four-class defect taxonomy** (functional, integration, performance, visual) maps 1-to-1 to the four testing paradigms (black, grey, responsive, AI-visual).
- **Always cite the tool version and license model** (e.g., "PEX is now distributed as IntelliTest in Visual Studio Enterprise 2022") — shows engineering maturity.
